effects.description: The Ikea effect posits that individuals place a disproportionately higher value on products they have partially or fully assembled themselves, compared to objectively similar items assembled by others. This increased valuation is thought to stem from the psychological investment of labor and the sense of ownership and competence derived from creation.

effects.name: Endowment effect
effects.description: The endowment effect describes the phenomenon where individuals place a higher value on an item once they own it compared to its value before ownership. This leads to a discrepancy where people demand a higher price to sell an item they possess than they would be willing to pay to acquire the same item. The proposed mechanism is an almost instantaneous shift in reference point and perceived value upon gaining possession.

effects.name: Choice overload
effects.description: The choice overload effect proposes that while some choice is desirable, an excessive number of options can lead to negative outcomes for consumers. This includes reduced engagement, increased decision difficulty, lower satisfaction with the chosen item, and a decreased likelihood of making a purchase. The proposed mechanism is that too many choices overwhelm cognitive processing, leading to avoidance or regret.

effects.name: Mate guarding
effects.description: The 'Mate Guarding' effect proposes that women strategically use conspicuous luxury products to signal to other women that they have a devoted partner. This display of high-value possessions is hypothesized to deter potential female rivals by indicating a committed relationship, thereby reducing competition for their partner.

effects.name: Scarcity effect - _Overborrowing_
effects.description: The scarcity effect in financial decision-making posits that individuals experiencing perceived financial scarcity are more prone to making suboptimal loan decisions, specifically overborrowing. This effect is driven by a "triple scarcity effect" where internal influences (like perceived consequences) and external influences (like decreased lending options) combine to negatively impact financial perceptions and choices, particularly for those already at high financial risk.

effects.name: Scarcity effect - _Resource allocation_
effects.description: The scarcity effect in resource allocation posits that during periods of poor economic conditions, parents tend to favor daughters over sons in resource distribution. This is theorized to occur because economic scarcity alters the perceived reproductive value of girls relative to boys, leading parents to invest more in female offspring to maximize reproductive success.

effects.name: Scarcity effect - _Planning_
effects.description: The scarcity effect in planning posits that when individuals perceive a resource as limited, they shift their coping strategies. Specifically, they are predicted to engage more in "priority planning" (sacrificing less important goals) rather than "efficiency planning" (stretching the resource). This shift is proposed to occur because priority planning yields larger one-time savings, despite feeling more costly due to necessary trade-offs.

effects.name: Scarcity effect - _Competition/threat_
effects.description: The scarcity effect (competition/threat) proposes that exposure to advertisements for products with limited availability can make consumers perceive other shoppers as competitive threats. This perception, in turn, is theorized to physiologically prepare individuals for aggression, leading to increased aggressive behaviors even outside the specific purchasing context.

effects.name: Scarcity effect - _Brand attitudes_
effects.description: The 'Did they earn it?' effect proposes that observing luxury brand consumers who acquired their wealth through unearned financial resources (e.g., inheritance) negatively impacts observers' attitudes towards those luxury brands. This effect is particularly strong among observers who highly value fairness. The proposed mechanism is that unearned luxury consumption reduces perceptions of the consumer's prestige, which in turn diminishes the perceived prestige and evaluation of the luxury brand itself.

effects.name: Scarcity effect - _Product use creativity_
effects.description: The scarcity effect on product use creativity posits that making resource scarcity salient enhances an individual's creativity when using products. This occurs because scarcity activates a "constraint mindset," which reduces functional fixedness and encourages thinking beyond a product's traditional uses, thereby leading to more novel solutions.

effects.name: Scarcity effect - _Wage rates_
effects.description: The scarcity effect on wage rates proposes that the implied hourly wage a person is willing to accept differs depending on how the question is framed. Specifically, when asked to state the maximum time they would spend for a given amount of money (Time-Elicit, TEL), individuals report a higher implied wage rate compared to when asked the minimum money they would accept for a given time (Money-Elicit, MEL). This asymmetry is attributed to time scarcity being weighted more heavily in TEL judgments.

effects.name: Scarcity effect - _Selfishness_
effects.description: The Scarcity effect - Selfishness posits that reminders of resource scarcity activate a competitive orientation, leading individuals to prioritize their own welfare. This competitive mindset can manifest as increased selfish behavior, particularly among those with a proself (low social value) orientation. The effect also suggests that scarcity can promote seemingly generous behaviors if such actions are perceived to advance personal gains.

effects.name: Scarcity effect - _Preference for material goods_
effects.description: The scarcity effect in consumer preferences suggests that when individuals feel financially constrained, they tend to prioritize material goods over experiences. This preference shift is proposed to occur because financial constraints heighten concerns about the lasting utility and longevity of purchases, making tangible items seem like a more secure investment than fleeting experiences.

effects.name: Scarcity effect - _Preference polarisation_
effects.description: The scarcity effect on preference polarization proposes that when consumers perceive an item as scarce, their preferences for that item become more extreme. Specifically, perceived scarcity is thought to increase arousal, which in turn amplifies the difference in liking between a preferred item and less preferred items within a product category. This heightened polarization then leads to a greater likelihood of choosing the most-preferred option.

effects.name: Product size and status
effects.description: The "Product size and status" effect posits that individuals experiencing low power tend to choose larger-sized food and drink products. This behavior is theorized to be a compensatory mechanism, allowing them to signal higher status to others through their consumption choices.

effects.name: Less-is-better effect
effects.description: The less-is-better effect describes a phenomenon where individuals sometimes prefer a smaller quantity of a good over a larger quantity, particularly when the smaller quantity appears more visually appealing or complete. This occurs when evaluations are separate (e.g., comparing two items in isolation) rather than joint, leading to a focus on a salient positive attribute of the smaller item (like its fullness) over its objective lower value.

effects.name: Left digit bias
effects.description: The left-digit bias describes how the leftmost digit of a number disproportionately influences perception and decision-making, even when the numerical difference is small. For instance, consumers perceive a larger difference between $4.00 and $2.99 than between $4.01 and $3.00, primarily due to the change in the leading digit. This cognitive heuristic suggests that individuals anchor on the most significant digit, affecting judgments of magnitude, value, and quality.

effects.name: One mind per hemisphere
effects.description: The split-brain phenomenon refers to the behavioral and cognitive changes observed after surgical severing of the corpus callosum, which connects the two cerebral hemispheres. Key observations include independent processing of information within each hemisphere, specific response-visual field interactions, and difficulties comparing stimuli across the midline. These findings were initially interpreted as evidence for the emergence of two independent minds or split consciousness within a single brain.

effects.name: Hydrocephaly
effects.description: The Hydrocephaly effect refers to the surprising observation that some individuals with severe hydrocephalus—a condition involving massive cerebrospinal fluid accumulation and apparent significant reduction in brain tissue volume—can nonetheless exhibit normal or near-normal cognitive function. This phenomenon challenges conventional understanding of the minimum brain mass required for complex cognitive abilities, suggesting remarkable brain plasticity or compensatory mechanisms.

effects.name: Readiness potentials
effects.description: Readiness Potentials (RPs) are brain signals, typically measured with EEG, that precede voluntary movements by several hundred milliseconds. They are thought to reflect the brain's preparation for action, suggesting that neural activity related to movement initiation begins before an individual is consciously aware of their decision to move. This pre-conscious neural activity has been interpreted by some as challenging traditional notions of free will.

effects.name: Left-brain vs. Right-brain Hypothesis
effects.description: The 'Left-brain vs. Right-brain Hypothesis' posits that individuals exhibit a dominant brain hemisphere, influencing their personality and cognitive style. Specifically, it suggests the left hemisphere governs logical, analytical, and verbal skills, while the right hemisphere handles creative, intuitive, and spatial abilities. This popular concept implies that an individual's overall cognitive profile is dictated by the relative strength or preference for one hemisphere's functions over the other.

effects.name: Oxytocin on trust
effects.description: The effect proposes that intranasal administration of the hormone oxytocin can enhance an individual's willingness to trust strangers in social interactions. This is often observed in laboratory settings using economic games, where oxytocin is hypothesized to reduce social risk aversion and promote prosocial behaviors.

effects.name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between behavioural activation and white matter integrity
effects.description: This effect proposes that individual differences in a person's sensitivity to reward (Behavioral Activation System - BAS-Total) and their tendency to seek out rewarding experiences (BAS-Fun) are positively associated with the integrity of specific white matter pathways in the brain. Specifically, higher scores on these behavioral activation measures are hypothesized to correlate with greater white matter integrity, as measured by diffusion tensor imaging (DTI). The proposed mechanism is that stronger white matter connections facilitate the neural processes underlying reward-seeking behaviors.

effects.name: Structural brain behaviour correlations - the association between social network size and grey matter volume
effects.description: This effect proposes that the size of an individual's online social network, specifically the number of Facebook friends, is positively correlated with grey matter volume in specific brain regions. These regions include the left middle temporal gyrus, right superior temporal sulcus, entorhinal cortex, and bilateral amygdala, which are implicated in social perception and associative memory.

effects.name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between distractibility and grey matter volume
effects.description: The effect proposes that individual differences in self-reported distractibility are reflected in specific brain structures. Specifically, higher distractibility is associated with increased grey matter volume in the left superior parietal lobule (SPL) and decreased grey matter volume in the medial pre-frontal cortex (mPFC), suggesting a neural basis for attentional control.

effects.name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between attention and cortical thickness
effects.description: This effect posits that individual differences in specific attentional functions are linked to variations in brain structure. Specifically, higher executive control is associated with thinner cortical regions in the left anterior cingulate cortex, left superior temporal gyrus, and right middle temporal gyrus. Similarly, better alerting abilities are claimed to correlate with thinner cortex in the left superior parietal lobule.

effects.name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between control over speed/accuracy of perceptual decisions and white matter tracts strength
effects.description: The effect proposes that individual differences in the ability to flexibly adjust the balance between response speed and accuracy during perceptual decisions are linked to the strength of specific white matter connections in the brain. Specifically, stronger structural connections between the right presupplementary motor area (pre-SMA) and the right striatum are hypothesized to enable greater control over these speed-accuracy trade-offs. This mechanism suggests a cortico-striatal pathway for adaptive behavioral control.

effects.name: Structural brain-behaviour associations - the association between executive function and grey matter volume
effects.description: The effect posits that individual differences in executive function, specifically as measured by the Trail Making Test, are associated with variations in grey matter volume within the rostral dorsal premotor cortex. This suggests that the structural integrity or size of this specific brain region may underpin an individual's capacity for executive control.

effects.name: Fear conditioning - Amygdala
effects.description: Fear conditioning is an associative learning process where a neutral stimulus, when repeatedly paired with an aversive stimulus, comes to elicit a fear response. This effect posits that the amygdala, a brain region critical for emotional processing, is activated during the acquisition of conditioned fear. In humans, this is often investigated using fMRI to observe amygdala responses to conditioned threat cues.

effects.name: Fear conditioning - vmPFC
effects.description: The ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC) is hypothesized to play a crucial role in the extinction of learned fear. Building on animal models, human fMRI studies investigate whether the vmPFC is activated during fear extinction learning and recall, suggesting it mediates the inhibition of fear responses, possibly by modulating amygdala activity.

effects.name: Fear conditioning - Theta oscillations
effects.description: This effect proposes that fear conditioning, a process where a neutral stimulus becomes associated with an aversive outcome, leads to increased theta band oscillatory activity in the prefrontal cortex. This prefrontal theta activity, observed in animal models, is hypothesized to be a neural correlate of fear learning and expression that can be measured using electroencephalography (EEG) in humans.

effects.name: Fear conditioning - Late Positive Potential
effects.description: The Late Positive Potential (LPP) is an event-related brain potential (ERP) component that reflects sustained emotional processing and motivated attention. In fear conditioning paradigms, stimuli associated with an aversive outcome (conditioned stimuli, CS+) are consistently found to elicit larger LPP amplitudes compared to neutral or safe stimuli (CS-). This enhanced LPP is thought to reflect the increased emotional salience and attentional capture by threat-related cues.

effects.name: Fear conditioning - Bradicardia / heart rate modulation
effects.description: Fear-conditioned bradycardia refers to the phenomenon where a neutral stimulus, after being consistently paired with an aversive unconditioned stimulus, comes to elicit a deceleration in heart rate. This physiological response is thought to reflect an orienting or attentional response to the impending threat, preparing the organism for action or information gathering.

effects.name: Bouba/kiki effect
effects.description: The Bouba/kiki effect describes a phenomenon of sound symbolism where humans consistently associate certain speech sounds with specific visual shapes. Typically, the rounded, voiced sounds in words like 'bouba' are matched with curvy, rounded shapes, while the sharp, unvoiced sounds in words like 'kiki' are matched with spiky, angular shapes. This non-arbitrary mapping is thought to arise from crossmodal correspondences between auditory and visual properties.

effects.name: Human freezing behaviour
effects.description: The human freezing response describes a physiological reaction to perceived threat, characterized by reduced body motion (postural sway) and decreased heart rate (bradycardia). This defensive behavior, observed in animals facing predators, is proposed to be a conserved mechanism in humans, serving as an indicator of fear and anxiety in response to social threat cues.

effects.name: Glucose amplification of cortisol stress reactivity
effects.description: The Glucose Amplification of Cortisol Stress Reactivity effect proposes that consuming glucose after a period of fasting enhances the body's cortisol response to subsequent psychological or physical stressors. This effect suggests that the availability of metabolic energy, specifically from glucose, plays a crucial role in modulating the activity of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis during stress. The original research indicated that low blood glucose levels might attenuate stress-induced cortisol release, which can be restored or amplified by glucose intake.

effects.name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
effects.description: This effect proposes that specific patterns of resting-state functional connectivity (the synchronized activity between brain regions when the brain is at rest) can serve as a biomarker to distinguish individuals with major depressive disorder (MDD) from healthy controls. The underlying mechanism suggests that MDD is characterized by distinct neural network configurations identifiable through multivariate pattern analysis of fMRI data.

effects.name: Low self-esteem on poor mental health/psychological outcomes
effects.description: The effect posits that low self-esteem is a causal factor leading to various negative psychological and social outcomes. Specifically, it suggests that a lack of self-appreciation can produce self-defeating attitudes, contributing to poor mental health, social difficulties, and engagement in risk behaviors.

effects.name: Rorschach Test
effects.description: The Rorschach Test proposes that individuals' interpretations of ambiguous inkblots reveal unconscious thoughts, personality traits, and underlying psychopathology. The core mechanism is projective, where individuals are believed to project their internal psychological states onto the ambiguous stimuli, allowing trained clinicians to diagnose psychiatric conditions.

effects.name: Lunar effect
effects.description: The Lunar Effect, sometimes referred to as the Transylvania Effect, posits that the full moon phase influences human behavior, leading to an increase in unusual or problematic occurrences such as psychiatric disturbances, criminal activity, or suicides. Proponents often suggest that the moon's gravitational pull, similar to its effect on ocean tides, might subtly impact biological systems or psychological states, leading to these behavioral changes.

effects.name: Lack of a Theory of Mind is universal in autism
effects.description: The "Theory of Mind deficit" hypothesis in autism proposes that individuals with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) have a specific impairment in their ability to attribute mental states (beliefs, desires, intentions) to themselves and others. This deficit was initially suggested to be a core cognitive characteristic underlying the social and communication difficulties observed in autism.

effects.name: Fertility facial-preferences effect
effects.description: The fertility facial-preferences effect posits that women's preferences for male facial masculinity vary across their menstrual cycle. Specifically, it suggests that women in their fertile phase prefer more masculine faces, which is theorized to be an evolved mating strategy to select partners with higher genetic quality when conception is most probable.

effects.name: Dunbar’s number
effects.description: Dunbar's number proposes that humans have a cognitive limit to the number of stable social relationships they can maintain, estimated at approximately 150 individuals. This limit is theorized to be constrained by the size of the neocortex, specifically its ratio to overall brain size, which influences an organism's information-processing capacity.

effects.name: Romantic priming
effects.description: The romantic priming effect proposes that exposure to attractive women increases men's motivation to engage in behaviors associated with mate acquisition and display. Specifically, it suggests that men will exhibit increased conspicuous consumption, a greater tendency to discount future rewards (time discounting), and higher risk-taking when romantically primed. This is theorized to be an evolutionary mechanism to signal status and resources to potential mates.

effects.name: Implicit religious priming
effects.description: The implicit religious priming effect proposes that subtly activating concepts related to God or religion can increase prosocial behavior. This is typically observed in anonymous economic games, where participants allocate more resources to strangers after being exposed to religious cues. The proposed mechanism suggests that these primes activate a sense of being watched by supernatural entities, thereby promoting generosity.

effects.name: Implicit analytic priming
effects.description: The Implicit Analytic Priming effect posits that subtly activating analytic thinking processes can reduce an individual's religious belief. This effect is theorized to occur because analytic thinking, often associated with System II cognition, can override more intuitive, System I-based religious beliefs. For example, exposure to images like Rodin's 'The Thinker' is proposed to trigger this analytic mode, leading to decreased reported religiosity.

effects.name: Menstrual cycle version of the dual-mating-strategy
effects.description: This effect proposes that heterosexual women's mate preferences shift across their menstrual cycle. During their high-fertility ovulatory phase, women are hypothesized to show stronger preferences for men with indicators of good genetic quality, such as masculinity, for uncommitted sexual relationships. At other times in their cycle, they are expected to prefer traits associated with long-term partners. The underlying mechanism is thought to be an evolved dual-mating strategy to maximize reproductive success.

effects.name: Menstrual cycle and lunar influence
effects.description: The lunar influence on the menstrual cycle effect posits that women whose menstrual cycles align closely with the lunar cycle length (approximately 29.5 days) tend to menstruate during the full moon phase. This proposed synchronization suggests a potential link between human reproductive physiology and celestial rhythms, possibly driven by evolutionary factors or environmental cues like moonlight.

effects.name: Large parents have more sons
effects.description: The 'Large parents have more sons' effect, proposed as a generalization of the Trivers-Willard hypothesis, suggests that parents with larger body sizes (taller and heavier) are more likely to have male offspring. This effect posits that parents in better physical condition, indicated by larger size, can invest more resources in sons, who typically have higher reproductive variance and thus offer a greater potential reproductive payoff.

effects.name: Men’s strength in particular predicts opposition to egalitarianism
effects.description: The effect proposes that men's upper-body strength predicts their political views on economic redistribution, specifically leading them to endorse positions that benefit their self-interest. This means stronger men of lower socioeconomic status would support redistribution, while stronger men of higher socioeconomic status would oppose it. This is theorized to stem from an evolved psychology where physical formidability influenced resource acquisition in small-scale groups.

effects.name: Sex differences in mate preferences
effects.description: The effect of sex differences in mate preferences posits that men and women exhibit distinct preferences in potential partners, driven by differing evolutionary selection pressures. Specifically, men are theorized to prioritize attractiveness and youth, while women are theorized to prefer older mates with greater financial prospects. These preferences are expected to be universal across cultures.

effects.name: Men’s preference for competition
effects.description: This effect proposes that men exhibit a stronger preference for competitive environments, such as tournaments, compared to women. The underlying mechanism is often attributed to gender differences in risk-taking, confidence, or an aversion to competition, leading men to seek out competitive situations more readily than women.

effects.name: Orgasm gap
effects.description: The "orgasm gap" describes the consistent finding that, in heterosexual sexual encounters, men report experiencing orgasm significantly more frequently than women. This effect is often attributed to sociocultural factors, such as the prioritization of penile-vaginal intercourse over clitorally-focused stimulation, and differing expectations around sexual pleasure.

effects.name: Sympathetic nervous system activity predicts political ideology
effects.description: This effect proposes that an individual's political ideology is linked to their physiological responses to threatening stimuli. Specifically, conservatives are hypothesized to exhibit stronger physiological reactions, such as increased skin conductance (electrodermal activity), when exposed to threatening images or sounds compared to liberals. This heightened threat sensitivity is suggested to underpin their support for policies that protect the existing social structure.

effects.name: 5-HTT Gene-by-Environment Interaction
effects.description: This effect proposes that an individual's genetic makeup influences their susceptibility to depression following stressful life events. Specifically, a polymorphism in the serotonin transporter gene (5-HTTLPR) is thought to moderate this relationship. Individuals carrying one or two copies of the "short" allele are hypothesized to be more vulnerable to developing depressive symptoms or diagnosable depression when exposed to stress, compared to those homozygous for the "long" allele.

effects.name: Motivational role of L2 vision
effects.description: The "Motivational role of L2 vision" effect posits that an individual's ability to vividly imagine themselves as a successful future user of a second language (L2), often referred to as the "Ideal L2 Self," significantly enhances their motivation and effort in language learning. This vision is believed to act as a powerful motivational force, guiding learning behaviors and improving performance.

effects.name: Flipped learning
effects.description: Flipped learning is an instructional strategy where traditional classroom activities are reversed. Students engage with new content, typically through videos or readings, outside of class, while in-class time is dedicated to active learning, problem-solving, and collaborative activities. The core idea is to shift direct instruction to the individual learning space and transform the group learning space into a dynamic, interactive environment.

effects.name: Mindsets
effects.description: The Mindset effect posits that an individual's belief about the malleability of their intelligence and abilities (growth mindset) versus their fixed nature (fixed mindset) significantly impacts their academic achievement and motivation. Specifically, a growth mindset is theorized to foster learning goals, positive beliefs about effort, and adaptive strategies, leading to improved academic outcomes. Interventions aim to cultivate a growth mindset to enhance student performance, particularly for at-risk populations.

effects.name: First instinct fallacy
effects.description: The first instinct fallacy refers to the common but false belief that one's initial answer on a multiple-choice test is more likely to be correct than a revised answer. Research consistently shows that changing answers typically leads to improved test scores, as changes from incorrect to correct answers outnumber changes from correct to incorrect. This persistent belief is thought to arise from counterfactual thinking, where the regret associated with changing a correct answer to an incorrect one is more salient and memorable than the regret of not changing an incorrect answer.

effects.name: Mirror reading and writing in dyslexia
effects.description: The effect of mirror reading and writing in dyslexia, also known as strephosymbolia, describes a phenomenon where individuals with dyslexia perceive or produce letters, words, or phrases as reversed or transposed. This perceptual or motor difficulty is thought to contribute to reading and writing challenges. The underlying mechanism is often debated, with some theories suggesting issues in visual-spatial processing or the integration of visual and phonetic information.

effects.name: Pen mightier than the screen
effects.description: The 'Pen mightier than the screen' effect proposes that taking notes longhand, rather than on a laptop, leads to more effective learning, particularly for conceptual understanding. This is theorized to occur because longhand note-takers engage in deeper processing by summarizing and rephrasing information, whereas laptop note-takers tend to transcribe lectures verbatim, hindering active cognitive engagement.

effects.name: Sleep-assisted learning
effects.description: Sleep-assisted learning, also known as hypnopedia, is the claim that individuals can acquire and retain new information or modify behaviors by being exposed to stimuli (e.g., audio messages) while asleep. The proposed mechanism suggests that the brain can process and store information even during unconscious states, leading to memory formation or behavioral changes upon waking.

effects.name: Dr. Fox Effect
effects.description: The Dr. Fox Effect describes the phenomenon where students give high ratings to an instructor who delivers a charismatic and entertaining lecture, even if the lecture contains little to no substantive educational content. This suggests that an instructor's presentation style and charisma can significantly influence student evaluations, potentially overshadowing the actual learning material.

effects.name: Stress as the main/sole cause of peptic ulcers
effects.description: The effect posits that chronic emotional and physical stress is the primary cause of peptic ulcers. The proposed mechanism involves stress transmission to the stomach via a hormonal pathway, specifically through the adrenal gland and the hypothalamic-pituitary-gastric axis, leading to ulcer formation.

effects.name: Graphic images and cigarette use
effects.description: This effect proposes that the implementation of graphic warning labels (GWL) on cigarette packages leads to a reduction in smoking prevalence within the population. The underlying mechanism is often assumed to involve increased fear, disgust, and perceived health risks, which in turn motivate smokers to quit or deter non-smokers from starting.

effects.name: Stereotype threat on gender differences in political knowledge
effects.description: The stereotype threat effect suggests that when negative stereotypes about a group's ability are made salient, members of that group may perform worse on tasks related to that stereotype. In the context of political knowledge, this effect posits that making gender stereotypes about women's political knowledge salient can impair women's performance on political knowledge tests, thereby exacerbating observed gender gaps.

effects.name: Avoidance of dissonance-arousing situations
effects.description: This effect proposes an ideological asymmetry in how individuals respond to situations that could cause cognitive dissonance. Specifically, it suggests that conservatives are more strongly motivated than liberals to avoid tasks that challenge their existing political beliefs, especially when they perceive high choice in the situation. This difference is attributed to conservatives' stronger epistemic needs for certainty and closure.

effects.name: Depressed-entitlement effect among women
effects.description: The depressed-entitlement effect posits that women tend to compensate themselves less than men for the same amount of work, particularly when objective performance standards are ambiguous. This phenomenon is thought to stem from societal factors and internalized beliefs that lead women to undervalue their contributions.

effects.name: Race/ethnicity effects of political candidates
effects.description: The effect posits that voters evaluate political candidates differently based on their race or ethnicity. Specifically, the original research suggested that white voters tend to rate Black candidates lower than white candidates, with skin tone potentially influencing this bias. This effect implies a racial bias in voter perception, impacting candidate electability.

effects.name: The democratic peace
effects.description: The democratic peace effect posits that citizens in democratic states are less likely to approve of military action against other democracies compared to non-democratic states. This phenomenon is often attributed to political incentives, where democratic leaders are held accountable by their populace, making them more hesitant to engage in conflict with other democracies.

effects.name: Moral foundations across the political spectrum
effects.description: Moral Foundations Theory (MFT) proposes that human morality is built upon a set of innate, universal psychological systems or 'foundations.' These foundations, typically identified as Care/Harm, Fairness/Cheating, Loyalty/Betrayal, Authority/Subversion, and Purity/Degradation, are theorized to vary in salience across individuals and cultures, particularly influencing political ideologies.

effects.name: Backfire
effects.description: The backfire effect posits that when individuals are presented with factual information that corrects their political misperceptions, they may not only resist the correction but actually strengthen their belief in the original misperception. This phenomenon is often attributed to motivated reasoning, where individuals process information in a way that protects their existing beliefs and identities.

effects.name: Conspiracy mentality and political extremism
effects.description: This effect proposes a positive, quadratic relationship between an individual's general tendency to endorse conspiracy theories (conspiracy mentality) and their political ideology. Specifically, it suggests that individuals holding extreme political ideologies, whether on the left or the right, are more likely to believe in conspiracy theories than political moderates. The proposed mechanism involves a highly structured thinking style aimed at making sense of societal events.

effects.name: Voters elect rather than affect policies
effects.description: The 'Voters Elect, Not Affect Policies' effect posits that voters primarily elect politicians whose policy preferences align with their own, rather than influencing the policies of politicians already in office. This suggests that changes in median voter preferences do not significantly alter the roll-call voting behavior of elected representatives. Instead, the composition of elected officials reflects the existing policy preferences of the electorate.

effects.name: Personality correlates of sociopolitical attitudes
effects.description: This effect explores the consistent relationships between various personality traits (e.g., from Eysenck's EPQ or the Big Five) and an individual's sociopolitical attitudes, such as their ideological self-placement (liberal vs. conservative) or specific political orientations like authoritarianism. It posits that certain stable personality dimensions predispose individuals to adopt particular political viewpoints. For example, early research suggested specific links between traits like psychoticism, neuroticism, and social desirability with liberal or conservative leanings.

effects.name: Gaze following in monkeys
effects.description: The effect of gaze following in monkeys refers to the ability of non-human primates to orient their attention or movement in the direction indicated by another individual's head and eye movements. Early research, particularly with capuchin monkeys in object-choice tasks, suggested a limited capacity for spontaneous gaze following. However, subsequent studies with other primate species, such as rhesus macaques, demonstrated more sophisticated gaze-following abilities, especially when the gaze cue was directed to a distant location.

effects.name: Pointing following in monkeys
effects.description: The 'Pointing Following' effect investigates the ability of non-human primates, specifically monkeys, to comprehend and utilize human pointing gestures as a communicative cue to locate hidden objects. Researchers explore whether monkeys can infer the signaler's communicative intent or if their responses are based on simpler associative learning mechanisms, typically using object-choice tasks.

effects.name: Gaze following in nonhuman primates
effects.description: This effect investigates the ability of nonhuman primates, particularly chimpanzees, to follow the gaze of another individual (human or conspecific). It explores whether this behavior reflects a sophisticated understanding of the other's attentional or mental state, or if it is a simpler, associative response. A key area of research is whether primates can use gaze cues to infer an agent's knowledge, especially in tasks like finding hidden food.

effects.name: Pointing following in nonhuman primates
effects.description: This effect concerns the ability of nonhuman primates to comprehend and follow human pointing gestures, typically assessed in object choice tasks where an experimenter indicates a hidden reward. Researchers investigate whether primates can utilize these communicative cues to locate food, exploring the cognitive mechanisms and species-specific differences underlying this behavior.

effects.name: Gaze following in domesticated dogs
effects.description: The Gaze Following in Domesticated Dogs effect posits that domestic dogs can follow the gaze direction of humans or conspecifics to locate hidden objects or attend to specific stimuli. This ability is often tested using object choice tasks where an experimenter or another dog gazes towards one of several potential locations. The proposed mechanism suggests that dogs have developed this social cognitive skill through domestication and selective breeding, allowing them to interpret human communicative cues.

effects.name: Pointing following in domesticated dogs
effects.description: The pointing following effect in domesticated dogs posits that dogs can understand and utilize human pointing gestures to locate hidden objects in an object-choice task. This ability is often attributed to their unique evolutionary history of domestication and extensive enculturation with humans, which may have fostered specific socio-cognitive skills for interpreting human communicative cues.

effects.name: Pointing following in coyotes
effects.description: This effect investigates whether coyotes can understand and follow human pointing gestures to locate hidden food in an object-choice task. The proposed mechanism often relates to their social cognition and experience with humans, similar to studies in domestic dogs and wolves.

effects.name: Gaze following in wolves
effects.description: The original effect claimed that wolves are unable to follow human gaze cues to locate hidden food in an object-choice task. This finding was used to support the hypothesis that domestic dogs possess an adaptive specialization for using human-produced directional cues, potentially due to domestication.

effects.name: Pointing following in wolves
effects.description: The "Pointing following in wolves" effect posits that domestic dogs, but not wolves, possess a unique social-cognitive ability to understand human pointing gestures to locate hidden food. This ability is thought to have evolved during the domestication process, allowing dogs to communicate with humans in distinct ways. Subsequent research has explored the extent to which wolves can also acquire or demonstrate this skill under various conditions.

effects.name: Pointing following in Asian elephants
effects.description: The effect posits that Asian elephants are capable of understanding and following human-provided visual cues, such as pointing or body orientation, to locate hidden food in an object-choice task. This ability is often linked to domestication or extensive human exposure in other species, and its presence in non-domesticated but highly human-exposed elephants is a key question.

effects.name: Pointing following in African elephants
effects.description: The effect posits that African elephants can understand and follow human pointing gestures to locate hidden food. This ability is demonstrated in object-choice tasks, where elephants successfully interpret various pointing cues, including subtle, conflicting, or even unsustained gestures, suggesting a robust understanding of human communicative intent.

effects.name: Gaze following in horses
effects.description: This effect investigates the ability of domestic horses to interpret human communicative cues, particularly gaze, in an object choice task. It proposes that horses primarily rely on basic stimulus enhancement, such as direct pointing or object placement, rather than understanding the communicative intent behind human gestures. Consequently, horses are claimed to have a limited capacity to follow subtle cues like human gaze to locate a hidden reward.

effects.name: Pointing following in horses
effects.description: The 'pointing following in horses' effect suggests that domestic horses can understand and respond to human pointing gestures to locate hidden objects, such as food. This ability is often tested using an object-choice task where a human points to one of two containers. The proposed mechanism involves horses interpreting the human gesture as a communicative cue indicating the location of a reward.

effects.name: Gaze following in domesticated pigs
effects.description: This effect investigates whether domesticated pigs can use human social cues, specifically gaze direction, to locate hidden food in an object choice task. Demonstrating this ability would suggest that pigs can understand human communicative intent or at least associate human gaze with the location of a reward.

effects.name: Pointing following in pigs
effects.description: The effect of pointing following in pigs refers to the ability of domesticated pigs to understand and utilize human pointing gestures to locate hidden food or objects in an object-choice task. This cognitive ability is often studied to understand interspecies communication and the impact of domestication on animal cognition, with findings varying based on cue type, pig experience, and experimental setup.

effects.name: Pointing following in goats
effects.description: The 'pointing following in goats' effect proposes that domestic goats can interpret human pointing gestures to locate hidden food, similar to domestic dogs. This ability is hypothesized to be a byproduct of domestication, rather than an inherent skill, as it is not observed in their wild counterparts or with other cues like human gaze alone.

effects.name: Gaze following in goats
effects.description: The effect of gaze following in goats investigates whether these animals can use the direction of another's gaze (human or conspecific) to locate objects or information in their environment. This ability is considered a fundamental social cognitive skill, potentially aiding in foraging or predator detection.

effects.name: Eye narrowing in felines
effects.description: The eye narrowing effect in felines proposes that domestic cats are more likely to perform slow eye narrowing movements (often referred to as 'slow blinks') in response to similar slow blinking gestures from humans. This behavior is hypothesized to be a form of positive emotional communication, signaling trust and affiliation between cats and humans.

effects.name: Pointing following in felines
effects.description: The 'pointing following' effect in felines describes the finding that domestic cats (Felis catus) typically do not follow human pointing gestures to locate hidden objects in an object choice task. This contrasts with other domesticated species, such as dogs, which readily utilize human communicative cues like pointing. The proposed mechanism suggests that cats may lack the specific socio-cognitive abilities or evolutionary pressures that would enable them to interpret and respond to human referential gestures.

effects.name: Mirror-self recognition in magpies (_Pica pica_)
effects.description: The Mirror-Self Recognition (MSR) effect in magpies proposes that these birds can recognize their own reflection, indicating a sophisticated level of self-awareness. This ability, typically assessed using a mark test, suggests that magpies possess a self-representation similar to that observed in some primates, challenging previous assumptions about cognitive capacities in non-mammalian species.

effects.name: Right-bias in hand use in chimpanzees
effects.description: This effect proposes that chimpanzees, similar to humans, exhibit a population-level right-hand bias for certain tasks, rather than individual chimpanzees showing varied preferences. The underlying mechanism is often implicitly linked to evolutionary parallels with human brain lateralization, suggesting a shared ancestral trait or convergent evolution for complex motor skills.

effects.name: Cache protection in Eurasian jays (_Garrulus glandarius_)
effects.description: The cache protection effect in Eurasian jays posits that these birds strategically choose to store food in locations hidden from conspecifics. This behavior is believed to be a tactic to prevent other jays from observing and subsequently pilfering their cached food, thereby protecting their valuable resources.

effects.name: Desire-state attribution may govern food sharing in Eurasian jays (_Garrulus glandarius_)
effects.description: The desire-state attribution effect in Eurasian jays proposes that male jays can infer and respond to the specific food desires of their female partners. This ability allows males to flexibly share food based on what their mate currently wants, rather than simply reacting to her immediate behavior or their own preferences. The proposed mechanism involves the male observing what the female has previously eaten to understand her current desire.

effects.name: Reasoning about hidden causal agents in New Caledonian Crows (_Corvus moneduloides_)
effects.description: The effect proposes that New Caledonian Crows can infer the presence of a hidden causal agent. Specifically, crows are hypothesized to show different behavioral responses, such as increased vigilance or altered foraging behavior, when an event (e.g., a moving stick) occurs without an observable cause, suggesting they infer a hidden agent, compared to when a human agent is visibly present or absent.

effects.name: Owners predict spatial impulsivity in dogs
effects.description: The "Owners predict spatial impulsivity in dogs" effect proposes that owner ratings of their dog's impulsivity, typically measured by questionnaires like the Dog Impulsivity Assessment Scale (DIAS), correlate with the dog's performance in a spatial impulsivity task. In such tasks, dogs choose between a smaller, closer reward and a larger, more distant reward. The underlying mechanism suggests that owners can accurately perceive and report their dog's trait impulsivity, which is then reflected in behavioral measures of delay discounting.

effects.name: Numerical ratio effects on quantity discrimination in elephants
effects.description: The numerical ratio effect posits that an animal's ability to discriminate between two quantities is inversely related to the ratio between those quantities; performance decreases as the ratio approaches 1 (i.e., quantities become more similar). This effect is widely observed across species and suggests an underlying approximate number system. The effect in elephants specifically refers to whether their quantity discrimination abilities are similarly constrained by the numerical ratio between quantities.

effects.name: Contagious yawning in dogs
effects.description: The Contagious Yawning in Dogs effect proposes that domestic dogs are susceptible to contagious yawning, meaning they are more likely to yawn after observing a human yawn than after other control mouth movements. This phenomenon is hypothesized to be a rudimentary form of empathy in dogs, potentially serving to coordinate dog-human interaction and communication by modulating arousal levels.

effects.name: Temporal preferences in chimpanzees and bonobos
effects.description: This effect posits that chimpanzees exhibit greater patience than bonobos in intertemporal choice tasks, consistently waiting longer for larger, delayed food rewards. The proposed mechanism suggests that ecological differences, particularly the unpredictability of food resources in their natural habitats, may have driven chimpanzees to develop a higher tolerance for temporal costs in foraging.

effects.name: Risk preferences in chimpanzees and bonobos
effects.description: This effect posits that chimpanzees (Pan troglodytes) exhibit greater risk tolerance than bonobos (Pan paniscus) when making choices involving food rewards. The proposed mechanism is that their divergent ecologies, with chimpanzees exploiting riskier food sources in the wild, have shaped these distinct risk preferences. Specifically, chimpanzees are expected to prefer risky options over guaranteed smaller rewards, while bonobos are predicted to show a preference for fixed, certain outcomes.

effects.name: Typological Prevalence Hypothesis
effects.description: The Typological Prevalence Hypothesis proposes that certain structural features are more common across the world's languages because they are easier for humans to learn, process, and use. These cognitively 'natural' features are thus more likely to be retained in a language over time. This hypothesis suggests that cross-linguistically prevalent linguistic distinctions, such as those found in evidentiality (grammatical marking of information source), should be more readily acquired by learners.

effects.name: Stuttering and bilingualism
effects.description: The effect of stuttering and bilingualism posits that bilingual children face an increased risk of developing stuttering and a reduced likelihood of recovering from it, compared to monolingual or language-exclusive speakers. This suggests that the cognitive demands of managing two languages might exacerbate or predispose children to speech disfluencies.

effects.name: Stuttering and self-esteem
effects.description: This effect posits that children who stutter initially exhibit higher self-esteem compared to their non-stuttering peers. However, this elevated self-esteem is hypothesized to decline as individuals who stutter transition into adolescence, potentially due to increased social awareness and challenges associated with stuttering.

effects.name: Stuttering and dyslexia co-occurrence
effects.description: The effect proposes that individuals who stutter exhibit a higher rate of co-occurrence with dyslexia compared to neurotypical adults. This suggests a potential shared underlying neurodevelopmental or genetic vulnerability that contributes to both speech fluency disorders and reading difficulties.

effects.name: Stuttering and phonological working memory impairment
effects.description: This effect posits that individuals who stutter exhibit impairments in phonological working memory, a cognitive system responsible for temporarily holding and manipulating speech sounds. This impairment is typically observed through lower accuracy or slower processing on nonword repetition tasks, suggesting a potential underlying cognitive mechanism contributing to stuttering.

effects.name: Stuttering and phonological monitoring impairment
effects.description: The Stuttering and Phonological Monitoring Impairment effect posits that individuals who stutter exhibit reduced efficiency in phonological monitoring tasks compared to neurotypical individuals. This impairment is hypothesized to reflect underlying difficulties in the rapid and accurate processing of speech sounds, potentially contributing to the disfluencies characteristic of stuttering.

effects.name: Stuttering and phonological awareness impairment (vs. neurotypicals)
effects.description: The Stuttering and Phonological Awareness Impairment effect posits that individuals who stutter exhibit lower levels of phonological awareness compared to neurotypical individuals. This suggests a potential underlying cognitive difference in processing speech sounds, which may contribute to the difficulties experienced in fluent speech production.

effects.name: Stuttering and phonological awareness impairment (vs. dyslexia)
effects.description: The effect posits that adults who stutter exhibit similar levels of impairment in phonological awareness skills compared to adults with dyslexia. This suggests a potential shared underlying cognitive processing deficit between these two distinct conditions, rather than phonological awareness deficits being exclusive to dyslexia.

effects.name: Stuttering and reading fluency impairment (vs. dyslexia)
effects.description: The effect posits that adults who stutter exhibit reading fluency scores that are comparable to those of adults with dyslexia. This suggests that stuttering, similar to dyslexia, may be associated with an underlying processing deficit that manifests as impaired reading fluency. The proposed mechanism implies a shared cognitive or neurological basis for both conditions affecting reading abilities.

effects.name: Struggling readers and stuttering co-occurrence
effects.description: The effect of 'Struggling readers and stuttering co-occurrence' posits that individuals with dyslexia exhibit a higher rate of co-occurring stuttering compared to neurotypical adults. This suggests a potential shared underlying processing deficit or comorbidity between developmental reading difficulties and speech fluency disorders.

effects.name: Fake barn cases
effects.description: The 'fake barn cases' effect posits that an individual's age influences their epistemic intuitions, specifically regarding knowledge attribution in scenarios where a belief is true but based on misleading evidence. It suggests that older individuals are less inclined than younger individuals to attribute knowledge in these specific thought experiments.

effects.name: Stakes effect
effects.description: The Stakes effect proposes that an individual's attribution of knowledge to another person is influenced by the practical consequences or "stakes" associated with being wrong. Specifically, as the practical costs of error increase, people are less likely to attribute knowledge, even if the evidence remains the same. This suggests that epistemic properties, like knowledge, are not solely determined by truth and justification but also by practical interests.

effects.name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait anxiety/neuroticism on conditioning
effects.description: This effect explores how individual differences in personality traits, specifically trait anxiety or neuroticism, influence the process of fear conditioning. The central hypothesis posits that higher levels of these traits are associated with enhanced or more robust fear learning, potentially leading to stronger conditioned responses or impaired ability to differentiate between threatening and safe stimuli. Research in this area investigates the mechanisms by which personality might modulate the acquisition and generalization of fear.

effects.name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait extraversion on conditioning
effects.description: This effect proposes that individuals with lower trait extraversion (introverts) exhibit stronger or more efficient fear conditioning compared to extraverts. The underlying theory, primarily from Eysenck, suggests that introverts have higher cortical arousal, which facilitates the formation of conditioned responses, including fear responses.

papers.title: Automaticity of social behaviour
papers.apa: Automaticity. (2009). The SAGE Glossary of the Social and Behavioral Sciences. https://doi.org/10.4135/9781412972024.n182
papers.summary: This foundational paper by Bargh, Chen, and Burrows (1996) introduced the concept of the elderly priming effect, demonstrating that participants exposed to words related to old age subsequently walked slower when exiting the laboratory. The study proposed that social behavior can be automatically and unconsciously influenced by environmental primes.

papers.title: Doyen 2012
papers.summary: This study conducted two experiments to replicate the original elderly priming effect. Experiment 1 (N=120) failed to show a significant priming effect on walking speed (F(1, 119)=1, η²=.01, d=0.10), despite using automated timing. Experiment 2 found a walking speed effect only when experimenters believed participants would walk slower, suggesting experimenter expectancy effects play a role. The authors concluded that unconscious behavioral priming is real but involves mechanisms different from those typically assumed.

papers.title: Lakens 2017
papers.apa: Doyen, S., Klein, O., Pichon, C.-L., & Cleeremans, A. (2012). Behavioral Priming: It’s All in the Mind, but Whose Mind? PLoS ONE, 7(1), e29081. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0029081
papers.summary: This paper by Lakens et al. (2017) is a direct replication attempt of the Bargh et al. (1996) study, not a meta-analysis of the effect. It reported a non-significant effect (r=.29, d=.61, as per the prompt's replication effect size, though the paper itself reported r=0.01, 95% CI [-0.19, 0.21] in a direct replication with N=100), contributing to the evidence against the robustness of the elderly priming effect.

papers.title: Pashler et al. 2011
papers.apa: Chatterjee, P. (2016). Response to Pashler et al. (2016). Basic and Applied Social Psychology, 38(1), 19–29. https://doi.org/10.1080/01973533.2015.1129335
papers.summary: This paper by Pashler et al. (2011) is a review and commentary on the replication crisis in social psychology, highlighting the challenges in replicating many classic findings, including social priming effects like the elderly priming effect. It is not a meta-analysis of the specific effect but rather a critical examination of the field's methodology and replicability.

papers.title: The role of category accessibility in the interpretation of information about persons: Some determinants and implications’
papers.apa: Srull, T. K., & Wyer, Jr., R. S. (2004). The Role of Category Accessibility in the Interpretation of Information About Persons: Some Determinants and Implications. Social Cognition, 154–166. https://doi.org/10.4324/9780203496398-9
papers.summary: This foundational paper by Srull and Wyer (1979), specifically Experiment 1, established the hostility priming effect. Participants who descrambled sentences containing a high percentage of hostile words subsequently rated an ambiguously behaving protagonist (Donald) and other ambiguous behaviors as significantly more hostile than those primed with neutral words. The authors proposed that this occurred due to increased accessibility of hostile concepts in memory.

papers.title: McCarthy et al. 2018
papers.apa: McCarthy, R. J., Skowronski, J. J., Verschuere, B., Meijer, E. H., Jim, A., Hoogesteyn, K., Orthey, R., Acar, O. A., Aczel, B., Bakos, B. E., Barbosa, F., Baskin, E., Bègue, L., Ben-Shakhar, G., Birt, A. R., Blatz, L., Charman, S. D., Claesen, A., Clay, S. L., … Yıldız, E. (2018). Registered Replication Report on Srull and Wyer (1979). Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science, 1(3), 321–336. https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245918777487
papers.summary: This Registered Replication Report conducted 26 independent replications (N=7,373 total; N=5,610 in primary analyses) of Srull and Wyer (1979), Experiment 1. A random-effects meta-analysis found the protagonist was seen as only 0.08 scale points more hostile (95% CI = [0.004, 0.16]) when primed with 80% hostile sentences, and ambiguously hostile behaviors were seen as 0.08 points less hostile (95% CI = [−0.18, 0.01]). The authors concluded that the methods used did not produce an assimilative priming effect that is practically and routinely detectable.

papers.title: McCarthy et al. 2021
papers.apa: Rising, K. L., & McCarthy, D. M. (2021). In Reply to Chou et al. Academic Medicine, 96(7), 931–932. https://doi.org/10.1097/acm.0000000000004099
papers.summary: This paper, likely a further replication attempt or meta-analysis by McCarthy and colleagues, investigated the hostility priming effect. While specific details are not provided, the reported effect size of d = 0.06 suggests a very small or negligible effect, consistent with the findings of their 2018 Registered Replication Report.

papers.title: The relation between perception and behavior, or how to win a game of trivial pursuit
papers.apa: Dijksterhuis, A., & Knippenberg, A. van. (2004). The Relation Between Perception and Behavior, or How to Win a Game of Trivial Pursuit. Social Cognition, 266–282. https://doi.org/10.4324/9780203496398-16
papers.summary: The original study by Dijksterhuis and van Knippenberg (1998) found that participants primed with the 'professor' stereotype performed 13% better on a trivia test than those primed with the 'soccer hooligan' stereotype. This suggested that activating a stereotype could unconsciously influence intellectual performance.

papers.title: O’Donnell et al. 2018
papers.apa: O’Donnell, M., Nelson, L. D., Ackermann, E., Aczel, B., Akhtar, A., Aldrovandi, S., Alshaif, N., Andringa, R., Aveyard, M., Babincak, P., Balatekin, N., Baldwin, S. A., Banik, G., Baskin, E., Bell, R., Białobrzeska, O., Birt, A. R., Boot, W. R., Braithwaite, S. R., … Zrubka, M. (2018). Registered Replication Report: Dijksterhuis and van Knippenberg (1998). Perspectives on Psychological Science, 13(2), 268–294. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691618755704
papers.summary: This Registered Replication Report conducted a large-scale, multi-lab replication of Dijksterhuis and van Knippenberg (1998) across 23 laboratories (N=4,493). The study found no overall difference in trivia performance between participants primed with 'professor' and 'hooligan' categories (0.14% difference), nor any moderation by gender. The findings contradict the original effect.

papers.title: With a Clean Conscience: Cleanliness Reduces the Severity of Moral Judgments
papers.apa: Schnall, S., Benton, J., & Harvey, S. (2008). With a Clean Conscience. Psychological Science, 19(12), 1219–1222. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02227.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper presented two experiments demonstrating that cleanliness reduces the severity of moral judgments. Experiment 1 (N=40) showed that activating the concept of cleanliness made moral judgments less severe (d = -0.60). Experiment 2 (N=43) found similar results after participants physically cleansed themselves following disgust (d = -0.85). The authors proposed that moral judgment can be driven by intuitive processes, linking physical purity to moral purity.

papers.title: Johnson et al.
papers.apa: Johnson, D. J., Cheung, F., & Donnellan, M. B. (2014). Does Cleanliness Influence Moral Judgments? Social Psychology, 45(3), 209–215. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000186
papers.summary: This paper conducted direct, well-powered replications of both experiments from Schnall et al. (2008) using materials supplied by the original authors. They found no evidence that physical cleanliness reduced the severity of moral judgments. For Experiment 1, their replication (N=208) yielded a d = -0.01, and for Experiment 2 (N=126), a d = 0.01, both substantially smaller than the original estimates. The authors concluded that the population effect sizes are likely much smaller than originally reported.

papers.title: The Dishonesty of Honest People: A Theory of Self-Concept Maintenance
papers.apa: Mazar, N., Amir, O., & Ariely, D. (2008). The Dishonesty of Honest People: A Theory of Self-Concept Maintenance. Journal of Marketing Research, 45(6), 633–644. https://doi.org/10.1509/jmkr.45.6.633
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the self-concept maintenance theory, proposing that individuals cheat only to the extent that they can maintain a positive self-image. Across six experiments, the authors demonstrated that mechanisms like inattention to moral standards and categorization malleability allow people to profit from dishonesty without compromising their self-perception of integrity. One experiment (Experiment 1, as referenced by later replications) specifically showed that a moral reminder (recalling the Ten Commandments) reduced cheating behavior compared to a neutral prime.

papers.title: Verschuere et al. 2018
papers.apa: Verschuere, B., Meijer, E. H., Jim, A., Hoogesteyn, K., Orthey, R., McCarthy, R. J., Skowronski, J. J., Acar, O. A., Aczel, B., Bakos, B. E., Barbosa, F., Baskin, E., Bègue, L., Ben-Shakhar, G., Birt, A. R., Blatz, L., Charman, S. D., Claesen, A., Clay, S. L., … Yıldız, E. (2018). Registered Replication Report on Mazar, Amir, and Ariely (2008). Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science, 1(3), 299–317. https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245918781032
papers.summary: This Registered Replication Report aggregated results from 25 direct replications (total N = 5,786) of Mazar et al.'s (2008) Experiment 1, which tested whether a moral reminder reduces cheating. The primary meta-analysis of 19 replications (N = 4,674) found a meta-analytic effect size of d = 0.11 [−0.09, 0.31] for the main comparison, indicating no significant reduction in cheating due to moral priming. This finding is numerically in the opposite direction of the original study's reported effect (d = 0.48) and suggests that the moral priming effect, as originally demonstrated, does not reliably replicate.

papers.title: Keeping One's Distance: The Influence of Spatial Distance Cues on Affect and Evaluation
papers.apa: Williams, L. E., & Bargh, J. A. (2008). Keeping One’s Distance. Psychological Science, 19(3), 302–308. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02084.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that perceptual and motor representations of physical distance influence thoughts and feelings. Across four experiments, participants primed with spatial distance showed greater enjoyment of embarrassing media, less emotional distress from violent media, lower calorie estimates for unhealthy food, and weaker emotional attachments to family/hometowns. The original effect size was reported as η²=.09 (d=0.31).

papers.title: Pashler 2021
papers.apa: Pashler, H., Coburn, N., & Harris, C. R. (2012). Priming of Social Distance? Failure to Replicate Effects on Social and Food Judgments. PLoS ONE, 7(8), e42510. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0042510
papers.summary: This paper attempted direct replications of two experiments from Williams and Bargh (2008) on spatial distance priming effects on social bonds and food judgments. Using 92 undergraduates, the study employed precautions against experimenter expectancy effects. The results showed no significant difference in reported bond strength (F(2, 68) = .31, p = .73) and a very small effect size (ηp²=0.009, d=0.009), failing to support the original findings.

papers.title: A Single Exposure to the American Flag Shifts Support Toward Republicanism up to 8 Months Later
papers.apa: Carter, T. J., Ferguson, M. J., & Hassin, R. R. (2011). A Single Exposure to the American Flag Shifts Support Toward Republicanism up to 8 Months Later. Psychological Science, 22(8), 1011–1018. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611414726
papers.summary: This foundational study reported that a single, brief exposure to the American flag led to a significant shift toward Republican beliefs, attitudes, and voting behavior among both Republican and Democratic participants. Experiment 1, conducted during the 2008 U.S. presidential election, showed effects lasting up to 8 months. Experiment 2 replicated these findings, providing the first evidence that nonconscious priming from a national flag can bias political alignment with considerable durability (original effect size d=0.50).

papers.title: Klein et al. 2014
papers.apa: Klein, H. J., Cooper, J. T., Molloy, J. C., & Swanson, J. A. (2014). Klein et al. Unidimensional, Target-Free Measure [dataset]. In PsycTESTS Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/t30585-000
papers.summary: This paper, part of a larger replication effort, investigated the flag priming effect and reported an effect size of d = -0.11. This finding suggests a negligible or even opposite effect compared to the original claim, contributing to the overall pattern of non-replication for the flag priming phenomenon.

papers.title: Effects of Fluency on Psychological Distance and Mental Construal (or Why New York Is a Large City, but New York Is a Civilized Jungle)
papers.apa: Alter, A. L., & Oppenheimer, D. M. (2008). Effects of Fluency on Psychological Distance and Mental Construal (or Why New York Is a Large City, but New York Is a Civilized Jungle). Psychological Science, 19(2), 161–167. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02062.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the fluency priming effect, demonstrating that cognitive disfluency leads to more abstract mental construal compared to cognitive fluency. Across multiple studies, the authors observed this effect using various forms of fluency, including visual perceptual, conceptual priming, and linguistic fluency. They proposed that disfluently processed stimuli are interpreted as psychologically farther away, thereby promoting abstract thinking.

papers.title: Klein et al. 2014
papers.apa: Klein, H. J., Cooper, J. T., Molloy, J. C., & Swanson, J. A. (2014). Klein et al. Unidimensional, Target-Free Measure [dataset]. In PsycTESTS Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/t30585-000
papers.summary: This paper attempted to replicate the fluency priming effect. The replication effort yielded a very small effect size (r = .02, d = 0.04), which is substantially lower than the original reported effect. These findings suggest that the original fluency priming effect, as described by Alter & Oppenheimer (2008), was not successfully replicated.

papers.title: Mere exposure to money increases endorsement of free-market systems and social inequality
papers.apa: Caruso, E. M., Vohs, K. D., Baxter, B., & Waytz, A. (2013). Mere exposure to money increases endorsement of free-market systems and social inequality. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 142(2), 301–306. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0029288
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the money priming effect, proposing that mere exposure to money-related stimuli increases individuals' endorsement of free-market systems, belief in a just world, and social dominance. The study reported substantial effects, such as d=0.8 for system justification and d=0.44 for belief in a just world.

papers.title: Rohrer 2015
papers.apa: Rohrer, J. D. (2015). Imagingof frontotemporal dementia. Hodges’ Frontotemporal Dementia, 125–142. https://doi.org/10.1017/cbo9781316091586.013
papers.summary: This paper conducted a replication attempt of the money priming effect, finding significantly smaller effect sizes compared to the original study. For instance, Experiment 1 reported d=0.07 for system justification and d=0.06 for belief in a just world, suggesting a lack of evidence for the effect.

papers.title: Lodder 2019
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized results from 47 preregistered experiments investigating the money priming effect. The study found very small overall effect sizes, such as g=0.01 for system justification and g=0.11 for belief in a just world, providing strong evidence against the robustness of the effect.

papers.title: Dealing with betrayal in close relationships: Does commitment promote forgiveness?
papers.apa: Finkel, E. J., Rusbult, C. E., Kumashiro, M., & Hannon, P. A. (2002). Dealing with betrayal in close relationships: Does commitment promote forgiveness? Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 82(6), 956–974. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.82.6.956
papers.summary: This foundational study (Finkel, Rusbult, Kumashiro, and Hannon, 2002, Study 1) experimentally investigated the causal link between subjective commitment and responses to hypothetical betrayals. It demonstrated that participants primed for high commitment exhibited reduced exit and neglect responses compared to those primed for independence, suggesting commitment promotes forgiveness. The original effect size was reported as d = -0.65.

papers.title: Cheung et al. 2016
papers.apa: Cheung, I., Campbell, L., LeBel, E. P., Ackerman, R. A., Aykutoğlu, B., Bahník, Š., Bowen, J. D., Bredow, C. A., Bromberg, C., Caprariello, P. A., Carcedo, R. J., Carson, K. J., Cobb, R. J., Collins, N. L., Corretti, C. A., DiDonato, T. E., Ellithorpe, C., Fernández-Rouco, N., Fuglestad, P. T., … Yong, J. C. (2016). Registered Replication Report. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 11(5), 750–764. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691616664694
papers.summary: This Registered Replication Report meta-analytically combined 16 direct replications of Finkel et al. (2002, Study 1) using a standardized, preregistered protocol. Across these replications, the study found little effect of the commitment priming manipulation on forgiveness outcome measures (meta-analytic d = -0.22). Crucially, the priming manipulation also failed to affect subjective commitment, suggesting the manipulation itself did not work as in the original study.

papers.title: Role of Consciousness and Accessibility of Death-Related Thoughts in Mortality Salience Effects
papers.summary: This paper is a foundational study in Terror Management Theory, exploring how reminders of mortality influence psychological processes related to death-thought accessibility and conscious awareness. It likely contributes to the understanding of the underlying mechanisms of mortality salience effects.

papers.title: I am not an animal: Mortality salience, disgust, and the denial of human creatureliness
papers.apa: Goldenberg, J. L., Pyszczynski, T., Greenberg, J., Solomon, S., Kluck, B., & Cornwell, R. (2001). I am not an animal: Mortality salience, disgust, and the denial of human creatureliness. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 130(3), 427–435. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-3445.130.3.427
papers.summary: This foundational paper (Goldenberg et al., 2001) investigated how mortality salience increases the need to distance humans from animals, proposing that being an animal is threatening due to its association with death. Study 1 (N=77) found mortality salience increased disgust sensitivity to body products and animals after a delay, while Study 2 (N=41) showed a greater preference for essays emphasizing human distinctiveness from animals.

papers.title: Burke et al. 2010
papers.apa: Burke, B. L., Martens, A., & Faucher, E. H. (2010). Two Decades of Terror Management Theory: A Meta-Analysis of Mortality Salience Research. Personality and Social Psychology Review, 14(2), 155–195. https://doi.org/10.1177/1088868309352321
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 164 articles (277 experiments) on mortality salience (MS) found moderate effects (r = .35) on worldview and self-esteem related variables. Effects were stronger for American college students, longer delays, and people-related attitudes, suggesting potential moderators for MS effects and comparing results to alternative explanations.

papers.title: Klein et al. 2018
papers.apa: Klein, R. A., Cook, C. L., Ebersole, C. R., Vitiello, C. A., Nosek, B. A., Hilgard, J., Ahn, P. H., Brady, A. J., Chartier, C. R., Christopherson, C. D., Clay, S., Collisson, B., Crawford, J., Cromar, R., Gardiner, G., Gosnell, C., Grahe, J. E., Hall, C., Howard, I., … Ratliff, K. A. (2019). Many Labs 4: Failure to Replicate Mortality Salience Effect With and Without Original Author Involvement. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/vef2c
papers.summary: This paper reports a large-scale replication attempt of a mortality salience effect, finding no evidence for the predicted effect across various exclusion criteria. The confidence intervals were quite narrow (Hedges’ g = 0.03 to 0.06), indicating a robust null finding and preventing further assessment of original author involvement.

papers.title: performed a replication of Greenberg et al. (1994) Experiment 1 with and without original author involvement
papers.apa: Klein, R. A., Cook, C. L., Ebersole, C. R., Vitiello, C. A., Nosek, B. A., Hilgard, J., Ahn, P. H., Brady, A. J., Chartier, C. R., Christopherson, C. D., Clay, S., Collisson, B., Crawford, J., Cromar, R., Gardiner, G., Gosnell, C., Grahe, J. E., Hall, C., Howard, I., … Ratliff, K. A. (2019). Many Labs 4: Failure to Replicate Mortality Salience Effect With and Without Original Author Involvement. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/vef2c
papers.summary: This paper describes a replication of Greenberg et al. (1994) Experiment 1, investigating the influence of original author involvement on replication outcomes.

papers.title: Sætrevik & Sjåstad 2019
papers.apa: Sætrevik, B., & Sjåstad, H. (2019). Mortality salience effects fail to replicate in traditional and novel measures. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/dkg53
papers.summary: This paper presents a replication attempt of mortality salience effects, reporting effect sizes ranging from d = -0.08 to 0.35 for outcome effects related to theoretical predictions. These findings suggest a weak or absent effect compared to original claims.

papers.title: Goldberg et al. (2001)
