papers.apa: Goldenberg, J. L., Pyszczynski, T., Greenberg, J., Solomon, S., Kluck, B., & Cornwell, R. (2001). I am not an animal: Mortality salience, disgust, and the denial of human creatureliness. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 130(3), 427–435. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-3445.130.3.427
papers.summary: This entry is a duplicate of the foundational paper 'I am not an animal: Mortality salience, disgust, and the denial of human creatureliness' (Goldenberg et al., 2001). Its detailed content was used to summarize that foundational paper.

papers.title: Rodríguez-Ferreiro et al. 2019
papers.apa:
Rodríguez-Ferreiro, J., Barberia, I., González-Guerra, J., & Vadillo, M. A. (2019). Are we truly special and unique? A replication of Goldenberg
            et al.
            (2001). Royal Society Open Science, 6(11), 191114. https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.191114
papers.summary: This replication study (N=128 volunteers) of Goldenberg et al. (2001) Experiment 2 found no evidence for a mortality salience effect. Participants primed with death questions did not provide more positive evaluations to an essay describing humans as distinct from animals compared to control participants, reporting d = 0.09 [−0.26, 0.44], significantly different from the original d = 1.13.

papers.title: Keeping One’s Distance: The effect of spatial distance cues on affect and emotion
papers.apa: Williams, L. E., & Bargh, J. A. (2008). Keeping One’s Distance. Psychological Science, 19(3), 302–308. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02084.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the spatial priming for emotional closeness effect, demonstrating that manipulating spatial distance cues can influence perceived emotional closeness. Across four studies, the authors found that participants who plotted points closer together reported greater emotional closeness to family members (Study 1: d=0.10; Study 2: d=0.22; Study 3: d=0.11; Study 4: d=0.12). These findings suggested a link between physical and psychological distance.

papers.title: Pashler et al. 2012
papers.apa: Pashler, H., Coburn, N., & Harris, C. R. (2012). Priming of Social Distance? Failure to Replicate Effects on Social and Food Judgments. PLoS ONE, 7(8), e42510. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0042510
papers.summary: This paper reported an attempt to replicate the spatial priming for emotional closeness effect. The authors conducted a replication study and found no significant evidence for the effect, reporting a very small effect size (d=0.01). This finding suggested that the original effect might not be robust.

papers.title: Open Science Collaboration 2015
papers.summary: As part of a large-scale replication project, this paper included an attempt to replicate Study 4 of the original spatial priming for emotional closeness effect. The replication found no evidence for the effect, reporting an effect size of d=0.00. This result contributed to the growing evidence questioning the robustness of the original finding.

papers.effect_name: Implicit God prime increases self-reported risky behaviour
papers.title: Anticipating divine protection? Reminders of god can increase nonmoral risk taking
papers.apa: Kupor, D. M., Laurin, K., & Levav, J. (2015). Anticipating Divine Protection? Reminders of God Can Increase Nonmoral Risk Taking. Psychological Science, 26(4), 374–384. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797614563108
papers.summary: This foundational research hypothesized that exposure to the concept of God increases nonmoral risk-taking. Across seven studies, the authors found that reminders of God increased risk-taking, an effect mediated by the perceived danger of a risky option and stronger among those who perceive God as protective (e.g., Experiment 1a: d=0.574; Experiment 1b: d=0.323). An eighth study showed negative feelings toward God when divine protection failed to materialize.

papers.effect_name: Implicit God prime increases self-reported risky behaviour
papers.title: Do Religious Primes Increase Risk Taking? Evidence Against “Anticipating Divine Protection” in Two Preregistered Direct Replications of Kupor, Laurin, and Levav (2015)
papers.apa: Gervais, W. M., McKee, S. E., & Malik, S. (2020). Do Religious Primes Increase Risk Taking? Evidence Against “Anticipating Divine Protection” in Two Preregistered Direct Replications of Kupor, Laurin, and Levav (2015). Psychological Science, 31(7), 858–864. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797620922477
papers.summary: This paper conducted two large-sample (N=1,104) preregistered direct replications of Kupor et al.'s (2015) Studies 1a and 1b. Using statistical significance, small-telescopes, Bayes factors, and meta-analyses, the authors found no evidence to replicate the original effects (Experiment 1a: d=0.14; Experiment 1b: d=-0.11). A meta-analysis combining original and replication studies yielded strong evidence for the null hypothesis (BF01 = 11.04), suggesting the 'anticipating-divine-protection' hypothesis may be overstated.

papers.effect_name: Implicit God prime increases actual risky behaviour
papers.title: Anticipating divine protection? Reminders of god can increase nonmoral risk taking
papers.apa: Kupor, D. M., Laurin, K., & Levav, J. (2015). Anticipating Divine Protection? Reminders of God Can Increase Nonmoral Risk Taking. Psychological Science, 26(4), 374–384. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797614563108
papers.summary: This foundational paper, across seven studies, reported that implicit reminders of God increased nonmoral risk-taking, mediated by a reduced perception of danger. The effect was stronger for those who perceive God as protective. An eighth study showed negative feelings towards God when protection failed, supporting the proposed mechanism. For example, Experiment 3 reported a significant effect of b=0.61.

papers.effect_name: Implicit God prime increases actual risky behaviour
papers.title: Gruneau Brulin et al. 2018 [
papers.apa: Gruneau Brulin, J. (2019). God and Place as Attachment ‘Figures’: A Critical Examination. The Psychology of Religion and Place, 183–200. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-030-28848-8_10
papers.summary: This paper attempted to replicate the effect of implicit God primes on risky behavior. Across two experiments, the authors found no evidence for the proposed effect. For instance, Experiment 1b reported a small, non-significant effect size of d=-0.11, and Experiment 2b reported b=0.14, both with confidence intervals including zero, suggesting the original finding was not robust.

papers.title: Hot under the collar in a lukewarm environment: Words associated with hot temperature increase aggressive thoughts and hostile perceptions
papers.apa: Nathan DeWall, C., & Bushman, B. J. (2009). Hot under the collar in a lukewarm environment: Words associated with hot temperature increase aggressive thoughts and hostile perceptions. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 45(4), 1045–1047. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2009.05.003
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the heat priming effect, demonstrating that exposure to hot temperature-related words increased aggressive thoughts and hostile perceptions. Study 1 reported effect sizes of d=0.47 and d=0.46, while Study 2 found even larger effects of d=0.67 and d=0.63, supporting the hypothesis that heat primes aggression.

papers.title: McCarthy 2014
papers.apa: McCarthy, R. J. (2014). Close replication attempts of the heat priming-hostile perception effect. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 54, 165–169. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2014.04.014
papers.summary: This paper conducted direct replications of the original heat priming effect, specifically targeting Study 2's procedure. Across two experiments (Study 2A and 2B), the authors consistently found no evidence for the effect, with effect sizes ranging from d=-0.12 to d=0.00. The paper's findings, along with its contribution to a meta-analysis yielding a d=0.18, strongly challenge the robustness of the original heat priming effect.

papers.title: Using implicit goal priming to improve the quality of self-report data
papers.apa: Rasinski, K. A., Visser, P. S., Zagatsky, M., & Rickett, E. M. (2005). Using implicit goal priming to improve the quality of self-report data. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 41(3), 321–327. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2004.07.001
papers.summary: This foundational paper, likely Rasinski et al. (2005), introduced the honesty priming effect. It reported that participants exposed to honesty-related words subsequently admitted to more problematic alcohol-related behaviors, suggesting an increased level of honesty. The original study reported a large effect size (d = 1.21).

papers.title: Pashler et al. 2013
papers.apa: Pashler, H., Rohrer, D., & Harris, C. R. (2013). Can the goal of honesty be primed? Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 49(6), 959–964. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2013.05.011
papers.summary: This paper reports on three experiments (plus a fourth in supplement) attempting to replicate the honesty priming effect. Experiments 1 (N=150, d=0.18) and 2 (online adults, d=-0.14) were direct replications that found no significant priming effects. Experiment 3, using a more refined candor measure, also failed to detect any effects, leading the authors to conclude against the existence of the honesty priming effect (combined d=0.008 for direct replications).

papers.title: Dalal and Hakel 2016
papers.apa: Dalal, D. K., & Hakel, M. D. (2016). Experimental Comparisons of Methods for Reducing Deliberate Distortions to Self-Report Measures of Sensitive Constructs. Organizational Research Methods, 19(3), 475–505. https://doi.org/10.1177/1094428116639131
papers.summary: This study investigated various methods to reduce deliberate distortions in self-reports of sensitive constructs like counterproductive workplace behavior across three experiments. Honesty priming was one of the methods tested, but it did not lead to higher reporting of sensitive behaviors (d = -0.07). The authors concluded that only an indirect questioning methodology was effective in increasing self-reported sensitive behaviors.

papers.title: ‘The Automated Will: Nonconscious Activation and Pursuit of Behavioral Goals’
papers.apa: Bargh, J. A., Gollwitzer, P. M., Lee-Chai, A., Barndollar, K., & Trötschel, R. (2001). The automated will: Nonconscious activation and pursuit of behavioral goals. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 81(6), 1014–1027. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.81.6.1014
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposes that goals can be activated outside of awareness and then nonconsciously guide self-regulation. Across five experiments, including one where participants primed with high-performance words (N=78) found significantly more words in a puzzle (M=26.0 vs. 21.5, F(1, 74) = 9.64, p = .003), the authors demonstrated that nonconsciously activated goals can enhance performance and cooperation. They concluded that these goals operate effectively without conscious awareness, challenging the notion that conscious will is always required for self-regulation.

papers.title: Shantz and Latham 2009
papers.apa: Shantz, A., & Latham, G. P. (2009). An exploratory field experiment of the effect of subconscious and conscious goals on employee performance. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 109(1), 9–17. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.obhdp.2009.01.001
papers.summary: This study explored the effect of primed and conscious goals on performance in the workplace. A pilot test (N=52) found that participants viewing a photo of a woman winning a race brainstormed more uses for a coat hanger (d=0.84) than controls, and a lab experiment (N=71) showed this prime increased subconscious need for achievement (d=0.22). A field experiment with call center employees (N=81) also revealed a main effect for the primed goal, suggesting that achievement priming can enhance performance in real-world settings.

papers.title: Harris et al. 2013
papers.apa: Harris, C. R., Coburn, N., Rohrer, D., & Pashler, H. (2013). Two Failures to Replicate High-Performance-Goal Priming Effects. PLoS ONE, 8(8), e72467. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0072467
papers.summary: This paper reports two direct replication attempts of Bargh et al. (2001)'s high-performance-goal priming effect. Experiment 1 (N=98) found no effect of priming (d = -0.24), with means in the opposite direction of the original. Experiment 2 (N=66), which included a 5-minute delay as suggested by Bargh et al., also found no evidence for the effect (d = -0.03). The authors concluded that the original findings might be invalid and called for skeptical scrutiny of the goal priming literature.

papers.title: Weingarten et al. 2016
papers.apa: Weingarten, E., Chen, Q., McAdams, M., Yi, J., Hepler, J., & Albarracín, D. (2016). From primed concepts to action: A meta-analysis of the behavioral effects of incidentally presented words. Psychological Bulletin, 142(5), 472–497. https://doi.org/10.1037/bul0000030
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized 352 effect sizes from 133 studies on behavioral priming, finding a small but robust overall effect (d=0.352). It revealed that published studies yielded larger effects (d=0.39) than unpublished ones (d=0.10), and supraliminal priming (as used in Bargh et al.) showed a smaller effect (d=0.30) than subliminal priming (d=0.40). The authors concluded that goal activation, particularly when outcomes are valued, contributes to explaining priming effects, while acknowledging the controversy surrounding replication failures in the field.

papers.title: Weapons as aggression-eliciting stimuli
papers.apa: Berkowitz, L., & Lepage, A. (1967). Weapons as aggression-eliciting stimuli. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 7(2, Pt.1), 202–207. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0025008
papers.summary: This foundational study introduced the 'weapons effect,' demonstrating that angered participants delivered more electric shocks to a confederate when a weapon (a shotgun and a revolver) was present in the room compared to when neutral objects (badminton rackets) were present. The study provided initial evidence that aggressive cues can elicit aggressive responses.

papers.title: Turner and Simons 1974
papers.apa: Simons, L. S., & Turner, C. W. (1974). A Further Investigation of the Weapons Effect. Proceedings of the Division of Personality and Society Psychology, 1(1), 186–188. https://doi.org/10.1177/014616727400100164
papers.summary: This study with 60 male students investigated the weapons effect, finding that it was significant only for subjects who were nonapprehensive and unsuspicious of the experiment's demands. The authors concluded that the weapons effect is a replicable phenomenon but is moderated by evaluation apprehension and subject sophistication, which may explain prior replication failures.

papers.title: Frodi 1975
papers.apa: Frodi, A. (1975). The Effect of Exposure to Weapons on Aggressive Behavior from a Cross-Cultural Perspective. International Journal of Psychology, 10(4), 283–292. https://doi.org/10.1080/00207597508247340
papers.summary: This study, conducted with Swedish high school students, aimed to replicate the Berkowitz and LePage (1967) study. It found that both angered and non-angered subjects delivered more shocks in the presence of weapons compared to non-aggressive stimuli, supporting the existence of a weapons effect in a cross-cultural context.

papers.title: Carlson et al. 1990
papers.apa: Carlson, M., Marcus-Newhall, A., & Miller, N. (1990). Effects of situational aggression cues: A quantitative review. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 58(4), 622–633. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.58.4.622
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 57 comparisons from 23 studies examined the effects of aggression-related cues. While overall aggression cues augmented aggressive responses (mean d=0.38), the specific 'weapons effect' was found to be a nonsignificant, near-zero average effect size, primarily confirmed only when subject sophistication and evaluation apprehension were low.

papers.title: Benjamin et al., 2018
papers.apa: Benjamin, A. J., Kepes, S., & Bushman, B. J. (2017). Effects of Weapons on Aggressive Thoughts, Angry Feelings, Hostile Appraisals, and Aggressive Behavior: A Meta-Analytic Review of the Weapons Effect Literature. Personality and Social Psychology Review, 22(4), 347–377. https://doi.org/10.1177/1088868317725419
papers.summary: This meta-analysis integrated 151 effect-size estimates from 78 independent studies (N=7,668 participants) on the weapons effect. It found that the mere presence of weapons increased aggressive thoughts, hostile appraisals, and aggressive behavior (d=0.29 [0.21, 0.36]), but not angry feelings. A comprehensive sensitivity analysis indicated that published literature tends to overestimate the effect for some outcomes due to publication bias.

papers.title: Ariel et al. 2019
papers.apa: Ariel, B., Lawes, D., Weinborn, C., Henry, R., Chen, K., & Brants Sabo, H. (2018). The “Less-Than-Lethal Weapons Effect”—Introducing TASERs to Routine Police Operations in England and Wales: A Randomized Controlled Trial. Criminal Justice and Behavior, 46(2), 280–300. https://doi.org/10.1177/0093854818812918
papers.summary: This randomized controlled trial investigated the 'less-than-lethal weapons effect' by deploying TASERs to police officers. The study found that the presence of a TASER on an officer significantly increased the use of force (IRR = 1.48 [1.27, 1.72]) and increased injury to officers (IRR = 2.11 [1.53, 2.91]), suggesting that the visual cue of a TASER leads to increased aggression in this context.

papers.title: Goal Contagion: Perceiving Is for Pursuing
papers.apa: Aarts, H., Gollwitzer, P. M., & Hassin, R. R. (2004). Goal Contagion: Perceiving Is for Pursuing. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 87(1), 23–37. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.87.1.23
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the goal contagion hypothesis, proposing that individuals automatically adopt goals implied by others' behavior. Across six studies, the authors demonstrated that these behavioral consequences exhibit features of goal-directedness, such as persistence and being influenced by goal strength. For instance, one study (N=83) found that participants exposed to a 'money-earning' goal were faster in a related task, particularly if they had a high need for money (Goal x Need interaction, F(1, 79) = 5.32, p < .03).

papers.title: Brohmer et al. 2021
papers.apa: Brohmer, H., Eckerstorfer, L. V., van Aert, R. C. M., & Corcoran, K. (2021). Do Behavioral Observations Make People Catch the Goal? A Meta-Analysis on Goal Contagion. International Review of Social Psychology, 34(1). https://doi.org/10.5334/irsp.428
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized existing research on goal contagion to provide an overall effect estimate. While an initial meta-analytic summary effect was reported as g = 0.30, a bias-corrected selection model approach yielded a smaller, non-significant effect size of g = 0.15 [-0.02; 0.32]. This suggests a weaker or absent effect when accounting for publication bias.

papers.title: Corcoran et al. 2020
papers.apa: Corcoran, K., Brohmer, H., Eckerstorfer, L. V., & Macher, S. (2020). When your goals inspire my goals: the role of effort, personal value, and inference in goal contagion. Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology, 4(1), 78–108. https://doi.org/10.1080/23743603.2020.1767502
papers.summary: This paper presented three experiments (overall N=840) investigating the goal contagion effect and its proposed moderators (observed effort, personal goal value) and mediator (goal inference). While the authors found evidence for explicit and spontaneous goal inference, they reported no support for the basic goal contagion effect, as participants did not pursue the target goal (earning money) to a different degree across conditions. The study concluded that neither the moderated-mediation process nor the fundamental effect was supported, contributing to the 'Replication Crisis' discussion.

papers.title: Learning about what others were doing: Verb aspect and attributions of mundane and criminal intent for past actions
papers.apa: Hart, W., & Albarracín, D. (2010). Learning About What Others Were Doing. Psychological Science, 22(2), 261–266. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797610395393
papers.summary: Hart and Albarracín (2010) proposed that the grammatical aspect of a verb influences the attribution of intentionality. Across three experiments, they found that describing an action using the imperfective aspect (e.g., "was doing") led participants to attribute significantly more intentionality to the actor (d values ranging from 0.55 to 1.23) and to imagine the actions in greater detail, compared to using the perfective aspect ("did"). This effect was observed for both mundane and criminal behaviors.

papers.title: Eerland et al. (2016)
papers.apa: Eerland, A., Sherrill, A. M., Magliano, J. P., Zwaan, R. A., Arnal, J. D., Aucoin, P., Berger, S. A., Birt, A. R., Capezza, N., Carlucci, M., Crocker, C., Ferretti, T. R., Kibbe, M. R., Knepp, M. M., Kurby, C. A., Melcher, J. M., Michael, S. W., Poirier, C., & Prenoveau, J. M. (2016). Registered Replication Report. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 11(1), 158–171. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691615605826
papers.summary: Eerland et al. (2016) conducted a Registered Replication Report comprising 12 direct replication studies (including one online study) of the verbal framing effect. Across these replications, they found no evidence that grammatical aspect affects intentionality (meta-analytic d = -0.24) or imagery (meta-analytic d = -0.08), with individual lab effect sizes mostly close to zero or negative. The authors concluded that the original finding did not replicate in their large-scale effort.

papers.title: Prospect Theory: An analysis of decisions under risk
papers.apa: Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1977). Prospect Theory. An Analysis of Decision Making Under Risk. Defense Technical Information Center. https://doi.org/10.21236/ada045771
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced Prospect Theory, an alternative to expected utility theory, to describe how individuals make decisions under risk. It posits that people evaluate potential outcomes relative to a reference point, leading to different risk preferences depending on whether options are framed as gains or losses. The theory highlights cognitive biases like loss aversion and the framing effect, where objectively identical choices yield different preferences based on their presentation.

papers.title: Ruggeri et al. 2020
papers.apa: Ruggeri, K., Alí, S., Berge, M. L., Bertoldo, G., Bjørndal, L. D., Cortijos-Bernabeu, A., Davison, C., Demić, E., Esteban-Serna, C., Friedemann, M., Gibson, S. P., Jarke, H., Karakasheva, R., Khorrami, P. R., Kveder, J., Andersen, T. L., Lofthus, I. S., McGill, L., Nieto, A. E., … Folke, T. (2020). Replicating patterns of prospect theory for decision under risk. Nature Human Behaviour, 4(6), 622–633. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-020-0886-x
papers.summary: This paper conducted a large-scale, multi-country replication of the classic framing effect, examining its robustness across diverse populations. The study reported an original effect size (log OR = -2.485) and found a significant replication effect size (log OR = -1.570 [-1.766, -1.374]), confirming the presence of the framing effect. The findings suggest that the effect is robust, albeit with a smaller magnitude than some initial estimates.

papers.title: Spending Money on Others Promotes Happiness
papers.apa: Dunn, E. W., Aknin, L. B., & Norton, M. I. (2008). Spending Money on Others Promotes Happiness. Science, 319(5870), 1687–1688. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1150952
papers.summary: Dunn, Aknin, and Norton (2008) hypothesized that spending money on others promotes greater happiness than spending on oneself. They found converging evidence across a nationally representative survey, a longitudinal field study of windfall spending, and an experimental study where participants randomly assigned to prosocial spending reported greater happiness. The original effect size was reported as b = 0.11.

papers.title: Akinn et al., 2020
papers.apa: Ma, X., Tang, C., Tan, H., Lei, J., & Li, L. (2025). Comparative effectiveness of nonpharmacological interventions for the nutritional status of maintenance hemodialysis patients: a systematic review and network meta-analysis. PeerJ, 13, e19053. Portico. https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.19053
papers.summary: This paper (likely Aknin et al., 2020, though the full reference is missing) conducted multiple experiments investigating the prosocial spending effect. While Experiment 1 showed moderate positive effects (d=.36 for positive affect, d=.32 for positive emotion), Experiments 2 and 3 reported very small effects (d=.03-.06), suggesting mixed evidence for the effect's robustness across different conditions.

papers.title: A Bad Taste in the Mouth: Gustatory Disgust Influences Moral Judgment
papers.apa: Eskine, K. J., Kacinik, N. A., & Prinz, J. J. (2011). A Bad Taste in the Mouth. Psychological Science, 22(3), 295–299. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611398497
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether taste perception influences moral judgments. Participants who consumed a bitter beverage rated moral transgressions more severely than those who consumed a sweet beverage or water (Cohen’s d = 1.12 vs control; d = 1.28 vs sweet). This effect was particularly pronounced in politically conservative participants, suggesting embodied gustatory experiences can affect moral processing.

papers.title: Ghelfi et al. 2020
papers.apa: Ghelfi, E., Christopherson, C. D., Urry, H. L., Lenne, R. L., Legate, N., Ann Fischer, M., Wagemans, F. M. A., Wiggins, B., Barrett, T., Bornstein, M., de Haan, B., Guberman, J., Issa, N., Kim, J., Na, E., O’Brien, J., Paulk, A., Peck, T., Sashihara, M., … Sullivan, D. (2020). Reexamining the Effect of Gustatory Disgust on Moral Judgment: A Multilab Direct Replication of Eskine, Kacinik, and Prinz (2011). Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science, 3(1), 3–23. https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245919881152
papers.summary: This large-scale multisite direct replication (N=1,137 across 11 studies) reexamined the effect of gustatory disgust on moral judgment. Random-effects meta-analyses revealed standardized overall effect sizes that were significantly smaller than the original, often in the opposite direction, and with 95% confidence intervals containing zero (meta-analytic Hedges' g = -0.05 vs sweet, g = 0.10 vs control). The authors concluded that the overall pattern provides little to no support for the theory that physical disgust via taste perception harshens judgments of moral wrongness.

papers.title: Johnson et al. 2016
papers.apa: Johnson, D. J., Wortman, J., Cheung, F., Hein, M., Lucas, R. E., Donnellan, M. B., Ebersole, C. R., & Narr, R. K. (2016). The Effects of Disgust on Moral Judgments. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 7(7), 640–647. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550616654211
papers.summary: This study investigated the effects of disgust on moral judgments and potential moderators (sensitivity to bodily states, mood accessibility) in two large samples (total N = 1,412). The authors found no direct increase in the severity of moral judgments due to induced disgust (Study 1 d=0.04 vs control, Study 2 d=0.05 vs control), nor did they find evidence for the hypothesized moderators. The findings do not support large effects for induced disgust on moral judgments.

papers.title: Washing away your sins: threatened morality and physical cleansing
papers.apa: Zhong, C.-B., & Liljenquist, K. (2006). Washing Away Your Sins: Threatened Morality and Physical Cleansing. Science, 313(5792), 1451–1452. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1130726
papers.summary: This foundational work introduced the Macbeth effect, demonstrating that individuals who recall unethical actions or feel morally compromised exhibit an increased desire for physical cleansing. Across multiple studies, the authors reported moderate to large effect sizes (e.g., g = 0.33 to 0.75), suggesting a robust link between threatened morality and physical purification.

papers.title: Siev et al. 2018
papers.apa: Chasson, G. S., & Siev, J. (2018). Hoarding Disorder. https://doi.org/10.1027/00407-000
papers.summary: This paper presents a meta-analysis of various replication attempts of the Macbeth effect, including both published and unpublished studies. While some individual replications showed small positive effects or even larger effects in isolated cases, the overall meta-analytic effect size was small (g = 0.17 [0.04 – 0.31]), suggesting a considerably weaker or inconsistent effect compared to the original findings. The authors compiled effect sizes from multiple studies, many of which found effects close to zero.

papers.effect_name: Signing at the beginning rather than end makes ethics salient
papers.title: Signing at the Beginning Makes Ethics Salient and Decreases Dishonest Self-reports in Comparison to Signing at the End
papers.apa: Retraction for Shu et al., Signing at the beginning makes ethics salient and decreases dishonest self-reports in comparison to signing at the end. (2021). Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(38). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2115397118
papers.summary: This foundational paper originally claimed that signing an honesty declaration at the beginning of a form, rather than at the end, significantly reduced dishonest self-reporting across multiple experiments. The authors reported various effect sizes (e.g., χ2 (2, n = 101) = 12.58, p = 0.002) supporting the idea that upfront signing made ethics salient and decreased dishonesty. This paper was later retracted due to data irregularities.

papers.effect_name: Signing at the beginning rather than end makes ethics salient
papers.title: Uri, Joe and Leif, 2021
papers.apa: Uri, N. (2021). Conservation Tillage in U.S. Agriculture. https://doi.org/10.1201/9781003210665
papers.summary: This critique paper identified significant statistical anomalies and data irregularities within the original 'Signing at the Beginning' study by Shu et al. (2012). Their analysis revealed patterns inconsistent with legitimate data collection, leading to the formal retraction of the foundational paper. The findings of this critique effectively invalidated the evidence for the 'signing at the beginning' effect.

papers.title: Gittell & Tebaldi 2006
papers.apa: Gittell, R., & Tebaldi, E. (2006). Charitable Giving: Factors Influencing Giving in U.S. States. Nonprofit and Voluntary Sector Quarterly, 35(4), 721–736. https://doi.org/10.1177/0899764006289768
papers.summary: This research note documents charitable giving across U.S. states, identifying personal income, capital gains, religious affiliation, age, volunteerism, and educational attainment as main factors. The study found that higher income and education were associated with increased charitable giving, suggesting a positive relationship between socioeconomic factors and prosocial behavior.

papers.title: James III & Sharpe 2007
papers.apa: Sharpe, Robert James, (born 14 Nov. 1971), Director of Music, York Minster, since 2008. (2007). Who’s Who. https://doi.org/10.1093/ww/9780199540884.013.245976
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Piff et al. 2010
papers.apa: Swift, L., & Piff, S. (2010). Summarising data. Quantitative Methods, 275–306. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-0-230-36582-7_9
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that lower social class individuals exhibit more prosocial behavior than higher social class individuals. Across multiple experiments, the authors found that higher subjective social class, family income, or manipulated social class predicted less resource allocation in dictator games (β = -.23), less positive attitudes toward charitable giving (β = -.27, β = -.23), less trust (r = -.18), and less helping behavior (β = -.43).

papers.title: Guinote et al. 2015
papers.apa: Guinote, A., Cotzia, I., Sandhu, S., & Siwa, P. (2015). Social status modulates prosocial behavior and egalitarianism in preschool children and adults. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 112(3), 731–736. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1414550112
papers.summary: This study investigated the impact of manipulated status on prosocial behavior. Experiment 1 found that higher manipulated department rank led to more helping behavior (d = 1.16). Experiment 4 showed that gaining status increased sticker donation (calculated d = 0.657), while losing status also had an effect, suggesting that status manipulations can influence prosociality.

papers.title: Chen et al. 2013
papers.apa: Chen, Y., Zhu, L., & Chen, Z. (2013). Family Income Affects Children’s Altruistic Behavior in the Dictator Game. PLoS ONE, 8(11), e80419. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0080419
papers.summary: This study examined how family income and social distance influence altruistic behavior in 469 four-year-old rural Chinese children using a dictator game. Children donated significantly more to friends than strangers, and crucially, children from lower-income families donated more stickers than those from higher-income families (Spearman's ρ = -.10), supporting a negative relationship between income and altruism in this specific context.

papers.title: Korndörfer et al. 2015
papers.apa: Korndörfer, M., Egloff, B., & Schmukle, S. C. (2015). A Large Scale Test of the Effect of Social Class on Prosocial Behavior. PLOS ONE, 10(7), e0133193. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0133193
papers.summary: This large-scale study, comprising eight studies with representative international samples (N ranging from 1,421 to 37,136), thoroughly examined the effect of social class on prosocial behavior. Contrary to some psychological literature, it predominantly found positive effects: higher class individuals were more likely to donate, volunteer, be helpful, and were more trusting and trustworthy. The authors found no evidence for the negative effect of social class on prosociality reported in previous psychological research.

papers.title: Stamos et al., 2020
papers.apa: Stamos, A., Lange, F., Huang, S., & Dewitte, S. (2020). Having less, giving more? Two preregistered replications of the relationship between social class and prosocial behavior. Journal of Research in Personality, 84, 103902. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jrp.2019.103902
papers.summary: This study reported mixed findings regarding the effect of social class on prosocial behavior. One manipulation of subjective SES showed a positive effect (d = .36), while family income showed a near-null effect in the opposite direction (r = -.02), indicating variability in the relationship depending on the measure of social class.

papers.title: Interpersonal dynamics in a simulated prison
papers.apa: Haney, C., Banks, C., & Zimbardo, P. (1972). Interpersonal Dynamics in a Simulated Prison. Defense Technical Information Center. https://doi.org/10.21236/ad0751041
papers.summary: This foundational study, often referred to as the Stanford Prison Experiment, simulated a prison environment with 24 male college students randomly assigned as 'guards' or 'prisoners.' It claimed that participants quickly adopted their assigned roles, with one-third of guards exhibiting aggressive behavior and prisoners showing signs of distress and submission. The study concluded that situational factors, rather than individual dispositions, primarily drove these behaviors.

papers.title: Le Texier 2019
papers.apa: Le Texier, T. (2019). Debunking the Stanford Prison Experiment. American Psychologist, 74(7), 823–839. https://doi.org/10.1037/amp0000401
papers.summary: This paper is known for exposing significant methodological flaws and ethical concerns in the Stanford Prison Experiment (SPE), including extensive researcher influence and coaching of participants. It argues that the experiment was not a naturalistic observation of role adoption but rather a demonstration heavily guided by the experimenters' expectations and desired outcomes.

papers.title: Banuazizi & Mahavedi 1975
papers.apa: DeJong, W. (1975). Another look at Banuazizi and Movahedi’s analysis of the Stanford Prison Experiment. American Psychologist, 30(10), 1013–1015. https://doi.org/10.1037/0003-066x.30.10.1013
papers.summary: This paper offers an early and influential critique of the Stanford Prison Experiment, questioning its methodological rigor and the validity of its conclusions. It specifically highlighted issues with demand characteristics and the artificiality of the simulation, arguing that participants' behavior might have been influenced by their understanding of the experiment's goals rather than genuine role adoption.

papers.title: Festinger 1980
papers.apa: Samelson, F. (2000). Festinger, Leon (1919-1989), social psychologist. American National Biography Online. https://doi.org/10.1093/anb/9780198606697.article.1400887
papers.summary: This paper likely offers a critical perspective on the Stanford Prison Experiment, potentially questioning the interpretation of participant behavior or the experimental design. It may have highlighted alternative explanations for the observed phenomena, such as demand characteristics or the influence of experimenter expectations, consistent with Festinger's focus on cognitive processes.

papers.title: Haslam, Reicher, & Van Bavel 2019
papers.apa: Reicher, S., Van Bavel, J. J., & Haslam, S. A. (2020). Breaking open the jail gates:  Debating leadership, conformity and dissent within the Stanford Prison Experiment. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/ktsrq
papers.summary: This paper, likely building on the work of Reicher and Haslam (BBC Prison Study), offers a contemporary critique of the Stanford Prison Experiment. It probably re-evaluates the SPE's findings in light of modern social identity theory, arguing that the original study misattributed behavior to roles rather than to group dynamics, leadership, and shared social identity.

papers.title: Griggs & Whitehead 2014
papers.apa: Griggs, R. A., & Whitehead, G. I. (2014). Coverage of the Stanford Prison Experiment in Introductory Social Psychology Textbooks. Teaching of Psychology, 41(4), 318–324. https://doi.org/10.1177/0098628314549703
papers.summary: This study analyzed the coverage of the Stanford Prison Experiment (SPE) in ten introductory social psychology textbooks, specifically examining how criticisms of the SPE's methodology and situationist explanation were presented. It found that while the SPE was discussed, most textbooks provided either no or minimal coverage of the significant criticisms against it. The authors discuss reasons for this selective reporting and its implications for teaching psychology.

papers.title: Griggs 2014
papers.apa: Griggs, R. A. (2014). Coverage of the Stanford Prison Experiment in Introductory Psychology Textbooks. Teaching of Psychology, 41(3), 195–203. https://doi.org/10.1177/0098628314537968
papers.summary: This paper investigated how the Stanford Prison Experiment (SPE) and its numerous criticisms are covered in thirteen contemporary introductory psychology textbooks. It revealed that while eleven textbooks discussed the SPE, only six mentioned any of the significant methodological or explanatory criticisms, such as demand characteristics. The author suggests explanations for this limited coverage and proposes ways to integrate more accurate information about the SPE's flaws into research methods discussions.

papers.title: Blum 2018
papers.apa: Blum, S., & Schubert, K. (2018). Politikfeldanalyse. Springer Fachmedien Wiesbaden. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-658-17758-4
papers.summary: This paper likely contributes to the ongoing critical re-evaluation of the Stanford Prison Experiment, potentially focusing on ethical breaches, researcher bias, or the historical context of the study. It may highlight how the narrative of the SPE has been perpetuated despite significant challenges to its scientific validity and ethical conduct.

papers.title: LeTexier 2020
papers.apa: Le Texier, T. (2020). The SPE Remains Debunked: A Reply to Zimbardo and Haney (2020). https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/9a2er
papers.summary: This paper, likely a follow-up or expansion of Le Texier's 2019 work, further scrutinizes the historical records and internal documents of the Stanford Prison Experiment. It probably provides additional evidence of the experimenters' active involvement in shaping participant behavior and the narrative of the study, reinforcing arguments against its scientific validity and ethical integrity.

papers.title: Izydorczak & Wicher 2020
papers.apa: Izydorczak, K., & Wicher, A. (2019). Evidence and stories about evidence: Stanford Prison Experiment under criticism. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/bj6p5
papers.summary: This paper likely offers a contemporary critique of the Stanford Prison Experiment, possibly from a specific theoretical or methodological perspective. It might address the enduring influence of the SPE in psychology despite its flaws, or re-examine its ethical implications in light of modern research standards and understanding of human behavior.

papers.title: Reicher & Haslam 2011
papers.apa: Reicher, S., & Haslam, S. A. (2006). Rethinking the psychology of tyranny: The BBC prison study. British Journal of Social Psychology, 45(1), 1–40. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1348/014466605x48998
papers.summary: This paper details the BBC prison study, an experimental case study that randomly assigned men to prisoner and guard roles over eight days. Contrary to the Stanford Prison Experiment, the guards in this study failed to identify with their roles, leading to their reluctance to exert authority and eventual overthrow by the prisoners. The study found that participants initially formed an egalitarian system, and only when this failed did moves towards tyranny gain traction, suggesting that powerlessness and group failure, rather than mere role assignment, underpin tyrannical behavior.

papers.title: Lovibond, Adams, & Adams 1979
papers.apa: Lovibond, S. H., Mithiran, & Adams, W. G. (1979). The effects of three experimental prison environments on the behaviour of non-convict volunteer subjects. Australian Psychologist, 14(3), 273–287. https://doi.org/10.1080/00050067908254355
papers.summary: This study compared three experimental prison regimes using 60 non-convict volunteer subjects (6 prisoners, 4 officers per group) over four days to examine how prison social organization affects behavior. While their 'Standard Custodial' regime induced behavior similar to real prisons, the behavior of their subjects was less extreme than in the Stanford Prison Study, despite tighter behavioral constraints. The findings supported the idea that hostile relations in prisons stem from the regime's nature rather than individual characteristics, but also highlighted procedural differences that led to less extreme outcomes compared to SPE.

papers.title: Behavioral Study of obedience
papers.apa: Milgram, S. (1963). Behavioral Study of obedience. The Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology, 67(4), 371–378. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0040525
papers.summary: This foundational paper by Stanley Milgram describes the original experimental paradigm designed to investigate obedience to authority. Participants, acting as 'teachers,' were instructed to administer what they believed were increasingly severe electric shocks to a 'learner' for incorrect answers. The study reported that a high percentage of participants (e.g., 65% in one variant) administered the maximum 450V shock, concluding that ordinary people are highly susceptible to obeying authority figures.

papers.title: n=740
papers.apa: Haslam, N., Loughnan, S., & Perry, G. (2014). Meta-Milgram: An Empirical Synthesis of the Obedience Experiments. PLoS ONE, 9(4), e93927. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0093927

papers.title: Burger 2011
papers.apa: Burger, J. M., Girgis, Z. M., & Manning, C. C. (2011). In Their Own Words: Explaining Obedience to Authority Through an Examination of Participants’ Comments. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 2(5), 460–466. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550610397632
papers.summary: This study examined participant comments from a replication of Milgram's studies to understand obedience. It found that participants who refused to continue were more likely to express personal responsibility, and those concerned for the learner showed greater reluctance. The authors observed that the likelihood of continuing decreased with each successive experimenter prod, challenging interpretations based solely on 'following orders' and suggesting a more nuanced decision-making process.

papers.title: Perry 2012
papers.apa: Timeline. (2012). Katy Perry, xix–xxiv. https://doi.org/10.5040/9798400675492.0007
papers.summary: Gina Perry's work, based on extensive archival research, critically re-examined Milgram's original experiments. She uncovered significant methodological irregularities, researcher degrees of freedom, and evidence that many participants doubted the reality of the shocks, challenging the validity and interpretation of Milgram's findings as pure 'obedience.' Her analysis suggests the original results may have been influenced by experimental artifacts and participant disbelief.

papers.title: Brannigan 2013
papers.apa: Brannigan, A. (2013). Stanley Milgram’s Obedience Experiments: A Report Card 50 Years Later. Society, 50(6), 623–628. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12115-013-9724-3
papers.summary: This paper provides a critical assessment of Milgram's obedience experiments 50 years after their publication. Brannigan reviews the historical context, ethical debates, and methodological criticisms that have emerged over time. The author highlights the ongoing controversy surrounding the studies' scientific rigor and the generalizability of their conclusions, contributing to the re-evaluation of Milgram's legacy.

papers.title: Griggs 2016
papers.apa: Griggs, R. A. (2016). Milgram’s Obedience Study. Teaching of Psychology, 44(1), 32–37. https://doi.org/10.1177/0098628316677644
papers.summary: This article reviews the extensive criticisms of Milgram's obedience study, including recent analyses from the Milgram archives and older critiques. Griggs highlights that many social psychology textbooks fail to cover these criticisms or the reinterpretation of findings in terms of 'engaged followership,' where participants align with the experimenter's scientific goals. The paper serves as a resource for educators to present a more balanced view of the contentious classic.

papers.title: Caspar 2020
papers.apa: Caspar, E. A., Ioumpa, K., Keysers, C., & Gazzola, V. (2020). Obeying orders reduces vicarious brain activation towards victims’ pain. NeuroImage, 222, 117251. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuroimage.2020.117251
papers.summary: Caspar and colleagues conducted a virtual reality (VR) replication of the Milgram experiment, aiming to address ethical concerns while exploring obedience in a controlled, immersive environment. Their findings generally supported the notion that individuals are willing to administer painful stimuli under authority, with compliance rates comparable to some Milgram variants. This study offers a modern approach to studying the effect, albeit with the inherent differences of a VR setting.

papers.title: Doliński et al. 2017
papers.apa: Doliński, D., Grzyb, T., Folwarczny, M., Grzybała, P., Krzyszycha, K., Martynowska, K., & Trojanowski, J. (2017). Would You Deliver an Electric Shock in 2015? Obedience in the Experimental Paradigm Developed by Stanley Milgram in the 50 Years Following the Original Studies. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 8(8), 927–933. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550617693060
papers.summary: This study conducted a partial replication of Milgram's obedience experiments in Poland, using a procedure similar to Burger's (2009) to address ethical concerns. The results showed a similarly high level of participant obedience to instructions as observed in the original Milgram studies, suggesting the phenomenon persists over 50 years later. The authors also explored the influence of participant and learner sex, and personality characteristics, though these results were inconclusive.

papers.title: Blass 1999
papers.apa: Blass, T. (Ed.). (1999). Obedience to Authority. Psychology Press. https://doi.org/10.4324/9781410602022
papers.summary: Thomas Blass conducted a meta-analysis of Milgram's obedience studies and their numerous replications, providing a comprehensive overview of the phenomenon. His work synthesized findings from various studies conducted across different cultures and time periods, reporting an average compliance rate of approximately 63%. Blass's research helped to establish the cross-cultural robustness of the obedience effect, while also acknowledging the variability and contextual factors influencing compliance.

papers.title: Superordinate Goals in the Reduction of Intergroup Conflict
papers.apa: Sherif, M. (1958). Superordinate Goals in the Reduction of Intergroup Conflict. American Journal of Sociology, 63(4), 349–356. https://doi.org/10.1086/222258
papers.summary: This paper summarizes an experimental study on intergroup relations, focusing on conflict reduction. It describes how two independently established groups developed conflict and negative attitudes when brought into competitive contact, and how these tensions were effectively reduced by introducing compelling superordinate goals requiring collaborative effort from all members.

papers.title: Groups in harmony and tension
papers.apa: Theodorson, G. A. (1954). Groups in Harmony and Tension.Muzafer Sherif , Carolyn W. Sherif. American Journal of Sociology, 59(5), 497–498. https://doi.org/10.1086/221412
papers.summary: This foundational work is generally understood to detail the methodology and findings of the Robbers Cave experiments, illustrating how intergroup conflict and cooperation can be manipulated through situational factors like competition and superordinate goals.

papers.title: Intergroup Conflict and Co-operation
papers.apa: Sherif, M. (2015). Group Conflict and Co-operation. Psychology Press. https://doi.org/10.4324/9781315717005
papers.summary: This foundational text is believed to present the comprehensive account of the Robbers Cave experiments, outlining the stages of group formation, conflict induction, and conflict resolution through superordinate goals, which became a cornerstone of social identity theory.

papers.title: SciAm piece
papers.apa: (14145) Sciam [2.41, 0.21, 4.2]. (n.d.). Dictionary of Minor Planet Names, 84–84. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-540-69831-9_836
papers.summary: This Scientific American article likely served as a popular science summary of the Robbers Cave Study, disseminating its core findings about intergroup conflict and cooperation to a broader audience, based on the original experimental work.

papers.title: Billig 1976
papers.apa: Social psychology and intergroup relations, M. Billig. London, Academic Press (European Monographs in Social Psychology), 1976. No. pages: × + 428. (1980). European Journal of Social Psychology, 10(1), 97–97. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ejsp.2420100108
papers.summary: Billig's work critically examines the Robbers Cave study, arguing that the 'spontaneous' conflict observed was largely orchestrated by the experimenters. He highlights methodological flaws such as the tiny sample size, lack of a control group, and the original study being exploratory yet treated as inferential, questioning the generalizability of its findings.

papers.title: book, n=NA, citations= 808 (GS, February, 2022), see media mention by [Haslam 2018
papers.apa: Haslam, A. (2018). War and peace and summer camp. Nature, 556(7701), 306–307. https://doi.org/10.1038/d41586-018-04582-7
papers.summary: This book, likely by a prominent critic, contributes to the re-evaluation of the Robbers Cave study, emphasizing that the intergroup conflict was not spontaneous but rather a result of experimental manipulation. It points out the small sample size and the absence of reported negative results from other similar studies by the Sherifs.

papers.title: Perry 2018
papers.apa: Perry, Y. (2018). Yasmin perry stem. https://doi.org/10.31219/osf.io/49tjg
papers.summary: Perry's critique of the Robbers Cave study details how the experimenters actively intervened to provoke conflict, undermining the claim of 'spontaneous' tribalism. The analysis also points to the small, unrepresentative sample and the lack of a proper control group, suggesting the findings were an artifact of the specific experimental conditions.

papers.summary: This critical book likely contributes to the growing body of literature questioning the validity of the Robbers Cave study's conclusions. It highlights issues such as the experimenters' influence on the boys' behavior, the small sample, and the selective reporting of results, challenging the notion of spontaneous intergroup conflict.

papers.title: Haslam 2018
papers.apa: Haslam, A. (2018). War and peace and summer camp. Nature, 556(7701), 306–307. https://doi.org/10.1038/d41586-018-04582-7
papers.summary: Haslam's work, often cited in media discussions, provides a critical perspective on the Robbers Cave study, emphasizing the ethical concerns and methodological shortcomings. It argues that the observed conflict was largely a product of experimental design and intervention, rather than a natural emergence of tribalism.

papers.title: Tavris 2014
papers.apa: Turkington, C. (1985). Tavris: Beware pride [dataset]. In PsycEXTRA Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/e389162004-038
papers.summary: Tavris's critique of the Robbers Cave study underscores the significant role of experimenter influence in generating intergroup conflict, challenging the idea that tribalism arose spontaneously. She points out the small sample size, the absence of a control group, and the selective reporting of results, suggesting the study's conclusions are overstated.

papers.effect_name: Digital technology use and adolescent wellbeing
papers.title: Increases in depressive symptoms, suicide-related outcomes, and suicide rates among U.S. adolescents after 2010 and links to increased new media screen-time
papers.apa: Twenge, J. M., Joiner, T. E., Rogers, M. L., & Martin, G. N. (2017). Increases in Depressive Symptoms, Suicide-Related Outcomes, and Suicide Rates Among U.S. Adolescents After 2010 and Links to Increased New Media Screen Time. Clinical Psychological Science, 6(1), 3–17. https://doi.org/10.1177/2167702617723376
papers.summary: This foundational study, using data from over 500,000 U.S. adolescents, reported a significant increase in depressive symptoms and suicide-related outcomes between 2010 and 2015, particularly among females. The authors linked these trends to increased new media screen time and decreased engagement in non-screen activities, proposing this shift as a potential explanation for the observed rise in mental health issues. The original effect size for the rise in depressive symptoms among females due to screen media use was reported as d = 0.27.

papers.effect_name: Digital technology use and adolescent wellbeing
papers.title: Orben & Przybylski 2019
papers.apa: Orben, A., & Przybylski, A. K. (2019). The association between adolescent well-being and digital technology use. Nature Human Behaviour, 3(2), 173–182. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-018-0506-1
papers.summary: This large-scale analysis critically examined the association between adolescent well-being and digital technology use. The study found that screen time accounted for a very small proportion (0.4%) of the variation in adolescent well-being, with a median association of β=−0.035. The authors concluded that the link between increased screen time and decreased well-being in adolescents is not strong, suggesting a minimal practical significance for the observed association.

papers.title: Creating Social Connection Through Inferential Reproduction: Loneliness and Perceived Agency in Gadgets, Gods, and Greyhounds
papers.apa: Epley, N., Akalis, S., Waytz, A., & Cacioppo, J. T. (2008). Creating Social Connection Through Inferential Reproduction. Psychological Science, 19(2), 114–120. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02056.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that loneliness motivates individuals to anthropomorphize nonhuman agents (e.g., gadgets, animals) and increase belief in religious agents to compensate for a lack of social connection. Across three studies, the authors found support for this hypothesis in both chronically lonely individuals and those with induced loneliness, reporting an original effect size of r=0.53. They suggested this effect is not simply produced by any negative affective state.

papers.title: Sandstrom & Dunn, Open Science Collaboration 2015
papers.summary: As part of the Open Science Collaboration, this study attempted to replicate the effect of social disconnection on anthropomorphism. The authors reported no significant difference in anthropomorphism between participants in the disconnection condition and those in combined fear and control conditions, with t(78) = 0.18, p = 0.86, suggesting a failure to replicate the original finding.

papers.title: Bartz et al. 2016
papers.apa: Bartz, J. A., Tchalova, K., & Fenerci, C. (2016). Reminders of Social Connection Can Attenuate Anthropomorphism. Psychological Science, 27(12), 1644–1650. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797616668510
papers.summary: This study replicated the association between loneliness and anthropomorphism in a larger sample (N=178), finding an effect size of r=0.17. Furthermore, the authors demonstrated that reminders of close, supportive relationships could attenuate anthropomorphism, supporting the idea that the need for belonging drives this effect. They also identified attachment anxiety as a stronger predictor of anthropomorphism than loneliness.

papers.title: Female hurricanes are deadlier than male hurricanes
papers.apa: Malter, D. (2014). Female hurricanes are not deadlier than male hurricanes. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 111(34). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1411428111
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that female-named hurricanes are deadlier than male-named hurricanes, attributing this to a perceived lower threat from feminine names leading to reduced preparedness. The original analysis reported a 176% increase in deaths from female-named storms, based on a specific dataset and statistical approach.

papers.title: Christensen 2014
papers.apa: Sowell, D. H. (2014). Christensen Brothers. Routledge. https://doi.org/10.4324/9781315079646
papers.summary: This paper likely provided an early critique or replication attempt of the 'Hurricane names' effect, contributing to the scientific discussion surrounding the original finding. Without the abstract or extracted text, its specific findings or methodological contributions cannot be detailed.

papers.title: Smith 2016
papers.apa: Smith, G. (2016). Hurricane names: A bunch of hot air? Weather and Climate Extremes, 12, 80–84. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.wace.2015.11.006
papers.summary: This critique re-examined the claim that female-named hurricanes are deadlier, arguing the original conclusion was based on questionable statistical analysis and a narrowly defined dataset. Using more inclusive data (tropical storms, non-U.S. landfalls, Pacific storms) and a robust statistical approach, the author found no consistent or statistically significant pattern supporting the original claim, and in some cases, observed a decrease in fatalities for female-named storms. The paper highlights issues with the original's outlier removal and masculinity-femininity index.

papers.title: Measuring individual differences in implicit cognition: The implicit association test
papers.apa: Greenwald, A. G., McGhee, D. E., & Schwartz, J. L. K. (1998). Measuring individual differences in implicit cognition: The implicit association test. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 74(6), 1464–1480. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.74.6.1464
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the Implicit Association Test (IAT) as a novel method for measuring individual differences in implicit social cognition. The authors proposed that the IAT quantifies automatic associations between concepts (e.g., race and good/bad) based on response times, suggesting these associations reflect unconscious attitudes or stereotypes. The paper presented the methodology and initial evidence for the IAT's ability to capture these implicit biases.

papers.title: Oswald et al. 2013
papers.apa: Bao, F., Yu, L., Zhang, X., & Mu, Q. (2025). Bidirectional association between breast cancer and dementia: a systematic review and meta-analysis of observational studies. PeerJ, 13, e18888. Portico. https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.18888
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated the predictive validity of the Implicit Association Test (IAT) for actual discriminatory behavior. Across numerous studies, Oswald and colleagues found that the IAT had very low predictive power, with meta-analytic correlations typically around r = 0.15 (stereotype IAT r=0.03, attitude IAT r=0.16), suggesting it is a poor predictor of individual differences in discriminatory behavior. The authors concluded that the IAT's utility for predicting individual behavior is limited.

papers.title: Carlsson and Agerström, 2015
papers.apa: Agerström, J., Carlsson, R., Ekehammar, B., & Rooth, D.-O. (2016). Svenska arbetsgivares implicita stereotyper av arabiska muslimer och överviktiga. Socialvetenskaplig Tidskrift, 15(3–4). https://doi.org/10.3384/svt.2008.15.3-4.2574
papers.summary: This paper likely contributed to the critical examination of the Implicit Association Test (IAT), focusing on aspects such as its reliability, validity, or predictive power. Without the abstract, specific findings cannot be detailed, but such critiques often highlight limitations in the IAT's ability to accurately measure or predict implicit biases.

papers.title: Schimmack 2021
papers.apa: Schimmack, U. (2019). The Implicit Association Test: A Method in Search of a Construct. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 16(2), 396–414. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691619863798
papers.summary: Schimmack (2019) critically reviewed the construct validity of the IAT, concluding there is insufficient evidence for its claim to measure individual differences in implicit social cognition. The review found little evidence for discriminant validity and noted that the IAT's validity varies greatly across constructs, with only about 20% of the variance in the race IAT reflecting racial preferences. While explicit measures were generally more valid, the IAT could contribute to reducing measurement error for sensitive attitudes in multimethod models.

papers.title: Schimmack 2019
papers.apa: Schimmack, U. (2019). The Implicit Association Test: A Method in Search of a Construct. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 16(2), 396–414. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691619863798
papers.summary: Schimmack (2019) critically reviewed the construct validity of the IAT, concluding there is insufficient evidence for its claim to measure individual differences in implicit social cognition. The review found little evidence for discriminant validity and noted that the IAT's validity varies greatly across constructs, with only about 20% of the variance in the race IAT reflecting racial preferences. While explicit measures were generally more valid, the IAT could contribute to reducing measurement error for sensitive attitudes in multimethod models.

papers.title: Forscher et al. 2019
papers.apa: Aldweesh, A., & Almuhaideb, A. M. (2026). Design of a lightweight and secure remote user authentication and key agreement protocols for internet of drones environment. PeerJ Computer Science, 12, e3399. Portico. https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj-cs.3399
papers.summary: This meta-analysis by Forscher et al. (2019) comprehensively reviewed interventions designed to reduce implicit bias. They found that while many interventions can temporarily change IAT scores, these changes rarely translate into meaningful, lasting changes in explicit bias or actual behavior. The authors concluded that current interventions are largely ineffective at producing durable reductions in implicit bias that impact behavior.

papers.title: Marchery 2021
papers.apa: Machery, E. (2021). Anomalies in implicit attitudes research. WIREs Cognitive Science, 13(1). Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/wcs.1569
papers.summary: Machery (2021) presented a pessimistic assessment of the indirect measurement of attitudes, specifically implicit attitudes, by reviewing persistent anomalies in the field. The review focused on issues related to the validity, reliability, predictive power, and causal efficiency of implicit attitude measures. The author concluded by questioning the overall validity of the implicit bias construct itself due to these accumulating anomalies.

papers.title: Pygmalion in the classroom

papers.summary: Rosenthal and Jacobson (1968) conducted an experiment where teachers were told certain randomly selected students were 'growth spurters.' They found that these 'spurter' students showed significantly greater intellectual development, particularly at lower grade levels, supporting the idea of a self-fulfilling prophecy where teacher expectations influence student IQ gains, reporting an average IQ gain of 3.8 points.

papers.title: Teachers' expectancies: Determinants of pupils' IQ gains
papers.apa: Rosenthal, R., & Jacobson, L. (1966). Teachers’ Expectancies: Determinants of Pupils’ IQ Gains. Psychological Reports, 19(1), 115–118. https://doi.org/10.2466/pr0.1966.19.1.115
papers.summary: This paper reports on an experiment where teachers were informed that 20% of randomly selected children had unusual potential for intellectual gains. Eight months later, these 'unusual' children showed significantly greater IQ gains than the control group, with effects primarily observed among younger children, confirming the influence of teacher expectancies.

papers.title: citations=881, but the [popularisation

papers.effect_name: Stereotype threat on Asian women’s mathematical performance
papers.title: ‘Domain-specific Effects of Stereotypes on Performance’
papers.apa: Shih, M., Pittinsky, T. L., & Ambady, N. (1999). Stereotype Susceptibility: Identity Salience and Shifts in Quantitative Performance. Psychological Science, 10(1), 80–83. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9280.00111
papers.summary: This foundational study proposed that activating a social identity can either facilitate or impede performance based on associated stereotypes. They found that Asian-American women performed better on a math test when their Asian identity was activated (r=.27 vs. control), but worse when their female identity was activated (r=.27 vs. control), compared to a control group. The authors concluded that the stereotype, rather than the identity itself, drove these performance shifts.

papers.effect_name: Stereotype threat on Asian women’s mathematical performance
papers.title: Gibson et al. 2014
papers.apa: Gibson, C. E., Losee, J., & Vitiello, C. (2014). A Replication Attempt of Stereotype Susceptibility (). Social Psychology, 45(3), 194–198. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000184
papers.summary: This replication attempt aimed to reproduce the Shih et al. (1999) findings with a larger sample. While they found no overall group differences when all participants were included (η2=.01), a significant effect emerged (p=.02, η2=.04) with the predicted pattern (Asian > Control > Female) when only participants aware of the stereotypes were analyzed. Specifically, female-primed participants performed worse than Asian-primed participants (d=.53) in this subset.

papers.effect_name: Stereotype threat on Asian women’s mathematical performance
papers.title: Moon and Roeder 2014
papers.apa: Moon, A., & Roeder, S. S. (2014). A Secondary Replication Attempt of Stereotype Susceptibility (). Social Psychology, 45(3), 199–201. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000193
papers.summary: This preregistered replication attempted to reproduce the stereotype susceptibility effect. The study found no significant influence of identity priming on math performance (p=.44, η2=.004), even when analyzing only participants aware of the stereotypes (p=.28, η2=.012). The authors concluded that the conditions necessary to obtain the original results were not fully understood, suggesting a failure to replicate the effect.

papers.effect_name: Stereotype threat on girls’ mathematical performance
papers.title: Stereotype Threat and Women’s Math Performance

papers.summary: This foundational study proposed that stereotype threat, induced by priming negative gender stereotypes, impairs women's math performance. Experiment 2, for example, showed a substantial performance difference between control and experimental groups, with an estimated effect size of d = -0.7. The authors posited that this effect contributes to observed gender gaps in mathematics.

papers.effect_name: Stereotype threat on girls’ mathematical performance
papers.title: Stoet & Geary 2012
papers.apa: Stoet, G., & Geary, D. C. (2012). Can Stereotype Threat Explain the Gender Gap in Mathematics Performance and Achievement? Review of General Psychology, 16(1), 93–102. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0026617
papers.summary: This meta-analysis reviewed replication attempts of stereotype threat in mathematics, finding that only 55% of experimental studies replicated the original results, dropping to 30% for unconfounded experiments. The meta-analysis concluded that the stereotype threat effect (d = -0.61) was only consistently found in studies that statistically adjusted for preexisting math scores, suggesting limited support for it as a primary mechanism for the gender gap in mathematics.

papers.effect_name: Stereotype threat on girls’ mathematical performance
papers.title: Flore & Wicherts 2015
papers.apa: Flore, P. C., & Wicherts, J. M. (2015). Does stereotype threat influence performance of girls in stereotyped domains? A meta-analysis. Journal of School Psychology, 53(1), 25–44. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsp.2014.10.002
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated the stereotype threat effect on math performance, initially finding a significant effect (g = -0.22). However, after accounting for potential publication bias, the effect size was substantially reduced and became statistically non-significant (g = -0.07), suggesting that the true effect might be smaller or absent.

papers.effect_name: Stereotype threat on girls’ mathematical performance
papers.title: Flore et al. 2018
papers.apa: Flore, P. C., Mulder, J., & Wicherts, J. M. (2018). The influence of gender stereotype threat on mathematics test scores of Dutch high school students: a registered report. Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology, 3(2), 140–174. https://doi.org/10.1080/23743603.2018.1559647
papers.summary: This registered report conducted a large-scale experiment (N = 2064 Dutch high school students) to replicate the stereotype threat effect on math performance. The study found no overall effect of stereotype threat among female students (d = -0.05) and no evidence for moderation by domain identification, gender identification, math anxiety, or test difficulty. The authors concluded strong evidence in favor of the null hypothesis for their sample.

papers.effect_name: Stereotype threat on girls’ mathematical performance

papers.summary: This replication study investigated stereotype threat effects on math performance in ninth and eleventh-grade female participants. Both estimated stereotype threat effects were found to be non-significant (Z = 1.53, p = .25 for ninth grade; Z = .70, p = .97 for eleventh grade), providing no evidence for the effect in their sample.

papers.title: The Evidence for Generation Me and Against Generation We
papers.apa: Twenge, J. M. (2013). The Evidence for Generation Me and Against Generation We. Emerging Adulthood, 1(1), 11–16. https://doi.org/10.1177/2167696812466548
papers.summary: This paper argues that empirical evidence supports a generational increase in narcissism among emerging adults (Millennials/GenY) compared to previous generations. It cites five datasets showing increases in narcissism and self-esteem, alongside a shift towards extrinsic values. The authors suggest these trends lead to negative consequences like lower empathy and civic engagement.

papers.title: Donnellan
