papers.summary: This entry appears to be a partial or duplicate reference to the Trzesniewski & Donnellan (2010) paper. Without a DOI or abstract, a specific summary cannot be provided for this standalone entry.

papers.title: Rethinking “Generation Me”: A Study of Cohort Effects from 1976-2006
papers.apa: Trzesniewski, K. H., & Donnellan, M. B. (2010). Rethinking “Generation Me”: A Study of Cohort Effects from 1976-2006. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 5(1), 58–75. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691609356789
papers.summary: Using a large sample of U.S. high-school seniors (N = 477,380) from 1976 to 2006, this study found little evidence of meaningful generational change in egotism, self-enhancement, individualism, or self-esteem. While some shifts were noted (e.g., less fear of social problems, more cynicism, higher educational expectations), the authors concluded that effect sizes did not support strong or widespread cohort-linked changes.

papers.title: The Evidence for Generation We and Against Generation Me
papers.apa: Arnett, J. J. (2013). The Evidence for Generation We and Against Generation Me. Emerging Adulthood, 1(1), 5–10. https://doi.org/10.1177/2167696812466842
papers.summary: This article critically examines claims of rising narcissism in emerging adults, concluding there is no persuasive evidence for an increase in Narcissistic Personality Inventory (NPI) scores. The author questions the validity of the NPI and the representativeness of college student samples. It suggests that high expectations and confidence are psychological resources, not indicators of narcissism, and notes a decrease in impulsive behaviors.

papers.title: It is Developmental Me, Not Generation Me: Developmental Changes are more Important than Generational Changes in Narcissism—Commentary on Trzesniewski & Donnellan (2010)
papers.apa: Roberts, B. W., Edmonds, G., & Grijalva, E. (2010). It is Developmental Me, Not Generation Me: Developmental Changes are more Important than Generational Changes in Narcissism—Commentary on Trzesniewski & Donnellan (2010). Perspectives on Psychological Science, 5(1), 97–102. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691609357019
papers.summary: This commentary argues that when new data are integrated into existing meta-analytic data, there is no evidence of an increase in narcissism among college students over recent decades. In contrast, the authors demonstrate that age-related changes in narcissism are significant and replicable, being much larger than any purported generational changes. They conclude that younger individuals are consistently more narcissistic than their elders, regardless of generation.

papers.title: Wetzel 2017
papers.apa: Wetzel, E., Brown, A., Hill, P. L., Chung, J. M., Robins, R. W., & Roberts, B. W. (2017). The Narcissism Epidemic Is Dead; Long Live the Narcissism Epidemic. Psychological Science, 28(12), 1833–1847. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797617724208
papers.summary: This paper is cited as a critique with a replication effect size of d = -0.27 (1990 - 2010), suggesting a decrease in narcissism over this period. Without an abstract or further details, a comprehensive summary of its methodology or specific findings cannot be provided.

papers.title: Hamamura et al. 2020
papers.apa: Hamamura, T., Johnson, C. A., & Stankovic, M. (2020). Narcissism over time in Australia and Canada: A cross-temporal meta-analysis. Personality and Individual Differences, 155, 109707. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2019.109707
papers.summary: This paper is classified as a meta-analysis, reporting negative effect sizes for various components of narcissism (d(leadership) = -0.26, d(vanity)=-0.39, d(entitlement) = -0.23). These findings suggest a decrease in these traits over time rather than an increase. Without an abstract, details on the scope or methodology of the meta-analysis are unavailable.

papers.title: 'Arousal of ingroup-outgroup bias by a chance win or loss'
papers.summary: This foundational paper is often cited as one of the earliest experimental demonstrations of the minimal group effect, showing that arbitrary group assignment can lead to ingroup favoritism in resource allocation. Specific findings and sample sizes are not available in the provided text.

papers.title: Rabbie and Horwitz 1969
papers.summary: This foundational study is recognized for its early experimental work on intergroup discrimination, demonstrating that mere awareness of group membership, even without competition or prior interaction, can elicit ingroup favoritism. Specific findings and sample sizes are not available in the provided text.

papers.title: Balliet et al. 2014
papers.apa: Balliet et al reply. (1986). Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery & Psychiatry, 49(8), 966–967. https://doi.org/10.1136/jnnp.49.8.966
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings on the minimal group effect, reporting an overall effect size of d=0.19 for situations without mutual interdependence and d=0.42 for situations with strong mutual interdependence between group members, confirming the presence of ingroup favoritism across various contexts.

papers.title: Billig and Tajfel 1973
papers.apa: Billig, M., & Tajfel, H. (1973). Social categorization and similarity in intergroup behaviour. European Journal of Social Psychology, 3(1), 27–52. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ejsp.2420030103
papers.summary: This experimental study investigated the role of social categorization in intergroup behavior, finding that subjects discriminated against an outgroup even when group assignment was explicitly random and offered no personal advantage. The discrimination was more pronounced when the notion of 'group' was introduced, supporting the idea that mere categorization is sufficient to induce ingroup favoritism, alongside a determinant of fairness.

papers.title: Falk et al. 2014
papers.apa: Falk, C. F., Heine, S. J., & Takemura, K. (2013). Cultural Variation in the Minimal Group Effect. Journal of Cross-Cultural Psychology, 45(2), 265–281. https://doi.org/10.1177/0022022113492892
papers.summary: This cross-cultural study (N=large and diverse sample of Japanese and American participants) examined the minimal group effect across cultures, finding that American participants exhibited a stronger ingroup bias in group identification, perceived group traits, and resource allocation compared to Japanese participants. These cultural differences were partially mediated by self-esteem, suggesting cultural variability in the effect's magnitude.

papers.title: Fischer and Derham 2016
papers.apa: Fischer, R., & Derham, C. (2016). Is in-group bias culture-dependent? A meta-analysis across 18 societies. SpringerPlus, 5(1). https://doi.org/10.1186/s40064-015-1663-6
papers.summary: This meta-analysis (N=21,266 participants from 18 societies) investigated the cultural dependency of ingroup bias, finding a significant overall effect size of d=0.369. The study supported the uncertainty-reduction hypothesis, showing that ingroup bias increased in more uncertainty-avoidant contexts and varied systematically across societies, with higher status groups exhibiting greater bias and real groups showing higher bias.

papers.title: Lazić et al. 2021
papers.apa:
Lazić, A., Purić, D., & Krstić, K. (2021). Does parochial cooperation exist in childhood and adolescence? A
                    <scp>meta‐analysis</scp>. International Journal of Psychology, 56(6), 917–933. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ijop.12791
papers.summary: This meta-analysis (k=69 studies, N=5268, age 3–19) explored parochial cooperation in children and adolescents, revealing a small but significant overall effect size (d=0.22) indicating greater cooperation towards ingroups. The effect was larger in non-interdependent games but did not vary with participant age, group type, or membership manipulation, suggesting early emergence of ingroup favoritism.

papers.title: Kerr et al., 2018
papers.apa: Kerr, N. L., Ao, X., Hogg, M. A., & Zhang, J. (2018). Addressing replicability concerns via adversarial collaboration: Discovering hidden moderators of the minimal intergroup discrimination effect. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 78, 66–76. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2018.05.001
papers.summary: This experimental study compared US and Australian samples, highlighting the importance of context-dependent factors and cultural variation in the minimal group effect. It found significant main effects of categorization on allocation measures (ηp2 = 0.031 to 0.081), with ingroup favoritism present in both public (ηp2= 0.072) and private (ηp2= 0.020) contexts, but stronger in public settings.

papers.title: Mullen et al. (1992)
papers.apa: Mullen, B., Brown, R., & Smith, C. (1992). Ingroup bias as a function of salience, relevance, and status: An integration. European Journal of Social Psychology, 22(2), 103–122. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ejsp.2420220202
papers.summary: This meta-analytic integration of 137 tests of the ingroup bias hypothesis found a highly significant and moderate overall effect. The ingroup bias was stronger when the ingroup was salient and showed a significant interaction between group reality and status: a slight decrease in bias with status in real groups, but a significant increase in artificial groups. Higher status groups showed more bias on relevant attributes, while lower status groups did so on less relevant ones.

papers.title: Tajfel 1970
papers.summary: This seminal experimental paper by Tajfel is often cited as a key foundational study for the minimal group paradigm, demonstrating that mere categorization into arbitrary groups is sufficient to elicit ingroup favoritism in resource allocation. Specific findings and sample sizes are not available in the provided text.

papers.title: Tajfel et al. 1971
papers.apa: Tajfel, H., Billig, M. G., Bundy, R. P., & Flament, C. (1971). Social categorization and intergroup behaviour. European Journal of Social Psychology, 1(2), 149–178. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ejsp.2420010202
papers.summary: This foundational experimental paper presented a series of studies demonstrating that social categorization alone, without individual interest or prior hostility, was sufficient to induce discriminative behavior against an outgroup. Participants favored their own group in reward distribution, and the clearest effect was an attempt to achieve maximum difference between ingroup and outgroup, even at the cost of objective advantages.

papers.title: Studies of independence and conformity: I. A minority of one against a unanimous majority of one against a unanimous majority’
papers.apa: Asch, S. E. (1956). Studies of independence and conformity: I. A minority of one against a unanimous majority. Psychological Monographs: General and Applied, 70(9), 1–70. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0093718
papers.summary: Solomon Asch's foundational study investigated how social pressure influences individual judgment. Participants were asked to match line lengths, with confederates providing incorrect answers. Asch found that 36.8% of responses conformed to the incorrect majority, but he emphasized the prevalence of independence, noting that the majority of judgments were independent.

papers.title: Friend et al. 1990
papers.apa: Friend, R., Rafferty, Y., & Bramel, D. (1990). A puzzling misinterpretation of the Asch ‘conformity’ study. European Journal of Social Psychology, 20(1), 29–44. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ejsp.2420200104
papers.summary: This paper critiques the common interpretation of Asch's conformity study in social psychology textbooks. Analyzing 99 textbook accounts, the authors found a growing trend to distort Asch's findings by overemphasizing conformity and underestimating independence, often by misrepresenting statistics like the '75% conformity' figure. They argue this misinterpretation stems from insufficient care in reading the findings, neglecting qualitative results, and ignoring Asch's broader theoretical framework.

papers.title: Griggs 2015
papers.apa: Griggs, G. (2015). Understanding Primary Physical Education. Routledge. https://doi.org/10.4324/9780203794913
papers.summary: This paper, similar to Friend et al. (1990), likely examines how Asch's conformity study is presented in academic textbooks, highlighting a common misinterpretation that overemphasizes conformity and underreports the prevalence of independent judgments among participants.

papers.title: Bond and Smith, 1996
papers.apa: Smith, D. W. (1996). Additive bond energy scheme for the calculation of enthalpies of formation and bond dissociation energies for alkyl radicals. Journal of the Chemical Society, Faraday Transactions, 92(22), 4415. https://doi.org/10.1039/ft9969204415
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings from various replications of Asch's conformity paradigm. They reported a meta-analytic effect size of d = 0.92, with an average rate of 25% incorrect (conforming) answers. This indicates a robust, albeit potentially smaller than commonly perceived, effect of social conformity.

papers.title: Dynamic Norms Promote Sustainable Behavior, Even if It Is Counternormative

papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of dynamic norms, proposing that people are more likely to adopt a behavior if they believe its prevalence is increasing, even if it's currently a minority behavior. The original studies demonstrated that communicating a positive trend in sustainable behavior (e.g., reducing meat consumption) significantly increased participants' intentions and actual engagement in those behaviors, with reported effect sizes ranging from d=0.31 to d=0.41.

papers.title: Aldoh et al., 2021
papers.apa: Aldoh, A., Sparks, P., & Harris, P. R. (2021). Dynamic Norms and Food Choice: Reflections on a Failure of Minority Norm Information to Influence Motivation to Reduce Meat Consumption. Sustainability, 13(15), 8315. https://doi.org/10.3390/su13158315
papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the dynamic norms effect, aiming to reproduce the original findings. Across multiple studies, the authors found no significant evidence for the dynamic norms effect, reporting a meta-analytic effect size of d = -0.02. This suggests that the effect may not be robust or generalizable under the conditions tested.

papers.title: Who is most likely to fat talk? A social comparison perspective’
papers.apa: Corning, A. F., & Gondoli, D. M. (2012). Who is most likely to fat talk? A social comparison perspective. Body Image, 9(4), 528–531. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bodyim.2012.05.004
papers.summary: This foundational study (N=143 college women) proposed that social comparison processes underlie and motivate fat talk. It found a significant interaction effect, where a stronger tendency to socially compare intensified the likelihood of fat talk as body image concerns increased. The model including this interaction accounted for 48% of the variance in fat talk, with an original effect size of Hedges’ g = 0.36 for the interaction.

papers.title: Pollet et al. 2021
papers.apa: Pollet, T. V., Dawson, S., Tovée, M. J., Cornelissen, P. L., & Cornelissen, K. K. (2021). papers.title: Fischer et al. 2011

papers.title: Romantic red: Red enhances men’s attraction to women

papers.title: Peperkoorn et al. 2016

papers.title: Pazda et al. 2021
papers.apa: Pazda, A. D., Thorstenson, C. A., & Elliot, A. J. (2021). The effect of red on attractiveness for highly attractive women. Current Psychology, 42(10), 8066–8073. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12144-021-02045-3

papers.title: Cues of being watched enhance co-operation in a real-world setting

papers.title: Carbon and Hesslinger 2006

papers.title: Northover et al. (2017
papers.apa: Northover, S. B., Pedersen, W. C., Cohen, A. B., & Andrews, P. W. (2017). Artificial surveillance cues do not increase generosity: two meta-analyses. Evolution and Human Behavior, 38(1), 144–153. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.evolhumbehav.2016.07.001
papers.summary: This paper conducted two meta-analyses to investigate the effect of artificial surveillance cues on generosity, given mixed results in the literature. The meta-analyses found no significant evidence that artificial surveillance cues increase generosity, either by increasing the amount given (meta-analytic g=0.03) or the probability of giving anything at all (meta-analytic g=0.13). The authors concluded that skepticism regarding surveillance cue effects is warranted.

papers.title: Imagining intergroup contact can improve intergroup attitudes
papers.apa: Turner, R. N., Crisp, R. J., & Lambert, E. (2007). Imagining Intergroup Contact Can Improve Intergroup Attitudes. Group Processes & Intergroup Relations, 10(4), 427–441. https://doi.org/10.1177/1368430207081533
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the Imagined Contact hypothesis, demonstrating in three experiments that imagining positive interactions with outgroup members (e.g., elderly, homosexual men) reduced intergroup bias and improved attitudes. The effect was mediated by reduced intergroup anxiety. Original effect sizes ranged from d = 0.42 to 0.86 and ηp² = 0.20.

papers.title: Firat and Ataca 2020
papers.apa: Firat, M., & Ataca, B. (2020). When Does Imagined Contact Reduce Prejudice? Social Psychology, 51(4), 254–266. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000413
papers.summary: This paper attempted to replicate the Imagined Contact effect, reporting a small effect size of ηp² = .01. Without further details, it suggests a much weaker or absent effect compared to the original findings.

papers.title: Hoffarth and Hodson 2016
papers.apa: Choma, B. L., Haji, R., Hodson, G., & Hoffarth, M. (2016). Intergroup Threat Scale--Modified [dataset]. In PsycTESTS Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/t50245-000
papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the Imagined Contact effect across two studies, focusing on attitudes towards gay people and Muslims. The authors reported small effect sizes, with the largest β values being .10 and .095, suggesting limited evidence for the effect in their samples.

papers.title: Miles and Turner 2014
papers.apa: Miles, E., & Crisp, R. J. (2013). A meta-analytic test of the imagined contact hypothesis. Group Processes & Intergroup Relations, 17(1), 3–26. https://doi.org/10.1177/1368430213510573
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of over 70 studies provided a quantitative review of the imagined contact effect, finding an overall significant reduction in intergroup bias (d+ = 0.35). The effect was robust across various measures, target outgroups, and contexts, and was stronger for behavioral intentions and for children compared to adults. The authors concluded that imagined contact is a viable strategy for promoting positive intergroup relations.

papers.title: Elaboration enhances the imagined contact effect
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the Imagined Contact effect, demonstrating that mentally simulating positive interactions with outgroup members can increase intentions for future contact. The studies reported large effect sizes (d=0.86 and d=1.13), suggesting a robust impact, and potentially highlighted that elaboration enhances this effect.

papers.title: Klein et al. 2014
papers.apa: Klein, R. A., Ratliff, K. A., Vianello, M., Adams, R. B., Bahník, Š., Bernstein, M. J., Bocian, K., Brandt, M. J., Brooks, B., Brumbaugh, C. C., Cemalcilar, Z., Chandler, J., Cheong, W., Davis, W. E., Devos, T., Eisner, M., Frankowska, N., Furrow, D., Galliani, E. M., … Nosek, B. A. (2014). Investigating Variation in Replicability. Social Psychology, 45(3), 142–152. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000178
papers.summary: This large-scale replication effort investigated the replicability of 13 effects across 36 independent samples (N=6,344). For the imagined contact effect, the study found weak support for replicability, reporting a meta-analytic effect size of d=0.13. The authors concluded that replicability is more dependent on the effect itself than on the sample and setting.

papers.title: Crisp et al. 2014
papers.apa: Crisp, J., & et al. (1985). A Programmable High Power Beam Damper for the Tevatron. Office of Scientific and Technical Information (OSTI). https://doi.org/10.2172/1156259
papers.summary: This paper appears to be a commentary or re-evaluation of the Imagined Contact effect, particularly in light of replication attempts. It discusses the discrepancy between the effect size observed in the Many Labs study (d=0.13) and previous estimates (d=0.35 overall, d=0.22 for religious outgroups), suggesting that the Many Labs result might not be the most appropriate comparison for all contexts.

papers.title: Miles and Crisp 2014
papers.apa: Miles, E., & Crisp, R. J. (2013). A meta-analytic test of the imagined contact hypothesis. Group Processes & Intergroup Relations, 17(1), 3–26. https://doi.org/10.1177/1368430213510573
papers.summary: This paper presents the first quantitative meta-analysis of the imagined contact hypothesis, synthesizing over 70 studies. It found that imagined contact significantly reduced intergroup bias across four dependent variables (attitudes, emotions, intentions, behavior), with an overall effect size of d+=0.35. The effect was stronger for behavioral intentions and for children, and was enhanced by elaboration instructions.

papers.title: Stereotype Susceptibility: Identity Salience and Shifts in Quantitative Performance
papers.apa: Shih, M., Pittinsky, T. L., & Ambady, N. (1999). Stereotype Susceptibility: Identity Salience and Shifts in Quantitative Performance. Psychological Science, 10(1), 80–83. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9280.00111
papers.summary: This foundational study introduced the stereotype susceptibility effect, demonstrating that implicit activation of a social identity can influence performance on quantitative tasks. Asian-American women performed better on a math test when their ethnic identity was activated, but worse when their gender identity was activated, compared to a control group. The authors proposed that performance shifts according to the stereotype associated with the activated identity (original effect size η2 = .07).

papers.title: Gibson et al. 2014
papers.apa: Gibson, C. E., Losee, J., & Vitiello, C. (2014). A Replication Attempt of Stereotype Susceptibility (). Social Psychology, 45(3), 194–198. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000184
papers.summary: This replication attempt of Shih et al. (1999) used a large sample to investigate stereotype susceptibility. The authors found a significant effect with the same pattern of means (replication effect size η2 = .01 to η2 = .04) only after removing participants who were unaware of the relevant race or gender stereotypes. No significant effect was observed when these participants were retained, suggesting the effect's dependency on stereotype awareness.

papers.title: Isen and Levin 1972
papers.apa: Isen, A. M., & Levin, P. F. (1972). Effect of feeling good on helping: Cookies and kindness. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 21(3), 384–388. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0032317
papers.summary: This foundational study is widely credited with demonstrating that inducing a positive mood, such as by finding a small amount of money, significantly increases the likelihood of helping behavior. It established the 'feel good, do good' phenomenon, showing that positive affect can lead to increased prosocial actions.

papers.title: Batson et al. 1979
papers.apa: Batson, C. D., Batson, J. G., Griffitt, C. A., Barrientos, S., Brandt, J. R., Sprengelmeyer, P., & Bayly, M. J. (1989). Negative-state relief and the empathy—altruism hypothesis. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 56(6), 922–933. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.56.6.922
papers.summary: This study likely investigated the conditions under which positive mood influences helping behavior. With a reported odds ratio of 4.3, the findings suggest a strong positive relationship between positive mood and increased helping, providing support for the effect.

papers.title: Blevins and Murphy 1974
papers.apa: Blevins, G. A., & Murphy, T. (1974). Feeling Good and Helping: Further Phonebooth Findings. Psychological Reports, 34(1), 326–326. https://doi.org/10.2466/pr0.1974.34.1.326
papers.summary: This replication attempt, possibly using a similar methodology to Isen and Levin (1972), explored the positive mood-helping effect. The calculated odds ratio of 0.9 suggests that this study found no evidence for the effect, indicating a failure to replicate the original finding.

papers.title: Carlson et al. 1988
papers.apa: Carlson, M., Charlin, V., & Miller, N. (1988). Positive mood and helping behavior: A test of six hypotheses. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 55(2), 211–229. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.55.2.211
papers.summary: As a meta-analysis, this paper systematically reviewed multiple studies on the relationship between positive mood and helping behavior. The reported overall effect size of d = 0.54 indicates a moderate positive effect, suggesting that across studies, positive mood generally increases helping.

papers.title: Weyant and Clark 1977
papers.apa: Weyant, J., & Clark, R. D. (1976). Dimes and Helping: The Other Side of the Coin. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 3(1), 107–110. https://doi.org/10.1177/014616727600300119
papers.summary: This study attempted to replicate and extend Levin and Isen's (1975) 'Feeling good and helping' experiment across three different settings. Contrary to the original findings, subjects who found a dime did not mail an apparently lost letter more often than those who did not find a dime. The authors concluded that their results failed to support the original effect.

papers.title: Job 1987
papers.apa: Job, R. F. S. (1987). The Effect of Mood on Helping Behavior. The Journal of Social Psychology, 127(4), 323–328. https://doi.org/10.1080/00224545.1987.9713711
papers.summary: This field experiment (N=100) used a lost-letter technique to investigate mood's effect on helping, finding that supporters of a losing team (negative mood) returned significantly more letters (58%) than supporters of a winning team (positive mood) (38%, χ²(1)=4.00, p<.05). The results suggest that negative mood, rather than positive mood, increased helping behavior in this context, challenging existing theories.

papers.title: On Making the Right Choice: The Deliberation-Without-Attention Effect
papers.apa: Dijksterhuis, A., Bos, M. W., Nordgren, L. F., & van Baaren, R. B. (2006). On Making the Right Choice: The Deliberation-Without-Attention Effect. Science, 311(5763), 1005–1007. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1121629
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the 'deliberation-without-attention' hypothesis, proposing that complex decisions are better made after a period of unconscious thought. Across four studies on consumer choice, both in laboratory settings and with actual shoppers, the authors found that complex product purchases were viewed more favorably when decisions were made in the absence of attentive deliberation.

papers.title: Acker 2008
papers.apa: van Acker, E. (2008). Governments and Marriage Education Policy. Palgrave Macmillan UK. https://doi.org/10.1057/9780230227576
papers.summary: This paper reported various effect sizes related to the deliberation-without-attention effect, including a re-analysis or summary of original findings and potentially its own replication or synthesis, with one reported effect size of g = 0.471. The specific methodology (e.g., whether it was a quantitative meta-analysis or a review) is not detailed in the provided text.

papers.title: Nieuwenstein et al. 2015
papers.apa: Dong, C., Liu, X., Zhang, D., & Jiang, D. (2025). Effect of cirrhosis on prognosis in patients with acute-on-chronic liver failure: a systematic review and meta-analysis. PeerJ, 13, e20049. Portico. https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.20049
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings from numerous studies on the deliberation-without-attention effect, reporting a pooled effect size of g = 0.15 [0.03, 0.26]. This indicates a small but statistically significant overall effect, though it highlights considerable variability across individual study outcomes.

papers.title: Consequences of Automatic Evaluation: Immediate Behavioral Predispositions to Approach or Avoid the Stimulus
papers.apa: Chen, M., & Bargh, J. A. (1999). Consequences of Automatic Evaluation: Immediate Behavioral Predispositions to Approach or Avoid the Stimulus. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 25(2), 215–224. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167299025002007
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that automatic evaluation of stimuli as good or bad directly leads to behavioral predispositions. Across two experiments, participants were faster to approach positive stimuli and avoid negative stimuli using a lever, even when evaluation was not their conscious task. Study 1 (conscious evaluation) showed a congruence effect of ηp2=0.168 (d=0.44), and Study 2 (automatic evaluation) showed ηp2=0.078 (d=0.29).

papers.title: Rotteveel et al. 2015
papers.summary: This study attempted a direct replication of Chen and Bargh (1999)'s two experiments with N=100. Using pre-registered Bayesian analyses, the authors failed to find strong evidence for the original effects, providing only anecdotal support for the null hypotheses. Exploratory frequentist analyses showed weak, marginally significant interaction effects (Study 1: ηp2=0.030, d=0.17; Study 2: ηp2=0.057, d=0.24), but these were not corroborated by Bayesian ANOVA.

papers.title: Phaf et al. 2014
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 29 studies (N=1538 combined) investigated the automaticity of the link between affective information processing and approach-avoidance. It found a significant small-to-medium overall effect after correcting for publication bias. Crucially, the analysis revealed no significant effect when instructions did not require conscious appraisals of affective valence (implicit evaluation), suggesting the link is influenced by conscious or non-conscious intentions rather than being purely automatic.

papers.title: ‘Self-control relies on glucose as a limited energy source: Willpower is more than a metaphor
papers.apa: Gailliot, M. T., Baumeister, R. F., DeWall, C. N., Maner, J. K., Plant, E. A., Tice, D. M., Brewer, L. E., & Schmeichel, B. J. (2007). Self-control relies on glucose as a limited energy source: Willpower is more than a metaphor. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 92(2), 325–336. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.92.2.325
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that self-control is a limited resource fueled by glucose. Across multiple studies, the authors demonstrated that engaging in self-control tasks decreased blood glucose levels, that lower glucose levels predicted poorer self-control performance, and that consuming glucose could restore self-control capacity. Original effect sizes ranged from ηp2 = 0.057 to d = 1.518, supporting the glucose model of self-control.

papers.title: Hagger et al. 2010
papers.apa: Sun, Z., Zeng, H., Liu, H., Hu, M., Tian, X., Mao, D., & Zhang, R. (2025). Effectiveness of multi-component exercise in individuals with type 2 diabetes: a systematic review and meta-analysis. PeerJ, 13, e20146. Portico. https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.20146
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated the ego depletion effect, including studies on glucose consumption and blood glucose level changes. The authors reported a meta-analytic effect size of d = 0.75 for glucose consumption effects (including the original study) and d = -0.87 for the decrease of blood glucose levels after self-control tasks (also including the original study). These findings suggested overall support for the glucose model of self-control when aggregating existing literature.

papers.title: Lange and Egger 2014
papers.apa: Lange, F., & Eggert, F. (2014). Sweet delusion. Glucose drinks fail to counteract ego depletion. Appetite, 75, 54–63. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.appet.2013.12.020
papers.summary: This replication attempt specifically examined the effect of glucose consumption on self-control performance. Contrary to the original findings, the study reported a very small effect size for glucose consumption (ηp2 = 0.02). This finding suggests that the glucose-restoration effect on self-control may be much smaller or non-existent than initially claimed, challenging the robustness of the original effect.

papers.title: Experiencing physical warmth promotes interpersonal warmth
papers.apa: Williams, L. E., & Bargh, J. A. (2008). Experiencing Physical Warmth Promotes Interpersonal Warmth. Science, 322(5901), 606–607. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1162548
papers.summary: Williams and Bargh (2008) proposed that physical warmth promotes interpersonal warmth, suggesting a link between physical and social warmth processing. In two experiments, they found that participants holding a warm object (hot coffee or therapeutic pad) subsequently judged a target person as having a "warmer" personality (N=41, d=0.65) or were more likely to choose a gift for a friend over themselves (N=53, d=0.65).

papers.title: Chabris et al. 2018
papers.apa: Chabris, C. F., Heck, P. R., Mandart, J., Benjamin, D. J., & Simons, D. J. (2019). No Evidence That Experiencing Physical Warmth Promotes Interpersonal 					Warmth. Social Psychology, 50(2), 127–132. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000361
papers.summary: Chabris et al. (2018) conducted high-powered, double-blind replications of both original experiments by Williams and Bargh (2008). With significantly larger sample sizes (N=128 and N=177), they found near-zero effects for both the personality judgment (d=-0.06) and prosocial gift-giving (d=0.04) tasks. Bayesian analyses provided strong evidence for the null hypothesis, suggesting no support for the physical warmth priming effect.

papers.title: Lynott et al. 2014
papers.apa: Lynott, D., Corker, K. S., Wortman, J., Connell, L., Donnellan, M. B., Lucas, R. E., & O’Brien, K. (2014). Replication of “Experiencing Physical Warmth Promotes Interpersonal Warmth” by. Social Psychology, 45(3), 216–222. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000187
papers.summary: Lynott et al. (2014) conducted three independent, high-powered replications of Williams and Bargh's (2008) Study 2, which examined prosocial gift-giving after holding a warm or cold pack. Across all three samples and in a combined analysis (total N=861), they found no evidence that exposure to warm therapeutic packs increased prosocial choices. The effect sizes were small and often in the opposite direction (e.g., d=-0.27, -0.05, -0.14).

papers.title: Power and Perspectives Not Taken
papers.apa: Galinsky, A. D., Magee, J. C., Inesi, M. E., & Gruenfeld, D. H. (2006). Power and Perspectives Not Taken. Psychological Science, 17(12), 1068–1074. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01824.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper presents four experiments and a correlational study exploring the relationship between power and perspective-taking. Findings indicated that high-power individuals were more likely to draw an 'E' self-oriented, less likely to consider others' privileged knowledge, and less accurate in determining others' emotions. The authors concluded that power reduces the tendency to comprehend how others see, think, and feel.

papers.title: Ebersole et al. 2016
papers.apa: Kahneman, D. (2016). Commentary on Ebersole et al. (2016). Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 67, 97. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2016.04.002
papers.summary: This paper conducted a large-scale replication attempt of the power impairs perspective-taking effect. Across multiple labs, the study found a meta-analytic effect size of d = 0.03, which was not statistically significant and substantially smaller than the original reported effect. The authors concluded that the original finding was not replicated.

papers.title: Social inequality and the reduction of ideological dissonance on behalf of the system: evidence of enhanced system justification among the disadvantaged
papers.apa: Jost, J. T., Pelham, B. W., Sheldon, O., & Ni Sullivan, B. (2002). Social inequality and the reduction of ideological dissonance on behalf of the system: evidence of enhanced system justification among the disadvantaged. European Journal of Social Psychology, 33(1), 13–36. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ejsp.127
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduces the strongest form of system justification theory, proposing that disadvantaged individuals are more likely to justify the existing social system due to a need to reduce ideological dissonance. Across five US national survey studies, the authors found that low-income respondents and African Americans were more likely to support limitations on criticism of the government, trust officials, and legitimize economic inequality. These findings, with various effect sizes (e.g., income, b = -0.22 on willingness to limit the press; race, b = -0.73), supported the idea that those who suffer most paradoxically question the system least.

papers.title: The Development of System Justification in the Developing World
papers.apa: Henry, P. J., & Saul, A. (2006). The Development of System Justification in the Developing World. Social Justice Research, 19(3), 365–378. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11211-006-0012-x
papers.summary: This study investigated system justification in a developing country, Bolivia, surveying 356 (out of 498 initially) schoolchildren aged 10-15. It found that low-status Indigenous children were more likely than high-status Hispanic children to endorse beliefs in government effectiveness and suppress political dissent, and felt less alienated from the government. These results (e.g., group status effects on support for dissent, ηp2 = 0.019 / d = 0.14) suggest system-justifying beliefs are present even among the most impoverished and youngest populations, supporting the original effect.

papers.title: Brandt 2013
papers.apa: Faulenbach, B. (2013). 7. Die Frage nach dem Menschen Willy Brandt. Willy Brandt, 86–90. https://doi.org/10.17104/9783406654671-86
papers.summary: The provided text does not include an abstract or extracted content for Brandt (2013). Based on the replication effect size data provided in the prompt, this study found mixed results for the status-legitimacy effect. Out of 14 tested effects (e.g., income, education, social class, race on trust in government/institutions), only one was supportive, six were significant and positive (reversed), and seven were non-significant.

papers.title: Caricati 2017
papers.apa: Caricati, L. (2016). Testing the status-legitimacy hypothesis: A multilevel modeling approach to the perception of legitimacy in income distribution in 36 nations. The Journal of Social Psychology, 157(5), 532–540. https://doi.org/10.1080/00224545.2016.1242472
papers.summary: This study tested the status-legitimacy hypothesis using cross-national data from 36 nations (N=~54,000 after exclusions) on perceived fairness of income distribution. Contrary to the hypothesis, results showed no support for the status-legitimacy effect; instead, higher social advantage (e.g., higher income, b = 0.022) was associated with a greater perception that income differences were fair. The study concluded that advantaged people were more likely to perceive social inequality as fair than disadvantaged people, especially in nations with greater freedom and equality.

papers.title: Henry and Saul 2006
papers.apa: Henry, P. J., & Saul, A. (2006). The Development of System Justification in the Developing World. Social Justice Research, 19(3), 365–378. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11211-006-0012-x
papers.summary: This study investigated system justification in a developing country, Bolivia, surveying 356 (out of 498 initially) schoolchildren aged 10-15. It found that low-status Indigenous children were more likely than high-status Hispanic children to endorse beliefs in government effectiveness and suppress political dissent, and felt less alienated from the government. These results (e.g., group status effects on support for dissent, ηp2 = 0.019 / d = 0.14) suggest system-justifying beliefs are present even among the most impoverished and youngest populations, supporting the original effect.

papers.title: Color and psychological functioning: The effect of red on performance attainment
papers.apa: Elliot, A. J., Maier, M. A., Moller, A. C., Friedman, R., & Meinhardt, J. (2007). Color and psychological functioning: The effect of red on performance attainment. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 136(1), 154–168. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-3445.136.1.154
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that the color red impairs performance on achievement tasks by evoking avoidance motivation due to its association with danger and failure. Across six experiments, including one with 71 US undergraduates, the authors demonstrated that brief exposure to red prior to tasks (e.g., IQ tests, anagrams) impaired performance and increased avoidance motivation, suggesting the effect occurs outside conscious awareness.

papers.title: Gnambs 2020
papers.apa: Gnambs, T. (2020). Limited evidence for the effect of red color on cognitive performance: A meta-analysis. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 27(6), 1374–1382. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-020-01772-1
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized 67 effect sizes from 38 samples to evaluate the effect of red color on cognitive performance. It found no significant effect for anagram (d = -0.06) or knowledge tests (d = -0.04), but a significant impairment for reasoning tests (d = -0.34). However, the analysis revealed substantially larger effects in initial studies and concluded that after correcting for publication bias, there was no evidential value for a robust effect of red color on intellectual performance.

papers.effect_name: Reduced prosociality of high SES effect
papers.title: Having less, giving more: the influence of social class on prosocial behavior
papers.apa: Piff, P. K., Kraus, M. W., Côté, S., Cheng, B. H., & Keltner, D. (2010). Having less, giving more: The influence of social class on prosocial behavior. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 99(5), 771–784. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0020092
papers.summary: This foundational paper presents four studies demonstrating that lower social class individuals exhibit greater prosocial behavior (e.g., generosity, charity, trust, helpfulness) compared to upper class individuals. Across studies, the authors found that lower class participants allocated more resources to others (e.g., β = -.23 in Study 1 with N=115) and were more supportive of charity, attributing this to a stronger commitment to egalitarian values and compassion. The findings suggest a causal link between lower social class and increased prosociality.

papers.effect_name: Reduced prosociality of high SES effect
papers.title: Andreoni et al. 2021
papers.apa: Andreoni, J., Nikiforakis, N., & Stoop, J. (2021). Higher socioeconomic status does not predict decreased prosocial behavior in a field experiment. Nature Communications, 12(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-021-24519-5
papers.summary: This paper reportedly found a reversed effect, suggesting that higher socioeconomic status individuals exhibited *more* prosocial behavior, contrary to the original findings. The reported mean correlation was r = 0.37, indicating a positive relationship between SES and prosociality. This challenges the original premise of the effect.

papers.effect_name: Reduced prosociality of high SES effect
papers.title: Stamos et al. 2020
papers.apa: Stamos, A., Lange, F., Huang, S., & Dewitte, S. (2020). Having less, giving more? Two preregistered replications of the relationship between social class and prosocial behavior. Journal of Research in Personality, 84, 103902. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jrp.2019.103902
papers.summary: This replication attempt found no significant relationship between socioeconomic status and prosocial behavior, with a reported correlation of r = 0.01. This non-significant finding suggests a failure to replicate the original effect, indicating that the link between SES and prosociality may not be robust.

papers.title: ‘Sinning Saints and Saintly Sinners’
papers.apa: Sachdeva, S., Iliev, R., & Medin, D. L. (2009). Sinning Saints and Saintly Sinners. Psychological Science, 20(4), 523–528. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2009.02326.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that moral behavior results from an internal balancing of moral self-worth. Across three experiments, participants who affirmed a moral identity (e.g., by writing about positive traits) subsequently engaged in less altruistic behavior (e.g., donated less money) compared to those whose moral identity was threatened. Original studies reported effect sizes of d = 0.62 and d = 0.59, suggesting that affirming a moral identity licenses immoral actions.

papers.title: Blanken et al. 2014
papers.apa: Gao, J., Dong, Q., Shen, L., & Zhu, X. (2025). Impact of paternal hepatitis B on pregnancy outcomes in couples undergoing assisted reproductive technology treatment: a systematic review and meta-analysis. PeerJ, 13, e19824. Portico. https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.19824
papers.summary: This paper conducted direct replication attempts of the moral licensing effect. Across multiple studies with Dutch student and US MTurk samples, the authors found no evidence for the effect, reporting effect sizes close to zero (d = -0.03, d = -0.31, d = 0.05). These findings suggest that the original effect may not be robust under direct replication.

papers.title: Blanken et al. 2015
papers.apa: Blanken, I., van de Ven, N., & Zeelenberg, M. (2015). A Meta-Analytic Review of Moral Licensing. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 41(4), 540–558. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167215572134
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized 91 studies (7,397 participants) comparing moral licensing conditions with control conditions. It estimated the magnitude of the moral licensing effect to be a Cohen's d of 0.31. The authors found that published studies tended to report larger effects than unpublished ones, but no other theorized moderators were empirically supported. They concluded that larger sample sizes are needed for robust conclusions.

papers.title: Simbrunner and Schlegelmilch 2015
papers.apa: Simbrunner, P., & Schlegelmilch, B. B. (2017). Moral licensing: a culture-moderated meta-analysis. Management Review Quarterly, 67(4), 201–225. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11301-017-0128-0
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 106 studies investigated the overall effect size of moral licensing and potential moderators, particularly in a cross-cultural marketing context. Using a random effects model, it found a generalized effect size of Cohen's d = 0.319. The study uniquely identified cultural background and type of comparison as significant moderators, explaining 23% of the total variation in effect size, suggesting the effect is robust but culturally influenced.

papers.title: Kuper and Bott 2019
papers.apa: Kuper, N., & Bott, A. (2019). Has the evidence for moral licensing been inflated by publication bias? Meta-Psychology, 3. https://doi.org/10.15626/mp.2018.878
papers.summary: This meta-analysis provided adjusted effect sizes for moral licensing, reporting d = -0.05 (using PET-PEESE) and d = 0.18 (using 3-PSM). These smaller and mixed effect sizes, with one being negative, suggest a more nuanced or weaker overall effect than some previous meta-analyses.

papers.title: Urban et al. 2019
papers.apa: Urban, J., Bahník, Š., & Kohlová, M. B. (2019). Green consumption does not make people cheat: Three attempts to replicate moral licensing effect due to pro-environmental behavior. Journal of Environmental Psychology, 63, 139–147. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvp.2019.01.011
papers.summary: This paper likely presents a replication attempt or critical commentary on the moral licensing effect. Without further details, its specific contribution to the literature remains to be fully characterized.

papers.title: failed conceptual replication of [Mazar and Zhong 2010
papers.apa: Mazar, N., & Zhong, C.-B. (2010). Do Green Products Make Us Better People? Psychological Science, 21(4), 494–498. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797610363538
papers.summary: This paper investigated how consumer choices, particularly regarding green products, influence subsequent moral behavior. It found that mere exposure to green products increased altruism, but *purchasing* green products led to less altruistic behavior and increased cheating and stealing. This demonstrates a specific instance of moral licensing where initial 'green' consumption licenses subsequent immoral acts.

papers.title: Rotella and Barclay 2020
papers.apa: Rotella, A., & Barclay, P. (2020). Failure to replicate moral licensing and moral cleansing in an online experiment. Personality and Individual Differences, 161, 109967. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2020.109967
papers.summary: This paper likely contributes to the moral licensing literature through a replication attempt or a critical analysis. Further details are needed to specify its findings and impact on the understanding of the effect.

papers.title: Blue or Red? Exploring the Effect of Color on Cognitive Task Performances
papers.apa: Mehta, R., & Zhu, R. (Juliet). (2009). Blue or Red? Exploring the Effect of Color on Cognitive Task Performances. Science, 323(5918), 1226–1229. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1169144
papers.summary: This foundational paper investigated how red and blue colors influence motivation and cognitive performance. Across six studies (N=69 to N=208), the authors found that red induced avoidance motivation and enhanced detail-oriented task performance (e.g., d=0.64 to 1.05), while blue induced approach motivation and enhanced creative task performance (e.g., d=0.56 to 0.69). They also provided process evidence suggesting motivational activation mediates these effects.

papers.title: Steele et al. 2010
papers.apa: Steele, M. (2017). reviewer comment on Zweng et al. https://doi.org/10.5194/essd-2017-63-rc1
papers.summary: This paper, part of a series of replication attempts, investigated the color-priming effect on anagram solution times. The provided replication effect size (ηp2 = 0.038 / d = 0.20) suggests a small effect, which the original description states was "not replicated." Without the abstract, specific findings and sample size cannot be detailed.

papers.title: Steele et al. 2013
papers.apa: Steele, M. (2017). reviewer comment on Zweng et al. https://doi.org/10.5194/essd-2017-63-rc1
papers.summary: This paper, another replication attempt, examined the color-priming effect. The provided replication effect size (ηp2 = 0.014 / d = 0.12) indicates a very small effect, leading to the conclusion that the effect was "not replicated." Without the abstract, specific findings and sample size cannot be detailed.

papers.title: Steele 2014
papers.apa: Steele, K. M. (2013). Failure to replicate the Mehta and Zhu (2009) color-priming effect on anagram solution times. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 21(3), 771–776. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-013-0548-3
papers.summary: This direct replication of Mehta and Zhu (2009) Study 1, using 263 participants (more than triple the original N), investigated the color-priming effect on anagram solution times. The study found no significant Background Color × Word Type interaction (F(4, 496) = 1.84, p = .12, η2 = .007), failing to support the original prediction. The authors concluded that the color-priming effect on anagram solution times was not replicated, with the interaction explaining less than 1% of the variance.

papers.title: ‘Influence of Popular Erotica on Judgments of Strangers and Mates’
papers.summary: This foundational study proposed the "Playboy Effect," finding that men exposed to erotic images subsequently rated their romantic partners as less sexually attractive (d=1.05) and reported less love for them (d=0.77) compared to a control group. The authors attributed this to a contrast effect, where exposure to highly attractive individuals in media shifts comparison standards. No such effect was observed in women.

papers.title: Balzarini et al. 2017
papers.apa: Balzarini, R. N., Dobson, K., Chin, K., & Campbell, L. (2017). Does exposure to erotica reduce attraction and love for romantic partners in men? Independent replications of Kenrick, Gutierres, and Goldberg (1989) study 2. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 70, 191–197. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2016.11.003
papers.summary: This paper presents three preregistered, high-powered independent replications of Kenrick et al.'s (1989) Study 2, conducted with input from the original authors. Across all three experiments, the authors found no significant effect of exposure to erotica on men's ratings of their partners' sexual attractiveness or love for their partners. A meta-analysis of their three studies yielded negligible effect sizes for both sexual attraction (d=0.02) and love (d=0.02), leading them to conclude the original finding was not replicated.

papers.title: It feels like yesterday: Self-esteem, valence of personal past experiences, and judgments of subjective distance
papers.apa: Ross, M., & Wilson, A. E. (2002). It feels like yesterday: Self-esteem, valence of personal past experiences, and judgments of subjective distance. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 82(5), 792–803. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.82.5.792
papers.summary: This foundational paper, across three studies (e.g., Study 1 N=540, Study 2 N=357), introduced the self-protective subjective temporal distance effect. The authors found that participants reported feeling farther from past experiences with unfavorable implications than from those with flattering implications. This distancing bias was stronger for high self-esteem participants, supporting a motivational interpretation. The original effect size was reported as ηp2 = .0185.

papers.title: Ebersole et al. 2016
papers.apa: Kahneman, D. (2016). Commentary on Ebersole et al. (2016). Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 67, 97. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2016.04.002
papers.summary: This paper attempted to replicate the self-protective subjective temporal distance effect. The replication effort found no evidence for the effect, reporting a substantially smaller effect size (ηp2 = .0001) compared to the original finding. This suggests that the original effect may not be robust.

papers.title: The substitutability of physical and social warmth in daily life’
papers.apa: Bargh, J. A., & Shalev, I. (2012). The substitutability of physical and social warmth in daily life. Emotion, 12(1), 154–162. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0023527
papers.summary: This foundational paper (Bargh & Shalev, 2012) introduced the concept that physical warmth can substitute for social warmth. Across two studies, it reported a significant positive association between trait loneliness and the use of warm showers/baths, with effect sizes of r = .57 (N=51) and r = .37 (N=41).

papers.title: Donnellan et al. 2014
papers.apa: Donnellan, M. B., Lucas, R. E., & Cesario, J. (2015). On the association between loneliness and bathing habits: Nine replications of Bargh and Shalev (2012) Study 1. Emotion, 15(1), 109–119. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0036079
papers.summary: This paper conducted nine replication studies (N=1,153 and N=1,920 across different sets of studies) of Bargh and Shalev (2012) Study 1. They found no statistically significant association between trait loneliness and bathing habits, with aggregated effect sizes ranging from r = -.01 to .10, which were statistically indistinguishable from zero. The authors concluded that their results cast doubt on a strong connection between trait loneliness and personal bathing habits related to warmth.

papers.title: Shalev and Bargh 2015
papers.apa: “On the association between loneliness and physical warmth-seeking through bathing: Reply to Donnellan et al. (2014) and three further replications of Bargh and Shalev (2012) Study 1”: Correction to Shalev and Bargh (2014). (2015). Emotion, 15(1), 123–123. https://doi.org/10.1037/emo0000046
papers.summary: This paper (Shalev and Bargh, 2015) re-examined the association between loneliness and physical warmth, reporting a replication of their original findings. They found a positive correlation between a loneliness-warmth index and showering (r = .143) and baths (r = .093), suggesting the effect was present.

papers.title: Wortman et al. 2014
papers.summary: This replication attempt (Wortman et al., 2014), across three samples with a total N=555, investigated the trait loneliness hot shower effect. Their findings indicated a very small and non-significant effect (d = 0.02) when comparing warm versus cold conditions, failing to support the original hypothesis.

papers.effect_name: American flag priming boosts Republican support
papers.title: A Single Exposure to the American Flag Shifts Support Toward Republicanism up to 8 Months Later
papers.apa: Carter, T. J., Ferguson, M. J., & Hassin, R. R. (2011). A Single Exposure to the American Flag Shifts Support Toward Republicanism up to 8 Months Later. Psychological Science, 22(8), 1011–1018. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611414726
papers.summary: This foundational paper reported that a brief exposure to the American flag significantly increased Republican voting intentions, political beliefs, and attitudes among both Republican and Democratic participants. Experiment 1, conducted during the 2008 election, showed effects lasting up to 8 months. The authors reported an original effect size of d = 0.50, suggesting a durable nonconscious priming effect.

papers.effect_name: American flag priming boosts Republican support
papers.title: Klein et al. 2014
papers.apa: Klein, H. J., Cooper, J. T., Molloy, J. C., & Swanson, J. A. (2014). Klein et al. Unidimensional, Target-Free Measure [dataset]. In PsycTESTS Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/t30585-000
papers.summary: This paper, part of the Many Labs 3 project, conducted a large-scale, multi-site replication of the American flag priming effect. Across numerous independent samples, the study found no evidence for the effect, with a median effect size of d = 0.02. The authors concluded that the original finding was likely a false positive or highly context-dependent.

papers.title: Keep Your Fingers Crossed!: How Superstition Improves Performance
papers.apa: Damisch, L., Stoberock, B., & Mussweiler, T. (2010). Keep Your Fingers Crossed! Psychological Science, 21(7), 1014–1020. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797610372631
papers.summary: This foundational paper presents four experiments demonstrating that activating good-luck superstitions (e.g., lucky charms, sayings) improves performance in tasks like golfing, motor dexterity, memory, and anagrams. The authors propose that these benefits are mediated by increased perceived self-efficacy, which boosts confidence and task persistence. Original effect sizes ranged from d=0.66 to d=0.98 across experiments.

papers.title: Aruguete et al. 2012
papers.apa: Echigo, T., Aruguete, D. M., Murayama, M., & Hochella, M. F. (2012). Influence of size, morphology, surface structure, and aggregation state on reductive dissolution of hematite nanoparticles with ascorbic acid. Geochimica et Cosmochimica Acta, 90, 149–162. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gca.2012.05.008
papers.summary: This paper conducted two experiments attempting to replicate the superstition effect. Experiment 1 found an estimated d = -0.07 and Experiment 2 found an estimated ηp2 = 0.01, both indicating no evidence for the effect.

papers.title: Calin-Jageman and Caldwell 2014
papers.apa: Calin-Jageman, R. J., & Caldwell, T. L. (2014). Replication of the Superstition and Performance Study by. Social Psychology, 45(3), 239–245. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000190
papers.summary: This paper conducted two high-powered replications of Damisch et al.'s golf task, finding no effect of superstition on performance (Experiment 1: d=0.05; Experiment 2: d=0.05). A meta-analysis across studies, including the original, indicated significant heterogeneity and an overall effect size of d=0.40 [0.14, 0.65], but noted this was heavily biased by the original studies. The authors concluded that the effect is elusive, especially in studies with strong designs.

papers.title: Dickhäuser et al. 2020
papers.apa: Dickhäuser, O., Heinze, A., Hamm, M. L., Bales, A. S., Bellmann, S. A., Böger, D., Gagelmann, L. K., Kuhlenkamp, F., Merger, J. K., Viehweger, H., Ziegler, J., & Neuenstein, A. von. (2020). Effekt Glückssache? Zeitschrift Für Pädagogische Psychologie, 34(1), 51–60. https://doi.org/10.1024/1010-0652/a000263
papers.summary: This paper presents two conceptual replications (N=101, N=175) of Damisch et al.'s anagram task, investigating whether inducing luck or bad luck affects performance and self-efficacy. Contrary to hypotheses, no statistically significant effect of luck or bad luck induction on performance was found. A small effect on self-efficacy was observed only in Study 2, and the authors concluded that the effect likely requires very specific, often non-replicable, boundary conditions.

papers.title: Lee et al. 2011
papers.apa: Lee, C., Linkenauger, S. A., Bakdash, J. Z., Joy-Gaba, J. A., & Profitt, D. R. (2011). Putting Like a Pro: The Role of Positive Contagion in Golf Performance and Perception. PLoS ONE, 6(10), e26016. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0026016
papers.summary: This study investigated the effect of 'positive contagion' by telling participants they were using a professional golfer's putter. They found that participants in the professional group (N=41) perceived the golf hole as larger (d=0.79) and sank more putts (d=0.67) than controls. The authors interpret these findings as support for positive contagion improving performance and perception, with a reported replication effect size for putting performance of d=0.74.

papers.title: Is It Light or Dark? Recalling Moral Behavior Changes Perception of Brightness
papers.apa: Banerjee, P., Chatterjee, P., & Sinha, J. (2012). Is It Light or Dark? Recalling Moral Behavior Changes Perception of Brightness. Psychological Science, 23(4), 407–409. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611432497
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that recalling moral behavior can influence the sensorimotor perception of brightness. Across two studies, participants who recalled unethical behavior perceived their environment as darker and desired more light-emitting products, with reported effect sizes ranging from d=0.64 to d=1.33. The authors suggest this occurs because metaphorical associations between abstract concepts like evil and concrete concepts like darkness can influence actual physical perception.

papers.title: Brandt et al. 2014
papers.apa: Curry, S., Surawicz, C., Binion, D., & Brandt, L. (2014). Response to Gorbach et al. American Journal of Gastroenterology, 109(10), 1684–1685. https://doi.org/10.1038/ajg.2014.229
papers.summary: This paper conducted multiple replication attempts of the 'unethicality darkens perception of light' effect, including online and lab studies. Across various measures (perceived brightness, estimated watts, product preference), individual studies consistently reported small, non-significant effect sizes (e.g., d=0.12, d=-0.11, d=0.05). A meta-analytic estimate across these studies also found small effects (mean d=0.14 for brightness judgments, d=0.13 for product desirability), suggesting a lack of robust evidence for the original effect.

papers.title: Firestone & Scholl 2013
papers.apa: Firestone, C., & Scholl, B. J. (2013). “Top-Down” Effects Where None Should Be Found. Psychological Science, 25(1), 38–46. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797613485092
papers.summary: This critique paper argues that many alleged 'top-down' perceptual effects, including the influence of morality on lightness perception, are likely not true perceptual effects but rather reflect influences on judgment, memory, or response bias. While two studies reported small to moderate effect sizes (d=0.38, d=0.46), the authors used the 'El Greco fallacy' to demonstrate that such effects cannot truly be on perception, challenging the interpretation of the original findings.

papers.title: The fluctuating female vote: politics, religion, and the ovulatory cycle
papers.apa: Durante, K. M., Rae, A., & Griskevicius, V. (2013). The Fluctuating Female Vote. Psychological Science, 24(6), 1007–1016. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797612466416
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed and tested the "fluctuating female vote" effect, suggesting that a woman's ovulatory cycle influences her political and religious views, and thus her voting behavior. Across two studies with large and diverse samples, the authors reported that ovulation made single women more liberal and likely to vote for Obama (d=0.32), while making women in relationships more conservative and likely to vote for Romney (d=0.37). They concluded that these changes in political orientation mediated voting behavior.

papers.title: Harris et al. 2014
papers.apa: Harris, C. R., Chabot, A., & Mickes, L. (2014). Women Can Keep the Vote. Psychological Science, 25(5), 1147–1149. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797613520236
papers.summary: This large-scale replication (N=1,206) aimed to re-examine the "fluctuating female vote" effect using similar methods. The study found no evidence for the predicted interactions between relationship status and fertility phase on religious or social political attitudes. While some voting data showed equivocal results (e.g., d=0.37 for paired women's hypothetical vote, d=0.40 for single women's actual vote), the authors expressed strong skepticism, concluding that they unequivocally failed to confirm the key findings from the original research.

papers.effect_name: Modulation of 1/f noise on the weapon identification task
papers.title: 1/f noise and effort on implicit measures of bias
papers.apa: Correll, J. (2008). 1/f noise and effort on implicit measures of bias. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 94(1), 48–59. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.94.1.48
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept that cognitive effort to modulate racial bias, particularly in tasks like weapon identification, can be measured by changes in 1/f noise in response patterns. It reported an original effect size of d=.59, suggesting a moderate effect where increased effort correlated with decreased 1/f noise.

papers.effect_name: Modulation of 1/f noise on the weapon identification task
papers.title: Madurski and LeBel 2015
papers.apa: Madurski, C., & LeBel, E. P. (2014). Making Sense of the Noise: Two Unsuccessful High-Powered Replication Attempts of Correll’s (2008) 1/f Noise Racial Bias Effect. SSRN Electronic Journal. https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.2435572
papers.summary: This paper attempted to replicate the original finding regarding the modulation of 1/f noise on the weapon identification task. Across two independent samples, the authors found substantially smaller and inconsistent effect sizes (Sample 1: d=.16; Sample 2: d=-0.09), failing to reproduce the original effect. The findings suggest the original effect may not be robust.

papers.title: Time, money, and happiness: How does putting a price on time affect our ability to smell the roses?
papers.apa: DeVoe, S. E., & House, J. (2012). Time, money, and happiness: How does putting a price on time affect our ability to smell the roses? Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 48(2), 466–474. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2011.11.012
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that thinking about time in terms of money impairs enjoyment of leisure activities by increasing impatience. Across three experiments, the authors found that calculating an hourly wage reduced happiness from internet leisure (Study 1, N=53, ηp2=.119) and music listening (Study 2, N=401, ηp2=.019), mediated by impatience. Study 3 (N=205, ηp2=.031) showed that monetary compensation for leisure attenuated this negative effect.

papers.title: Connors et al.
papers.apa: Connors, S., Khamitov, M., Moroz, S., Campbell, L., & Henderson, C. (2016). Time, money, and happiness: Does putting a price on time affect our ability to smell the roses? Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 67, 60–64. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2015.08.005
papers.summary: This paper conducted two preregistered, high-powered close replications of DeVoe and House's (2012) Experiment 3, with sample sizes of N=266 and N=254 respectively. Neither replication attempt, nor the combined sample, replicated the original pattern of findings for happiness or impatience. While one replication showed a significant interaction, its nature was contrary to the original, leading the authors to conclude that the interactive effects are not robust.

papers.title: The Physical Burdens of Secrecy
papers.apa: Slepian, M. L., Masicampo, E. J., Toosi, N. R., & Ambady, N. (2012). The physical burdens of secrecy. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 141(4), 619–624. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0027598
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that secrets are experienced as physical burdens, influencing perception. Across multiple studies, it reported that individuals recalling or suppressing important secrets estimated hills as steeper and distances as farther, and that secret-related thoughts increased perceived physical effort. Original effect sizes ranged from d=0.67 to d=1.03.

papers.title: LeBel and Wilbur 2014
papers.apa: LeBel, E. P., & Wilbur, C. J. (2013). Big secrets do not necessarily cause hills to appear steeper. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 21(3), 696–700. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-013-0549-2
papers.summary: This paper reports two high-powered, preregistered direct replication attempts of Slepian et al.'s finding that big secrets make hills appear steeper. Despite faithfully following the original methodology, both studies (N unspecified) failed to replicate the target finding, reporting small, non-significant effect sizes (d=0.176 and d=-0.319). The authors concluded that the original finding was not corroborated.

papers.title: Pecher et al. 2015
papers.apa: Slepian, M. L., Masicampo, E. J., & Ambady, N. (2013). Relieving the Burdens of Secrecy. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 5(3), 293–300. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550613498516
papers.summary: This paper conducted three studies attempting to replicate the effect of secrets on perceived hill steepness and distance. All three studies (N unspecified) failed to find significant evidence for the effect, reporting small effect sizes (d=0.08, d=0.21, d=0.21) and concluding non-replication. The provided abstract and DOI were for a different paper.

papers.title: Slepian et al. 2014
papers.apa: Slepian, M. L., Masicampo, E. J., & Ambady, N. (2013). Relieving the Burdens of Secrecy. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 5(3), 293–300. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550613498516
papers.summary: This paper investigated whether revealing a secret could alleviate its perceived physical burden. Two studies demonstrated that while thinking about a secret increased judgments of hill slant (Study 1: r=.29/d=0.61) and distance (Study 2: r=.24/d=0.49), revealing the secret eliminated these effects. The findings suggest that sharing secrets may relieve the physical burden associated with them.

papers.title: Slepian et al. 2015
papers.apa: Dong, C., Liu, X., Zhang, D., & Jiang, D. (2025). Effect of cirrhosis on prognosis in patients with acute-on-chronic liver failure: a systematic review and meta-analysis. PeerJ, 13, e20049. Portico. https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.20049
papers.summary: This paper presented four studies on the embodiment of secrets. One study (N unspecified) failed to replicate the effect of secrets on hill steepness (d=0.31, non-significant), while three other studies (N unspecified) reported successful replications of related effects, with effect sizes ranging from d=0.22 to d=0.58. This indicates mixed findings within the paper itself.

papers.title: Warmer hearts, warmer rooms: How positive communal traits increase estimates of ambient temperature
papers.apa: Szymkow, A., Chandler, J., IJzerman, H., Parzuchowski, M., & Wojciszke, B. (2013). Warmer Hearts, Warmer Rooms. Social Psychology, 44(2), 167–176. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000147
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed the warmer-hearts-warmer-room effect, suggesting that priming social warmth (communal traits) can increase the perception of physical room temperature. The original study reported a large effect size of d = 0.86, indicating that participants primed with 'warm' traits estimated the room to be significantly warmer.

papers.title: Ebersole et al. 2016
papers.apa: Kahneman, D. (2016). Commentary on Ebersole et al. (2016). Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 67, 97. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2016.04.002
papers.summary: This paper conducted a replication attempt of the warmer-hearts-warmer-room effect. Contrary to the original finding, the replication reported a very small effect size of d = 0.06, with a confidence interval spanning zero. This finding suggests a failure to replicate the original effect.

papers.title: Treating” Prejudice: An Exposure-Therapy Approach to Reducing Negative Reactions Toward Stigmatized Groups
papers.apa: Birtel, M. D., & Crisp, R. J. (2012). “Treating” Prejudice. Psychological Science, 23(11), 1379–1386. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797612443838
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the "Treating Prejudice with Imagery" effect, proposing that imagining a negative encounter followed by a positive one with a stigmatized group reduces prejudice. Across three experiments targeting adults with schizophrenia, gay men, and British Muslims, the authors found that this mixed-valence imagery significantly reduced prejudice compared to purely positive interventions. Key original effect sizes included d=0.76 for anxiety towards adults with schizophrenia and d=1.08 for anxiety towards homosexual men.

papers.title: McDonald et al. 2014
papers.apa: McDonald, M. M., Donnellan, M. B., Lang, R., & Nikolajuk, K. (2014). Treating Prejudice With Imagery. Psychological Science, 25(3), 837–839. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797613516010
papers.summary: This replication attempt closely followed the original procedures but used significantly larger sample sizes (N=240 for Study 1, N=175 for Study 2a). Across two studies, the authors found no support for the "Treating Prejudice with Imagery" effect, with non-significant effect sizes (e.g., d=0.10 for anxiety towards adults with schizophrenia, d=-0.19 for anxiety towards homosexual men). The authors concluded that they had high power to detect the original effect sizes and that the original findings were not replicated.

papers.title: Learning About What Others Were Doing: Verb Aspect and Attributions of Mundane and Criminal Intent for Past Actions
papers.apa: Hart, W., & Albarracín, D. (2010). Learning About What Others Were Doing. Psychological Science, 22(2), 261–266. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797610395393
papers.summary: This foundational study proposed that describing actions using imperfective aspect (e.g., 'was doing') rather than perfective aspect ('did') increases attributions of intentionality. Across three experiments, participants who read imperfective descriptions showed enhanced accessibility of intention-related concepts and attributed more intentionality to both mundane and criminal behaviors, with reported effect sizes (d) ranging from 0.66 to 1.23. The authors suggested this effect is mediated by more detailed imagined actions.

papers.title: Eerland et al. 2016
papers.apa: Sherrill, A. M., Eerland, A., Zwaan, R. A., & Magliano, J. P. (2015). Understanding How Grammatical Aspect Influences Legal Judgment. PLOS ONE, 10(10), e0141181. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0141181
papers.summary: This study explored whether grammatical aspect influences legal judgments of criminal intentionality across four experiments (e.g., N=123 for Exp 1). While findings suggested indirect influences on legal judgments via temporal dynamics (imperfective aspect led to more murder actions), direct effects of aspect on perceived intentionality were largely absent or weak (e.g., Exp 1 found no significant effect of murder aspect on intentionality, ηp2 = .008). Experiments 2 and 3 did find small effects of murder intentionality judgement (d=0.19, d=0.20 respectively), but Experiment 4 found a non-significant d=0.15. The authors concluded that aspect primarily primes temporal information, which can then indirectly influence more abstract processing like intentionality, and that effects are context-dependent.

papers.title: Registered Replication Report: Grammar influences perceived intentionality
papers.apa: Eerland, A., Sherrill, A. M., Magliano, J. P., Zwaan, R. A., Arnal, J. D., Aucoin, P., Berger, S. A., Birt, A. R., Capezza, N., Carlucci, M., Crocker, C., Ferretti, T. R., Kibbe, M. R., Knepp, M. M., Kurby, C. A., Melcher, J. M., Michael, S. W., Poirier, C., & Prenoveau, J. M. (2016). Registered Replication Report. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 11(1), 158–171. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691615605826
papers.summary: This Registered Replication Report (RRR) involved 12 direct replication studies, including one MTurk study, to re-evaluate the effect of grammatical aspect on intentionality and imagery. A meta-analysis of these studies, designed to provide a precise estimate free from publication bias, found no evidence that grammatical aspect affects intentionality (meta-analytic d between 0 and -0.24, non-significant) or imagery (meta-analytic d = -0.08, non-significant). The authors concluded that the original findings were not replicated.

papers.title: Warm Thoughts: Attachment Anxiety and Sensitivity to Temperature Cues
papers.apa: Vess, M. (2012). Warm Thoughts. Psychological Science, 23(5), 472–474. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611435919
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that attachment anxiety predicts sensitivity to temperature cues. Study 1 (N=56) found that high attachment anxiety predicted a greater desire for warm refreshments after recalling a romantic breakup (β = -0.42, p = .028). Study 2 (N=112) showed that warmth priming increased relationship satisfaction for individuals with high attachment anxiety (β = 0.25, p = .033). The authors concluded that anxiously attached individuals may use physical warmth as a regulatory strategy for intimacy.

papers.title: LeBel and Campbell 2013
papers.apa: LeBel, E. P., & Campbell, L. (2013). Heightened Sensitivity to Temperature Cues in Individuals With High Anxious Attachment. Psychological Science, 24(10), 2128–2130. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797613486983
papers.summary: This paper attempted to replicate the attachment-warmth embodiment effect. Across two samples, the authors reported very small effect sizes (Sample 1: d=0.03; Sample 2: d=0.05), which are substantially lower than the original reported effect. These findings suggest a failure to replicate the original effect.
