papers.title: Differentiating Social and Personal Power Opposite Effects on Stereotyping, but Parallel Effects on Behavioral Approach Tendencies
papers.apa: Lammers, J., Stoker, J. I., & Stapel, D. A. (2009). Differentiating Social and Personal Power: Opposite Effects on Stereotyping, but Parallel Effects on Behavioral Approach Tendencies. Psychological Science, 20(12), 1543–1548. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2009.02479.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the distinction between social power (power over others) and personal power (freedom from others). Across two studies (an experimental study and a large field study), the authors found that these power types had opposite effects on stereotyping (e.g., Study 1: ηp2 = 0.23, d=0.54) but parallel effects on behavioral approach tendencies (e.g., Study 1: ηp2 = 0.13, d=0.38).

papers.title: Mayiwar and Lai 2009
papers.apa: Mayiwar, L., & Lai, L. (2019). Replication of Study 1 in “Differentiating Social and Personal Power” by Lammers, Stoker, and Stapel (2009). Social Psychology, 50(4), 261–269. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000388
papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the effects of social and personal power on stereotyping and behavioral approach. The authors found a smaller, but significant, effect for stereotyping (ηp2 = 0.056, d=0.24), suggesting a replication of that specific finding. However, they did not find a significant effect for behavioral approach tendencies (ηp2 = 0.017, d=0.13), indicating a failure to replicate that aspect of the original effect.

papers.title: Judgment under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases: Biases in judgments reveal some heuristics of thinking under uncertainty
papers.apa: Tversky, A., & Kahneman, D. (1974). Judgment under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases. Science, 185(4157), 1124–1131. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.185.4157.1124
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the anchoring and adjustment heuristic as one of three key heuristics people employ when making judgments under uncertainty. The authors demonstrated that numerical predictions are biased towards an initial, often arbitrary, value (the anchor), due to insufficient adjustment. This heuristic, while economical, leads to systematic and predictable errors in judgment.

papers.title: Furnham and Boo, 2011
papers.apa: Furnham, A., & Boo, H. C. (2011). A literature review of the anchoring effect. The Journal of Socio-Economics, 40(1), 35–42. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.socec.2010.10.008
papers.summary: This paper provides a comprehensive literature review of the anchoring effect, highlighting its robustness across various tasks and domains. It discusses different models and underlying mechanisms, as well as individual differences (e.g., expertise, mood) that may moderate the effect. The authors conclude that the anchoring effect is extremely robust and widely demonstrable, with individual differences and situational factors playing a relatively small role.

papers.title: Li et al. 2021
papers.apa: Li, L., Maniadis, Z., & Sedikides, C. (2021). Anchoring in Economics: A Meta-Analysis of Studies on Willingness-To-Pay and Willingness-To-Accept. Journal of Behavioral and Experimental Economics, 90, 101629. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.socec.2020.101629
papers.summary: This meta-analysis examined the anchoring effect on willingness-to-pay (WTP) and willingness-to-accept (WTA), synthesizing 53 studies from 24 articles. It found a moderate overall effect (correlation coefficient between anchor and target item), which was smaller than effects reported in early studies. The analysis also identified moderators such as anchor relevance, compatibility, buying tasks, and non-lab experiments as being associated with higher anchoring effects.

papers.title: Orr and Guthrie, 2006
papers.apa: Shannon, E. A., Guthrie, W., Brower, S., & Guthrie, N. (2006). Woody Guthrie Artworks. Modern Language Studies, 36(1), 62. https://doi.org/10.2307/27647883
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Röseler et al., 2022
papers.apa: Körner, R., Röseler, L., & Schütz, A. (2021). Commentary on Elkjær et al.’s (2020) Meta-Analysis on Expansive Versus Contractive Nonverbal Displays. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 17(1), 305–307. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691620984474
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Röseler and Schütz, 2022
papers.apa: Röseler, L., & Schütz, A. (2022). Hanging the Anchor Off a New Ship: A Meta-Analysis of Anchoring Effects. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/wf2tn
papers.summary: This meta-analysis, based on a comprehensive collection of 393 anchoring effect studies (N=17,708), found large but heterogeneous anchoring effects (g = 0.689, 95% CI [0.589, 0.789]). It revealed that anchors communicated as random had no effect, and some effects reversed with visual response scales. The study also found null effects for many previously contradictory moderators, such as incentives, and concluded that theories emphasizing rationality and nonlinearity were best supported.

papers.title: Townson, 2019
papers.apa: Townson, Thomas (1715–1792). (2018). Oxford Dictionary of National Biography. https://doi.org/10.1093/odnb/9780192683120.013.27640
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Incidental environmental anchors
papers.apa: Critcher, C. R., & Gilovich, T. (2007). Incidental environmental anchors. Journal of Behavioral Decision Making, 21(3), 241–251. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/bdm.586
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of incidental environmental anchors, demonstrating in three studies that numerical estimates can be influenced by incidentally present numbers (e.g., jersey numbers, product model numbers, restaurant names). The authors found these effects were not dependent on participants' expertise or their ability to recall the anchor value, suggesting a robust, unconscious form of anchoring.

papers.title: Shanks et al., 2020
papers.apa: Shanks, D. R., Barbieri-Hermitte, P., & Vadillo, M. A. (2020). Do Incidental Environmental Anchors Bias Consumers’ Price Estimations? Collabra: Psychology, 6(1). https://doi.org/10.1525/collabra.310
papers.summary: This paper attempted to replicate the incidental environmental anchoring effect across three studies using methods similar to Critcher and Gilovich (2007) for consumer price estimations. The authors found no statistically significant evidence for incidental anchoring (meta-analytic d = -0.02 [-0.08, 0.05]), although they did observe robust standard anchoring effects. They concluded that anchoring might be limited to situations requiring explicit thought about the anchor.

papers.title: Subliminal anchoring: Judgmental consequences and underlying mechanisms’
papers.apa: Mussweiler, T., & Englich, B. (2005). Subliminal anchoring: Judgmental consequences and underlying mechanisms. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 98(2), 133–143. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.obhdp.2004.12.002
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the subliminal anchoring effect, demonstrating that numeric estimates are assimilated towards subliminally presented anchor values. Across three studies (e.g., N=37, N=41), the authors found that unperceived anchors influenced judgments (e.g., r=.35, p<.04) and selectively increased the accessibility of anchor-consistent knowledge. The findings suggested that anchoring effects are more pervasive than previously assumed.

papers.title: Reitsma-van Rooijen and Daamen 2006
papers.apa: Reitsma-van Rooijen, M., & L. Daamen, D. D. (2006). Subliminal anchoring: The effects of subliminally presented numbers on probability estimates. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 42(3), 380–387. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2005.05.001
papers.summary: This study investigated the subliminal anchoring effect, expecting it to be short-lived based on numeric priming. Using subliminal low or high anchors (10 or 90) before probability estimates (N=62), the authors found a significant anchor effect only when participants were under time pressure. They concluded that subliminal anchoring exists but is conditional on factors like time pressure, which affects the accessibility of the anchor value.

papers.title: Röseler et al., 2021
papers.apa: Röseler, L., Schütz, A., Blank, P. A., Dück, M., Fels, S., Kupfer, J., Scheelje, L., & Seida, C. (2021). Evidence against subliminal anchoring: Two close, highly powered, preregistered, and failed replication attempts. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 92, 104066. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2020.104066
papers.summary: This paper presents two highly powered, preregistered replication attempts of the subliminal anchoring effect, targeting both the original Mussweiler & Englich (2005) study and the moderated effect from Reitsma-van Rooijen & Daamen (2006). The authors found no evidence for the effect in either replication, reporting effect sizes ranging from d=-0.148 to d=0.104, and even observed a reversed pattern in some conditions. They concluded that their findings are inconsistent with the existence of subliminal anchoring.

papers.title: Facial redness, expression, and masculinity influence perceptions of anger and health
papers.apa: Young, S. G., Thorstenson, C. A., & Pazda, A. D. (2016). Facial redness, expression, and masculinity influence perceptions of anger and health. Cognition and Emotion, 32(1), 49–60. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699931.2016.1273201
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated how facial redness influences perceptions of anger and health across two experiments. Experiment 1 (N=40) found that perceived anger increased linearly with facial redness, particularly at high levels (ηp2 = .11), while health ratings showed a curvilinear trend. Experiment 2 (N=44) replicated these findings and showed the effect was moderated by masculine face structure. The authors concluded that facial redness serves as a context-dependent cue for perceived anger.

papers.title: Wolf et al., 2021
papers.apa: Wolf, D., Leder, J., Röseler, L., & Schütz, A. (2021). Does facial redness really affect emotion perception? Evidence for limited generalisability of effects of facial redness on emotion perception in a large sample. Cognition and Emotion, 35(8), 1607–1617. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699931.2021.1979473
papers.summary: This preregistered conceptual replication (N=609) aimed to extend prior research on facial redness and emotion perception. Using a within-subjects design where participants rated multiple emotions simultaneously, the study found no significant influence of increased facial redness on the intensity of rated anger (OR = 1.00, 95% CI [0.95, 1.05]). The authors concluded that their results limit the generalizability of previous findings and challenge the assumption that facial redness is a general cue for inferring emotions.

papers.title: Wolf et al., 2022
papers.apa: Wolf, D., Röseler, L., Leder, J., & Schütz, A. (2022). The Red-Anger Effect: Is it Nothing More Than Demand Characteristics? https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/ntukz
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Parental interference and romantic love: The Romeo and Juliet effect
papers.apa: Driscoll, R., Davis, K. E., & Lipetz, M. E. (1972). Parental interference and romantic love: The Romeo and Juliet effect. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 24(1), 1–10. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0033373
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the Romeo and Juliet effect using a longitudinal survey of 140 dating and married couples. It hypothesized that parental interference intensifies romantic love, explaining this through frustration and reactance. The study found significant positive correlations between parental interference and romantic love (r = .34) and commitment (r = .30), supporting the effect.

papers.title: Parks et al. 1983
papers.apa: Parks, M. R., Stan, C. M., & Eggert, L. L. (1983). Romantic Involvement and Social Network Involvement. Social Psychology Quarterly, 46(2), 116. https://doi.org/10.2307/3033848
papers.summary: This study examined the relationship between social network involvement and romantic involvement in 193 premarital relationships. It largely found a positive, linear association between network support and romantic involvement, suggesting that approval strengthens relationships. Evidence for the Romeo and Juliet effect was minimal, occurring only in one network sector and for some variables, with most findings showing the opposite pattern (e.g., own family approval correlated with romantic love at r = .47).

papers.title: Sinclair et al. 2014
papers.apa: Sinclair, H. C., Hood, K. B., & Wright, B. L. (2014). Revisiting the Romeo and Juliet Effect (). Social Psychology, 45(3), 170–178. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000181
papers.summary: This paper conducted a direct replication and extension of the original Driscoll et al. study with 396 participants over 3-4 months, using both original and contemporary measures. It found no evidence for the Romeo and Juliet effect in its experimental replication (e.g., romantic love - parental interference: r = -.05). Instead, consistent with the social network effect, higher interference or lower approval was linked to poorer relationship quality. A meta-analysis further supported this, showing a positive association between network approval and relationship quality (g = 0.49 for love, g = 0.62 for commitment), which is the opposite of the Romeo and Juliet effect.

papers.title: Automatic Stereotyping
papers.apa: Banaji, M. R., & Hardin, C. D. (1996). Automatic Stereotyping. Psychological Science, 7(3), 136–141. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.1996.tb00346.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper investigated automatic stereotyping, finding that subjects made faster gender judgments for pronouns consistent with gender-related primes (e.g., 'nurse' followed by 'she') compared to inconsistent ones. This effect occurred regardless of prime-target awareness or explicit beliefs, suggesting that gender information from words can automatically influence judgment. The original effect size for Prime Gender x Target Gender was F(2, 144) = 15.28, p<.001.

papers.title: Müller
papers.apa: Müller, F., & Rothermund, K. (2014). What Does It Take to Activate Stereotypes? Simple Primes Don’t Seem Enough. Social Psychology, 45(3), 187–193. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000183
papers.summary: This study attempted to replicate automatic stereotype activation, specifically from Banaji and Hardin (1996), while ruling out response priming. The authors found strong priming effects when response priming was possible, but no evidence for automatic stereotype activation when response priming was excluded. They concluded that automatic stereotype activation is not a reliable and global phenomenon but is restricted to more specific conditions.

papers.title: Rothermund
papers.apa: Müller, F., & Rothermund, K. (2014). What Does It Take to Activate Stereotypes? Simple Primes Don’t Seem Enough. Social Psychology, 45(3), 187–193. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000183
papers.summary: This is a duplicate entry for the Müller & Rothermund (2014) paper.

papers.effect_name: Sex difference in distress to infidelity
papers.title: Jealousy and the nature of beliefs about infidelity: Tests of competing hypotheses about sex differences in the United States, Korea, and Japan
papers.apa: BUSS, D. M., SHACKELFORD, T. K., KIRKPATRICK, L. A., CHOE, J. C., LIM, H. K., HASEGAWA, M., HASEGAWA, T., & BENNETT, K. (1999). Jealousy and the nature of beliefs about infidelity: Tests of competing hypotheses about sex differences in the United States, Korea, and Japan. Personal Relationships, 6(1), 125–150. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1475-6811.1999.tb00215.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper presents four studies (total N > 1800 across samples from the US, Korea, and Japan) demonstrating a robust sex difference in jealousy. Men were consistently more distressed by sexual infidelity, while women were more distressed by emotional infidelity, supporting an evolutionary explanation over a belief-based one. For example, t(494) = 6.09 and t(624) = 6.82 were reported for specific comparisons.

papers.effect_name: Sex difference in distress to infidelity
papers.title: Shackelford and Voracek, 2004
papers.apa: Shackelford, T. K., Voracek, M., Schmitt, D. P., Buss, D. M., Weekes-Shackelford, V. A., & Michalski, R. L. (2004). Romantic jealousy in early adulthood and in later life. Human Nature, 15(3), 283–300. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12110-004-1010-z
papers.summary: This study investigated the sex difference in jealousy in an older sample (N=202, mean age 67) and compared it to a younger sample (N=234, mean age 20, data from Buss et al., 1999). It found that the sex difference (men more distressed by sexual, women by emotional infidelity) replicated in the older sample, though with a smaller magnitude. The re-analysis of the younger sample data yielded a significant effect (d = 1.29, t(232) = 9.89, p < .001). Notably, older women were less distressed by emotional infidelity than younger women, while men showed no age-related difference.

papers.title: The logic of social exchange: Has natural selection shaped how humans reason? Studies with the Wason selection task
papers.apa: Cosmides, L. (1989). The logic of social exchange: Has natural selection shaped how humans reason? Studies with the Wason selection task. Cognition, 31(3), 187–276. https://doi.org/10.1016/0010-0277(89)90023-1
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of the cheater detection module, proposing that humans possess an innate, specialized cognitive mechanism for detecting social contract violations. Using the Wason selection task, the authors demonstrated that reasoning performance significantly improves when the task is framed as detecting a cheater in a social exchange scenario, compared to abstract or descriptive versions of the task.

papers.title: Van Lier et al.2013
papers.apa: Van Lier, J., Revlin, R., & De Neys, W. (2013). Detecting Cheaters without Thinking: Testing the Automaticity of the Cheater Detection Module. PLoS ONE, 8(1), e53827. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0053827
papers.summary: This study replicated the cheater detection effect, finding a significant effect size of d=0.77. The findings support the idea that reasoning performance on the Wason selection task is enhanced when the task involves detecting social cheaters, consistent with the original theory.

papers.title: Deviation, rejection, and communication
papers.apa: Schachter, S. (1950). Deviation, rejection and communication. Theory and Experiment in Social Communication., 51–82. https://doi.org/10.1037/10646-004
papers.summary: Stanley Schachter's seminal 1951 study investigated how groups respond to members who deviate from group norms. Participants discussed a juvenile delinquent case, with confederates adopting either a modal, slider, or deviant position. The study found that the deviant confederate received the most communication initially, followed by a sharp decline and eventual rejection from desirable group roles, demonstrating the group's attempt to persuade and then ostracize the deviant.

papers.title: Wesselmann 2014
papers.apa: Nickenig, K., & Wesselmann, C. (2014). Angewandtes Rechnungswesen. Springer Fachmedien Wiesbaden. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-658-04420-6
papers.summary: This replication attempt of Schachter's (1951) study investigated the dissenting deviant social rejection effect across multiple measures. The authors found mixed results: the effect was replicated on the sociometric test (χ2(2) = 14.74, p < .01) and for differential communication patterns (F(2, 32) = 20.83, p < 0.01, ηp2 = 0.57), but not for the committee nomination measure (χ2(4) = 0.79, p = 0.94) or overall communication changes over time. This suggests the effect's manifestation might be dependent on the specific measure used.

papers.effect_name: Sex differences in implicit maths attitudes
papers.title: Math = male, me = female, therefore math ≠ me
papers.apa: Nosek, B. A., Banaji, M. R., & Greenwald, A. G. (2002). Math = male, me = female, therefore math ≠ me. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 83(1), 44–59. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.83.1.44
papers.summary: This foundational paper likely introduced or provided early evidence for the phenomenon of implicit gender-math stereotypes, demonstrating that individuals, particularly women, implicitly associate math with males and themselves with non-math domains. It would have used implicit measures, such as the IAT, to reveal these automatic associations and their potential impact on attitudes towards math.

papers.effect_name: Sex differences in implicit maths attitudes
papers.title: Klein et al. 2014
papers.apa: Klein, R. A., Ratliff, K. A., Vianello, M., Adams, R. B., Bahník, Š., Bernstein, M. J., Bocian, K., Brandt, M. J., Brooks, B., Brumbaugh, C. C., Cemalcilar, Z., Chandler, J., Cheong, W., Davis, W. E., Devos, T., Eisner, M., Frankowska, N., Furrow, D., Galliani, E. M., … Nosek, B. A. (2014). Investigating Variation in Replicability. Social Psychology, 45(3), 142–152. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000178
papers.summary: This study investigated the replicability of 13 classic and contemporary effects across 36 independent samples (N=6,344). For the sex differences in implicit math attitudes effect, the authors reported a replication effect size of d=0.56 [0.45, 0.68], indicating consistent evidence for the effect. Overall, 10 of the 13 effects replicated consistently, suggesting that replicability is often more dependent on the effect itself than on the sample or setting.

papers.effect_name: Low versus high category scale effect on behaviour self-report
papers.title: Response Scales: Effects of Category Range on Reported Behavior and Comparative Judgments
papers.apa: Schwarz, N., Hippler, H.-J., Deutsch, B., & Strack, F. (1985). Response Scales: Effects of Category Range on Reported Behavior and Comparative Judgments. Public Opinion Quarterly, 49(3), 388. https://doi.org/10.1086/268936
papers.summary: This foundational work demonstrated that the range of response options provided in self-report scales can significantly influence participants' reported behaviors and comparative judgments. For example, participants presented with a low-frequency scale (e.g., 'less than once a week') reported engaging in the behavior less often than those presented with a high-frequency scale (e.g., 'more than twice a week'). The original effect size was reported as d=0.50 [0.15, 0.84].

papers.effect_name: Low versus high category scale effect on behaviour self-report
papers.title: Klein et al. 2014
papers.apa: Klein, R. A., Ratliff, K. A., Vianello, M., Adams, R. B., Bahník, Š., Bernstein, M. J., Bocian, K., Brandt, M. J., Brooks, B., Brumbaugh, C. C., Cemalcilar, Z., Chandler, J., Cheong, W., Davis, W. E., Devos, T., Eisner, M., Frankowska, N., Furrow, D., Galliani, E. M., … Nosek, B. A. (2014). Investigating Variation in Replicability. Social Psychology, 45(3), 142–152. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000178
papers.summary: This large-scale replication project investigated the replicability of 13 classic and contemporary psychological effects across 36 independent samples totaling 6,344 participants. For the low versus high category scale effect, the study found strong evidence for replication, reporting an effect size of d=0.51 [0.42, 0.61]. The authors concluded that replicability is often more dependent on the effect itself than on the specific sample or setting.

papers.title: Prestige, Suggestion, and Attitudes
papers.apa: Lorge, I., & Curtiss, C. C. (1936). Prestige, Suggestion, and Attitudes. The Journal of Social Psychology, 7(4), 386–402. https://doi.org/10.1080/00224545.1936.9919891
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated how the perceived prestige of an information source influences attitudes towards statements attributed to it. Participants rated their regard for various sources and then their agreement with quotations, with source attribution manipulated. The study aimed to demonstrate that agreement with a statement is a function of the regard for its presumed authority, even when that authority is false or later revealed to be true.

papers.title: Klein et al. 2014
papers.apa: Klein, R. A., Ratliff, K. A., Vianello, M., Adams, R. B., Bahník, Š., Bernstein, M. J., Bocian, K., Brandt, M. J., Brooks, B., Brumbaugh, C. C., Cemalcilar, Z., Chandler, J., Cheong, W., Davis, W. E., Devos, T., Eisner, M., Frankowska, N., Furrow, D., Galliani, E. M., … Nosek, B. A. (2014). Investigating Variation in Replicability. Social Psychology, 45(3), 142–152. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000178
papers.summary: This large-scale replication project investigated the replicability of 13 classic and contemporary psychological effects across 36 independent samples (N=6,344). For the 'information source on attitudes' effect, the study found consistent evidence for replication, reporting a meta-analytic effect size of d = 0.31 [0.19, 0.42]. The authors concluded that this effect, among others, replicated robustly across diverse settings and samples.

papers.title: ‘Reciprocal concessions procedure for inducing compliance: The door-in-the-face technique
papers.apa: Cialdini, R. B., Vincent, J. E., Lewis, S. K., Catalan, J., Wheeler, D., & Darby, B. L. (1975). Reciprocal concessions procedure for inducing compliance: The door-in-the-face technique. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 31(2), 206–215. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0076284
papers.summary: Cialdini et al. (1975) conducted three experiments to test the effectiveness of a 'rejection-then-moderation' procedure for inducing compliance. They found that making an extreme initial request, which was refused, and then a smaller request, significantly increased compliance with the smaller request compared to only making the smaller request. For example, in Experiment 1 (N=72), compliance with the smaller request was significantly higher in the rejection-moderation condition, supporting the hypothesis that the effect is mediated by a rule for reciprocation of concessions.

papers.title: Genschow et al. 2021
papers.apa: Genschow, O., Westfal, M., Crusius, J., Bartosch, L., Feikes, K. I., Pallasch, N., & Wozniak, M. (2021). Does social psychology persist over half a century? A direct replication of Cialdini et al.’s (1975) classic door-in-the-face technique. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 120(2), e1–e7. https://doi.org/10.1037/pspa0000261
papers.summary: Genschow et al. (2021) conducted a direct replication of Cialdini et al.'s (1975) door-in-the-face technique. Their findings showed a similar effect size (OR=2.082, d=0.40) to the original study (OR=2.36, d=0.47), indicating that the effect persists. The authors' work suggests that the door-in-the-face effect remains robust and replicable.

papers.title: Compliance without pressure: The foot-in-the-door technique
papers.apa: Freedman, J. L., & Fraser, S. C. (1966). Compliance without pressure: The foot-in-the-door technique. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 4(2), 195–202. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0023552
papers.summary: This foundational paper presents two experiments demonstrating the foot-in-the-door effect. The first study, a field experiment with 156 housewives, found that over 50% of participants who agreed to a small initial request later complied with a larger request, compared to less than 20% in a control group. The authors propose that agreeing to a small request changes a person's self-perception, making them more likely to see themselves as cooperative and thus more inclined to agree to subsequent requests, even if made by a different person or for a dissimilar task.

papers.title: Gamian-Wilk and Dolinski 2019
papers.apa: Gamian-Wilk, M., & Dolinski, D. (2019). The Foot-in-the-Door Phenomenon 40 and 50 Years Later: A Direct Replication of the Original Freedman and Fraser Study in Poland and in Ukraine. Psychological Reports, 123(6), 2582–2596. https://doi.org/10.1177/0033294119872208
papers.summary: This study conducted a direct replication of Freedman and Fraser's original foot-in-the-door experiment in Poland and Ukraine across two time points (2003 and 2013). The authors note that while the effect is widely studied, meta-analyses often report low effect sizes, and the original large effect size has rarely been replicated. Their findings indicate a general drop in compliance over time, with the foot-in-the-door strategy being effective in Ukraine in 2003 but insignificant in both Ukraine and Poland by 2013, suggesting the effect's robustness may vary culturally and temporally.

papers.title: Klein et al. 2014
papers.apa: Klein, R. A., Ratliff, K. A., Vianello, M., Adams, R. B., Bahník, Š., Bernstein, M. J., Bocian, K., Brandt, M. J., Brooks, B., Brumbaugh, C. C., Cemalcilar, Z., Chandler, J., Cheong, W., Davis, W. E., Devos, T., Eisner, M., Frankowska, N., Furrow, D., Galliani, E. M., … Nosek, B. A. (2014). Investigating Variation in Replicability. Social Psychology, 45(3), 142–152. https://doi.org/10.1027/1864-9335/a000178
papers.summary: This large-scale replication project investigated the replicability of 13 psychological effects across 36 independent samples with a total of 6,344 participants. For the Ingroup-Outgroup Norm of Reciprocity effect, the study found consistent evidence for replication, reporting an effect size of d=0.27 [0.18, 0.36]. Overall, the authors concluded that 10 of the 13 effects replicated consistently, suggesting that replicability is more dependent on the effect itself than on the sample and setting.

papers.title: Body Politics: Power, Sex, and Nonverbal Communication
papers.apa: Veevers, J. E., & Henley, N. M. (1979). Body Politics: Power, Sex, and Nonverbal Communication. Contemporary Sociology, 8(4), 636. https://doi.org/10.2307/2065224
papers.summary: This foundational book synthesizes existing research and theory to argue that nonverbal communication systematically reflects and reinforces social hierarchies, particularly in terms of power and status. It proposes that specific nonverbal cues are used to express and perceive dominance and submission in social interactions.

papers.title: Hall et al. 2005
papers.apa: Davis, L. G., Hall, R. A., & Willis, S. C. (2006). Response to Moss et al. “An Early Holocene/Late Pleistocene Archaeological Site on the Oregon Coast? Comments on Hall et al. (2005).” Radiocarbon, 48(3), 469–472. https://doi.org/10.1017/s0033822200038893
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated both beliefs about and actual relationships between verticality (dominance/status) and various nonverbal behaviors. It found that beliefs about these relationships were often moderate to strong (e.g., interruptions r=.61, pausing r=-.78), but actual observed correlations were generally very weak or close to zero for many cues (e.g., smiling r=-.03, gazing r=-.01). Some actual behaviors, like loudness (r=.24) and interpersonal distance (r=-.17), showed small to moderate effects. The study highlights a significant discrepancy between perceived and actual nonverbal indicators of status.

papers.title: Postdecision changes in the desirability of alternatives
papers.apa: Brehm, J. W. (1956). Postdecision changes in the desirability of alternatives. The Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology, 52(3), 384–389. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0041006
papers.summary: This foundational paper by Brehm (1956) introduced the free-choice paradigm, demonstrating that individuals alter their preferences for chosen and rejected items after making a decision. This 'spreading of alternatives' was interpreted as evidence for cognitive dissonance reduction, where the desirability of the chosen item increases and the rejected item decreases.

papers.title: Enisman et al. 2021
papers.apa: Enisman, M., Shpitzer, H., & Kleiman, T. (2021). Choice changes preferences, not merely reflects them: A meta-analysis of the artifact-free free-choice paradigm. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 120(1), 16–29. https://doi.org/10.1037/pspa0000263
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized studies on the free-choice paradigm that addressed previous methodological critiques. It found a significant, moderate effect of choice on preference change (d=0.40 [0.32, 0.49]), concluding that choice does indeed change preferences, rather than merely reflecting them, when artifacts are controlled.

papers.title: Izuma and Murayama 2013
papers.apa: Izuma, K., & Murayama, K. (2013). Choice-Induced Preference Change in the Free-Choice Paradigm: A Critical Methodological Review. Frontiers in Psychology, 4. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00041
papers.summary: This paper critically reviewed the free-choice paradigm, highlighting a methodological flaw (Chen & Risen, 2010) that could produce artificial preference changes without true dissonance. Through simulations and a meta-analysis, the authors demonstrated that while choice-induced preference change can exist when the critique is addressed, the effect size is considerably smaller than previously reported, and past findings using the traditional paradigm should be re-evaluated. They advocate for avoiding the conventional paradigm in future research.

papers.title: Dissonance arousal: Physiological evidence
papers.apa: Croyle, R. T., & Cooper, J. (1983). Dissonance arousal: Physiological evidence. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 45(4), 782–791. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.45.4.782
papers.summary: This paper conducted two experiments. Experiment 1 replicated the standard induced compliance paradigm, demonstrating the expected pattern of attitude change. Experiment 2, using the same paradigm, found significantly more nonspecific skin conductance responses in high-choice counterattitudinal conditions, supporting dissonance as an an arousal process. The authors attributed the absence of attitude change in Experiment 2 to subjects misattributing their arousal to the physiological recording device.

papers.title: Kenworthy et al. 2011
papers.apa: Kenworthy, J. B., Miller, N., Collins, B. E., Read, S. J., & Earleywine, M. (2011). A trans-paradigm theoretical synthesis of cognitive dissonance theory: Illuminating the nature of discomfort. European Review of Social Psychology, 22(1), 36–113. https://doi.org/10.1080/10463283.2011.580155
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized cognitive dissonance research across five paradigms, including induced compliance, to evaluate theoretical perspectives. It reported a reliable medium-to-large overall mean effect size for dissonance (d=0.61) and specifically found the induced compliance paradigm to have the largest effect size (d=0.81). The authors concluded that guilt, rather than general discomfort, strongly predicted dissonance effect sizes.

papers.title: Kim et al. 2014
papers.apa: Kim, S.-Y., Allen, M., Preiss, R. W., & Peterson, B. (2014). Meta-Analysis of Counterattitudinal Advocacy Data: Evidence for an Additive Cues Model. Communication Quarterly, 62(5), 607–620. https://doi.org/10.1080/01463373.2014.949385
papers.summary: This meta-analysis combined 230 studies on counterattitudinal advocacy (CAA), a key component of the induced compliance paradigm, finding a moderate mean effect size for attitude change (r=0.223, N=17,505). The study showed that while individual elements of CAA induction (choice, commitment) did not significantly improve impact, the magnitude of attitude change increased with the number of elements present. This supports the effectiveness of CAA in producing attitude change, though the underlying mechanism remains underspecified.

papers.title: Vaidis et al. 2018
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Overcoming denial and increasing the intention to use condoms through the induction of hypocrisy
papers.apa: Aronson, E., Fried, C., & Stone, J. (1991). Overcoming denial and increasing the intention to use condoms through the induction of hypocrisy. American Journal of Public Health, 81(12), 1636–1638. https://doi.org/10.2105/ajph.81.12.1636
papers.summary: This foundational study induced feelings of hypocrisy in college students to increase condom use. Participants were made mindful of their past failures to use condoms and then asked to persuade others about the importance of condoms. The authors found that this induction of hypocrisy decreased denial and led to a greater intent to improve condom use compared to control conditions.

papers.title: Priolo et al. 2019
papers.apa: Priolo, D., Pelt, A., Bauzel, R. St., Rubens, L., Voisin, D., & Fointiat, V. (2019). Three Decades of Research on Induced Hypocrisy: A Meta-Analysis. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 45(12), 1681–1701. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167219841621
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized 29 published and 9 unpublished studies on induced hypocrisy. It confirmed that hypocrisy (vs. control) significantly increased both behavioral intention (meta-analytic r=.35 [.22, .46]) and actual behavior (meta-analytic r=.30 [.10, .48]). The study also explored the necessity of both steps in the hypocrisy procedure and found inconclusive results regarding psychological discomfort.

papers.title: Jaubert et al. 2020
papers.apa: Jaubert, S., Fillon, A. A., Souchet, L., & Girandola, F. (2020). Vicarious Dissonance : Meta-analysis. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/hc2n3
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated the overall effect of vicarious cognitive dissonance, particularly within the induced-compliance paradigm. It reported an effect size of d=0.35 for the induced-compliance paradigm and a global effect of d=0.41, which reduced to d=0.22 (95% CI [0.008, 0.43]) after correcting for publication bias, suggesting a smaller but still present effect.

papers.title: Gaffney et al. 2012
papers.apa: Gaffney, A. M., Hogg, M. A., Cooper, J., & Stone, J. (2012). Witness to hypocrisy: Reacting to ingroup hypocrites in the presence of others. Social Influence, 7(2), 98–112. https://doi.org/10.1080/15534510.2012.663741
papers.summary: This study (N=78) investigated how the presence and group membership of a third-party observer influence reactions to ingroup hypocrisy. It found that participants showed stronger pro-environmental attitudes when an outgroup member negatively remarked on the hypocrisy, and adhered to group norms when another ingroup member was present. The study reported an interaction effect on attitudes of ηp2 = 0.142 (d = 0.40).

papers.title: Jaubert et al. 2020
papers.apa: Jaubert, S., Fillon, A. A., Souchet, L., & Girandola, F. (2020). Vicarious Dissonance : Meta-analysis. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/hc2n3
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification. The prompt indicates this paper reported an effect of vicarious dissonance on attitude change (η2p=0.03) and an effect toward the induced-compliance paradigm (d=0.46 [0.27, 0.64]).

papers.title: Monin et al. 2004
papers.apa: Monin, B., Norton, M. I., Cooper, J., & Hopg, M. A. (2004). Reacting to an Assumed Situation vs. Conforming to an Assumed Reaction: The                 Role of Perceived Speaker Attitude in Vicarious Dissonance. Group Processes & Intergroup Relations, 7(3), 207–220. https://doi.org/10.1177/1368430204046108
papers.summary: This research (two studies) demonstrated the vicarious dissonance effect among high ingroup identifiers, showing that observing an ingroup member's counter-attitudinal behavior can lead to attitude change in the observer. The effect was found even when the speaker clearly disagreed with the position (Study 1, d=0.30) and was driven by foreseeable aversive consequences rather than perceived speaker attitude (Study 2, d=0.46). The authors concluded the effect is a group phenomenon, not driven by empathy.

papers.title: The imposter phenomenon in high achieving women: Dynamics and therapeutic intervention
papers.apa: Clance, P. R., & Imes, S. A. (1978). The imposter phenomenon in high achieving women: Dynamics and therapeutic intervention. Psychotherapy: Theory, Research & Practice, 15(3), 241–247. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0086006
papers.summary: Clance and Imes (1978) introduced the concept of the imposter phenomenon, describing it as an internal experience of intellectual phoniness among high-achieving women. They proposed that these individuals attribute their success to external factors and fear being exposed as frauds, despite objective evidence of competence. The original paper was conceptual and clinical, not involving statistical analyses.

papers.title: Bravata et al. 2020
papers.apa: Bravata, D. M., Watts, S. A., Keefer, A. L., Madhusudhan, D. K., Taylor, K. T., Clark, D. M., Nelson, R. S., Cokley, K. O., & Hagg, H. K. (2019). Prevalence, Predictors, and Treatment of Impostor Syndrome: a Systematic Review. Journal of General Internal Medicine, 35(4), 1252–1275. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11606-019-05364-1
papers.summary: Bravata et al. (2019) conducted a systematic review of 62 studies involving 14,161 participants to evaluate the prevalence, predictors, comorbidities, and treatment of imposter syndrome. They found prevalence rates ranging from 9% to 82%, noting it is common among both men and women and particularly high among ethnic minority groups. The review highlighted comorbidities with depression and anxiety, and associations with impaired job performance and burnout, but found no published studies evaluating treatments.

papers.effect_name: Ability EI as a factor of intelligence
papers.title: Emotional Intelligence is a second-stratum factor of intelligence: evidence from hierarchical and bifactor models
papers.apa: MacCann, C., Joseph, D. L., Newman, D. A., & Roberts, R. D. (2014). Emotional intelligence is a second-stratum factor of intelligence: Evidence from hierarchical and bifactor models. Emotion, 14(2), 358–374. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0034755
papers.summary: This foundational paper empirically tested various models of how Ability EI relates to other cognitive abilities, including hierarchical and bifactor structures. The authors concluded that a hierarchical model, where Ability EI functions as a second-stratum factor, provided the best fit to the data, thus contextualizing Ability EI within broader models of intelligence.

papers.effect_name: Ability EI as a factor of intelligence
papers.title: Evans et al. 2020
papers.apa: Evans, T. R., Hughes, D. J., & Steptoe-Warren, G. (2020). A conceptual replication of emotional intelligence as a second-stratum factor of intelligence. Emotion, 20(3), 507–512. https://doi.org/10.1037/emo0000569
papers.summary: This replication study aimed to reproduce the modeling process of the foundational MacCann et al. (2014) paper using a new sample. The authors reported drawing the same conclusion, confirming that Ability EI is best understood as a second-stratum factor within a hierarchical model of intelligence.

papers.title: The Matthew Matilda Effect in Science
papers.apa: Rossiter, M. W. (1993). The Matthew Matilda Effect in Science. Social Studies of Science, 23(2), 325–341. https://doi.org/10.1177/030631293023002004
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduces the "Matilda Effect," naming it after Matilda J. Gage, to describe the systematic under-recognition and denial of credit to women scientists throughout history. It argues that this sex-linked bias in scientific recognition practices complements Merton's "Matthew Effect" and calls for a more comprehensive history of science that includes overlooked female contributions.

papers.title: Feeley and Lee 2015
papers.apa: Wong, S., Ning, A., Lee, C., & Feeley, B. T. (2015). Return to sport after muscle injury. Current Reviews in Musculoskeletal Medicine, 8(2), 168–175. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12178-015-9262-2
papers.summary: This study investigated citation patterns, finding that female lead authors were cited, on average, 19.34 times compared to male lead authors (18.05 times), a non-significant difference. However, male-typed topics received more citations (M=22.43) than female-typed topics (M=17.87), with a small effect size (η² = 0.003). This suggests mixed evidence for the Matilda effect depending on the specific metric.

papers.title: Feeley and Yang 2022
papers.apa: Feeley, T. H., & Yang, Z. (2021). Is there a Matilda Effect in Communication Journals? Communication Reports, 35(1), 1–11. https://doi.org/10.1080/08934215.2021.1974505
papers.summary: This study examined the Matilda Effect in eight communication journals over ten years (N=3,324 articles), finding mixed results. Only two of the eight journals exhibited a Matilda effect, with very small effect sizes (less than 1% to almost 2% of variance explained). The authors concluded that while some evidence exists, the effect is small and not consistently present across all journals, suggesting the question of bias remains open.

papers.title: Rajko et al. 2023
papers.apa: Rajkó, A., Herendy, C., Goyanes, M., & Demeter, M. (2023). The Matilda Effect in Communication Research: The Effects of Gender and Geography on Usage and Citations Across 11 Countries. Communication Research, 52(2), 209–232. https://doi.org/10.1177/00936502221124389
papers.summary: Analyzing performance data from 5,500 communication scholars across 11 countries, this study found that female scholars are significantly underrepresented among prolific authors. After controlling for country, total papers, and views, female scholars' papers received significantly fewer citations than male peers (β = −.05; p < .001), despite being more viewed. This provides empirical evidence for a twofold Matilda effect at both production and performance levels.

papers.effect_name: Being slightly behind increases the chance of winning
papers.title: Can Losing Lead to Winning?
papers.apa: Berger, J., & Pope, D. (2011). Can Losing Lead to Winning? Management Science, 57(5), 817–827. https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.1110.1328
papers.summary: This foundational study proposed that being slightly behind at halftime increases the likelihood of winning in professional basketball. The authors found that NBA teams behind by one point at halftime won 5.8 to 8 percentage points more often than expected, and NCAA teams won 2.1 to 2.5 percentage points more often, with most results being statistically significant.

papers.effect_name: Being slightly behind increases the chance of winning
papers.title: Teeselink et al. 2022
papers.apa: Klein Teeselink, B., van den Assem, M. J., & van Dolder, D. (2023). Does Losing Lead to Winning? An Empirical Analysis for Four Sports. Management Science, 69(1), 513–532. https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.2022.4372
papers.summary: This replication study extended the analysis to other sports (Australian football, American football, rugby) and found no evidence for the effect. Re-examining basketball with larger samples, the authors found a significant effect only for NBA matches within the original study's timeframe (5 percentage points more often than expected), but not for other NBA periods, NCAA, or WNBA. High-powered meta-analyses across 12 leagues and sports did not reject the null hypothesis, suggesting the effect is not generalizable and, if present, is likely small.

papers.title: E Pluribus Unum: Diversity and Community in the Twenty-first Century The 2006 Johan Skytte Prize Lecture
papers.apa: Putnam, R. D. (2007). E Pluribus Unum: Diversity and Community in the Twenty‐first Century The 2006 Johan Skytte Prize Lecture. Scandinavian Political Studies, 30(2), 137–174. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9477.2007.00176.x
papers.summary: Putnam (2007) presented evidence from the US suggesting that while immigration and diversity may offer long-term benefits, in the short run, they reduce social solidarity and capital. The study found that residents in ethnically diverse neighborhoods tend to 'hunker down,' exhibiting lower trust (even within their own race), reduced altruism, and less community cooperation. For example, trust in neighbors was found to increase by 0.18 (on a 4-point scale) when moving from a maximally heterogeneous to a maximally homogeneous community (t=5.1).

papers.title: Abascal et al. 2015
papers.apa: Abascal, M., & Baldassarri, D. (2015). Love Thy Neighbor? Ethnoracial Diversity and Trust Reexamined. American Journal of Sociology, 121(3), 722–782. https://doi.org/10.1086/683144
papers.summary: Abascal and Baldassarri (2015) re-examined Putnam's (2007) claims using the Social Capital Community Benchmark Survey (SCCBS) data, which included nearly 30,000 respondents. They found that while a similar negative association between diversity and trust appeared in the full US sample (trust decreased by 0.12), this effect was largely explained by pre-existing attitudinal differences and residential sorting, rather than diversity itself. When using random subsamples, a significant effect was found in only 4 out of 30 models (average t=-0.76), leading the authors to conclude that diversity is a negligible predictor of trust compared to classic sociological indicators of inequality. They also critiqued the use of summary diversity indexes for masking hierarchical relations and in-group/out-group distinctions.

papers.effect_name: Greed moderates the relationship between SES and unethical behaviour
papers.title: Higher social class predicts increased unethical behavior
papers.apa: Piff, P. K., Stancato, D. M., Côté, S., Mendoza-Denton, R., & Keltner, D. (2012). Higher social class predicts increased unethical behavior. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 109(11), 4086–4091. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1118373109
papers.summary: This foundational paper presented seven studies using diverse methods, demonstrating that higher social class predicts increased unethical behavior. The authors found that upper-class individuals were more likely to break traffic laws, make unethical decisions, take goods, lie, cheat, and endorse unethical workplace behavior. They proposed that this tendency is partly mediated by more favorable attitudes toward greed, suggesting greed moderates the relationship between SES and unethical behavior (interaction effect b=−0.24 [−0.44 , −0.04]).

papers.effect_name: Greed moderates the relationship between SES and unethical behaviour
papers.title: Balakrishnan et al. 2017
papers.apa: Balakrishnan, A., Palma, P. A., Patenaude, J., & Campbell, L. (2017). A 4-study replication of the moderating effects of greed on socioeconomic status and unethical behaviour. Scientific Data, 4(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/sdata.2016.120
papers.summary: This paper conducted four independent replication attempts of the interaction effect between greed and socioeconomic status on unethical behavior. Across all four studies, no significant interaction effect was found (replication 1: b=0.11 [−0.02 , 0.24]; replication 2: b=−0.06 [−0.16 , 0.04]; replication 3: b=0.01 [−0.10 , 0.12]; replication 4: b=0.10 [−0.10 , 0.29]). The findings consistently failed to replicate the original moderation effect.

papers.title: For Better or for Worse?: Education and the Prevalence of Domestic Violence in Turkey
papers.apa: Erten, B., & Keskin, P. (2018). For Better or for Worse?: Education and the Prevalence of Domestic Violence in Turkey. American Economic Journal: Applied Economics, 10(1), 64–105. https://doi.org/10.1257/app.20160278
papers.summary: This foundational study used a regression discontinuity design in Turkey to examine the causal effects of increased education on domestic violence. It found that a 1-1.5 year increase in schooling for rural women, linked to improved labor market outcomes, led to a significant increase in self-reported psychological violence and financial control behavior, but not physical violence. The authors reported a 0.12 standard deviation increase in reported domestic psychological violence (SE: 0.057).

papers.title: Akyol et al. 2020
papers.apa: Akyol, S., S Kursunlu, F., & Akyol, O. (2020). Bone resorption matrix metalloproteinases and caffeic acid phenethyl ester. Acta Orthopaedica et Traumatologica Turcica, 50(6). https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aott.2016.08.010
papers.summary: This paper attempted to replicate the original findings using the same regression discontinuity design and sample, but with a different definition of rural areas. It found a 0.099 standard deviation increase in reported domestic violence (SE: 0.061), which was not statistically significant at the 5% or 10% level, failing to replicate the original significant finding.

papers.title: ’Does Economic Growth Improve the Human Lot? Some Empirical Evidence’
papers.apa: EASTERLIN, R. A. (1974). Does Economic Growth Improve the Human Lot? Some Empirical Evidence. Nations and Households in Economic Growth, 89–125. https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-205050-3.50008-7
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of the Easterlin paradox, presenting evidence that while within-country and cross-country comparisons show a positive link between income and happiness, long-term time series data for individual countries (e.g., the US) do not show a corresponding increase in average happiness despite significant economic growth.

papers.title: Easterlin 1974
papers.apa: EASTERLIN, R. A. (1974). Does Economic Growth Improve the Human Lot? Some Empirical Evidence. Nations and Households in Economic Growth, 89–125. https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-205050-3.50008-7
papers.summary: This paper, often cited as the origin of the Easterlin paradox, presented empirical observations suggesting that despite substantial economic growth in developed nations over several decades, average self-reported happiness levels did not show a corresponding upward trend, highlighting a disconnect between economic prosperity and subjective well-being over time.

papers.title: Easterlin 2005
papers.apa: Easterlin, R. A. (2005). Feeding the Illusion of Growth and Happiness: A Reply to Hagerty and Veenhoven. Social Indicators Research, 74(3), 429–443. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11205-004-6170-z
papers.summary: This paper directly refutes Hagerty and Veenhoven (2003), arguing their conclusion that growing national income increases happiness is based on flawed methodology, specifically by mixing noncomparable surveys. Easterlin re-analyzes H-V's data, finding that the hypothesis is rejected in 15 of 21 cases when focusing on statistically significant relationships. He concludes that the US experience and other studies do not support a consistent link between economic growth and rising happiness.

papers.title: Hagerty and Veenhoven 2003
papers.apa: Hagerty, M. R., & Veenhoven, R. (2003). Wealth and Happiness Revisited – Growing National Income Does Go with Greater Happiness. Social Indicators Research, 64(1), 1–27. https://doi.org/10.1023/a:1024790530822
papers.summary: This study challenges previous findings by arguing that low statistical power obscured the link between income and happiness. Using longer time series and more nations (N=21), they found that increasing national income does correlate with increasing national happiness, with 7 countries showing significant positive effects (e.g., regression coefficient b=1.26). They conclude that absolute utility models are supported, though short-term effects on happiness are larger than long-term effects for a given income rise.

papers.title: Sacks et al. 2012
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2012). Exemplar Abstract for Roseovarius halocynthiae Kim et al. 2012, Aliiroseovarius halocynthiae (Kim et al. 2012) Park et al. 2015 emend. Hördt et al. 2020 and Pseudoroseovarius halocynthiae (Kim et al. 2012) Sun et al. 2015 emend. Hördt et al. 2020 pro synon. Aliiroseovarius halocynthiae (Kim et al. 2012) Park et al. 2015 emend. Hördt et al. 2020. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.22952
papers.summary: This paper presents evidence from multiple large-scale surveys (e.g., World Values Survey, Eurobarometer) showing a robust positive relationship between average happiness and GDP per capita both within countries over time and across countries. They report significant positive coefficients (e.g., β=0.505, SE=0.109 for WVS; β=0.278, SE=0.164 for Eurobarometer), suggesting that economic growth is indeed associated with increased well-being.

papers.title: Veenhoven and Hagerty 2006
papers.apa: Veenhoven, R., & Hagerty, M. (2006). Rising Happiness in Nations 1946–2004: A Reply to Easterlin. Social Indicators Research, 79(3), 421–436. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11205-005-5074-x
papers.summary: This paper responds to Easterlin, arguing that recent data contradict the Easterlin paradox, showing a slight increase in average happiness in rich nations and a considerable increase in poor nations. They emphasize that when longevity is also considered (Happy Life Years), the improvement in quality of life is even more pronounced, with Americans gaining 5.2 and Western Europeans 6.3 happy life years between 1973-2004. They conclude that economic growth *does* improve the human lot.

papers.title: Individual differences in personal humor styles: Identification of prominent patterns and their associates
papers.apa: Galloway, G. (2010). Individual differences in personal humor styles: Identification of prominent patterns and their associates. Personality and Individual Differences, 48(5), 563–567. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2009.12.007
papers.summary: This foundational paper by Galloway (2010) first proposed that individuals can be categorized into distinct humour style clusters. Using cluster analysis, the study identified four specific profiles of humour use, characterized by different combinations of positive (affiliative, self-enhancing) and negative (aggressive, self-defeating) humour styles. This work laid the groundwork for understanding individual differences in humour beyond single dimensions.

papers.title: Chang et al., 2015
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2014). Exemplar Abstract for Lactobacillus formosensis Chang et al. 2015 and Companilactobacillus formosensis (Chang et al. 2015) Zheng et al. 2020. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.26180
papers.summary: This study aimed to replicate the humour style clusters identified by Galloway (2010). While specific details on sample size or effect sizes are not provided, the paper reportedly replicated the four-cluster solution, suggesting consistency in these humour profiles.

papers.title: Evans & Steptoe-Warren 2018
papers.apa: Evans, T. R., & Steptoe-Warren, G. (2015). Humor Style Clusters: Exploring Managerial Humor. International Journal of Business Communication, 55(4), 443–454. https://doi.org/10.1177/2329488415612478
papers.summary: This study explored managerial humour styles using cluster analysis with 202 employed adults. It identified three managerial humour clusters, which mostly replicated existing literature, and found significant differences between these clusters in relation to workplace facets like stress, communication, and job satisfaction. The authors emphasize the importance of a holistic view of humour use in organizational contexts.

papers.title: Evans et al. 2020
papers.apa: Evans, T. R., Johannes, N., Winska, J., Glinksa-Newes, A., van Stekelenburg, A., Nilsonne, G., Dean, L., Fido, D., Galloway, G., Jones, S., Masson, I., Soares, A., Steptoe-Warren, G., Thompson, N., & Ungson, N. (2020). Exploring the consistency and value of humour style profiles. Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology, 4(1), 1–24. https://doi.org/10.1080/23743603.2020.1756239
papers.summary: This large-scale study (N=863) across three world regions investigated the generalizability and value of humour style profiles. It found inconsistencies in the identified humour style profiles across different countries and compared to existing literature, suggesting potential cultural differences. The authors concluded that individual humour types, rather than clusters, offered greater predictive value for outcomes like friendship and well-being.

papers.title: Fox et al. 2016
papers.apa: Fox, C. L., Hunter, S. C., & Jones, S. E. (2016). Children’s humor types and psychosocial adjustment. Personality and Individual Differences, 89, 86–91. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2015.09.047
papers.summary: This paper explored the presence of distinctive humour types specifically in childhood. While specific details are not provided, it suggests that humour style clusters may also be identifiable in younger populations, contributing to the developmental understanding of humour.

papers.title: Leist and Muller 2013
papers.apa: Leist, A. K., & Müller, D. (2012). Humor Types Show Different Patterns of Self-Regulation, Self-Esteem, and Well-Being. Journal of Happiness Studies, 14(2), 551–569. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10902-012-9342-6
papers.summary: This study investigated humour types and their association with well-being in a German sample using an online questionnaire. It identified three distinct humour types (endorsers, humour deniers, and self-enhancers) through hierarchical clustering, which showed different patterns of self-esteem, self-regulatory strategies, and well-being. The findings support the positive correlates of self-enhancing humour and suggest that humour styles should be considered in context.

papers.title: Sirigatti et al. 2016
papers.apa: Sirigatti, S., Penzo, I., Giannetti, E., Casale, S., & Stefanile, C. (2016). Relationships between humorism profiles and psychological well-being. Personality and Individual Differences, 90, 219–224. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2015.11.011
papers.summary: This paper identified three distinct humour styles, contributing to the ongoing discussion about the number and nature of humour style clusters. While specific details are not provided, it suggests variability in the clustering solutions found across different studies.

papers.title: Where do we look during potentially offensive behavior?
papers.apa: Crosby, J. R., Monin, B., & Richardson, D. (2008). Where Do We Look During Potentially Offensive Behavior? Psychological Science, 19(3), 226–228. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02072.x
papers.summary: This foundational study (N=25) investigated where people look during potentially offensive behavior. Participants watched a video where a Black individual was present during a critical comment about affirmative action, with a manipulation of whether the Black individual could hear the comment. The study found that participants looked significantly longer at the Black individual when they believed he could hear the offensive remark (F(3, 69) = 5.15, p < .005), supporting the social referencing hypothesis.

papers.title: Jonas and Skorinko 2015
papers.summary: This replication attempt reported no significant evidence for the social referencing effect, with an F(1.86, 101.7) = 0.07, p=0.917. This suggests a failure to replicate the original finding.

papers.title: Rabagliati et al. 2020
papers.apa: Rabagliati, H., Corley, M., Dering, B., Hancock, P. J. B., King, J. P. J., Levitan, C. A., Loy, J. E., & Millen, A. E. (2020). Many Labs 5: Registered Replication of Crosby, Monin, and Richardson (2008). Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science, 3(3), 353–365. https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245919870737
papers.summary: This Many Labs 5 project (N=283 across 3 labs) conducted a preregistered replication of Crosby et al. (2008), also testing a protocol manipulation to increase effect size. While the manipulation had no effect, the study did replicate the original social referencing effect, though with a much smaller effect size than initially reported. The authors also noted that a previous direct replication in the Reproducibility Project: Psychology had not found a significant result.

papers.title: Recognition for faces of own and other race
papers.apa: Malpass, R. S., & Kravitz, J. (1969). Recognition for faces of own and other race. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 13(4), 330–334. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0028434
papers.summary: This foundational study explored the other-race effect by testing both Black and White subjects' ability to recognize own-race and other-race faces. Using d' as a measure of discriminability, the authors found that White faces were more discriminable than Black faces, and subjects showed higher acuity for faces of their own race. The study involved 40 participants and suggested differential experience might play a role, though direct questionnaire data didn't fully support it.

papers.title: Lee and Penrod 2022
papers.apa: Lee, J., & Penrod, S. D. (2022). Three‐level meta‐analysis of the other‐race bias in facial identification. Applied Cognitive Psychology, 36(5), 1106–1130. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/acp.3997
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized 159 journal articles (1969-2021) on the other-race bias in facial identification, yielding moderate pooled effect sizes (Hedge's g=0.54) for higher hit rates and discriminability for own-race faces. Sensitivity and publication bias analyses supported the robustness of the bias, with participant race and retention interval identified as moderators. The authors concluded that the other-race bias remains a robust effect despite increasing racial diversity.

papers.title: Memories of unethical actions become obfuscated over time
papers.apa: Kouchaki, M., & Gino, F. (2016). Memories of unethical actions become obfuscated over time. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 113(22), 6166–6171. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1523586113
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of 'unethical amnesia,' proposing that memories of one's own unethical actions become less clear, vivid, and accurate over time compared to ethical actions. Across nine studies with over 2,100 participants, the authors reported consistent evidence for this effect, with various effect sizes (e.g., ηp2 ranging from 0.04 to 0.20, d from 0.33 to 0.57), suggesting that unethical actions are less likely to be remembered in detail.

papers.title: Stanley et al. 2018
papers.apa: Stanley, M. L., Yang, B. W., & De Brigard, F. (2018). No evidence for unethical amnesia for imagined actions: A failed replication and extension. Memory & Cognition, 46(5), 787–795. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-018-0803-y
papers.summary: This paper conducted three high-powered replication attempts (N=228, N=232, N=228) of Kouchaki and Gino's (2016) Study 5, which was the only original study measuring objective memory accuracy. Across all three studies, the authors found no evidence for unethical amnesia affecting memory accuracy, with non-significant effect sizes (d = 0.26, 0.04, and 0.17). The authors concluded that the claim of impaired memory accuracy for imagined unethical actions is not supported by their findings.

papers.title: The contaminating effects of building instrumental ties: How networking can make us feel dirty
papers.apa: Casciaro, T., Gino, F., & Kouchaki, M. (2014). The Contaminating Effects of Building Instrumental Ties. Administrative Science Quarterly, 59(4), 705–735. https://doi.org/10.1177/0001839214554990
papers.summary: Casciaro, Gino, and Kouchaki (2014) introduced the concept that instrumental networking for professional goals can lead to feelings of moral impurity and 'dirtiness.' Across four studies, including experiments and a survey of lawyers, they found evidence that instrumental networking causes feelings of dirtiness and a desire for cleansing. They also reported that high power can mitigate these feelings, with an original effect size of d=0.98 for the core effect.

papers.title: Ziani 2022
papers.apa: Ziani, Z. (2022). Does Networking Make People Feel “Dirty”?  Reconsidering the Evidence in Casciaro, Gino, Kouchaki, 2014. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/ys9dh
papers.summary: Ziani (2022) conducted a replication attempt of the 'feeling dirty after networking' effect. This study reported effect sizes ranging from d=0.02 to d=0.51, suggesting a substantially weaker or inconsistent effect compared to the original finding (d=0.98).

papers.effect_name: Public exposure influences shame and guilt differently
papers.title: The role of public exposure in moral and nonmoral shame and guilt
papers.apa: Webster, J. M., Morgan, K. A., & Smith, R. H. (2000). The Role of Public Exposure in Moral and Nonmoral Shame and Guilt: A Literary Study [dataset]. In PsycEXTRA Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/e413792005-311
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed and provided initial evidence that public exposure to a transgression increases feelings of shame more significantly than feelings of guilt. For example, Study 1 reported an effect size of f=.39 for shame and f=.27 for guilt, supporting the differential impact of public exposure on these emotions.

papers.effect_name: Public exposure influences shame and guilt differently
papers.title: Zhang et al. 2022
papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the differential impact of public exposure on shame and guilt. Across their studies, the authors found no evidence for the proposed differential effect, reporting very similar effect sizes for shame (e.g., Study 1: ηp2=.14) and guilt (e.g., Study 1: ηp2=.13) in response to public exposure, thus failing to replicate the original finding.

papers.title: Power Posing: Brief Nonverbal Displays Affect Neuroendocrine Levels and Risk Tolerance
papers.apa: Carney, D. R., Cuddy, A. J. C., & Yap, A. J. (2010). Power Posing. Psychological Science, 21(10), 1363–1368. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797610383437
papers.summary: This foundational study (N=42, as noted by Ranehill et al.) proposed that adopting high-power poses leads to increased testosterone, decreased cortisol, heightened feelings of power, and greater risk tolerance. The authors concluded that these brief nonverbal displays cause significant psychological, physiological, and behavioral changes, suggesting embodiment extends beyond mere thinking and feeling.

papers.title: Garrison et al. 2016
papers.apa: Garrison, K. E., Tang, D., & Schmeichel, B. J. (2016). Embodying Power. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 7(7), 623–630. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550616652209
papers.summary: This replication attempt (N=305) investigated the power pose effect, also adding an eye gaze manipulation. It found no influence of body posture on gambling decisions and, contrary to the original hypothesis, expansive poses reduced feelings of power (ηp2 = .016). The study concluded that power posing did not have the predicted effects on key outcomes.

papers.title: Metzler and Grezes 2019
papers.apa: Metzler, H., & Grèzes, J. (2019). Repeatedly adopting power postures does not affect hormonal correlates of dominance and affiliative behavior. PeerJ, 7, e6726. Portico. https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.6726
papers.summary: This study, based on provided replication effect sizes, found no significant effects of power posing on cortisol (ηp2 = 0.02) or testosterone (ηp2 = 0.01). These small effect sizes suggest a failure to replicate the physiological changes reported in the original research.

papers.title: Ranehill 2015
papers.apa: Ranehill, E., Dreber, A., Johannesson, M., Leiberg, S., Sul, S., & Weber, R. A. (2015). Assessing the Robustness of Power Posing. Psychological Science, 26(5), 653–656. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797614553946
papers.summary: This well-powered conceptual replication (N=200) found a significant effect of power posing on self-reported feelings of power (d=0.344), consistent with the original. However, it failed to replicate effects on hormonal levels (cortisol: d=-0.157; testosterone: d=-0.200) or financial risk-taking (d=-0.176), concluding that the physiological and behavioral effects were not robust.

papers.title: Ronay 2017
papers.apa: Ronay, R., Tybur, J. M., van Huijstee, D., & Morssinkhof, M. (2016). Embodied power, testosterone, and overconfidence as a causal pathway to risk-taking. Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology, 2(1), 28–43. https://doi.org/10.1080/23743603.2016.1248081
papers.summary: This direct replication attempt (N=108), closely following the original methodology, found no support for power posing affecting subjective feelings of power (d=0.226), risk-taking, testosterone (d=0.121), or cortisol (d=0.034). The authors suggest the original findings might be a false positive or moderated by cultural differences, emphasizing their failure to replicate any of the core effects.

papers.title: Inhibiting and Facilitating Conditions of the Human Smile: A Nonobtrusive Test of the Facial Feedback Hypothesis
papers.apa: Strack, F., Martin, L. L., & Stepper, S. (1988). Inhibiting and facilitating conditions of the human smile: A nonobtrusive test of the facial feedback hypothesis. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 54(5), 768–777. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.54.5.768
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the facial feedback hypothesis, proposing that manipulating facial expressions can directly influence emotional states. Using a non-obtrusive method like holding a pen in the mouth to induce a smile or frown, the study reported significant effects, with an original effect size of d=0.82 or d=0.43, suggesting that smiling can cause a good mood and pouting a bad mood.

papers.title: Coles et al. 2019
papers.apa: Coles, N. A., Larsen, J. T., & Lench, H. (2017). A Meta-Analysis of the Facial Feedback Literature: Effects of Facial Feedback on Emotional Experience Are Small and Variable. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/svjru
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 98 studies found a small but statistically significant overall effect for the facial feedback hypothesis (d=0.2, 95% CI [0.14, 0.26]), corresponding to an absolute 5% increase in a happiness metric. The authors reported no evidence of publication bias, though the provided context notes this finding is questionable given the small sample sizes (mostly N < 100) of the included studies, which would typically require much larger N to detect such a small effect with adequate power.

papers.title: Wagenmakers et al. 2016
papers.apa: Gronau, Q. F., & Wagenmakers, E.-J. (2020). Hydroxychloroquine in Patients with COVID-19 (Chen et al., 2020): Absence of Evidence, Not Evidence of Absence. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/rqhgj
papers.summary: This large-scale, multi-lab replication attempt (across 36 labs, N=4,493) directly tested the original 'pen-in-mouth' paradigm of the facial feedback hypothesis. The study found no evidence for the effect, reporting a meta-analytic effect size of d=0.03 with a confidence interval overlapping zero. The authors concluded that the original finding was likely a false positive.

papers.title: Schimmack 2017
papers.apa: Schimmack, U., & Brunner, J. (2017). Z-Curve. https://doi.org/10.31219/osf.io/wr93f
papers.summary: This replicability analysis examined a subset of facial feedback studies, finding strong evidence of publication bias when using a proper power analysis. The author noted that direct replications of the original 'pen-in-mouth' protocol tend to fail, while some newer conceptual replications appear to show an effect, suggesting a nuanced picture of the hypothesis's replicability.

papers.title: Positive Self-Statements: Power for Some, Peril for Others
papers.apa: Wood, J. V., Elaine Perunovic, W. Q., & Lee, J. W. (2009). Positive Self-Statements. Psychological Science, 20(7), 860–866. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2009.02370.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper investigated the impact of positive self-statements on mood and self-esteem. Across two experiments, the authors found that individuals with low self-esteem who repeated positive affirmations (e.g., “I'm a lovable person”) reported worse mood compared to controls, while those with high self-esteem reported a limited mood boost. A meta-analysis of their studies suggested a benefit for high self-esteem individuals (d=0.66) and a negative impact for low self-esteem individuals (d=0.72).

papers.title: Flynn and Bordieri 2020
papers.apa: Flynn, M. K., & Bordieri, M. J. (2020). On the failure to replicate past findings regarding positive affirmations and self-esteem. Journal of Contextual Behavioral Science, 16, 49–61. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcbs.2020.03.003
papers.summary: This paper presents two replication attempts of Wood et al. (2009). Study 1 (N=225) directly replicated the original design, finding no significant differences in mood or state self-esteem between positive affirmation and control conditions, and no moderating effect of trait self-esteem. Study 2 (N=237) conceptually replicated and extended the work, again finding no significant effects of positive affirmations on mood, self-esteem, or goal completion. The authors concluded a failure to replicate the original findings, suggesting the original results might have been a Type I error.

papers.title: An Outpatient Program in Behavioral Medicine for Chronic Pain Patients Based on the Practice of Mindfulness Meditation
papers.apa: Kabat-Zinn, J. (1982). An outpatient program in behavioral medicine for chronic pain patients based on the practice of mindfulness meditation: Theoretical considerations and preliminary results. General Hospital Psychiatry, 4(1), 33–47. https://doi.org/10.1016/0163-8343(82)90026-3
papers.summary: This foundational paper likely introduced or significantly developed the concept of Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) as an intervention for chronic pain patients. It established the initial framework for applying mindfulness practices in a clinical setting to alleviate suffering and improve well-being.

papers.title: Fumero et al. 2020
papers.apa: Fumero, A., Peñate, W., Oyanadel, C., & Porter, B. (2020). The Effectiveness of Mindfulness-Based Interventions on Anxiety Disorders. A Systematic Meta-Review. European Journal of Investigation in Health, Psychology and Education, 10(3), 704–719. https://doi.org/10.3390/ejihpe10030052
papers.summary: This systematic review found that 75% (9/12) of included reviews reported a positive effect of Mindfulness-Based Interventions (MBIs) on anxiety, with a pooled SMD of 0.57 [0.22, 0.89] for positive results. However, 25% of reviews showed negative results (SMD = -0.27 [-0.52, 0.02]), indicating variability in findings. Effect sizes for positive results ranged from small to large.

papers.title: Coronado-Montoya 2016
papers.apa: Coronado-Montoya, S., Levis, A. W., Kwakkenbos, L., Steele, R. J., Turner, E. H., & Thombs, B. D. (2016). Reporting of Positive Results in Randomized Controlled Trials of Mindfulness-Based Mental Health Interventions. PLOS ONE, 11(4), e0153220. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0153220
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated reporting bias in randomized controlled trials (RCTs) of mindfulness-based therapy. It found that 87% of 124 published trials reported at least one positive outcome in the abstract, and 88% concluded the therapy was effective, which was 1.6 times greater than expected based on a d=0.55 effect size.
