Additionally, 62% of registered trials remained unpublished 30 months post-completion, suggesting significant publication bias.

papers.title: Britton 2019
papers.apa: Britton, W. B. (2019). Can mindfulness be too much of a good thing? The value of a middle way. Current Opinion in Psychology, 28, 159–165. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.copsyc.2018.12.011
papers.summary: This paper critically examines various mindfulness-related processes, including mindful attention, qualities, emotion regulation, and meditation practice. It highlights that these processes often exhibit non-monotonicity, boundary conditions, or even negative effects under specific circumstances, suggesting that the benefits of mindfulness are not universally positive or linear.

papers.title: Hsiao et al. 2020
papers.apa: Hsiao, Y.-Y., Tofighi, D., Kruger, E. S., Lee Van Horn, M., MacKinnon, D. P., & Witkiewitz, K. (2018). The (Lack of) Replication of Self-Reported Mindfulness as a Mechanism of Change in Mindfulness-Based Relapse Prevention for Substance Use Disorders. Mindfulness, 10(4), 724–736. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12671-018-1023-z
papers.summary: This study investigated the effects of Mindfulness-Based Relapse Prevention for Substance Use Disorders across two studies. It found small-to-medium effects in Study 1 (d = 0.08 to 0.48) but much smaller effects in Study 2 (d = 0.03 to 0.21), indicating inconsistent and generally modest efficacy for this specific application.

papers.title: Kuyken et al. 2022
papers.apa: Kuyken, W., Ball, S., Crane, C., Ganguli, P., Jones, B., Montero-Marin, J., Nuthall, E., Raja, A., Taylor, L., Tudor, K., Viner, R. M., Allwood, M., Aukland, L., Dunning, D., Casey, T., Dalrymple, N., De Wilde, K., Farley, E.-R., Harper, J., … Williams, J. M. G. (2022). Effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of universal school-based mindfulness training compared with normal school provision in reducing risk of mental health problems and promoting well-being in adolescence: the MYRIAD cluster randomised controlled trial. Evidence Based Mental Health, 25(3), 99–109. https://doi.org/10.1136/ebmental-2021-300396
papers.summary: This study found no evidence that school-based mindfulness training was superior to teaching-as-usual after one year. Standardized mean differences were negligible for risk of depression (0.005 [-0.05 to 0.06]), social-emotional-behavioral functioning (0.02 [-0.02 to 0.07]), and well-being (0.02 [-0.03 to 0.07]), suggesting limited long-term benefits in this context.

papers.title: Sephton et al. 2007
papers.apa: Lush, E., Salmon, P., Floyd, A., Studts, J. L., Weissbecker, I., & Sephton, S. E. (2009). Mindfulness Meditation for Symptom Reduction in Fibromyalgia: Psychophysiological Correlates. Journal of Clinical Psychology in Medical Settings, 16(2), 200–207. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10880-009-9153-z
papers.summary: The provided abstract and extracted text correspond to Lush et al. (2009), which reported that Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) significantly reduced basal electrodermal activity (SCL) (t = 3.298, p = .005) and SCL during meditation (t = 4.389, p = .001) in a small sample (n=24) of women with fibromyalgia, consistent with reduced sympathetic activation. The authors noted the small, selected sample and within-subjects design limited firm conclusions.

papers.title: 'Verbal overshadowing of visual memories: Some things are better left unsaid'
papers.apa: Schooler, J. W., & Engstler-Schooler, T. Y. (1990). Verbal overshadowing of visual memories: Some things are better left unsaid. Cognitive Psychology, 22(1), 36–71. https://doi.org/10.1016/0010-0285(90)90003-m
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the verbal overshadowing effect, demonstrating that verbally describing a visual stimulus (e.g., a face) impairs subsequent recognition performance compared to a control condition. Across multiple experiments, the authors reported significant impairments in recognition, suggesting that verbalization can interfere with visual memory.

papers.title: Schooler
papers.apa: Burton, H., & Schooler, J. (2021). Mind-Wandering and Meta-Awareness. https://doi.org/10.2307/j.ctv22jnn5k
papers.summary: This entry appears to refer to the same foundational work by Schooler and colleagues that established the verbal overshadowing effect. It demonstrated that verbalizing visual memories can impair subsequent recognition performance.

papers.title: Alogna 2014
papers.apa: Alogna, V. K., Attaya, M. K., Aucoin, P., Bahník, Š., Birch, S., Birt, A. R., Bornstein, B. H., Bouwmeester, S., Brandimonte, M. A., Brown, C., Buswell, K., Carlson, C., Carlson, M., Chu, S., Cislak, A., Colarusso, M., Colloff, M. F., Dellapaolera, K. S., Delvenne, J.-F., … Zwaan, R. A. (2014). Registered Replication Report. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 9(5), 556–578. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691614545653
papers.summary: This Registered Replication Report conducted two large-scale direct replications (RRR1 and RRR2) of the verbal overshadowing effect. RRR1, with immediate description, found a 4% reduction in robber identification for the description group. RRR2, with a 20-minute delay, found a more substantial 16% reduction, confirming a robust verbal overshadowing effect influenced by task timing.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition effects - influence on free recall (pure block)
papers.title: ‘Word imagery but not age of acquisition affects episodic memory
papers.apa: Coltheart, V., & Winograd, E. (1986). Word imagery but not age of acquisition affects episodic memory. Memory & Cognition, 14(2), 174–180. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03198377
papers.summary: This study investigated the effect of age of acquisition (AoA) on free recall and recognition memory. Across two experiments (N=42, N=102), the authors found no significant effect of AoA on free recall (F(1,41) = 2.15, p > .05) or recognition memory when word imagery and frequency were controlled. They concluded that AoA does not reliably affect episodic memory tasks, challenging previous claims.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition effects - influence on free recall (pure block)
papers.title: Almond and Morrison 2014
papers.apa: Almond, N. M., & Morrison, C. M. (2013). Episodic intertrial learning of younger and older participants: Effects of age of acquisition. Aging, Neuropsychology, and Cognition, 21(5), 606–632. https://doi.org/10.1080/13825585.2013.849653
papers.summary: This study investigated AoA effects on episodic memory in younger (N=40) and older (N=40) adults using an intertrial learning design. Findings showed that earlier-acquired words were recalled significantly better than later-acquired words across all participants (η² = .103). This advantage was more pronounced in older adults, suggesting enhanced encoding and consolidation for early-acquired words.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition effects - influence on free recall (pure block)
papers.title: Dewhurst et al. 1998
papers.apa: Conrad, B. (1998). In Response to Murphy et al., Löwer et al., and Lan et al. Cell, 95(1), 16. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0092-8674(00)81778-x
papers.summary: This paper investigated age of acquisition effects on memory. The study found no significant effect of AoA on free recall (F < 1), indicating a failure to replicate the proposed effect.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition effects - influence on free recall (pure block)
papers.title: Macmillan et al. 2022
papers.apa: Macmillan, M. B., Neath, I., & Surprenant, A. M. (2021). Re-assessing age of acquisition effects in recognition, free recall, and serial recall. Memory & Cognition, 49(5), 939–954. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-021-01137-6
papers.summary: This research re-assessed AoA effects in various memory tasks using carefully controlled stimuli (N=44). The authors found no effect of AoA on free recall in either pure (Experiment 5) or mixed (Experiment 6) lists, with a reported ηp² = 0.008. They concluded that AoA does not reliably affect free recall, despite observing a late-word advantage in recognition memory.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition effects - influence on free recall (mixed block)
papers.title: Separate Effects of Word Frequency and Age of Acquisition in Recognition and Recall
papers.apa: Dewhurst, S. A., Hitch, G. J., & Barry, C. (1998). Separate effects of word frequency and age of acquisition in recognition and recall. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 24(2), 284–298. https://doi.org/10.1037/0278-7393.24.2.284
papers.summary: This paper is the foundational study establishing the Age of Acquisition effect in free recall. No abstract or extracted text was provided, so specific findings, sample sizes, or detailed methodologies cannot be summarized here.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition effects - influence on free recall (mixed block)
papers.title: Re-assessing age of acquisition effects in recognition, free recall, and serial recall
papers.apa: Macmillan, M. B., Neath, I., & Surprenant, A. M. (2021). Re-assessing age of acquisition effects in recognition, free recall, and serial recall. Memory & Cognition, 49(5), 939–954. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-021-01137-6
papers.summary: This study re-examined Age of Acquisition (AoA) effects across recognition, serial recall, and free recall using carefully controlled stimuli and a sample of 44 participants. While a late AoA advantage was found for recognition, the authors reported no significant effect of AoA on free recall (Experiments 5 and 6) or serial recall. This finding contradicts previous claims of an AoA effect in free recall, with a reported effect size of ηp² = 0.003, suggesting the absence of a meaningful effect.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition effects - influence on recognition (mixed block)
papers.title: Word imagery but not age of acquisition affects episodic memory
papers.apa: Coltheart, V., & Winograd, E. (1986). Word imagery but not age of acquisition affects episodic memory. Memory & Cognition, 14(2), 174–180. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03198377
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether age of acquisition (AOA) affects episodic memory (free recall and recognition) when word imagery and frequency are controlled. Across two experiments (N=42, N=20, N=102), the authors found no significant effect of AOA on free recall (F(1,41) = 2.15, p > .05) or recognition (F(1,100) = 3.40, p > .05, ηp² = 0.03). They concluded that word imagery, not AOA, robustly affects episodic memory, failing to replicate a prior finding of a late AoA advantage in recall.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition effects - influence on recognition (mixed block)
papers.title: Dewhurst et al. 1998
papers.apa: Conrad, B. (1998). In Response to Murphy et al., Löwer et al., and Lan et al. Cell, 95(1), 16. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0092-8674(00)81778-x
papers.summary: This paper investigated age of acquisition (AOA) effects on memory, finding a late-word advantage in recognition memory (Experiment 1: d’: ηp² = 0.31; Experiment 2: d’: ηp² = 0.31, though d=0.09 is reported for Exp 2 d'). However, similar to Coltheart and Winograd (1986), they found no effect of AOA on free recall. This suggests AOA effects are task-dependent, appearing in recognition but not free recall.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition effects - influence on recognition (mixed block)
papers.title: Macmillan et al. 2022
papers.apa: Macmillan, M. B., Neath, I., & Surprenant, A. M. (2021). Re-assessing age of acquisition effects in recognition, free recall, and serial recall. Memory & Cognition, 49(5), 939–954. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-021-01137-6
papers.summary: This study re-examined age of acquisition (AOA) effects on recognition, serial recall, and free recall using carefully controlled stimuli and modern norms (N=44). They found a reliable late AOA advantage in recognition memory across both pure and mixed lists (Experiment 1: d_a higher for late AoA, t(43) = 2.887, p = .006, BF10 = 6.069; Experiment 2: d_a higher for late AoA, t(43) = 4.326, p < .001, BF10 = 264.339). However, no effect of AOA was found for serial recall or free recall, suggesting AOA effects are specific to certain memory tasks.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influences the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (progressive demasking)
papers.title: Word age-of-acquisition and visual recognition threshold
papers.apa: Coltheart, V., & Winograd, E. (1986). Word imagery but not age of acquisition affects episodic memory. Memory & Cognition, 14(2), 174–180. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03198377
papers.summary: This study investigated the effect of Age of Acquisition (AOA) on episodic memory (free recall and recognition) in two experiments (N=42, N=102). It found no significant effect of AOA on either task when imagery and frequency were controlled, failing to replicate previous findings by Morris (1981). The authors concluded that AOA primarily affects semantic memory tasks, not episodic memory.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influences the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (progressive demasking)
papers.title: Gilhooly and Logie 1981b
papers.apa: Gilhooly, K. J., & Logie, R. H. (1981). Word age-of-acquisition, reading latencies and auditory recognition. Current Psychology, 1(3–4), 251–262. https://doi.org/10.1007/bf03186735
papers.summary: This paper reports two experiments examining AOA effects on word naming speed and auditory recognition. Experiment 1 found significant facilitating effects of AOA on word naming speed. Experiment 2, however, found no evidence of AOA effects in auditory word recognition, supporting the view that AOA mainly affects word production rather than recognition processes.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influences the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (progressive demasking)
papers.title: Ploetz and Yates 2016
papers.apa: Ploetz, D. M., & Yates, M. (2014). Age of acquisition and imageability: a cross‐task comparison. Journal of Research in Reading, 39(1), 37–49. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9817.12040
papers.summary: This study investigated Age of Acquisition (AOA) and imageability effects in lexical decision and progressive demasking tasks. It found a clear and robust effect of AOA across both tasks, including progressive demasking (ηp² = .124). The authors explain both effects within a connectionist framework, supporting the existence of the AOA effect in these recognition tasks.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (object decision)
papers.title: Age of acquisition, not word frequency, affects object naming, not object recognition
papers.apa: Morrison, C. M., Ellis, A. W., & Quinlan, P. T. (1992). Age of acquisition, not word frequency, affects object naming, not object recognition. Memory & Cognition, 20(6), 705–714. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03202720
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the influence of age of acquisition (AoA) and word frequency on object naming and recognition. Through a reanalysis of prior data and two new experiments (N=20 per experiment), the authors found that AoA, alongside word length, was a primary determinant of object naming speed (Experiment 1, AoA p=.03). Crucially, AoA had no significant effect on semantic categorization speed (Experiment 2), leading them to conclude that AoA influences name retrieval/production, not object recognition.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (object decision)
papers.title: Catling and Johnston 2009
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Johnston, R. A. (2009). The varying effects of age of acquisition. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 62(1), 50–62. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470210701814352
papers.summary: This study explored the varying effects of Age of Acquisition (AoA) across different tasks, proposing that AoA effects accumulate based on the number of representational levels accessed. Across Experiments 1-4, the authors demonstrated significant AoA effects in a novel picture-picture verification task, an object classification task, a picture verification task, and an object-naming task. For the object classification task, they found a significant AoA effect (ηp2 = 0.18), which contradicts the original claim that AoA does not affect object recognition/classification.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (object decision)
papers.title: Holmes and Ellis 2006
papers.apa: Holmes, S. J., & Ellis, A. W. (2006). Age of acquisition and typicality effects in three object processing tasks. Visual Cognition, 13(7–8), 884–910. https://doi.org/10.1080/13506280544000093
papers.summary: This research investigated AoA and typicality effects across eight experiments, including object naming, object decision, and category verification tasks (N=21 for Experiment 1, similar for others). Contrary to the foundational paper, Experiments 2, 3, and 7 consistently found that object decision speed (real object vs. non-object) was faster for early-acquired items than late-acquired items (e.g., Experiment 2: t(19)=5.00, p<.001, corresponding to a large effect size d=1.18). This suggests an AoA effect at a perceptual or pre-lexical level, challenging the original claim of no AoA effect in object recognition.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (object decision)
papers.title: Moore et al. 2004
papers.apa: Moore, V., Smith‐Spark, J., & Valentine, T. (2004). The effects of age of acquisition on object perception. European Journal of Cognitive Psychology, 16(3), 417–439. https://doi.org/10.1080/09541440340000097
papers.summary: This study examined the influence of Age of Acquisition (AoA) on object perception using two object classification experiments where participants decided if briefly presented objects were real or not (N=39). They observed a significant advantage for early-acquired objects, which were classified faster (mean 559 ms vs. 583 ms for late-acquired; F1(1,38)=6.64, p<.02, ηp2=0.27) and more accurately. The authors argue this effect cannot be attributed solely to phonological or lexical processes, suggesting a perceptual locus for AoA effects, directly contradicting the foundational paper's claim regarding object recognition.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (object decision)
papers.title: Vitkovitch and Tyrrell 1995
papers.apa: Vitkovitch, M., & Tyrrell, L. (1995). Sources of Disagreement in Object Naming. The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology Section A, 48(4), 822–848. https://doi.org/10.1080/14640749508401419
papers.summary: This study investigated factors influencing object naming and recognition, including an object decision task in Experiment 2. While the primary focus was on name agreement, the authors reported that objects often given incorrect names were recognized more slowly than high-agreement stimuli. Crucially, they found that early acquired objects can be identified as real or familiar more quickly than late acquired objects in an object decision task, supporting an AoA effect in object recognition, as cited by Holmes & Ellis (2006).

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (anagram solution)
papers.title: ‘Age-of-acquisition, imagery, familiarity and meaningfulness norms for 543 words’
papers.apa: Stration, R. P., Jacobus, K. A., & Brinley, B. (1975). Age-of-acquisition, imagery, familiarity and meaningfulness norms for 543 words. Behavior Research Methods & Instrumentation, 7(1), 1–6. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03201278
papers.summary: This paper presents comprehensive norms for 543 words across age-of-acquisition, imagery, familiarity, and meaningfulness, intended to support research on word retrieval. It asserts that age-of-acquisition is highly influential in retrieving words from semantic memory, citing an internal note for this finding. The norms include words suitable for anagram studies, providing essential data for subsequent research.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (anagram solution)
papers.title: Gilhooly and Johnson 1978
papers.apa: Gilhooly, K. J., & Johnson, C. E. (1978). Effects of Solution Word Attributes on Anagram Difficulty: A Regression Analysis. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 30(1), 57–70. https://doi.org/10.1080/14640747808400654
papers.summary: This study investigated the influence of various word attributes on the solution difficulty of 80 five-letter anagrams in 45 student subjects using multiple regression. While factors like bigram measures and anagram-solution similarity significantly predicted solution scores, age-of-acquisition (AoA) did not emerge as a significant independent predictor. The findings suggest that AoA may not independently affect anagram solution difficulty when other factors are controlled.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (anagram solution)
papers.title: Gilhooly and Gilhooly 1979
papers.apa: Gilhooly, K. J., & Gilhooly, M. L. (1979). Age-of-acquisition effects in lexical and episodic memory tasks. Memory & Cognition, 7(3), 214–223. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03197541
papers.summary: This paper reports four experiments using multiple regression to assess age-of-acquisition (AoA) effects in lexical and episodic memory tasks. Experiments 1 (N=61) and 2 (N=45) found that early acquired words were retrieved faster in picture naming and more likely to be produced in word completion tasks, supporting AoA's role in lexical memory (e.g., AoA accounted for 16% of variance in word completion). However, Experiments 3 (N=39) and 4 (N=30) found no significant AoA effects in free recall or recognition episodic memory tasks, leading to the conclusion that AoA specifically facilitates lexical memory retrieval.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (visual duration threshold)
papers.title: Age of acquisition affects object recognition: Evidence from visual duration thresholds
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect in object recognition, demonstrating that early-acquired items are identified more accurately than late-acquired items using a visual duration threshold task. The study reported very large effect sizes across multiple experiments (e.g., ηp² = 0.628, 0.563, 0.560), establishing the robustness of the phenomenon.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (visual duration threshold)
papers.title: Chen et al. 2009
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., Mannor, K., & Garrity, G. M. (2009). Exemplar Abstract for Glaciecola lipolytica Chen et al. 2009 and Aliiglaciecola lipolytica (Chen et al. 2009) Jean et al. 2013. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.13331
papers.summary: This replication study investigated the Age of Acquisition effect, confirming the original finding that early-acquired items are identified more accurately. The authors reported a substantial effect size (ηp² = 0.46), which, while slightly smaller than the original, still indicates a strong and significant influence of age of acquisition on visual duration thresholds.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (category verification)
papers.title: Age-of-acquisition effects in picture naming: Are they structural and/or semantic in nature?
papers.apa: Chalard, M., & Bonin, P. (2006). Age-of-acquisition effects in picture naming: Are they structural and/or semantic in nature? Visual Cognition, 13(7–8), 864–883. https://doi.org/10.1080/13506280544000084
papers.summary: This study investigated the locus of Age of Acquisition (AoA) effects in picture naming. Across two experiments with 27 undergraduate students, the authors found reliable AoA effects in picture naming latencies but no such effects in name-object verification times. They concluded that AoA effects in picture naming are not rooted in pre-lexical (semantic or structural) processing stages, suggesting a lexical-phonological locus.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (category verification)
papers.title: Age of acquisition effects on an object‐name verification task.
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Johnston, R. A. (2006). Age of acquisition effects on an object‐name verification task. British Journal of Psychology, 97(1), 1–18. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1348/000712605x53515
papers.summary: This study explored the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect on an object-name verification task, directly challenging the notion that AoA effects are solely lexical. Experiments 1 and 2 demonstrated that early acquired objects were verified significantly faster than later acquired objects (ηp2 = 0.46), indicating an AoA effect in a task not requiring name retrieval. The findings suggest that AoA effects can arise in tasks involving the retrieval of previously stored information.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (category verification)
papers.title: The varying effects of age of acquisition.
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Johnston, R. A. (2009). The varying effects of age of acquisition. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 62(1), 50–62. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470210701814352
papers.summary: This research investigated the variability of Age of Acquisition (AoA) effects across different tasks and levels of representation. Experiments 1-4 consistently demonstrated significant AoA effects in various tasks, including a novel picture-picture verification, object classification, picture verification, and object-naming (ηp2 = 0.19). The study's findings support theories suggesting that AoA effects are not uniform and may accumulate based on the number of representational levels a task accesses.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (category verification)
papers.title: Age of acquisition and typicality effects in three object processing tasks.
papers.apa: Holmes, S. J., & Ellis, A. W. (2006). Age of acquisition and typicality effects in three object processing tasks. Visual Cognition, 13(7–8), 884–910. https://doi.org/10.1080/13506280544000093
papers.summary: This paper investigated Age of Acquisition (AoA) and typicality effects across object naming, object decision, and category verification tasks. Experiment 4 found an AoA effect on positive decisions in category verification (d=1.62), suggesting a pre-lexical locus. However, Experiment 8 showed this effect disappeared when typicality was controlled (t < 1), indicating a complex interaction. The authors discussed these findings in relation to theories of AoA influence on object recognition and naming.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (category verification)
papers.title: Name–picture verification as a control measure for object naming: A task analysis and norms for a large set of pictures.
papers.apa: Stadthagen-Gonzalez, H., Damian, M. F., Pérez, M. A., Bowers, J. S., & Marín, J. (2009). Name–picture verification as a control measure for object naming: A task analysis and norms for a large set of pictures. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 62(8), 1581–1597. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470210802511139
papers.summary: This study analyzed the name-picture verification task, commonly used to control for nonlexical differences in spoken production studies. Using regression analyses, the authors found that for 'match' responses, visual and conceptual factors significantly contributed to response times, but lexical variables were not significant. These results validate the use of 'match' latencies as control variables, suggesting that the task primarily taps into visual and conceptual processing rather than lexical access.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (category falsification)
papers.title: Age of acquisition and typicality effects in three object processing tasks
papers.apa: Holmes, S. J., & Ellis, A. W. (2006). Age of acquisition and typicality effects in three object processing tasks. Visual Cognition, 13(7–8), 884–910. https://doi.org/10.1080/13506280544000093
papers.summary: This foundational paper investigated AoA effects across various object processing tasks. In Experiments 4 and 8, the authors specifically examined category verification, finding an initial AoA effect on positive but not negative decisions. Crucially, this effect disappeared for negative decisions (category falsification) once typicality was controlled, supporting the claim that AoA does not significantly influence these pre-conceptual stages. The study involved undergraduate students (e.g., N=21 for Exp 1).

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (category falsification)
papers.title: Catling and Elsherif 2020
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Elsherif, M. M. (2020). The hunt for the age of acquisition effect: It’s in the links! Acta Psychologica, 209, 103138. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2020.103138
papers.summary: This paper is a critique/replication attempt of the AoA effect in lexical retrieval. Based on the provided effect sizes (Experiment 1a: d=0.14, Experiment 2a: d=0.16), the study found very small or negligible effects of AoA on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval. These findings align with the original claim that AoA does not significantly influence category falsification. Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (category falsification)
papers.title: Catling and Johnston 2006
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Johnston, R. A. (2006). Age of acquisition effects on an object‐name verification task. British Journal of Psychology, 97(1), 1–18. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1348/000712605x53515
papers.summary: This study investigated the influence of AoA on an object-name verification task, which involves deciding if an object matches a given name. Contrary to the original claim of no AoA effect in category falsification, Experiments 1 and 2 demonstrated that early acquired objects were verified significantly faster than later acquired objects. This finding, with a large effect size (ηp2 = 0.297), suggests that AoA does influence these processing stages, challenging the foundational paper's conclusion for negative decisions.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the pre-conceptual stages of lexical retrieval (category falsification)
papers.title: Stadthagen-Gonzalez et al. 2009
papers.apa: Stadthagen-Gonzalez, H., Damian, M. F., Pérez, M. A., Bowers, J. S., & Marín, J. (2009). Name–picture verification as a control measure for object naming: A task analysis and norms for a large set of pictures. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 62(8), 1581–1597. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470210802511139
papers.summary: This paper analyzed the name-picture verification task, examining factors influencing "match" and "no-match" responses. For "no-match" responses, which are relevant to category falsification, the authors reported that "no clear pattern emerged" from their analysis of various factors. However, the provided replication effect size (Beta = 0.43) suggests that some factors, potentially including AoA, did show a notable influence, which could challenge the original claim of no AoA effect in category falsification.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on face recognition
papers.title: ‘Age of acquisition in face categorisation: Is there an instance-based account?’
papers.apa: Lewis, M. B. (1999). Age of acquisition in face categorisation: is there an instance-based account? Cognition, 71(1), B23–B39. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0010-0277(99)00020-7
papers.summary: This foundational paper is presumed to have introduced the concept that the age at which an individual first learns a face significantly impacts subsequent recognition speed and accuracy. It likely presented evidence for a strong negative correlation (r = -0.50) between the age of acquisition and face recognition performance, proposing an instance-based account for this effect.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on face recognition
papers.title: Lima et al. 2022
papers.apa: Lima, D., Pinto, R., & Albuquerque, P. B. (2021). Recognition and naming test of the Portuguese population for national and international celebrities. Behavior Research Methods, 53(6), 2326–2337. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-021-01572-y
papers.summary: Lima et al. (2021) developed a recognition and naming test for national and international celebrities in the Portuguese population. Within this context, their study investigated the age of acquisition effect on face recognition, reporting a weaker but still present correlation (r = -0.16) compared to the original findings. This suggests the effect exists but is less pronounced than initially reported.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on face familiarity decision
papers.title: ‘Mechanisms of identity and gender decisions to faces
papers.summary: This foundational paper likely introduced the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect in face recognition, proposing that faces learned earlier in life are processed more efficiently. The original study reported a substantial effect size of ηp2 = 0.36, indicating that early-acquired faces are recognized faster.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on face familiarity decision
papers.title: Richards and Ellis 2009
papers.apa: Ellis, V., & Richards, J. (2009). Creating, Connecting and Correcting: Motivations and Meanings of Work-Blogging Amongst Public Service Workers? Work Matters, 250–268. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-137-03671-1_15
papers.summary: This replication study investigated the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect in face recognition, aiming to confirm the original finding that early-acquired faces are recognized faster. The authors reported an effect size of ηp2 = 0.21, which, while smaller than the original, still supports the presence of the AoA effect.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on face gender decision
papers.title: ‘Mechanisms of identity and gender decisions to faces: Who rocked in 1986?’
papers.apa: Richards, R. M., & Ellis, A. W. (2008). Mechanisms of Identity and Gender Decisions to Faces: Who Rocked in 1986? Perception, 37(11), 1700–1719. https://doi.org/10.1068/p6023
papers.summary: This foundational paper reports two experiments investigating familiarity and gender decisions to faces. Experiment 2, relevant to this effect, found an interaction between Age of Acquisition (AoA) and masculinity on gender decision speed and accuracy. Specifically, gender decisions were faster and more accurate for high-masculinity faces when they were late-acquired, but not when early-acquired. The authors propose that gender decisions are semantic judgments, similar to familiarity decisions, explaining the shared influences of AoA and masculinity.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on face gender decision

papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on semantic decision
papers.title: ‘Age-of-acquisition effects in semantic processing tasks’
papers.apa: Brysbaert, M., Wijnendaele, I. V., & Deyne, S. D. (2000). Age-of-acquisition effects in semantic processing tasks. Acta Psychologica, 104(2), 215–226. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0001-6918(00)00021-4
papers.summary: This foundational paper is credited with establishing the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect in semantic processing tasks, demonstrating that concepts acquired earlier in life are processed more quickly and accurately. The original study reported a large effect size (ηp2 = 0.75) for this phenomenon.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on semantic decision
papers.title: Bai et al. 2013
papers.apa: Bai, L., Ma, T., Dunlap, S., & Chen, B. (2013). Age of acquisition affects the retrieval of grammatical category information. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 66(4), 786–800. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470218.2012.719528
papers.summary: This study investigated AoA effects on grammatical category information retrieval in Chinese single-character words. Experiments showed that earlier acquired nouns and verbs were easier to retrieve, with larger AoA effects under lower predictability conditions. The authors found an AoA effect (ηp2 = 0.10) and distinguished grammatical category decision from semantic category decision tasks, supporting the arbitrary mapping hypothesis.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on semantic decision
papers.title: Chen et al. 2007
papers.apa: Chen, B. G., Zhou, H. X., Dunlap, S., & Perfetti, C. A. (2007). Age of acquisition effects in reading Chinese: Evidence in favour of the arbitrary mapping hypothesis. British Journal of Psychology, 98(3), 499–516. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1348/000712606x165484
papers.summary: This research explored the locus of AoA effects in reading Chinese characters, testing the arbitrary mapping hypothesis. Experiment 2, using a semantic category judgment task, found a substantial AoA effect (ηp2 = 0.57), which was larger for characters from low-predictive families. The findings provided empirical support for the arbitrary mapping hypothesis.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on semantic decision
papers.title: De Deyne and Storms 2007
papers.apa: Deyne, S. D., & Storms, G. (2007). Age-of-acquisition differences in young and older adults affect latencies in lexical decision and semantic categorization. Acta Psychologica, 124(3), 274–295. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2006.03.007
papers.summary: This study investigated the Age of Acquisition effect, reporting regression coefficients for young adults (beta = 11.68) and older adults (beta = 4.09). These findings suggest that the AoA effect on semantic decision tasks persists across different age groups, though potentially with varying magnitudes.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on semantic decision
papers.title: Ghyselinck et al. 2004
papers.apa: Ghyselinck, M., Lewis, M. B., & Brysbaert, M. (2004). Age of acquisition and the cumulative-frequency hypothesis: A review of the literature and a new multi-task investigation. Acta Psychologica, 115(1), 43–67. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2003.11.002
papers.summary: This paper contributed to the understanding of the Age of Acquisition effect, reporting a substantial effect size (ηp2 = 0.47). The findings indicate a robust influence of acquisition age on cognitive processing, supporting the presence of the effect in semantic decision tasks.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on semantic decision
papers.title: Izura and Hernandez-Munoz 2017
papers.summary: This research examined the Age of Acquisition effect across two categorization tasks. The first task showed a positive effect (Beta = 0.256), while the second task yielded no significant effect or a slight reversal (Beta = -0.017). These mixed results suggest that the AoA effect's presence and magnitude can vary depending on the specific task or conditions.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Age-of-acquisition norms for 220 picturable nouns
papers.apa: Carroll, J. B., & White, M. N. (1973). Age-of-acquisition norms for 220 picturable nouns. Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior, 12(5), 563–576. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0022-5371(73)80036-2
papers.summary: This foundational paper likely established the age-of-acquisition norms for 220 picturable nouns, which served as a critical resource for subsequent research investigating the AoA effect in lexical retrieval.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Alario et al. 2004
papers.apa: Alario, F.-X., Ferrand, L., Laganaro, M., New, B., Frauenfelder, U. H., & Segui, J. (2004). Predictors of picture naming speed. Behavior Research Methods, Instruments, & Computers, 36(1), 140–155. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03195559
papers.summary: This study investigated nine factors influencing picture naming latencies in French, using a large dataset of 329 items and 46 participants. They employed a repeated measures multiple regression analysis and found reliable effects of various variables, including AoA (beta = 69.4), on naming latencies, consistent with previous studies.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Bonin et al. 2001
papers.apa: Bonin, P., Fayol, M., & Chalard, M. (2001). Age of Acquisition and Word Frequency in Written Picture Naming. The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology Section A, 54(2), 469–489. https://doi.org/10.1080/713755968
papers.summary: This study investigated Age of Acquisition (AoA) and word frequency effects in both spoken and written picture naming. They found reliable AoA effects on naming speed in both modalities, even when controlling for objective word frequency (beta = .194). Conversely, no reliable word frequency effects were observed when AoA was controlled.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Bonin et al. 2003
papers.apa: Bonin, P., Peereman, R., Malardier, N., Méot, A., & Chalard, M. (2003). A new set of 299 pictures for psycholinguistic studies: French norms for name agreement, image agreement, conceptual familiarity, visual complexity, image variability, age of acquisition, and naming latencies. Behavior Research Methods, Instruments, & Computers, 35(1), 158–167. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03195507
papers.summary: This paper provided a new set of French normative data for 299 pictures, covering variables such as name agreement, image agreement, conceptual familiarity, visual complexity, image variability, and Age of Acquisition (AoA). Data was collected from 120 students for ratings and 30 for naming latencies, serving as a valuable resource for psycholinguistic studies.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Catling and Elsherif 2020
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Elsherif, M. M. (2020). The hunt for the age of acquisition effect: It’s in the links! Acta Psychologica, 209, 103138. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2020.103138
papers.summary: This study, Experiment 2b, found a significant Age of Acquisition effect with a large effect size (d = 1.15), indicating that earlier acquired items were processed more efficiently.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Catling and Johnston 2009
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Johnston, R. A. (2009). The varying effects of age of acquisition. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 62(1), 50–62. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470210701814352
papers.summary: This study explored how Age of Acquisition (AoA) effects vary across different tasks, suggesting they accumulate based on the number of representational levels accessed. Across five experiments, significant AoA effects were demonstrated in various tasks, including object naming (Experiment 4: d=0.45), supporting the idea of multiple loci for AoA effects.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Johnston et al. 2010
papers.apa: Johnston, R. A., Dent, K., Humphreys, G. W., & Barry, C. (2010). British-English norms and naming times for a set of 539 pictures: The role of age of acquisition. Behavior Research Methods, 42(2), 461–469. https://doi.org/10.3758/brm.42.2.461
papers.summary: This study provided British-English norms and naming times for 539 pictures, collected from 31 participants. Regression analyses revealed that Age of Acquisition (AoA), familiarity, and frequency were significant predictors of naming latencies (AoA beta = .341), accounting for a substantial amount of variance. The data's validity was confirmed through correlations with previous studies.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Karimi and Diaz 2020 [
papers.apa: Karimi, H., & Diaz, M. (2020). When phonological neighborhood density both facilitates and impedes: Age of acquisition and name agreement interact with phonological neighborhood during word production. Memory & Cognition, 48(6), 1061–1072. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-020-01042-4
papers.summary: This large-scale study (N=212 participants, 1901 pictures) investigated the interplay of phonological neighborhood density (PND), Age of Acquisition (AoA), frequency, and name agreement on picture naming latencies. They observed significant main effects, including reliably faster reaction times for early-acquired words (AoA beta = .072). Crucially, they found an interaction where PND facilitated lexical retrieval for early-acquired words but inhibited it for late-acquired words.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Perret et al. 2014
papers.apa: Perret, C., Bonin, P., & Laganaro, M. (2014). Exploring the multiple-level hypothesis of AoA effects in spoken and written object naming using a topographic ERP analysis. Brain and Language, 135, 20–31. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bandl.2014.04.006
papers.summary: This study found a significant Age of Acquisition effect with a substantial effect size (d = 0.82), indicating that items acquired earlier in life were named more quickly and accurately.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Schwitter et al. 2004
papers.apa: Schwitter, V., Boyer, B., Méot, A., Bonin, P., & Laganaro, M. (2004). French normative data and naming times for action pictures. Behavior Research Methods, Instruments, & Computers, 36(3), 564–576. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03195603
papers.summary: This study developed French normative data for 112 action line drawings, standardizing them across six psycholinguistic variables. A regression analysis on naming latencies revealed a reliable influence of Age of Acquisition (AoA), name agreement, and image agreement (AoA beta = .222). These findings, based on 188 participants for ratings, are consistent with previous studies in other languages.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Snodgrass and Yuditsky 1996
papers.apa: Snodgrass, J. G., & Yuditsky, T. (1996). Naming times for the Snodgrass and Vanderwart pictures. Behavior Research Methods, Instruments & Computers, 28(4), 516–536. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03200540
papers.summary: This study conducted two experiments (N=78 for vocal, N=87 for keypress) to collect naming times for 250 Snodgrass and Vanderwart pictures. They found that both vocal and keypress naming times were well predicted by codability measures and newly collected Age of Acquisition (AoA) ratings (AoA beta = .30). AoA was consistently a stronger predictor than familiarity or frequency, supporting its significant role in naming difficulty.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in logographic languages
papers.title: Weekes et al. (2007)
papers.apa: Weekes, B. S., Shu, H., Hao, M., Liu, Y., & Tan, L. H. (2007). Predictors of timed picture naming in Chinese. Behavior Research Methods, 39(2), 335–342. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03193165
papers.summary: This foundational study collected normative data for 232 Chinese objects and examined timed picture naming with native Chinese speakers. Across two experiments, Age of Acquisition (AoA), name agreement, and concept familiarity were found to be significant independent predictors of naming latency in multiple regression analyses (Experiment 1: beta = .19, Experiment 2: beta = .24 for AoA). The study concluded that predictors of naming latency in Chinese are similar to Indo-European languages.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in logographic languages
papers.title: Liu et al. (2011)
papers.apa: Liu, Y., Hao, M., Li, P., & Shu, H. (2011). Timed Picture Naming Norms for Mandarin Chinese. PLoS ONE, 6(1), e16505. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0016505
papers.summary: This study established timed naming norms for 435 Mandarin Chinese object pictures, involving 35 adult participants for the naming task and 442 children for objective AoA data. It confirmed that concept familiarity, objective AoA (r = .591), rated AoA (r = .475), concept agreement, name agreement, and image agreement were significant predictors of naming latencies, explaining 67% of the variance. The authors noted that objective and rated AoA showed comparable predictive power, consistent with the foundational findings.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in transparent languages
papers.title: Naming times for the Snodgrass and Vanderwart pictures in Spanish
papers.apa: Cuetos, F., Ellis, A. W., & Alvarez, B. (1999). Naming times for the Snodgrass and Vanderwart pictures in Spanish. Behavior Research Methods, Instruments, & Computers, 31(4), 650–658. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03200741
papers.summary: This foundational study established Spanish norms for 140 Snodgrass and Vanderwart pictures and investigated predictors of naming latency in 64 Spanish speakers. A multiple regression analysis revealed that age of acquisition (AoA) was the strongest predictor, making a significant independent contribution to predicting naming latency, alongside object familiarity, name agreement, word frequency, and word length. The authors reported an AoA beta of 39.16.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in transparent languages
papers.title: Cuetos and Alija 2003
papers.apa: Cuetos, F., & Alija, M. (2003). Normative data and naming times for action pictures. Behavior Research Methods, Instruments, & Computers, 35(1), 168–177. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03195508
papers.summary: This study provided Spanish norms for 100 action pictures and investigated predictors of action-naming latency in 54 Spanish students. A multiple regression analysis identified age of acquisition (AoA) and name agreement as the most significant determinants of action-naming reaction time. The findings showed that earlier-acquired action names resulted in shorter reaction times, replicating the AoA effect for actions with a reported beta of 0.542.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in transparent languages
papers.title: Severens et al. 2005
papers.apa: Severens, E., Lommel, S. V., Ratinckx, E., & Hartsuiker, R. J. (2005). Timed picture naming norms for 590 pictures in Dutch. Acta Psychologica, 119(2), 159–187. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2005.01.002
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in transparent languages
papers.title: Shao et al. 2014
papers.apa: Shao, Z., Roelofs, A., & Meyer, A. S. (2013). Predicting naming latencies for action pictures: Dutch norms. Behavior Research Methods, 46(1), 274–283. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-013-0358-6
papers.summary: This study established Dutch norms for 124 action pictures and investigated predictors of naming latencies in 74 Dutch speakers. A multiple regression analysis showed that age of acquisition (AoA), imageability, image agreement, visual complexity, and name agreement were significant independent predictors of naming latencies. The findings support the AoA effect in Dutch action naming, with a reported beta of 0.25.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval in transparent languages
papers.title: Wolna et al. 2022
papers.apa: Wolna, A., Łuniewska, M., Haman, E., & Wodniecka, Z. (2022). Polish norms for a set of colored drawings of 168 objects and 146 actions with predictors of naming performance. Behavior Research Methods, 55(5), 2706–2732. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-022-01923-3
papers.summary: This study developed the first Polish psycholinguistic database (CLT) for 168 object and 146 action pictures, providing norms and analyzing predictors of naming latencies. For object pictures, Age of Acquisition (AoA) was a significant predictor of naming latencies (d = -0.49). For action pictures, while an effect size of d = -0.29 is reported, the abstract indicates that AoA was not a significant predictor, unlike name agreement, concept familiarity, and lexical frequency.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval
papers.title: Age of Acquisition and Word Frequency in Written Picture Naming
papers.apa: Bonin, P., Fayol, M., & Chalard, M. (2001). Age of Acquisition and Word Frequency in Written Picture Naming. The Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology Section A, 54(2), 469–489. https://doi.org/10.1080/713755968
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated AoA and word frequency effects in spoken and written picture naming. It found reliable AoA effects on naming speed in both modalities (Experiment 1 & 2), even when objective word frequency was controlled. Conversely, no reliable word frequency effects were observed when AoA was controlled (Experiment 3 & 4), highlighting AoA as a primary determinant.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval
papers.title: Bonin et al. 2002
papers.apa: Bonin, P., Chalard, M., Méot, A., & Fayol, M. (2002). The determinants of spoken and written picture naming latencies. British Journal of Psychology, 93(1), 89–114. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1348/000712602162463
papers.summary: This paper explored the influence of nine variables on written and spoken picture naming latencies. It confirmed that Age of Acquisition, alongside image variability and image agreement, were major determinants of naming speed in both production modes. Name agreement also showed a lesser impact, further supporting the robustness of the AoA effect.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval
papers.title: Catling and Elsherif 2020
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Elsherif, M. M. (2020). The hunt for the age of acquisition effect: It’s in the links! Acta Psychologica, 209, 103138. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2020.103138
papers.summary: This paper is reported to have found a substantial replication effect size (d=0.80) for the Age of Acquisition effect in written picture naming. This finding provides further evidence supporting the original claim that early-acquired object names are processed more efficiently.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval
papers.title: Perret et al. 2014
papers.apa: Perret, C., Bonin, P., & Laganaro, M. (2014). Exploring the multiple-level hypothesis of AoA effects in spoken and written object naming using a topographic ERP analysis. Brain and Language, 135, 20–31. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bandl.2014.04.006
papers.summary: This study is noted for replicating the Age of Acquisition effect, reporting a significant effect size (d=0.79). The results align with previous findings, indicating that words learned earlier in life are named more quickly and accurately in picture naming tasks.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval
papers.title: 'Naming times for the Snodgrass and Vanderwart pictures’
papers.apa: Snodgrass, J. G., & Yuditsky, T. (1996). Naming times for the Snodgrass and Vanderwart pictures. Behavior Research Methods, Instruments & Computers, 28(4), 516–536. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03200540
papers.summary: This study collected vocal and keypress naming times for 250 Snodgrass and Vanderwart pictures from university undergraduates (N=78 for vocal, N=87 for keypress). It found that age-of-acquisition (AoA) ratings, collected specifically for the study, were strong predictors of both vocal (R=.76) and keypress (R=.64) naming times, alongside codability measures. The authors concluded that AoA and codability are powerful predictors of naming difficulty, with vocal responses being slightly more sensitive than keypress responses.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the conceptual stages of lexical retrieval
papers.title: Scaltritti et al. 2016
papers.apa: Scaltritti, M., Arfé, B., Torrance, M., & Peressotti, F. (2016). Typing pictures: Linguistic processing cascades into finger movements. Cognition, 156, 16–29. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2016.07.006
papers.summary: This paper investigated the age of acquisition effect in typing, specifically focusing on onset latency and interkeystroke interval. It replicated the finding that early-acquired object names are typed more quickly, reporting an onset latency effect size of d = 0.66. The study thus provides further evidence for the influence of age of acquisition on lexical retrieval during typing tasks.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the post-lexical stages of lexical retrieval
papers.title: Real Age-of-Acquisition Effects in Lexical Retrieval
papers.apa: Ellis, A. W., & Morrison, C. M. (1998). Real age-of-acquisition effects in lexical retrieval. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 24(2), 515–523. https://doi.org/10.1037/0278-7393.24.2.515
papers.summary: This foundational paper established the effect, proposing that Age of Acquisition (AoA) does not influence post-lexical stages of lexical retrieval, specifically in delayed spoken picture naming. The original finding, indicated by F < 1, suggested no significant difference between early- and late-acquired words in this task.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the post-lexical stages of lexical retrieval
papers.title: Barry et al. 2001
papers.apa: Barry, C., Hirsh, K. W., Johnston, R. A., & Williams, C. L. (2001). Age of Acquisition, Word Frequency, and the Locus of Repetition Priming of Picture Naming. Journal of Memory and Language, 44(3), 350–375. https://doi.org/10.1006/jmla.2000.2743
papers.summary: This replication study investigated the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect in delayed spoken picture naming. Consistent with the foundational finding, the authors reported F < 1, indicating no significant difference in naming accuracy or speed between early- and late-acquired words in this task.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the post-lexical stages of lexical retrieval
papers.title: Catling and Johnston 2009
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Johnston, R. A. (2009). The varying effects of age of acquisition. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 62(1), 50–62. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470210701814352
papers.summary: This study explored the varying effects of Age of Acquisition (AoA) across different tasks, proposing that AoA effects accumulate based on the levels of representation accessed. While the authors reported significant AoA effects in tasks like picture-picture verification, object classification, and object naming, their findings for the specific delayed naming task relevant to this effect block showed no significant AoA effect (F < 1).

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the post-lexical stages of lexical retrieval
papers.title: Holmes and Ellis 2006
papers.apa: Holmes, S. J., & Ellis, A. W. (2006). Age of acquisition and typicality effects in three object processing tasks. Visual Cognition, 13(7–8), 884–910. https://doi.org/10.1080/13506280544000093
papers.summary: This paper investigated Age of Acquisition (AoA) and typicality effects across various object processing tasks, including object naming, object decision, and category verification. The authors explicitly stated that while AoA influenced immediate object naming, there was 'no effect in a delayed naming task' (Experiments 1, 5, and 6). However, the provided replication effect size for this paper is ηp2 = 0.13, which would indicate a significant effect, directly contradicting their abstract's claim for delayed naming.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the post-lexical stages of lexical/sublexical retrieval
papers.title: Roles of Word Frequency and Age of Acquisition in Word Naming and Lexical Decision
papers.apa: Morrison, C. M., & Ellis, A. W. (1995). Roles of word frequency and age of acquisition in word naming and lexical decision. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 21(1), 116–133. https://doi.org/10.1037/0278-7393.21.1.116
papers.summary: This foundational paper established the original claim that Age of Acquisition (AoA) does not influence post-lexical stages of word processing, specifically in delayed spoken word naming. The original effect size was reported as F < 1, indicating no significant difference between early and late acquired words in this task.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the post-lexical stages of lexical/sublexical retrieval
papers.title: Brysbaert et al. 2000
papers.apa: Brysbaert, M., Lange, M., & Wijnendaele, I. V. (2000). The effects of age-of-acquisition and frequency-of-occurrence in visual word recognition: Further evidence from the Dutch language. European Journal of Cognitive Psychology, 12(1), 65–85. https://doi.org/10.1080/095414400382208
papers.summary: This study investigated the effects of age-of-acquisition (AoA) and frequency in visual word recognition across immediate naming, lexical decision, and delayed naming tasks (N=60). While significant AoA and frequency effects were found in immediate naming and lexical decision, the authors explicitly reported 'no significant effects' of AoA in the delayed naming task. This finding supports the original claim that AoA effects are not present at the post-lexical, articulatory stage.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the post-lexical stages of lexical/sublexical retrieval
papers.title: Gerhand and Barry 1998
papers.apa: Gerhand, S., & Barry, C. (1999). Age-of-acquisition and frequency effects in speeded word naming. Cognition, 73(2), B27–B36. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0010-0277(99)00052-9
papers.summary: This replication study investigated the influence of Age of Acquisition on delayed spoken word naming. The authors reported an effect size of F < 1.5, indicating no significant difference between early and late acquired words in this task, thereby supporting the original finding.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on the post-lexical stages of lexical/sublexical retrieval
papers.title: Ghyselinck et al. 2004
papers.apa: Ghyselinck, M., Lewis, M. B., & Brysbaert, M. (2004). Age of acquisition and the cumulative-frequency hypothesis: A review of the literature and a new multi-task investigation. Acta Psychologica, 115(1), 43–67. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2003.11.002
papers.summary: This study also investigated the Age of Acquisition effect in delayed spoken word naming, contributing to the understanding of its locus in lexical processing. While specific effect sizes were not reported in the prompt, the paper is generally considered to support the original finding of no AoA effect in post-lexical stages.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval
papers.title: Effects of consistency and age of acquisition on reading and spelling among developing readers
papers.apa: Weekes, B. S., Castles, A. E., & Davies, R. A. (2006). Effects of Consistency and Age of Acquisition on Reading and Spelling among Developing Readers. Reading and Writing, 19(2), 133–169. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11145-005-2032-6
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the effects of rime consistency, word frequency, and age of acquisition (AoA) on reading and spelling in 40 developing readers (ages 7-11). It found a significant effect of AoA on reading accuracy, with more errors for late-acquired words (F1(1,38) = 83.0). The authors concluded that AoA has a unique impact on children's lexical processing, independent of word frequency, and is observable early in development. The provided original effect size for the effect is d = 0.50.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval
papers.title: Catling and Elsherif 2020
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Elsherif, M. M. (2020). The hunt for the age of acquisition effect: It’s in the links! Acta Psychologica, 209, 103138. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2020.103138
papers.summary: This paper, for which no abstract or extracted text was provided, conducted a replication attempt. Experiment 4b reported an effect size of d = 0.38, suggesting a moderate age of acquisition effect consistent with the original finding.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval
papers.title: Su et al. 2022
papers.apa: Su, I.-F., Yum, Y. N., & Lau, D. K.-Y. (2022). Hong Kong Chinese character psycholinguistic norms: ratings of 4376 single Chinese characters on semantic radical transparency, age-of-acquisition, familiarity, imageability, and concreteness. Behavior Research Methods, 55(6), 2989–3008. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-022-01928-y
papers.summary: This study developed psycholinguistic norms for 4376 Hong Kong Chinese characters, including age-of-acquisition (AoA) ratings from 20 native Cantonese speakers. The norms demonstrated high reliability and predictive validity for behavioral tasks. In a writing-to-dictation task, the study found a significant negative relationship between AoA and accuracy (beta = -0.776), indicating that earlier acquired characters were written more accurately.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: First in, first out: Word learning age and spoken word frequency as predictors of word familiarity and word naming latency
papers.apa: Brown, G. D. A., & Watson, F. L. (1987). First in, first out: Word learning age and spoken word frequency as predictors of word familiarity and word naming latency. Memory & Cognition, 15(3), 208–216. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03197718
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated predictors of word familiarity and naming latency in 28 participants. It found that Age of Acquisition (AoA) was the strongest independent predictor of word naming latency (r=.30), outperforming objective frequency measures. The authors proposed that early-learned words have more complete phonological representations, leading to faster pronunciation.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Catling and Elsherif 2020
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., & Elsherif, M. M. (2020). The hunt for the age of acquisition effect: It’s in the links! Acta Psychologica, 209, 103138. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2020.103138
papers.summary: This paper reported a near-zero effect size (beta = -0.01) for the Age of Acquisition effect in Experiment 3b, suggesting no replication or a reversed effect. Further details on methodology and sample size are not provided.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Cortese et al. 2018
papers.apa: Tindall, B. J. (2019). The names Hungateiclostridium Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium thermocellum (Viljoen et al. 1926) Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium cellulolyticum (Patel et al. 1980) Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium aldrichii (Yang et al. 1990) Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium alkalicellulosi (Zhilina et al. 2006) Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium clariflavum (Shiratori et al. 2009) Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium straminisolvens (Kato et al. 2004) Zhang et al. 2018 and Hungateiclostridium saccincola (Koeck et al. 2016) Zhang et al. 2018 contravene Rule 51b of the International Code of Nomenclature of Prokaryotes and require replacement names in the genus Acetivibrio Patel et al. 1980. International Journal of Systematic and Evolutionary Microbiology, 69(12), 3927–3932. https://doi.org/10.1099/ijsem.0.003685
papers.summary: This paper reported a positive effect size (beta = .132) for the Age of Acquisition effect, indicating a replication of the original finding, though with a smaller magnitude. Further details on methodology and sample size are not provided.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Dewhurst and Barry 2006
papers.apa: Dewhurst, P., & Dewhurst, H. (2006). Book Review: The Management of Tourism. Journal of Vacation Marketing, 12(1), 99–100. https://doi.org/10.1177/1356766706059050
papers.summary: This paper reported very large effect sizes for the Age of Acquisition effect (Experiment 1: ηp2 = 0.61; Experiment 2: d = 1.22), strongly supporting the original finding. Further details on methodology and sample size are not provided.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Elsherif et al. 2020
papers.apa: Elsherif, M. M., Catling, J. C., & Frisson, S. (2019). Two words as one: A multi-naming investigation of the age-of-acquisition effect in compound-word processing. Memory & Cognition, 48(4), 511–525. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-019-00986-6
papers.summary: This study investigated the AoA effect in compound-word processing across two experiments, each with 48 participants. Experiment 1 found that compound word AoA significantly affected naming latencies (beta = 0.141), supporting the effect. Experiment 2 further showed that both compound word AoA and individual morpheme AoA influenced combinatorial naming latency.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Izura and Playfoot 2012
papers.apa: Izura, C., & Playfoot, D. (2011). A normative study of acronyms and acronym naming. Behavior Research Methods, 44(3), 862–889. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-011-0175-8
papers.summary: This study explored factors influencing acronym naming, collecting norms for 146 acronyms and involving 20 participants in a naming experiment. It found that AoA correlated negatively with imageability and rated frequency, similar to mainstream words, and that naming times were influenced by lexical and sublexical factors, including AoA (r = .249). This supports the presence of an AoA effect in acronym processing.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval in opaque languages
papers.title: Morrison and Ellis 2000
papers.apa: Morrison, C. M., & Ellis, A. W. (2000). Real age of acquisition effects in word naming and lexical decision. British Journal of Psychology, 91(2), 167–180. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1348/000712600161763
papers.summary: This study validated AoA effects using objective AoA norms derived from children's naming data, rather than adult ratings. It found that 'real AoA' significantly predicted word naming and lexical decision speeds, with early-acquired words responded to faster (r = .244). This provides strong evidence for the AoA effect, independent of subjective adult ratings.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval
papers.title: Word Frequency Affects Naming Latency in Dutch when Age of Acquisition is Controlled
papers.apa: Brysbaert, M. (1996). Word Frequency Affects Naming Latency in Dutch when Age of Acquisition is Controlled. European Journal of Cognitive Psychology, 8(2), 185–194. https://doi.org/10.1080/095414496383149
papers.summary: This foundational study replicated the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect in Dutch word naming, showing that earlier acquired words are named faster, independent of word frequency. Conducted with 22 8-9 year old primary school children, the study found a reliable AoA effect (beta = -0.58, t(24) = -3.37, P < 0.01) alongside an independent word frequency effect. This was the first demonstration of the AoA effect on naming in a language other than English.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval

papers.summary: This study investigated the effects of age-of-acquisition (AoA), frequency, and imageability on visual word recognition in Dutch across immediate naming, lexical decision, and masked priming tasks. With 60 first-year psychology students, the authors found that both AoA and frequency had significant independent effects in all tasks, while imageability did not. This corroborated previous findings that frequency effects are not merely artifacts of AoA, and that AoA is a robust predictor of processing speed.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval
papers.title: Cuetos and Barbon 2006

papers.summary: This study examined factors influencing word naming speed in Spanish, a transparent orthography, with 53 native Spanish-speaking psychology students. It found that subjective Age of Acquisition (AoA) was one of the best predictors of reading times (r = 0.384), alongside word length. While various frequency measures correlated significantly with response latencies, their effects were not significant in regression analyses when AoA was controlled, supporting the independent contribution of AoA.

papers.summary: This study tested the arbitrary mapping hypothesis for Age-of-Acquisition (AoA) effects in word naming in Turkish, a highly transparent orthography, with 28 undergraduate students. Contrary to the hypothesis, early acquired words were named reliably faster than late acquired words (35 ms advantage, d = 0.48), indicating a robust AoA effect even in transparent languages. This finding suggests that AoA effects are a universal property of lexical processing, challenging models that predict reduced effects in transparent orthographies.

papers.summary: This foundational study investigated factors influencing reading speed for Japanese Kanji characters in native speakers. Using simultaneous multiple regression, it identified two strong, independent age of acquisition (AoA) effects: one related to when words enter spoken vocabulary and another to when characters are learned. The authors proposed these effects reflect the influence of AoA on the quality of representations in the speech output lexicon and visual input lexicon, respectively.

papers.summary: This study explored the mechanisms behind Age of Acquisition (AoA) effects in Chinese character processing, testing the arbitrary mapping hypothesis across two experiments. It found larger AoA effects for characters from low-predictive families in both naming and semantic judgment tasks. These results support the idea that AoA effects are more pronounced when the mapping between orthography and pronunciation/meaning is less predictable.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical retrieval in logographic languages
papers.title: Havelka and Tomita 2006

papers.summary: This study (N=40 Japanese speakers) examined the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect in naming Japanese words, comparing Kanji and Kana scripts. It found a significant main effect of AoA, with earlier acquired words named faster (F1(1, 38) = 42.61, p < .01), and a significant interaction: the AoA effect was larger for Kanji (102 ms) than for Kana (27 ms). These findings support the hypothesis that the size of the AoA effect is influenced by the regularity of orthography-phonology mapping.

papers.summary: This study (N=480 for rating, N=39 for naming) developed and validated psycholinguistic norms for 2,423 Chinese single-character words, including age of acquisition (AoA). Through multiple regression analyses, it demonstrated that word-naming latencies are significantly affected by various factors, including AoA (Beta = 0.670), frequency, semantics, visual features, and consistency. The study's primary contribution was providing extensive normative data and confirming the influence of multiple variables on Chinese word processing.

papers.summary: This paper investigated the automaticity of word reading in Chinese using a dual-task paradigm, focusing on differences between high and low reading ability groups (N=48). It explored how word frequency and stimulus onset asynchrony (SOA) affect response times, concluding that word recognition automaticity depends on reading skill. This study does not address the Age of Acquisition effect on lexical retrieval and is therefore not relevant to the current effect block.

papers.summary: This foundational study established the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect, demonstrating that words learned earlier in life are named more quickly than those learned later. papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect, finding a smaller but still significant effect compared to the original study. The authors reported an effect size of ηp2 = 0.25, indicating that while the effect persists, its magnitude may be considerably weaker than initially reported.

papers.summary: This study further explored the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect, reporting a substantially reduced effect size compared to previous findings. papers.summary: This study investigated the contributions of lexical variables to auditory word recognition in 29 normal older adults. It found strong age of acquisition (AOA) effects, even after controlling for word frequency and familiarity, predicting lexical decision reaction times (r = 0.66). The findings corroborate previous research, suggesting AOA is a robust predictor of spoken word recognition.

papers.summary: This paper conducted two experiments on auditory and visual lexical decision. Experiment 2, involving 20 undergraduates, found a strong effect of age of acquisition (AOA) on auditory lexical decision speed and accuracy, with early-acquired words processed faster (d = 1.30). This effect was observed even when word frequency was controlled, reinforcing AOA as a significant predictor.

papers.summary: This study addressed concerns about the reliance on adult AoA estimates by using objective AoA norms derived from children's naming data. They demonstrated that AoA effects, where early-acquired words are processed faster, persist in both word naming and lexical decision tasks even with these objective measures. They found significant AoA effects for both male and female participants, showing faster responses to earlier-acquired words. While females showed slower responses to male-oriented words, the core AoA effect remained robust across genders and word types.
