papers.summary: This study investigated the independent effects of AoA and word frequency on auditory lexical decision speed across two experiments (N=20 in Exp 1). Experiment 1 found no significant effect of word frequency when AoA was controlled (p=.41). In contrast, Experiment 2 demonstrated a strong effect of AoA on auditory lexical decision speed when word frequency was controlled, with more errors for late-acquired words. This suggests that previously reported frequency effects might have been confounded by AoA.

papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the effects of age-of-acquisition (AoA) and frequency-of-occurrence on visual word recognition in Dutch. Across three experiments with 60 first-year psychology students, using immediate naming, lexical decision, and masked priming tasks, the authors found that AoA was a significant variable in all tasks, influencing response latencies. They reported an AoA effect of 11-12 msec in Dutch, corroborating previous findings and suggesting it's not an artifact of imageability.

The findings showed that AoA ratings significantly accounted for additional variance in lexical processing (response time and error rate) beyond word frequency, particularly for low-frequency words. This provides further evidence for the AoA effect in Chinese word recognition, with reported correlations of r=.409 for RT and r=.285 for accuracy.

papers.summary: This foundational paper likely established the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect in eye-tracking during reading, demonstrating that earlier-acquired words lead to shorter fixation durations. The original reported effect sizes were: First fixation: beta = 4.01, Single fixation: beta = 6.55, Gaze duration: beta = 6.62, Total duration: beta = 7.00.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on silent reading

It found that earlier-learned words consistently led to shorter fixations across all eye-tracking measures (e.g., single fixation d=0.95, gaze duration d=0.81, total reading time d=0.71), independent of other lexical variables. The authors concluded that AoA influences reading times even in coherent text, supporting semantic or mapping hypotheses.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on silent reading

papers.apa: Juhasz, B. J. (2018). Experience with compound words influences their processing: An eye movement investigation with English compound words. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 71(1), 103–112. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470218.2016.1253756

It found that AoA and familiarity had the most robust effects on fixation durations, even when controlling for length, word frequency, and lexeme frequencies. The study reported effect sizes such as first fixation d=0.27, single fixation d=0.29, gaze duration d=0.34, and total fixation d=0.37, extending the AoA effect to morphologically complex words.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on silent reading

papers.summary: This study (N=32) investigated the influence of both Age of Acquisition (AoA) and word frequency on eye fixation durations during sentence reading across two experiments. Both experiments consistently demonstrated that AoA affects eye fixation durations, with earlier-acquired words being read faster (e.g., Exp 1 gaze duration ηp2=0.43, Exp 2 gaze duration d=0.35), even when word frequency was controlled. The findings suggest that AoA effects observed in isolated word tasks generalize to more natural reading contexts.

papers.summary: This study (N=47) explored the time course of Age of Acquisition (AoA) effects on eye movements during sentence reading using distributional analyses. It confirmed that earlier-acquired words lead to shorter fixation durations across various measures (e.g., first fixation d=0.22, gaze duration d=0.20), suggesting an early and lasting influence on word recognition. Survival analysis pinpointed the earliest discernable AoA effect on first-fixation durations at 158 ms.

papers.summary: This foundational paper conducted three experiments (N not specified in abstract) to investigate the Age of Acquisition (AoA) effect on naming famous faces. They found that earlier acquisition significantly predicted faster and more accurate naming, even on initial exposure, though this effect diminished with repeated presentations. The study established AoA as a key predictor in face naming models.

papers.apa: Izura, C., & Ellis, A. W. (2004). Age of acquisition effects in translation judgement tasks. Journal of Memory and Language, 50(2), 165–181. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jml.2003.09.004
papers.summary: This study investigated the Age of Acquisition effect in bilinguals, providing further evidence for its presence in translation tasks. It likely explored the influence of both L1 and L2 AoA on translation speed, contributing to the understanding of how word acquisition timing impacts lexical processing. Specific findings, sample sizes, and detailed methodologies are not available from the provided text.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition on lexical-semantic processes
papers.title: Bowers and Kennison 2011
papers.apa: Bowers, J. M., & Kennison, S. M. (2011). The Role of Age of Acquisition in Bilingual Word Translation: Evidence from Spanish-English Bilinguals. Journal of Psycholinguistic Research, 40(4), 275–289. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10936-011-9169-z
papers.summary: This study with 36 highly proficient Spanish-English bilinguals investigated the hypothesis that L1 AoA influences language processing, specifically that early-learned L1 words have stronger conceptual links. They found that categorical interference (longer translation times for semantically blocked trials) occurred only when early AoA L1 words were translated into L2 (η² = 0.12), supporting the idea that early AoA words have more robust conceptual representations. This suggests a nuanced role for AoA in bilingual translation, particularly for L1 to L2 translation.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical-semantic processes (picture-word interference)
papers.title: ‘Age of Acquisition, Word Frequency, and Picture–Word Interference
papers.apa: Catling, J. C., Dent, K., Johnston, R. A., & Balding, R. (2010). Age of Acquisition, Word Frequency, and Picture–Word Interference. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 63(7), 1304–1317. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470210903380830
papers.summary: This foundational paper describes two experiments investigating how picture name Age of Acquisition (AoA) and distractor word properties (frequency, AoA) influence picture-word interference. They found that early-acquired pictures were named faster. Both word frequency and distractor AoA modulated interference, but did not interact with picture AoA. Experiment 2 reported an original effect size of ηp² = 0.40 for the picture AoA effect.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical-semantic processes (picture-word interference)
papers.title: de Zubicaray et al. 2012
papers.apa: Gonzalez-Calvo et al. supplementary material. (n.d.). https://doi.org/10.1192/j.eurpsy.2025.10117.sm001
papers.summary: This paper is presented as a replication attempt that found a larger effect size for the Age of Acquisition influence on lexical-semantic processes. The reported replication effect size for Experiment 2 is ηp² = 0.79, suggesting a strong confirmation of the original finding.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical change
papers.title: Age of acquisition predicts rate of lexical evolution’
papers.apa: Monaghan, P. (2014). Age of acquisition predicts rate of lexical evolution. Cognition, 133(3), 530–534. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2014.08.007
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that words acquired earlier in life are less likely to change their meaning over time compared to later-acquired words. The original effect size was reported as Beta = 0.120.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical change
papers.title: Vejdemo and Hörberg 2016
papers.apa: Vejdemo, S., & Hörberg, T. (2016). Semantic Factors Predict the Rate of Lexical Replacement of Content Words. PLOS ONE, 11(1), e0147924. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0147924
papers.summary: This study investigated semantic factors influencing lexical replacement rate using 167 content words from the Swadesh list. They found that synonyms, imageability, and number of senses predicted lexical replacement, even when controlling for frequency. Crucially, they reported no significant relationship between age of acquisition and lexical replacement rate when frequency was controlled (Beta = -0.35), contradicting the original effect.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on lexical change
papers.title: Monaghan and Roberts 2021
papers.apa: Monaghan, P., & Roberts, S. G. (2021). Iconicity and Diachronic Language Change. Cognitive Science, 45(4). Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/cogs.12968
papers.summary: This study examined factors influencing word stability over time, analyzing 784 English words. They found that stable words were higher in iconicity, longer, and earlier acquired, supporting the idea that age of acquisition plays a role in lexical stability. The authors reported a pseudo-R2 of 1.7% for their model.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on learning (conceptual learning)
papers.title: Order of acquisition in learning perceptual categories: A laboratory analogue of the age-of-acquisition effect?
papers.apa: STEWART, N., & ELLIS, A. W. (2008). Order of acquisition in learning perceptual categories: A laboratory analogue of the age-of-acquisition effect? Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 15(1), 70–74. https://doi.org/10.3758/pbr.15.1.70
papers.summary: This foundational study (N=27) investigated the Age-of-Acquisition (AoA) effect using a laboratory analogue where participants learned to categorize novel checkerboard stimuli sequentially. They found that early-learned boards were categorized significantly faster than late-learned ones, even when cumulative frequency was equal, with an original effect size of ηp2 = 0.17. The authors concluded that order of acquisition, rather than simple frequency or intrinsic stimulus properties, drives this effect, providing a controlled experimental paradigm for future research.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on learning (conceptual learning)
papers.title: Catling et al. 2013
papers.apa: Catling, J., Dent, K., Preece, E., & Johnston, R. (2013). Age-of-Acquisition Effects in Novel Picture Naming: A Laboratory Analogue. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 66(9), 1756–1763. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470218.2013.764903
papers.summary: This replication study used a laboratory analogue with novel Greeble pictures to investigate AoA effects. Participants learned to name Greebles, with some introduced early and others later. The study found that early-acquired stimuli were named significantly faster (ηp2 = 0.23) and had smaller visual duration thresholds (ηp2 = 0.27) than late-acquired items, even with equal cumulative frequency. These findings support that order-of-acquisition effects can be observed over short timescales in the lab, distinct from mere frequency of exposure.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on learning (conceptual learning)
papers.title: Izura et al. 2011
papers.apa: Izura, C., Pérez, M. A., Agallou, E., Wright, V. C., Marín, J., Stadthagen-González, H., & Ellis, A. W. (2011). Age/order of acquisition effects and the cumulative learning of foreign words: A word training study. Journal of Memory and Language, 64(1), 32–58. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jml.2010.09.002
papers.summary: This paper, across multiple experiments, investigated the age-of-acquisition effect in various tasks. It reported significant effects in experiments on delayed picture naming (d = 0.36, ηp2 = 0.12), corrected latencies (ηp2 = 0.15), lexical decision (ηp2 = 0.32), and semantic categorization (ηp2 = 0.20), with other experiments showing ηp2 values of 0.49 and 0.20. These findings consistently support the presence of age-of-acquisition effects across different cognitive processes.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on learning (procedural)
papers.title: ‘Acquisition and long-term retention of a simple serial perceptual-motor skill
papers.apa: Neumann, E., & Ammons, R. B. (1957). Acquisition and long-term retention of a simple serial perceptual-motor skill. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 53(3), 159–161. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0041719
papers.summary: This foundational paper established the effect that the order of learning components in a procedural skill influences their acquisition and retention. It proposed that earlier-learned actions are recalled with greater speed and accuracy. The original study reported a substantial effect size of ηp2= 0.23.

papers.effect_name: Age of acquisition influence on learning (procedural)
papers.title: Magil 1976
papers.apa: Magill, R. A. (1976). Order of Acquisition of the Parts of a Serial-Motor Task. Research Quarterly. American Alliance for Health, Physical Education and Recreation, 47(1), 134–139. https://doi.org/10.1080/10671315.1976.10615350
papers.summary: This study investigated the existence of a U-shaped learning curve in a serial-motor task, similar to those found in verbal learning. Across 105 male participants learning three serial positions on a manual lever, the authors found that position 1 was learned first, followed by position 2, then position 3, indicating a primacy effect. While a U-shaped curve was not observed, the findings support the influence of acquisition order on learning, with reported ηp2 values ranging from 0.01 to 0.11 for differences between positions in later blocks.

papers.title: Ego Depletion: Is the Active Self a Limited Resource?
papers.apa: Baumeister, R. F., Bratslavsky, E., Muraven, M., & Tice, D. M. (1998). Ego depletion: Is the active self a limited resource? Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 74(5), 1252–1265. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.74.5.1252
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of ego depletion, proposing that self-control is a limited resource that diminishes after exertion, leading to impaired performance on subsequent self-regulatory tasks. While specific effect sizes were not reported in the original paper, subsequent calculations suggested a large effect (d = -1.96).

papers.title: Xu et al. 2014
papers.apa: Xu, X., Demos, K. E., Leahey, T. M., Hart, C. N., Trautvetter, J., Coward, P., Middleton, K. R., & Wing, R. R. (2014). Failure to Replicate Depletion of Self-Control. PLoS ONE, 9(10), e109950. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0109950
papers.summary: This study conducted four replication attempts (two with community adults, two with young adults) using the letter-crossing depletion task and handgrip persistence or modified Stroop as dependent measures. Across all studies, no significant evidence for ego depletion was found, with combined effect sizes for handgrip persistence (d = -0.20) and Stroop interference (d = -0.06) being small and non-significant. The authors concluded that depletion effects might be more limited than previously thought.

papers.title: Hagger 2016
papers.apa: Hagger, M. S., Chatzisarantis, N. L. D., Alberts, H., Anggono, C. O., Batailler, C., Birt, A. R., Brand, R., Brandt, M. J., Brewer, G., Bruyneel, S., Calvillo, D. P., Campbell, W. K., Cannon, P. R., Carlucci, M., Carruth, N. P., Cheung, T., Crowell, A., De Ridder, D. T. D., Dewitte, S., … Zwienenberg, M. (2016). A Multilab Preregistered Replication of the Ego-Depletion Effect. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 11(4), 546–573. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691616652873
papers.summary: This Registered Replication Report involved 23 laboratories (N = 2,141) attempting to replicate a standardized ego-depletion protocol. A meta-analysis of the replication studies revealed a very small and non-significant ego-depletion effect (d = 0.04, 95% CI [−0.07, 0.15]), with the confidence interval encompassing zero. The findings cast doubt on the existence and size of the ego-depletion effect as conceptualized by the resource depletion model.

papers.title: Vohs et al. 2021
papers.apa: Vohs, K. D., Schmeichel, B. J., Lohmann, S., Gronau, Q. F., Finley, A. J., Ainsworth, S. E., Alquist, J. L., Baker, M. D., Brizi, A., Bunyi, A., Butschek, G. J., Campbell, C., Capaldi, J., Cau, C., Chambers, H., Chatzisarantis, N. L. D., Christensen, W. J., Clay, S. L., Curtis, J., … Albarracín, D. (2021). A Multisite Preregistered Paradigmatic Test of the Ego-Depletion Effect. Psychological Science, 32(10), 1566–1581. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797621989733
papers.summary: This preregistered multilaboratory project (k = 36, N = 3,531) used a paradigmatic replication approach to test ego-depletion effects. Confirmatory tests found a nonsignificant effect (d = 0.06), and Bayesian meta-analyses indicated the data were 4 times more likely under the null hypothesis than an informed alternative. Exploratory analyses found a statistically significant but small effect (d = 0.08), but Bayesian analysis showed equal likelihood for null and alternative hypotheses, ultimately providing no robust evidence for a depletion effect.

papers.title: Unskilled and unaware of it: how difficulties in recognizing one's own incompetence lead to inflated self-assessments
papers.apa: Kruger, J., & Dunning, D. (1999). Unskilled and unaware of it: How difficulties in recognizing one’s own incompetence lead to inflated self-assessments. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 77(6), 1121–1134. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.77.6.1121
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the Dunning-Kruger effect, proposing that individuals with low competence in a domain not only perform poorly but also lack the metacognitive ability to recognize their own incompetence. Across four studies (e.g., N=65 for humor, N=45 for logic, N=84 for grammar), the authors found that participants in the bottom quartile grossly overestimated their abilities (e.g., actual 12th percentile, estimated 58th-66th percentile), while top performers sometimes underestimated. This miscalibration was linked to deficits in metacognitive skill.

papers.title: Gignac 2020
papers.apa: Gignac, G. E., & Zajenkowski, M. (2020). The Dunning-Kruger effect is (mostly) a statistical artefact: Valid approaches to testing the hypothesis with individual differences data. Intelligence, 80, 101449. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.intell.2020.101449
papers.summary: This paper re-examined the Dunning-Kruger effect, particularly in the context of IQ, arguing that the original statistical analyses might be confounded. While replicating the effect using the original methodology (η2 = 0.20), the author found no evidence for the effect (r = -0.05/d = -0.1 and r = 0.02/d = 0.04) when employing less-confounded statistical tests. The author suggests that the Dunning-Kruger effect might be largely an artifact of the statistical methods used.

papers.title: Nuhfer 2016
papers.apa: de Zubicaray, G. I. (2026). Modelling in the midst of a replication crisis for embodied action semantics: a commentary on Carriere et al. (2026). https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/hzgqe_v1
papers.summary: No abstract or extracted text was provided for this paper.

papers.title: Nuhfer 2017
papers.apa: David Dunning Defines Dunning-Kruger Effect. (2016). https://doi.org/10.4135/9781473960022
papers.summary: No abstract or extracted text was provided for this paper.

papers.title: Luu 2015
papers.apa: David Dunning Defines Dunning-Kruger Effect. (2016). https://doi.org/10.4135/9781473960022
papers.summary: No abstract or extracted text was provided for this paper.

papers.title: Greenberg 2018
papers.apa: David Dunning Defines Dunning-Kruger Effect. (2016). https://doi.org/10.4135/9781473960022
papers.summary: No abstract or extracted text was provided for this paper.

papers.title: Yarkoni 2010
papers.apa: David Dunning Defines Dunning-Kruger Effect. (2016). https://doi.org/10.4135/9781473960022
papers.summary: No abstract or extracted text was provided for this paper.

papers.title: Jansen 2021
papers.apa: Jansen, R. A., Rafferty, A. N., & Griffiths, T. L. (2021). A rational model of the Dunning–Kruger effect supports insensitivity to evidence in low performers. Nature Human Behaviour, 5(6), 756–763. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-021-01057-0
papers.summary: This study investigated the Dunning-Kruger effect using Bayesian models in domains like grammar and logical reasoning. The authors reported that their Bayesian analyses supported the existence of the effect in their data, replicating the original claim that low-performing individuals tend to overestimate their abilities. Specific effect sizes were not reported in the provided information.

papers.title: Muller 2020
papers.apa: Muller, A., Sirianni, L. A., & Addante, R. J. (2020). Neural correlates of the Dunning–Kruger effect. European Journal of Neuroscience, 53(2), 460–484. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/ejn.14935
papers.summary: This study explored the Dunning-Kruger effect in episodic memory, designing a novel method to elicit the effect while recording EEG. Participants in the bottom 25th percentile significantly overestimated their performance (d=4.73), while those in the top 75th percentile underestimated theirs (d=-0.88), consistent with the classic DKE. The findings also revealed neural correlates, suggesting differing cognitive processes (familiarity vs. recollection) between over-estimators and under-estimators.

papers.title: Judgment of contingency in depressed and nondepressed students: sadder but wiser?
papers.apa: Alloy, L. B., & Abramson, L. Y. (1979). Judgment of contingency in depressed and nondepressed students: Sadder but wiser? Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 108(4), 441–485. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-3445.108.4.441
papers.summary: This foundational study by Alloy and Abramson introduced the concept of depressive realism, demonstrating that non-depressed individuals tended to overestimate their control over outcomes in situations where control was objectively absent, while depressed individuals did not exhibit this bias. This led to the hypothesis that depressed individuals possess a more accurate perception of reality. A calculated effect size for bias about contingency was d = -0.32.

papers.title: Moore and Fresco 2012
papers.apa: Moore, M. T., & Fresco, D. M. (2012). Depressive realism: A meta-analytic review. Clinical Psychology Review, 32(6), 496–509. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpr.2012.05.004
papers.summary: This meta-analysis systematically reviewed the literature on depressive realism, synthesizing findings from multiple studies. It reported a much smaller overall effect size (d = -0.07) compared to the original claims, suggesting that the depressive realism effect is either very weak or largely absent across studies. The authors generally concluded that the evidence for a robust depressive realism effect is limited.

papers.title: Extraneous factors in judicial decisions
papers.apa: Danziger, S., Levav, J., & Avnaim-Pesso, L. (2011). Extraneous factors in judicial decisions. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 108(17), 6889–6892. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1018033108
papers.summary: This foundational study analyzed 1,112 judicial rulings from 8 Israeli judges over 10 months, finding that the probability of a favorable ruling steadily declined from approximately 65% at the beginning of a session to nearly 0% before a food break, then immediately jumped back to 65% after the break. The authors attributed this pattern to ego depletion, suggesting that mental fatigue from making decisions depletes cognitive resources, leading to a default 'no' decision.

papers.title: Weinshall-Margel 2011
papers.apa: David Dunning Defines Dunning-Kruger Effect. (2016). https://doi.org/10.4135/9781473960022
papers.summary: This paper re-analyzed the data from Danziger et al. (2011), arguing that the observed pattern was an artifact of case scheduling rather than judge hunger. It suggested that cases requiring more deliberation or those with unrepresented prisoners (who are less likely to be granted parole) were disproportionately scheduled later in sessions, confounding the effect of meal breaks.

papers.title: Glöckner 2016
papers.apa: Weinshall-Margel, K., & Shapard, J. (2011). Overlooked factors in the analysis of parole decisions. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 108(42). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1110910108
papers.summary: This critique re-examined the statistical methods and conclusions of the original 'hungry judge effect' paper, highlighting potential methodological flaws and alternative explanations for the observed patterns. It argued that the original analysis might have overestimated the effect due to issues like non-random case assignment and the ecological fallacy, suggesting that the evidence for ego depletion as a mechanism was weak.

papers.title: Lakens 2017
papers.apa: Glöckner, A. (2016). External Budgetary Auditing. Global Encyclopedia of Public Administration, Public Policy, and Governance, 1–10. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-31816-5_2303-1
papers.summary: This paper provided a statistical re-analysis and commentary on the 'hungry judge effect,' emphasizing the implausibility of the reported effect size given prior probabilities and the lack of robust evidence for the ego depletion mechanism in general. It highlighted issues with the original study's statistical interpretation and the potential for false positives in studies with low statistical power or questionable research practices.

papers.title: Frames of Mind: The Theory of Multiple Intelligences
papers.apa: Bornstein, M. H., & Gardner, H. (1986). Frames of Mind: The Theory of Multiple Intelligences. Journal of Aesthetic Education, 20(2), 120. https://doi.org/10.2307/3332707
papers.summary: This foundational book introduced the theory of Multiple Intelligences, proposing that human intelligence is not a single entity but a collection of distinct abilities. Gardner outlined several intelligences, including linguistic, logical-mathematical, musical, bodily-kinesthetic, spatial, interpersonal, and intrapersonal, based on criteria from various fields like developmental psychology and neuroscience.

papers.title: Shearer and Karanian 2017
papers.apa: Shearer, C. B., & Karanian, J. M. (2017). The neuroscience of intelligence: Empirical support for the theory of multiple intelligences? Trends in Neuroscience and Education, 6, 211–223. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tine.2017.02.002
papers.summary: This paper explored the empirical basis for Multiple Intelligences, reporting descriptive statistics and neural patterns that were found to be consistent with Gardner’s hypothesis. While suggesting some alignment, the study's findings contribute to the ongoing discussion about the theory's empirical validation.

papers.title: Sternberg 1994
papers.apa: Sternberg, R. J. (1994). Commentary: Reforming School Reform: Comments on Multiple Intelligences: The Theory in Practice. Teachers College Record: The Voice of Scholarship in Education, 95(4), 561–569. https://doi.org/10.1177/016146819409500411
papers.summary: This commentary critically examines the theory of Multiple Intelligences, particularly its application in educational reform. Sternberg, a prominent intelligence researcher, discusses the practical implications and theoretical challenges of Gardner's framework, offering a perspective on its strengths and weaknesses in educational settings.

papers.title: Tirri and Nokelainen 2008
papers.apa: Waterhouse, L. (2006). Multiple Intelligences, the Mozart Effect, and Emotional Intelligence: A Critical Review. Educational Psychologist, 41(4), 207–225. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15326985ep4104_1
papers.summary: This article (Waterhouse, 2006) critically reviews the empirical evidence for multiple intelligences theory, alongside the Mozart effect and emotional intelligence. It argues that despite their popularity in education, these theories, including MI, lack adequate empirical support and should not form the basis for educational practice. The author compares them to better-supported theories in cognitive psychology and neuroscience, concluding that MI theory is not supported by sound or consistent validating empirical evidence.

papers.title: Visser et al. 2006
papers.apa: Visser, B. A., Ashton, M. C., & Vernon, P. A. (2006). Beyond g: Putting multiple intelligences theory to the test. Intelligence, 34(5), 487–502. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.intell.2006.02.004
papers.summary: This study used factor analysis to investigate the empirical structure of multiple intelligences, finding only modest support for Gardner's proposed distinct intelligences. The analysis revealed strong loadings on a general intelligence (g) factor, suggesting that a significant portion of variance in cognitive abilities is still explained by a unitary intelligence.

papers.title: Waterhouse 2006
papers.apa: Waterhouse, L. (2006). Multiple Intelligences, the Mozart Effect, and Emotional Intelligence: A Critical Review. Educational Psychologist, 41(4), 207–225. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15326985ep4104_1
papers.summary: This article critically reviews the empirical evidence for multiple intelligences theory, alongside the Mozart effect and emotional intelligence. It argues that despite their popularity in education, these theories, including MI, lack adequate empirical support and should not form the basis for educational practice. The author compares them to better-supported theories in cognitive psychology and neuroscience, concluding that MI theory is not supported by sound or consistent validating empirical evidence.

papers.title: Improving fluid intelligence with training on working memory
papers.apa: D’Esposito, M. (2008). Faculty Opinions recommendation of Improving fluid intelligence with training on working memory. [dataset]. In Faculty Opinions – Post-Publication Peer Review of the Biomedical Literature. H1 Connect. https://doi.org/10.3410/f.1109007.566174
papers.summary: This paper is widely considered the foundational study proposing that working memory training, specifically the Dual n-Back task, can lead to improvements in fluid intelligence. It reported a significant effect size (d=0.4) over control groups shortly after training.

papers.title: Melby-Lervåg 2013
papers.apa: Melby-Lervåg, M., & Hulme, C. (2013). Is working memory training effective? A meta-analytic review. Developmental Psychology, 49(2), 270–291. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0028228
papers.summary: This meta-analytic review of 23 studies (30 group comparisons) found that working memory training produced reliable short-term improvements in working memory skills (near-transfer effects). However, it found no convincing evidence of generalization to other skills like nonverbal and verbal ability, inhibitory processes, word decoding, or arithmetic. For nonverbal reasoning, a small initial improvement became zero when focusing on studies with robust designs and active controls.

papers.title: Gwern 2012
papers.apa: Lakens, D. (2017). Impossibly hungry judges. https://doi.org/10.59350/q63bt-hxh69
papers.summary: This meta-analysis, often cited in discussions of brain training, synthesized findings on the Dual n-Back task. It reported a meta-analytic effect size of d=0.1397 among studies using active controls, suggesting a small positive effect, though considerably smaller than initial claims.

papers.title: Reddick 2013
papers.apa: Redick, T. S., Shipstead, Z., Harrison, T. L., Hicks, K. L., Fried, D. E., Hambrick, D. Z., Kane, M. J., & Engle, R. W. (2013). No evidence of intelligence improvement after working memory training: A randomized, placebo-controlled study. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 142(2), 359–379. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0029082
papers.summary: This study conducted a replication attempt of brain training effects. It reported finding no positive transfer to any of the cognitive ability tests measured, with all partial eta-squared values less than 0.054, suggesting a lack of far-transfer effects.

papers.title: Lampit 2014
papers.apa: Lampit, A., Hallock, H., & Valenzuela, M. (2014). Computerized Cognitive Training in Cognitively Healthy Older Adults: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Effect Modifiers. PLoS Medicine, 11(11), e1001756. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pmed.1001756
papers.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis of 52 randomized controlled trials (N=4,885) in healthy older adults found a small but statistically significant overall effect size for computerized cognitive training (g=0.22). Small to moderate effects were observed for nonverbal memory (g=0.24), verbal memory (g=0.08), working memory (g=0.22), processing speed (g=0.31), and visuospatial skills (g=0.30), but no significant effects for executive functions and attention. It also highlighted that home-based and high-frequency training were ineffective.

papers.title: Berger 2020
papers.apa: Jaušovec, N., & Jaušovec, K. (2012). Working memory training: Improving intelligence – Changing brain activity. Brain and Cognition, 79(2), 96–106. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bandc.2012.02.007
papers.summary: This randomized controlled trial investigated brain training in 6-7 year olds, reporting effect sizes of d=0.2 to 0.4. However, it noted that many of the observed far-transfer effects appeared 6-12 months after training, a duration well beyond most prior studies, raising questions about the immediacy and directness of the training's impact.

papers.title: Simons 2016
papers.apa: Simons, D. J., Boot, W. R., Charness, N., Gathercole, S. E., Chabris, C. F., Hambrick, D. Z., & Stine-Morrow, E. A. L. (2016). Do “Brain-Training” Programs Work? Psychological Science in the Public Interest, 17(3), 103–186. https://doi.org/10.1177/1529100616661983
papers.summary: This comprehensive review evaluated the efficacy of 'brain-training' programs by examining studies cited by leading brain-training companies. It found extensive evidence for improvements on trained tasks (near-transfer), less evidence for closely related tasks, and little evidence for far-transfer to distantly related tasks or everyday cognitive performance. The authors also identified major shortcomings in the design and analysis of many published intervention studies.

papers.effect_name: Brain training on intelligence - music lessons improve intelligence
papers.title: Music lessons enhance IQ
papers.apa: Schellenberg, E. G. (2004). Music Lessons Enhance IQ. Psychological Science, 15(8), 511–514. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.0956-7976.2004.00711.x
papers.summary: This experimental study with 144 children randomly assigned to music (keyboard or voice), drama, or no lessons, investigated the impact of music training on IQ. The authors reported that children in the music groups exhibited greater increases in full-scale IQ compared to control groups, with a relatively small but generalized effect (d=1.948). The drama group unexpectedly showed improvements in adaptive social behavior.

papers.effect_name: Brain training on intelligence - music lessons improve intelligence
papers.title: Mehr et al. 2013
papers.apa: Mehr, S. A., Schachner, A., Katz, R. C., & Spelke, E. S. (2013). Two Randomized Trials Provide No Consistent Evidence for Nonmusical Cognitive Benefits of Brief Preschool Music Enrichment. PLoS ONE, 8(12), e82007. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0082007
papers.summary: This paper presents two randomized controlled trials investigating the cognitive effects of brief preschool music classes. Experiment 1 (N=29) compared music to visual arts, finding weak and inconsistent spatial ability differences. Experiment 2 (larger sample) compared music to a no-treatment control. Overall, the combined results showed no consistent evidence that music classes improved children's skills in spatial, linguistic, or numerical reasoning (Wilks' λ = .742, F(4,24) = 2.09, p = .113), highlighting the need for replication and caution in interpreting past positive findings.

papers.effect_name: Brain training on intelligence - music lessons improve intelligence
papers.title: D’Souza & Wiseheart 2018
papers.apa: D’Souza, A. A., & Wiseheart, M. (2018). Cognitive effects of music and dance training in children. Archives of Scientific Psychology, 6(1), 178–192. https://doi.org/10.1037/arc0000048
papers.summary: This study investigated the cognitive effects of music and dance training in children across various cognitive domains. Using Bayesian analysis, the authors found weak evidence for an effect on task switching (BF inclusion=1.964) but weak evidence for the null hypothesis across measures of processing speed, working memory, inference control, and nonverbal intelligence (BF inclusion values ranging from 0.004 to 0.778). The findings generally did not support significant cognitive transfer from music or dance training to broader cognitive abilities.

papers.effect_name: Bilingual advantages in executive control - inhibition
papers.title: How does bilingualism improve executive control? A comparison of active and reactive inhibition mechanisms’
papers.summary: This paper is considered foundational for establishing the bilingual advantage in executive control, particularly focusing on inhibition mechanisms. Without the abstract, specific findings or sample sizes cannot be detailed.

papers.effect_name: Bilingual advantages in executive control - inhibition
papers.title: De Bruin et al. 2015
papers.apa: de Bruin, A., Treccani, B., & Della Sala, S. (2014). Cognitive Advantage in Bilingualism. Psychological Science, 26(1), 99–107. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797614557866
papers.summary: This study investigated publication bias in the bilingual advantage literature by analyzing conference abstracts and subsequent publications from 1999-2012. It found that studies fully supporting the bilingual advantage were most likely to be published, while those challenging it were least likely, indicating a significant publication bias in the field (e.g., ηp2 = .073 for challenge vs. support).

papers.effect_name: Bilingual advantages in executive control - inhibition

papers.summary: This meta-analysis examined the bilingual advantage in overall executive function, reporting a significant but marginal effect (g = 0.06). The authors also noted indications of publication bias, suggesting that the observed advantage might be inflated by selective reporting.

papers.effect_name: Bilingual advantages in executive control - inhibition
papers.title: Kappes 2015
papers.summary: This paper presents a replication attempt or critical commentary on the bilingual advantage in executive control. It reported an effect size of r = 0.06 ± 0.36, suggesting a very small or negligible effect, but specific details are not available.

papers.effect_name: Bilingual advantages in executive control - inhibition
papers.title: Paap et al. 2013
papers.apa: Paap, K. R., & Greenberg, Z. I. (2013). There is no coherent evidence for a bilingual advantage in executive processing. Cognitive Psychology, 66(2), 232–258. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cogpsych.2012.12.002
papers.summary: Across three studies (N=80, 86, 110), this paper compared bilinguals and monolinguals on 15 executive processing indicators, including Simon, flanker, and switching tasks. It found no main effect of group and only one significant Group x Condition interaction, which indicated a bilingual disadvantage. The authors concluded there is no coherent evidence for a bilingual advantage and noted a lack of convergent validity across tasks.

papers.effect_name: Bilingual advantages in executive control - inhibition
papers.title: Sanchez-Azanza et al. 2017
papers.apa: Sanchez-Azanza, V. A., López-Penadés, R., Buil-Legaz, L., Aguilar-Mediavilla, E., & Adrover-Roig, D. (2017). Is bilingualism losing its advantage? A bibliometric approach. PLOS ONE, 12(4), e0176151. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0176151
papers.summary: This bibliometric study analyzed 189 papers (2005-2015) on bilingualism and cognitive control, classifying them by their stance on the bilingual advantage. It found a significant increase in papers challenging the advantage in 2014-2015, with these papers accumulating more citations than supporting ones in 2014 (e.g., ηp2 = .363 for paper category), suggesting a shift in publication trends and potential earlier publication bias.

papers.summary: This paper is generally cited for establishing the mechanisms of language switching in bilinguals, particularly the asymmetrical costs involved in switching between a dominant and non-dominant language. While foundational for understanding language control, its direct contribution to establishing a *bilingual advantage in non-verbal task switching* is not explicitly detailed in the title or provided information.

papers.summary: This study yielded mixed results regarding the bilingual advantage in non-verbal task switching. While some analyses showed a large effect (ηp2 = .74 for language group X trial type), other key measures like proportional switching costs and other language group X trial type interactions were non-significant, indicating an inconsistent pattern of findings.

papers.effect_name: Bilingual advantages in executive control - Non-verbal task switching

papers.summary: Across three studies, this research consistently found no evidence for a bilingual advantage in non-verbal task switching. The reported effect sizes for bilingual vs. monolingual differences were very small (ηp2 ranging from .000 to .014), leading to a conclusion that the effect was not replicated.

While some interactions showed substantial effects (e.g., ηp2 (CTI)=.892), the critical interaction between CTI and language group was small (ηp2 = .037) or non-significant in certain analyses. A language group advantage (d = .34) was found in the first half of trials but not the second, indicating inconsistent support for the effect.

papers.effect_name: Bilingual advantages in executive control - Non-verbal task switching
papers.title: Timmermeister et al. 2020
papers.summary: This study compared 27 monolingual and 27 bilingual children on a nonverbal switching task and found no significant between-group differences, with small effect sizes (ηp2 ranging from 0.03 to 0.13). The authors concluded that bilinguals do not have better cognitive control than monolinguals in this context. However, within the bilingual group, response times on language switching and nonverbal switching tasks were related, suggesting shared underlying cognitive control mechanisms.

papers.title: The effects of bilingualism on theory of mind development
papers.apa: GOETZ, P. J. (2003). The effects of bilingualism on theory of mind development. Bilingualism: Language and Cognition, 6(1), 1–15. https://doi.org/10.1017/s1366728903001007
papers.summary: This foundational paper is credited with establishing the bilingual advantage in Theory of Mind, suggesting that bilingual children outperform monolingual children on ToM tasks, particularly those involving unexpected transfer. The original effect size was reported as ηp2 = 0.06.

papers.title: Dahlgren et al. 2017
papers.apa: Dahlgren, S., Almén, H., & Dahlgren Sandberg, A. (2017). Theory of Mind and Executive Functions in Young Bilingual Children. The Journal of Genetic Psychology, 178(5), 303–307. https://doi.org/10.1080/00221325.2017.1361376
papers.summary: This study compared 14 young bilingual children with 14 monolingual children on ToM, executive function (EF), and language ability. While bilingual children showed significantly lower verbal ability, they outperformed monolinguals on EF tasks. However, the study found no differences in ToM performance between the groups, failing to replicate earlier findings, potentially due to the young age of participants.

papers.title: Diaz and Farrar 2018
papers.apa: Diaz, V., & Farrar, M. J. (2018). Do bilingual and monolingual preschoolers acquire false belief understanding similarly? The role of executive functioning and language. First Language, 38(4), 382–398. https://doi.org/10.1177/0142723717752741
papers.summary: This study compared 32 Spanish-English bilingual and 33 English monolingual preschoolers on false belief (FB) understanding, language proficiency, and executive functioning (EF). Monolinguals initially outperformed bilinguals on language, but after controlling for language, bilinguals showed superior FB reasoning (ηp2 = 0.063) and marginally better EF. General language ability predicted FB in both groups, while short-term memory and inhibitory control predicted FB only for monolinguals.

papers.title: Farhadian et al. 2010
papers.apa: Dahlgren, S., Almén, H., & Dahlgren Sandberg, A. (2017). Theory of Mind and Executive Functions in Young Bilingual Children. The Journal of Genetic Psychology, 178(5), 303–307. https://doi.org/10.1080/00221325.2017.1361376
papers.summary: Based on the provided (duplicate) abstract, this study compared 14 young bilingual children with 14 monolingual children on ToM, executive function (EF), and language ability. It found no differences in ToM performance between the groups, failing to replicate earlier findings, despite bilinguals outperforming monolinguals on EF tasks. This contradicts the reported replication effect size of d=0.40 for this paper.

papers.summary: This replication attempt reported a small effect size (d=0.123) for the bilingual advantage in Theory of Mind. Without further details, it suggests a weak or non-replication of the original effect.

papers.effect_name: Bilingual advantages - perspective taking in referential communication
papers.title: The exposure advantage: Early exposure to a multilingual environment promotes effective communication
papers.apa: Fan, S. P., Liberman, Z., Keysar, B., & Kinzler, K. D. (2015). The Exposure Advantage. Psychological Science, 26(7), 1090–1097. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797615574699
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether multilingual exposure enhances perspective-taking in children. They found that both bilingual children and children merely exposed to a multilingual environment significantly outperformed monolingual children on a perspective-taking task (bilingual vs. monolingual: d = 0.83). The authors proposed that these communicative advantages are social in origin, rather than solely attributable to enhanced executive control.

papers.effect_name: Bilingual advantages - perspective taking in referential communication
papers.title: Navarro and Conway 2021
papers.apa: Navarro, E., & Conway, A. R. (2021). Adult bilinguals outperform monolinguals in theory of mind. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 74(11), 1841–1851. https://doi.org/10.1177/17470218211009159
papers.summary: This study investigated the bilingual advantage in Theory of Mind (ToM) among adults using the director task. They found that bilingual adults outperformed monolinguals in perspective-dependent trials (experimental condition: d = -0.51, control condition: d = 0.29), suggesting an association between bilingualism and the ability to take another's perspective. The authors also noted that exposure to multiple cultures, independent of bilingualism, was linked to ToM performance.

papers.effect_name: Exposure to another language in social communication - perspective taking in referential communication
papers.title: The exposure advantage: Early exposure to a multilingual environment promotes effective communication
papers.apa: Fan, S. P., Liberman, Z., Keysar, B., & Kinzler, K. D. (2015). The Exposure Advantage. Psychological Science, 26(7), 1090–1097. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797615574699
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether early exposure to a multilingual environment enhances perspective-taking in children. They found that monolingual children performed significantly worse on a referential communication task requiring perspective-taking compared to both bilingual children and children merely exposed to a second language (d=0.74). The authors concluded that these communicative advantages are likely social in origin, rather than due to enhanced executive control.

papers.summary: This foundational study investigated non-verbal ideational fluency in 1408 primary school children in Singapore using the Torrance Non-Verbal Test of Creativity. It found that students learning in vernacular languages generally showed greater creative fluency than those in English medium, with several comparisons being statistically significant (e.g., Chinese III: Diff -5, CR -3.8, p < .01). The authors attributed this to the 'added burden of learning English' reducing creativity, even for non-verbal tasks.

papers.title: Kharkhurin 2017
papers.summary: This study investigated language mediated concept activation (LMCA) in bilingual memory and its relation to divergent thinking in 58 Russian-English bilinguals and 28 Russian monolinguals. Contrary to the 'disadvantage' hypothesis, bilinguals outperformed monolinguals on the divergent thinking trait of cognitive flexibility, and this performance was explained by the TLP effect. The findings suggest that bilingualism may facilitate cognitive flexibility rather than hinder fluency.

papers.title: Music and spatial task performance
papers.apa: McLachlan, J. C. (1993). Music and spatial task performance. Nature, 366(6455), 520–520. https://doi.org/10.1038/366520a0
papers.summary: This foundational study reported that college students who listened to Mozart's Sonata for Two Pianos in D Major (KV 448) for 10 minutes showed a temporary increase in spatial-temporal reasoning scores, specifically on the Stanford-Binet IQ test's paper-folding and cutting subtest, compared to those who listened to relaxation instructions or silence. The original study involved 36 college students and reported a significant effect (d=1.5), suggesting a temporary enhancement in cognitive ability.

papers.title: etschnig et al. 2010
papers.apa: Pietschnig, J., Voracek, M., & Formann, A. K. (2010). Mozart effect–Shmozart effect: A meta-analysis. Intelligence, 38(3), 314–323. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.intell.2010.03.001
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings from numerous studies investigating the Mozart effect, including a large number of participants. It concluded that while a small positive effect on spatial-temporal tasks might exist, it is considerably smaller than initially reported (meta-analytic d = 0.37 [0.23, 0.52]) and not indicative of a significant enhancement in general intelligence. The authors suggested that the effect is likely due to arousal and mood rather than a specific 'Mozart effect.'

papers.title: Steele et al. 1999a
papers.apa: Steele, K. M., Bella, S. D., Peretz, I., Dunlop, T., Dawe, L. A., Humphrey, G. K., Shannon, R. A., Kirby, J. L., & Olmstead, C. G. (1999). Prelude or requiem for the ‘Mozart effect’? Nature, 400(6747), 827–827. https://doi.org/10.1038/23611
papers.summary: This paper, likely a replication attempt or critical commentary, aimed to investigate the robustness of the Mozart effect. Without specific abstract content, it is inferred to contribute to the body of evidence regarding the effect's replicability, likely reporting findings that did not support the original claims or found a much smaller effect.

papers.title: Steele et al. 1999
papers.apa: Steele, K. M., Brown, J. D., & Stoecker, J. A. (1999). Failure to Confirm the Rauscher and Shaw Description of Recovery of the Mozart Effect. Perceptual and Motor Skills, 88(3), 843–848. https://doi.org/10.2466/pms.1999.88.3.843
papers.summary: This study, involving 206 college students, attempted to confirm Rauscher and Shaw's (1998) suggestion that a verbal distractor could prevent carryover effects from a pretest and recover the Mozart effect. The experiment found no significant difference in paper folding and cutting scores among groups exposed to different sequences of pretest, verbal distractor, and Mozart listening, thus failing to support the proposed recovery mechanism or the Mozart effect itself.

papers.title: How much does schooling influence general intelligence and its cognitive components? A reassessment of the evidence
papers.apa: Ceci, S. J. (1991). How much does schooling influence general intelligence and its cognitive components? A reassessment of the evidence. Developmental Psychology, 27(5), 703–722. https://doi.org/10.1037/0012-1649.27.5.703
papers.summary: This foundational paper likely reviewed existing evidence or proposed a theoretical framework suggesting a causal link between educational attainment and general intelligence. It would have laid the groundwork for the hypothesis that schooling actively enhances cognitive abilities, rather than merely reflecting pre-existing intelligence.

papers.title: Ritchie and Tucker-Drob 2018
papers.apa: Ritchie, S. J., & Tucker-Drob, E. M. (2018). How Much Does Education Improve Intelligence? A Meta-Analysis. Psychological Science, 29(8), 1358–1369. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797618774253
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings from 142 effect sizes across 42 datasets, involving over 600,000 participants, to investigate the causal impact of education on intelligence. It found consistent evidence that each additional year of education is associated with a gain of approximately 1 to 5 IQ points. The authors concluded that education is a robust and durable method for enhancing cognitive abilities across the lifespan.

papers.title: Akzel 2012
papers.apa: Aczel, B., Bago, B., & Foldes, A. (2012). Is there evidence for automatic imitation in a strategic context? Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences, 279(1741), 3231–3233. https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2012.0500
papers.summary: Aczel et al. (2012) replicated a study on automatic imitation in a strategic context (Rock-Paper-Scissors game) with double the original sample size. Their thorough examination of the original analyses and their own replication did not support the conclusion that automatic imitation occurs in this strategic context, finding the original finding problematic.

papers.apa: Aczel, B., Kekecs, Z., Bago, B., Szollosi, A., & Foldes, A. (2015). An empirical analysis of the methodology of automatic imitation research in a strategic context. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 41(4), 1049–1062. https://doi.org/10.1037/xhp0000081
papers.summary: Aczel et al. (2015) conducted an empirical analysis of automatic imitation methodology in strategic games (N=102). By video recording games, they found over 80% of data in previous studies were irrelevant due to methodological issues like gaze aversion or timing. After filtering, they found no evidence of automatic imitation, questioning the strength of prior findings and proposing methodological improvements.

papers.title: Brass et al. 2000
papers.apa: Brass, M., Bekkering, H., Wohlschläger, A., & Prinz, W. (2000). Compatibility between Observed and Executed Finger Movements: Comparing Symbolic, Spatial, and Imitative Cues. Brain and Cognition, 44(2), 124–143. https://doi.org/10.1006/brcg.2000.1225
papers.summary: Brass et al. (2000) conducted a series of experiments (e.g., N=8 in Exp 1) using a stimulus-response compatibility paradigm to demonstrate automatic imitation. They found that observed finger movements strongly influenced movement execution, leading to significant facilitation (27ms) and interference (29ms) effects when congruent or incongruent with a symbolic cue. The study concluded that observed movements have a stronger, more automatic influence on execution than symbolic or spatial cues, establishing key evidence for the effect.

papers.title: Cracco et al. 2018
papers.apa: Cracco, E., Bardi, L., Desmet, C., Genschow, O., Rigoni, D., De Coster, L., Radkova, I., Deschrijver, E., & Brass, M. (2018). Automatic imitation: A meta-analysis. Psychological Bulletin, 144(5), 453–500. https://doi.org/10.1037/bul0000143
papers.summary: Cracco et al. (2018) conducted a meta-analysis on automatic imitation, synthesizing findings from numerous studies to provide a comprehensive overview of the effect's robustness and moderators. The meta-analysis reported a significant overall effect size of gz = 0.95 [0.88, 1.02], indicating a robust automatic imitation effect across studies.

papers.title: Semantic priming and retrieval from lexical memory: Roles of inhibitionless spreading activation and limited-capacity attention
papers.apa: Neely, J. H. (1977). Semantic priming and retrieval from lexical memory: Roles of inhibitionless spreading activation and limited-capacity attention. Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 106(3), 226–254. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-3445.106.3.226
papers.summary: This paper is listed as foundational, likely contributing to the initial understanding or theoretical underpinnings of cognitive control mechanisms. Without an abstract or extracted text, specific findings cannot be summarized.

papers.title: Aczel et al. 2021
papers.apa:
Aczel, B., Kovacs, M., Bognar, M., Palfi, B., Hartanto, A., Onie, S., Tiong, L. E., & Evans, T. R. (2021). Is there evidence for cross-domain congruency sequence effect? A replication of Kan
                    et al
                    . (2013). Royal Society Open Science, 8(3). https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.191353
papers.summary: This multi-site replication of Kan et al. (2013) investigated the cross-domain congruency sequence effect across syntactic to non-syntactic and perceptual to verbal domains. Across three experiments, the authors found only partial support for the original claims, with most results being inconclusive or contradicting the theoretical hypothesis. Reaction time analyses showed ηp2 = 0.00 to 0.02, and accuracy analyses showed ηp2 = 0.00 to 0.04, indicating a lack of robust evidence for the effect.

papers.title: Gratton et al. 1992
papers.apa: Torrance, J. D., & Bartlett, G. R. (1970). Altitude hypoxia and erythrocyte phosphates. Biochimica et Biophysica Acta (BBA) - General Subjects, 215(2), 409–411. https://doi.org/10.1016/0304-4165(70)90041-3
papers.summary: This paper is widely considered foundational for establishing the congruency sequence effect (Gratton effect). It likely demonstrated that congruency effects are smaller following incongruent trials compared to congruent trials, with reported effect sizes for compatible vs. incompatible trials ranging from ηp2 = 0.88 to 0.94 for RT and ηp2 = 0.59 to 0.98 for error rate.

papers.title: Gyurkovics et al. 2020
papers.apa: Kan, I. P., Teubner-Rhodes, S., Drummey, A. B., Nutile, L., Krupa, L., & Novick, J. M. (2013). To adapt or not to adapt: The question of domain-general cognitive control. Cognition, 129(3), 637–651. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2013.09.001
papers.summary: This entry, containing content from Kan et al. (2013), reports novel findings on domain-general cognitive control, demonstrating conflict adaptation from syntactic to non-syntactic domains and from perceptual to verbal domains. The authors propose that conflict detection triggers general-purpose cognitive control mechanisms that enhance subsequent conflict resolution. They found significant cross-task adaptation in accuracy and reaction time, with ηp2 values ranging from 0.14 to 0.57 for accuracy and 0.19 to 0.46 for RT in Stroop tasks.

papers.title: Kan et al. 2013
papers.apa: Kan, I. P., Teubner-Rhodes, S., Drummey, A. B., Nutile, L., Krupa, L., & Novick, J. M. (2013). To adapt or not to adapt: The question of domain-general cognitive control. Cognition, 129(3), 637–651. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2013.09.001
papers.summary: This paper reports novel findings on domain-general cognitive control, demonstrating conflict adaptation from syntactic to non-syntactic domains and from perceptual to verbal domains. The authors propose that conflict detection triggers general-purpose cognitive control mechanisms that enhance subsequent conflict resolution. They found significant cross-task adaptation in accuracy and reaction time, with ηp2 values ranging from 0.14 to 0.57 for accuracy and 0.19 to 0.46 for RT in Stroop tasks.

papers.title: Grounding language in action
papers.apa: Glenberg, A. M., & Kaschak, M. P. (2002). Grounding language in action. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 9(3), 558–565. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03196313
papers.summary: Glenberg and Kaschak (2002) introduced the Action-sentence Compatibility Effect (ACE), demonstrating that participants responded faster to sentences when the implied action direction matched their physical response direction. Across three experiments (N=44, N=70, N=72), they found a significant ACE (e.g., Exp 1: ηp2=0.186; Exp 2A: ηp2=0.051) when physical movement was required. The authors interpreted these findings as strong evidence for an embodied theory of language comprehension, where meaning is grounded in action simulation.

papers.title: Morey et al. 2022
papers.apa: Morey, R. D., Kaschak, M. P., Díez-Álamo, A. M., Glenberg, A. M., Zwaan, R. A., Lakens, D., Ibáñez, A., García, A., Gianelli, C., Jones, J. L., Madden, J., Alifano, F., Bergen, B., Bloxsom, N. G., Bub, D. N., Cai, Z. G., Chartier, C. R., Chatterjee, A., Conwell, E., … Ziv-Crispel, N. (2021). A pre-registered, multi-lab non-replication of the action-sentence compatibility effect (ACE). Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 29(2), 613–626. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-021-01927-8
papers.summary: Morey et al. (2022) conducted a pre-registered, multi-lab replication of the ACE across 18 labs (N=4,493, as per prompt's replication effect size section). Their findings showed no reliable ACE in any individual lab, with a meta-analytic estimate of the effect size being essentially zero (d=0.0036 for native English speakers). The authors concluded that the original finding was likely not robust and did not replicate.

papers.title: Some consequences of having too little
papers.apa: Shah, A. K., Mullainathan, S., & Shafir, E. (2012). Some Consequences of Having Too Little. Science, 338(6107), 682–685. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1222426
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that scarcity imposes a cognitive burden, leading to poorer choices by biasing cognitive processes. Through laboratory experiments, it provided evidence that being poor biases cognitive processes. Specifically, Study 1 (Wheel of Fortune task) found that scarcity-induced focus led to cognitive fatigue, measured by poorer performance on a subsequent cognitive ability task (original r = .267).

papers.title: Camerer et al. 2018
papers.apa: Camerer, C. F., Dreber, A., Holzmeister, F., Ho, T.-H., Huber, J., Johannesson, M., Kirchler, M., Nave, G., Nosek, B. A., Pfeiffer, T., Altmejd, A., Buttrick, N., Chan, T., Chen, Y., Forsell, E., Gampa, A., Heikensten, E., Hummer, L., Imai, T., … Wu, H. (2018). Evaluating the replicability of social science experiments in Nature and Science between 2010 and 2015. Nature Human Behaviour, 2(9), 637–644. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-018-0399-z
papers.summary: This large-scale replication project attempted to replicate 21 social science experiments, including one from Shah et al. (2012). For the scarcity effect's cognitive fatigue component (Study 1), they found no significant effect in the same direction as the original (r = -0.015), suggesting a failure to replicate this specific finding. Overall, they found 62% of studies replicated, with effect sizes about 50% of original.

E., Foushee, R., Jansen, R., Jarvis, S. N., Lundell-Creagh, R., Ocampo, J. M., Okafor, G. N., Azad, Z. R., Rosenblum, M., … Nelson, L. D. (2021). Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: This paper conducted an empirical audit and review of research on the psychological consequences of scarcity, replicating 20 studies from the field. Their replication of the cognitive fatigue aspect of the Shah et al. (2012) study found no evidence for the effect (r = -0.039). This suggests a failure to replicate this specific finding within their broader audit, despite variability in replicability across other scarcity effects.

papers.title: Shah et al. 2019
papers.apa: Shah, A. K., Mullainathan, S., & Shafir, E. (2019). An exercise in self-replication: Replicating Shah, Mullainathan, and Shafir (2012). Journal of Economic Psychology, 75, 102127. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joep.2018.12.001
papers.summary: This paper presents high-powered self-replications of all studies from Shah et al. (2012), with target sample sizes of 250 participants per cell (e.g., 997 for Experiment 1). For Experiment 1 (cognitive fatigue), they found no evidence for the original hypothesis, with results running opposite (r = -0.007, original ηp2 = .031 vs replication ηp2 = .001). They concluded that more work is needed to understand conditions for scarcity-induced depletion, though other aspects of scarcity did replicate.

papers.title: Abeyta, A. A., Routledge, C., Kersten, M., & Cox, C. R. (2017). The existential cost of economic insecurity: Threatened financial security undercuts meaning. The Journal of Social Psychology, 157(6), 692–702. https://doi.org/10.1080/00224545.2016.1270892
papers.apa: Abeyta, A. A., Routledge, C., Kersten, M., & Cox, C. R. (2017). The existential cost of economic insecurity: Threatened financial security undercuts meaning. The Journal of Social Psychology, 157(6), 692–702. https://doi.org/10.1080/00224545.2016.1270892
papers.summary: This foundational paper investigated the link between financial security and meaning in life across three studies. Study 1 (N=131) found that financial insecurity predicted lower meaning in life, independent of income (r=.180). Studies 2 and 3 experimentally demonstrated that threats to financial security (e.g., recession salience, government shutdown) causally reduced perceptions of meaning in life, with Study 2 reporting a small effect (η² = .03).

papers.summary: This paper presents an empirical audit and review of research on the psychological consequences of scarcity, including replications of 20 studies. While the abstract notes variability in replicability across studies, the replication of the scarcity-meaning in life effect (Abeyta et al., 2017) within this audit yielded an effect size of r = .055, suggesting a substantially smaller effect than originally reported.

papers.title: Stuck in time: Negative income shock constricts the temporal window of valuation spanning the future and the past
papers.apa: Bickel, W. K., Wilson, A. G., Chen, C., Koffarnus, M. N., & Franck, C. T. (2016). Stuck in Time: Negative Income Shock Constricts the Temporal Window of Valuation Spanning the Future and the Past. PLOS ONE, 11(9), e0163051. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0163051
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated how acute changes in resource availability affect delay discounting. Using a sample of 599 mTurk participants, the authors found that a hypothetical negative income shock significantly increased discounting rates for both future and past monetary gains and losses. They proposed that income reduction constricts the temporal window of valuation, leading to more short-term choices, reporting an original effect size of r = 0.480.

Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: This paper presents an empirical audit and review of research on the psychological consequences of scarcity, including replications of 20 studies. While the abstract does not detail findings for each specific effect, the provided data indicates that for the scarcity effect on discounting, their replication found a substantially smaller effect size of r = 0.104. The authors noted considerable variability in replicability across the audited studies.

papers.title: Chou, E. Y., Parmar, B. L., & Galinsky, A. D. (2016). Economic Insecurity Increases Physical Pain. Psychological Science, 27(4), 443–454. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797615625640
papers.apa: Chou, E. Y., Parmar, B. L., & Galinsky, A. D. (2016). Economic Insecurity Increases Physical Pain. Psychological Science, 27(4), 443–454. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797615625640
papers.summary: This foundational paper established the link between economic insecurity and physical pain, demonstrating in five experimental studies that insecurity increases pain and reduces pain tolerance. A sixth study with N=33,720 households found economic insecurity predicted over-the-counter painkiller use. The authors proposed that a psychological experience of lacking control mediates this link, with meta-analyses of their studies supporting a reliable connection (original r=.155).

papers.summary: This paper conducted an empirical audit and review, including replications of 20 studies on the psychological consequences of scarcity, one of which targeted the economic insecurity-physical pain link. While the overall audit found variable replicability across different scarcity effects, the provided replication effect size (r=.209) for the physical pain effect suggests a successful replication, even stronger than the original (r=.155). The authors emphasize that their method facilitates assessing evidence strength for specific effects.

papers.title: Knowing who you are and adding to it: Reduced self-concept clarity predicts reduced self-expansion
papers.apa: Emery, L. F., Walsh, C., & Slotter, E. B. (2014). Knowing Who You Are and Adding to It. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 6(3), 259–266. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550614555029
papers.summary: This foundational research proposed that lower self-concept clarity reduces an individual's desire for self-expansion. Across three studies, the authors demonstrated that individuals primed with low self-concept clarity expressed less interest in expanding their self-concept, both outside of romantic relationships and by incorporating attributes from a potential partner. The original effect size for this relationship was reported as r = 0.257.

Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: This paper presents an empirical audit and review of research on the psychological consequences of scarcity, including replications of 20 studies. While the overall audit found variable replicability across different effects, the replication attempt for the self-concept clarity and self-expansion effect yielded an effect size of r = -0.004. This finding suggests a lack of support for the original effect.

papers.title: Reframing the ordinary: Imagining time as scarce increases well-being
papers.apa: Layous, K., Kurtz, J., Chancellor, J., & Lyubomirsky, S. (2017). Reframing the ordinary: Imagining time as scarce increases well-being. The Journal of Positive Psychology, 13(3), 301–308. https://doi.org/10.1080/17439760.2017.1279210
papers.summary: This foundational study (N=139 undergraduate students) investigated whether imagining time as scarce could increase well-being. Participants in the 'live this month like it's your last' condition showed steeper gains in well-being over 4 weeks compared to a control group, with an increase of nearly half a standard deviation (d=0.46). This effect was mediated by increased psychological need satisfaction (connectedness, competence, autonomy).

papers.summary: This paper describes a large-scale 'Empirical audit and review' project that replicated 20 studies on the psychological consequences of scarcity. The authors reported variable replicability across the studies, with some successes and some failures, aiming to assess evidentiary value in the field.

papers.title: Poverty impedes cognitive function
papers.apa: Mani, A., Mullainathan, S., Shafir, E., & Zhao, J. (2013). Poverty Impedes Cognitive Function. Science, 341(6149), 976–980. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1238041
papers.summary: This foundational study proposed that poverty impedes cognitive function by imposing a cognitive load. Through experiments with New Jersey shoppers and Tamil Nadu farmers, the authors found that financial concerns (e.g., expensive car repairs) led lower-income individuals to perform worse on cognitive tasks, while higher-income individuals were unaffected. Farmers also performed better on cognitive tasks post-harvest when financially less constrained, with an original effect size of r = 0.205.

papers.summary: This paper conducted an empirical audit and review of 20 studies on the psychological consequences of scarcity, including replications across various domains like pain sensitivity and resource allocation. The audit found considerable variability in replicability, with both successes and failures. While the abstract does not detail specific replication outcomes for the Mani et al. (2013) effect, the provided replication effect size of r = 0.024 for this effect suggests a failure to replicate this specific finding within the broader audit.

papers.title: Evidence for opportunity cost neglect in the poor
papers.apa: Plantinga, A., Krijnen, J. M. T., Zeelenberg, M., & Breugelmans, S. M. (2017). Evidence for Opportunity Cost Neglect in the Poor. Journal of Behavioral Decision Making, 31(1), 65–73. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/bdm.2041
papers.summary: Plantinga et al. (2017) investigated the hypothesis that poor individuals spontaneously consider opportunity costs more often. Across five high-powered experiments (total N = 2325), they found no support for this claim. Both high-income and low-income participants showed an equally strong decrease in willingness to buy when reminded of opportunity costs, suggesting that both groups equally neglect them. The original effect size reported for the overall effect was r = -0.03.

papers.summary: O’Donnell et al. (2021) conducted an empirical audit and review, including replications of 20 studies on the psychological consequences of scarcity. For the specific effect concerning opportunity cost neglect in the poor, their replication yielded an effect size of r = -0.006. The overall project found considerable variability in replicability across the diverse set of scarcity-related studies.

papers.title: Money in the mental lives of the poor
papers.apa: Shah, A. K., Zhao, J., Mullainathan, S., & Shafir, E. (2018). Money in the Mental Lives of the Poor. Social Cognition, 36(1), 4–19. https://doi.org/10.1521/soco.2018.36.1.4
papers.summary: Shah et al. (2018) investigated how financial scarcity influences the content of cognition. Across four scenarios, they found that lower-income participants (N=495) were significantly more likely to report thoughts about cost-related items (d = 0.19, r = 0.111) compared to higher-income participants, even in everyday situations not explicitly involving financial decisions. This suggests that financial concerns intrude more readily into the consciousness of the poor.

papers.summary: O’Donnell et al. (2021) conducted an empirical audit and review of 20 studies on the psychological consequences of scarcity, including a replication of the conscious thoughts effect. While their overall audit found variable replicability across studies, for this specific effect, they reported a replication effect size of r = 0.027. This indicates a much smaller effect than originally reported, contributing to the 'not replicated' status.

papers.title: Scarcity frames value
papers.apa: Shah, A. K., Shafir, E., & Mullainathan, S. (2015). Scarcity Frames Value. Psychological Science, 26(4), 402–412. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797614563958
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept that scarcity frames value, suggesting it makes individuals less susceptible to classic context effects by focusing attention on pressing needs and trade-offs. While the abstract broadly discusses how scarcity influences cognition, one specific finding reported was that poorer individuals view losses more absolutely than wealthier individuals, with an original effect size of r = .264.

papers.summary: This paper presents an empirical audit and review of research on the psychological consequences of scarcity, conducting replications of 20 studies across various domains. While the audit found variable replicability across studies, their replication of the 'absoluteness of losses' effect found a substantially smaller effect size of r = .090. This suggests that the original finding for this specific effect did not robustly replicate.

papers.title: ‘Bottomless bowls: why visual cues of portion size may influence intake
papers.apa: Wansink, B., Painter, J. E., & North, J. (2005). Bottomless Bowls: Why Visual Cues of Portion Size May Influence Intake**. Obesity Research, 13(1), 93–100. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1038/oby.2005.12
papers.summary: This foundational study by Wansink et al. (2007) investigated the impact of visual cues on food intake. Participants unknowingly eating from continuously refilling 'bottomless' bowls consumed 73% more soup (256g vs 143g) than those with normal bowls, without reporting increased satiety. The authors concluded that external visual cues, rather than internal physiological signals, primarily regulate food intake (N=54).
