papers.title: Heathers 2018
papers.apa: Herman, C. P. (2005). Lessons from the Bottomless Bowl. Obesity Research, 13(1), 2–2. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1038/oby.2005.2
papers.summary: Heathers (2018) likely refers to critical analyses or commentaries, often co-authored with Nick Brown, that scrutinized the methodology and statistical reporting of Wansink's work, including the 'bottomless soup bowl' study. These critiques frequently highlight inconsistencies, implausible data patterns, and potential data fabrication, casting significant doubt on the validity and reliability of the original findings.

papers.title: Choice reaction time as a function of angular stimulus-response correspondence and age
papers.apa: SIMON, J. R., & WOLF, J. D. (1963). CHOICE REACTION TIME AS A FUNCTION OF ANGULAR STIMULUS-RESPONSE CORRESPONDENCE AND AGE. Ergonomics, 6(1), 99–105. https://doi.org/10.1080/00140136308930679
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated choice reaction time as a function of stimulus-response correspondence and age across younger (20-30) and older (65-86) groups. Results indicated significant differences in reaction time based on display angle, with responses slowing by 30% in the least compatible (180°) condition, demonstrating the Simon effect. The authors discussed the role of spatial cues in translating display information into control action.

papers.title: Ehrenstein 1994
papers.apa: Ehrenstein, W. H. (1994). The Simon effect and visual motion. Psychological Research, 56(3), 163–169. https://doi.org/10.1007/bf00419703
papers.summary: This study explored S-R compatibility and Simon effects using real visual motion across two experiments, each with 12 subjects. While strong compatibility effects were observed, a Simon effect only occurred when stimulus position was task-irrelevant in Experiment 1, and was absent in Experiment 2. The findings suggest that the Simon effect may depend on attention orienting triggered by saccade programs, not extending to pursuit programs.

papers.title: Marble and Proctor 2000
papers.apa: Herman, C. P. (2005). Lessons from the Bottomless Bowl. Obesity Research, 13(1), 2–2. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1038/oby.2005.2
papers.summary: This paper's content was not provided. However, it is referenced by Proctor et al. (2000) as having found a 'reverse Simon effect'.

papers.title: Proctor et al. 2000
papers.apa: Proctor, R. W., Gerred Marble, J., & Vu, K.-P. L. (2000). Mixing incompatibly mapped location-relevant trials with location-irrelevant trials: effects of stimulus mode on the reverse Simon effect. Psychological Research, 64(1), 11–24. https://doi.org/10.1007/s004260000041
papers.summary: This research replicated and extended findings on the 'reverse Simon effect,' where responses are faster for spatially non-corresponding stimuli under specific conditions. Across four experiments with 64 participants, they found the reverse effect when location information was presented consistently (e.g., both spatial or both verbal), but it was absent when presentation modes differed between location-relevant and location-irrelevant trials. This implies that short-term stimulus-response associations are relatively mode-specific.

papers.title: Theeuwes et al. 2014
papers.apa: Marble, J. G., Proctor, R. W., & Epp, D. G. (1997). Influence of location mapping on the Simon effect [dataset]. In PsycEXTRA Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/e536982012-318
papers.summary: This paper's content was not provided, but reported effect sizes (ηp² ranging from .07 to .21) suggest that the Simon effect was observed under various conditions related to compatible/incompatible S-R instructions.

papers.title: Late Vertex Positivity in Event-Related Potentials as a Guilty Knowledge Indicator: A New Method of Lie Detection
papers.apa: Rosenfeld, J. P., Nasman, V. T., Whalen, R., Cantwell, B., & Mazzeri, L. (1987). Late Vertex Positivity in Event-Related Potentials as a Guilty Knowledge Indicator: A New Method of Lie Detection. International Journal of Neuroscience, 34(1–2), 125–129. https://doi.org/10.3109/00207458708985947
papers.summary: This foundational study (N=10 experimental, N=6 control) demonstrated that subjects showed distinct positive waves (400-700 ms, P300-like) in their ERPs to chosen, crime-related items compared to novel items (p < .001). The authors proposed this P300 response as an indicator of 'guilty knowledge' in a lie detection paradigm.

papers.title: Abootalebi et al. 2006
papers.apa: Abootalebi, V., Moradi, M. H., & Khalilzadeh, M. A. (2006). A comparison of methods for ERP assessment in a P300-based GKT. International Journal of Psychophysiology, 62(2), 309–320. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijpsycho.2006.05.009
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Bergström et al. 2013
papers.apa: Bergström, Z. M., Anderson, M. C., Buda, M., Simons, J. S., & Richardson-Klavehn, A. (2013). Intentional retrieval suppression can conceal guilty knowledge in ERP memory detection tests. Biological Psychology, 94(1), 1–11. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biopsycho.2013.04.012
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Mertens & Allen 2008
papers.apa: Mertens, R., & Allen, J. J. B. (2007). The role of psychophysiology in forensic assessments: Deception detection, ERPs, and virtual reality mock crime scenarios. Psychophysiology, 45(2), 286–298. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-8986.2007.00615.x
papers.summary: This study replicated and extended previous work using a virtual reality crime scenario, finding that guilty subjects were detected significantly less frequently than in prior studies, and countermeasures further reduced hit rates. The authors concluded that while guilty verdicts might be accurate, innocent or indeterminate verdicts from ERP-based deception detection are not useful in applied settings.

papers.title: Rosenfeld et al. 2004
papers.apa: Rosenfeld, J. P., Soskins, M., Bosh, G., & Ryan, A. (2004). Simple, effective countermeasures to P300‐based tests of detection of concealed information. Psychophysiology, 41(2), 205–219. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1469-8986.2004.00158.x
papers.summary: This research demonstrated that P300-based concealed information tests can be readily defeated by simple countermeasures, such as covertly responding to irrelevant stimuli. Hit rates for detecting concealed information dropped significantly (e.g., from 82% to 18% and 92% to 50%) when countermeasures were employed, indicating a vulnerability in the method.

papers.title: Wang et al. 2016
papers.apa: Review Wang et al., 2016. (2016). https://doi.org/10.5194/tc-2016-38-rc1
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Of Two Minds: Forming and Changing Valence-Inconsistent Implicit and Explicit Attitudes
papers.apa: Rydell, R. J., McConnell, A. R., Mackie, D. M., & Strain, L. M. (2006). Of Two Minds. Psychological Science, 17(11), 954–958. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01811.x
papers.summary: This foundational study hypothesized that implicit and explicit attitudes are differentially sensitive to information. They found that explicit attitudes were influenced by consciously accessible behavioral information (η²=0.71, d=1.54), while implicit attitudes were influenced by subliminally presented primes (η²=0.13, d=0.38). This led to the formation of conflicting implicit and explicit attitudes when the two types of information had opposite valences.

papers.title: Heycke et al. 2018
papers.apa: Heycke, T., Gehrmann, S., Haaf, J. M., & Stahl, C. (2018). Of two minds or one? A registered replication of Rydell et al. (2006). Cognition and Emotion, 32(8), 1708–1727. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699931.2018.1429389
papers.summary: This registered replication (N not specified in provided text) aimed to test the differential influence on implicit and explicit attitudes. In Experiment 1, they replicated the explicit attitude effect (η²=0.757, d=1.75) but found the implicit attitude effect was reversed (η²=0.075, d=0.28), with implicit attitudes aligning with explicit information. Experiment 2, using original instructions and US students, again replicated the explicit effect (η²=0.828, d=2.17) but found the implicit effect reversed (η²=0.102, d=0.33), concluding that subliminal primes did not affect implicit evaluations as originally claimed.

papers.title: ‘Memory in a monolingual mode: When are bilinguals at a disadvantage?’
papers.apa: Ransdell, S. E., & Fischler, I. (1987). Memory in a monolingual mode: When are bilinguals at a disadvantage? Journal of Memory and Language, 26(4), 392–405. https://doi.org/10.1016/0749-596x(87)90098-2
papers.summary: This foundational paper is presumed to have established the initial evidence for a bilingual deficit in lexical retrieval, comparing bilinguals and monolinguals on tasks requiring word access. Specific findings, sample sizes, and effect sizes are not available from the provided content.

papers.title: Bialystok et al. 2007
papers.apa: Bialystok, E., Craik, F. I. M., & Luk, G. (2008). Lexical access in bilinguals: Effects of vocabulary size and executive control. Journal of Neurolinguistics, 21(6), 522–538. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jneuroling.2007.07.001
papers.summary: This paper is classified as a critique or replication attempt of the bilingual lexical retrieval deficit. Specific findings, sample sizes, and effect sizes are not available from the provided content.

papers.title: Gollan et al. 2002
papers.apa: Figure 2: Forest plot showing impact of paternal HBV infection on clinical pregnancy rate (per women) (Bu et al., 2014; Kong et al., 2017; Lubis et al., 2018; Meng et al., 2024; Shi et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2021; Ye et al., 2014; Zhao et al., 2007; Zheng et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2024). (n.d.). https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.19824/fig-2
papers.summary: This paper is classified as a critique or replication attempt of the bilingual lexical retrieval deficit. Specific findings, sample sizes, and effect sizes are not available from the provided content.

papers.title: Gollan et al. 2005
papers.apa: Gollan, T. H., Montoya, R. I., Fennema-Notestine, C., & Morris, S. K. (2005). Bilingualism affects picture naming but not picture classification. Memory & Cognition, 33(7), 1220–1234. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03193224
papers.summary: This study (N=62, 31 monolinguals, 31 bilinguals) found that English-dominant bilinguals were slower and made more errors than monolinguals in picture naming, even after repeated presentations, supporting a lexical retrieval deficit. However, no differences were found in picture classification, suggesting the deficit is at a post-conceptual, lexical access level. Interestingly, knowing a word in both languages facilitated retrieval in the target language for bilinguals.

papers.title: Rosselli et al. 2000
papers.apa: Rosselli, M., Ardila, A., Araujo, K., Weekes, V. A., Caracciolo, V., Padilla, M., & Ostrosky-Solí, F. (2000). Verbal Fluency and Repetition Skills in Healthy Older Spanish-English Bilinguals. Applied Neuropsychology, 7(1), 17–24. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15324826an0701_3
papers.summary: This study (N=82 older adults, 45 English monolinguals, 18 Spanish monolinguals, 19 bilinguals) found that bilinguals performed similarly to monolinguals on most verbal fluency and repetition tasks, but showed a deficit in semantic verbal fluency (generating fewer words in categories like 'animals' or 'fruits'). The authors suggest task demands, language interference, and level of bilingualism as influencing factors.

papers.title: Rosselli et al. 2002
papers.apa: ROSSELLI, M., ARDILA, A., SALVATIERRA, J., MARQUEZ, M., LUIS, M., & WEEKES, V. A. (2002). A CROSS-LINGUISTIC COMPARISON OF VERBAL FLUENCY TESTS. International Journal of Neuroscience, 112(6), 759–776. https://doi.org/10.1080/00207450290025752
papers.summary: Using the same sample (N=82 older adults) as their 2000 study, this paper further explored verbal fluency, finding that bilinguals produced significantly fewer words in semantic fluency tasks (e.g., 'animals' in English) compared to English monolinguals. However, no such deficit was observed in phonemic fluency tasks. The authors attributed this to potential language interference in semantic tasks involving concrete nouns.

papers.title: Nostalgia: A Psychological Perspective
papers.apa: Batcho, K. I. (1995). Nostalgia: A Psychological Perspective. Perceptual and Motor Skills, 80(1), 131–143. https://doi.org/10.2466/pms.1995.80.1.131
papers.summary: This foundational study surveyed 648 respondents to explore the psychological nature of nostalgia, which was previously viewed as a disease. It found that nostalgia is related to a favorable perception of the past (regression coefficient = 0.22, p < .0001) and is a multifaceted construct composed of several factors. The study laid the groundwork for understanding nostalgia as a complex, positive emotional experience.

papers.title: Wildschut et al. 2006
papers.apa: Wildschut, T., Sedikides, C., Arndt, J., & Routledge, C. (2006). Nostalgia: Content, triggers, functions. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 91(5), 975–993. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.91.5.975
papers.summary: This paper, through seven diverse studies (e.g., N=42 narrative analysis, N=172 undergraduates, N=62 undergraduates), explored the content, triggers, and functions of nostalgia. It found that nostalgic experiences feature the self in interactions with close others, contain more positive than negative affect (e.g., ηp2 = 0.783 for narrative affect), and often depict redemption. The studies also established that nostalgia is triggered by negative mood and loneliness, and functions to bolster social bonds (ηp2 = 0.205), increase positive self-regard (ηp2 = 0.238), and generate positive affect (ηp2 = 0.139).

papers.title: Memory: A contribution to experimental psychology
papers.apa: Ebbinghaus, H. (1913). Memory: A contribution to experimental psychology. (H. A. Ruger & C. E. Bussenius, Trans.). Teachers College Press. https://doi.org/10.1037/10011-000
papers.summary: Hermann Ebbinghaus's pioneering work laid the foundation for the scientific study of memory, including the initial empirical observations of the spacing effect. Through systematic self-experimentation with nonsense syllables, he demonstrated that distributing learning over time led to better retention than massed practice, establishing a fundamental principle of memory.

papers.title: Cepeda et al. 2006
papers.apa: Figure 2—source data 3. Examples of [2Fe-2S], [3Fe-4S], and [4Fe-4S] cluster binding motifs that are not found in Cgr2 (Zhang et al., 2010; Nakamaru-Ogiso et al., 2002; Lee et al., 2004; Pandelia et al., 2011; Schnackerz et al., 2004; Leech et al., 2003; Gorodetsky et al., 2008; Lee et al., 2010; Weiner et al., 2007; Klinge et al., 2007; Dickert et al., 2002; Conover et al., 1990; Schneider and Schmidt, 2005; Iwasaki et al., 2000; Banci et al., 2013; Dailey and Dailey, 2002; Jung et al., 2000). (n.d.). https://doi.org/10.7554/elife.33953.013
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized a large body of research on the spacing effect, providing a comprehensive quantitative review of its magnitude and boundary conditions. The authors reported a substantial average effect size (Cohen’s d = 0.567) for the difference in accuracy between massed and spaced learning trials in verbal recall tasks, confirming the robust nature of the effect across various paradigms.

papers.title: Janiszewski et al. 2003
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., Taylor, D., & Garrity, G. M. (2010). Exemplar Abstract for Mycobacterium massiliense Adékambi et al. 2006, Mycobacteroides abscessus massiliense (Adékambi et al. 2006) Gupta et al. 2018, Mycobacterium abscessus massiliense (Adékambi et al. 2006) Tortoli et al. 2016 emend. Nouioui et al. 2018 and Mycobacterium abscessus bolletii (Adékambi et al. 2006) Leao et al. 2011 emend. Nouioui et al. 2018. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.10322
papers.summary: This paper investigated the relationship between the spacing of learning events and recall accuracy, particularly in the context of consumer memory. The findings indicated a significant positive relationship, with a linear effect (ηp2 = 0.093) and a log relationship (ηp2 = 0.051) between the number of lags between learning events and recall, further supporting the generalizability of the spacing effect.

papers.title: Reconstruction of Automobile Destruction: An Example of the Interaction Between Language and Memory
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated how language used in questioning can influence eyewitness memory. Participants watched films of car accidents and were then asked about the speed of the cars, with the verb in the question (e.g., 'smashed,' 'hit,' 'contacted') manipulated. The study found that the verb used significantly affected speed estimates and subsequent recall of non-existent details, suggesting that post-event information can reconstruct memories.

papers.title: Goldschmied et al. 2016
papers.apa: Goldschmied, N., Sheptock, M., Kim, K., & Galily, Y. (2017). Appraising Loftus and Palmer (1974) Post-Event Information versus Concurrent Commentary in the Context of Sport. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 70(11), 2347–2356. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470218.2016.1237980
papers.summary: This study conducted two experiments examining framing effects in sport, including a conceptual replication of Loftus & Palmer (1974). Experiment 1, which replicated the post-event information paradigm, found no significant differences in participants' reports of skating speed, intentions, or outcome based on the verb used (η2 = .00 to .02). Experiment 2, using concurrent commentary, found some effects, but the core post-event information effect was not replicated.

papers.title: Salovich et al. 2020, unpublished replication
papers.summary: This unpublished replication attempt investigated the influence of question phrasing on memory. The study reported small effect sizes (d = 0.07 and d = 0.22) for the impact of question phrasing, indicating a lack of replication for the original effect. The authors concluded that the effect of question phrasing was not reliably present in their findings.

papers.title: Winter and Marmolejo 2001
papers.apa: Manolopoulos, K. N., Karpe, F., & Frayn, K. N. (2010). Response to Janiszewski et al. International Journal of Obesity, 34(6), 1101–1101. https://doi.org/10.1038/ijo.2010.82
papers.summary: This replication study examined the effect of leading questions on eyewitness testimony. The authors reported a very small and negative effect size (ηp2 = -.02), which is inconsistent with the original findings and suggests no evidence for the effect of question phrasing on memory distortion. This indicates a failure to replicate the original phenomenon.

papers.title: ‘Context-dependent memory in two natural environments: On land and underwater’
papers.apa: GODDEN, D. R., & BADDELEY, A. D. (1975). CONTEXT‐DEPENDENT MEMORY IN TWO NATURAL ENVIRONMENTS: ON LAND AND UNDERWATER. British Journal of Psychology, 66(3), 325–331. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2044-8295.1975.tb01468.x
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated context-dependent memory in divers learning word lists on land or underwater. Participants recalled words best when the learning and recall environments matched. The study concluded that environmental context significantly influences free recall, with an estimated original effect size of dz = 1.35.

papers.title: Godden and Baddeley 1980
papers.apa: Godden, D., & Baddeley, A. (1980). When does context influence recognition memory? British Journal of Psychology, 71(1), 99–104. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2044-8295.1980.tb02735.x
papers.summary: This study explored whether context influences recognition memory, using the same land/underwater environments as their previous work. In contrast to their earlier recall study, the authors found no evidence of context dependency for recognition performance. This suggests that environmental context effects might be specific to recall tasks rather than recognition.

papers.title: Isarida et al. 2012
papers.apa: Isarida, T., Isarida, T. K., & Sakai, T. (2012). Effects of study time and meaningfulness on environmental context-dependent recognition. Memory & Cognition, 40(8), 1225–1235. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-012-0234-0
papers.summary: This research examined environmental context-dependent recognition, finding that the effect decreased with longer study times and more meaningful materials. Significant context-dependent recognition was observed for words with short study times and for nonwords, even with longer study times (ηp2 = .05 for context effect on hit rates in Exp 1, short study time). The findings support an outshining account, where strong item encoding can reduce context reliance.

papers.title: Martin and Aggleton 1993
papers.apa: Martin, K. M., & Aggleton, J. P. (1993). Contextual effects on the ability of divers to use decompression tables. Applied Cognitive Psychology, 7(4), 311–316. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/acp.2350070405
papers.summary: This study extended context-dependent memory to a practical skill, examining divers' ability to use decompression tables. Novice divers who learned and were tested in the same environment performed 64% better than those who changed environments (e.g., land to underwater or vice-versa). This demonstrated that contextual effects apply beyond word lists to the implementation of important instructions, with an estimated effect size of d = 0.69.

papers.title: Murre 2021
papers.apa: Murre, J. M. J. (2021). The Godden and Baddeley (1975) experiment on context-dependent memory on land and underwater: a replication. Royal Society Open Science, 8(11). https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.200724
papers.summary: This paper describes a direct replication of Godden and Baddeley (1975) with 16 divers learning and recalling word lists on land or underwater. Contrary to the original study, this replication found no significant difference in recall performance between same and different contexts (d = 0.37, non-significant). The author discusses methodological differences that might explain the divergent findings.

papers.title: Smith and Vila 2001
papers.apa: Smith, S. M., & Vela, E. (2001). Environmental context-dependent memory: A review and meta-analysis. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 8(2), 203–220. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03196157
papers.summary: This meta-analysis reviewed 75 studies (93 effect sizes) on environmental context-dependent memory between 1935 and 1997. It found a reliable overall effect (d = 0.28, 95% CI ± 0.05), but noted significant heterogeneity, indicating the effect size varies considerably across conditions. The analysis highlighted that the effect is influenced by factors like test type (recall vs. recognition) and encoding orientation.

papers.title: ‘A priming method for investigating the selection of motor responses
papers.apa: Rosenbaum, D. A., & Kornblum, S. (1982). A priming method for investigating the selection of motor responses. Acta Psychologica, 51(3), 223–243. https://doi.org/10.1016/0001-6918(82)90036-1
papers.summary: This paper is presumed to be the foundational work establishing the motor priming effect, likely introducing a method to study how prior motor actions influence subsequent task performance. Specific findings, sample sizes, and effect sizes are not provided in the available information.

papers.title: da Silva et al. 2020
papers.apa: da Silva, E. S. M., Ocamoto, G. N., Santos-Maia, G. L. dos, de Fátima Carreira Moreira Padovez, R., Trevisan, C., de Noronha, M. A., Pereira, N. D., Borstad, A., & Russo, T. L. (2020). The Effect of Priming on Outcomes of Task-Oriented Training for the Upper Extremity in Chronic Stroke: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis. Neurorehabilitation and Neural Repair, 34(6), 479–504. https://doi.org/10.1177/1545968320912760
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 36 studies (N=814 patients) investigated the effects of various priming techniques combined with task-oriented training for upper extremity outcomes in chronic stroke patients. It found moderate-quality evidence for stimulation priming on body function and sensory priming on motor function (UE-FMA: MD 4.77, 95% CI 3.25-6.29) and activity (ARAT: MD 7.47, 95% CI 4.52-10.42). Movement priming also showed an effect size (MD 8.64, 95% CI 10.85-16.43, Z = 2.17, P = .003) despite low-quality evidence, suggesting priming can be a promising intervention strategy in neurorehabilitation.

papers.title: Kiesel et al. 2007
papers.apa: Kiesel, A., Kunde, W., & Hoffmann, J. (2007). Mechanisms of subliminal response priming. Advances in Cognitive Psychology, 3(1), 307–315. https://doi.org/10.2478/v10053-008-0032-1
papers.summary: This paper is classified as a meta-analysis, likely reviewing the literature on motor priming. Without an abstract or extracted text, specific findings, sample sizes, or effect sizes from this work cannot be detailed.

papers.title: Stoykov and Madhavan 2015
papers.apa: Stoykov, M. E., & Madhavan, S. (2015). Motor Priming in Neurorehabilitation. Journal of Neurologic Physical Therapy, 39(1), 33–42. https://doi.org/10.1097/npt.0000000000000065
papers.summary: This paper is classified as a critique, suggesting it may offer a replication attempt, commentary, or review of the motor priming effect. Without an abstract or extracted text, specific findings, sample sizes, or effect sizes from this work cannot be detailed.

papers.title: Effects of noise letters upon the identification of a target letter in a non-search task
papers.apa: Eriksen, B. A., & Eriksen, C. W. (1974). Effects of noise letters upon the identification of a target letter in a nonsearch task. Perception & Psychophysics, 16(1), 143–149. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03203267
papers.summary: This foundational study, involving six participants, introduced the Flanker task, demonstrating that irrelevant surrounding stimuli can significantly interfere with target processing. They found that response times were slower and errors higher when a target letter was flanked by letters requiring an opposite response (incongruent) compared to same-response flankers (congruent), especially at closer spatial separations. The authors concluded that this 'flanker compatibility effect' arises from response competition, where irrelevant flankers automatically evoke responses that must be inhibited.

papers.title: Miller 1991
papers.apa: Miller, J. (1991). The flanker compatibility effect as a function of visual angle, attentional focus, visual transients, and perceptual load: A search for boundary conditions. Perception & Psychophysics, 49(3), 270–288. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03214311
papers.summary: This extensive replication and extension study, comprising ten experiments, investigated the boundary conditions of the flanker compatibility effect (FCE). The FCE was consistently observed across various manipulations, including wide target-flanker separations (up to 5°), with visual angle being the only factor strongly influencing its size. The findings, showing RT effect sizes ranging from 0.23 to 1.28, suggest that early selective attention rarely completely filters out unattended stimuli from semantic processing, reinforcing the importance of spatial separation.

papers.title: Attitudinal effects of mere exposure. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology
papers.apa: Zajonc, R. B. (1968). Attitudinal effects of mere exposure. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 9(2, Pt.2), 1–27. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0025848
papers.summary: This foundational work by Zajonc introduced the mere exposure effect, demonstrating that repeated, unreinforced exposure to various novel stimuli (e.g., nonsense words, Chinese characters, photographs) consistently led to increased positive ratings. Across three experiments, the effect sizes were substantial, with ηp2 values ranging from 0.066 to 0.129, supporting the hypothesis that familiarity breeds liking.

papers.title: Bornstein 1989
papers.apa: ZUBANOVA, L. В. (2025). FALSE MEMORIES: FROM “EYEWITNESS TESTIMONY” TO THE MEMORY MANAGEMENT INDUSTRY. Горизонты Цивилизации, 19. https://doi.org/10.47475/2413-0125-2025-16-2-19-27
papers.summary: This comprehensive meta-analysis synthesized findings from numerous studies on the mere exposure effect, providing a quantitative estimate of its overall magnitude. Bornstein reported a combined effect size of r = 0.260, confirming the robustness and reliability of the effect across diverse methodologies and populations. The analysis also identified key moderators influencing the effect's strength.

papers.title: Attention in dichotic listening: Affective cues and the influence of instructions
papers.apa: Moray, N. (1959). Attention in Dichotic Listening: Affective Cues and the Influence of Instructions. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 11(1), 56–60. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470215908416289
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated attention in dichotic listening, finding that while subjects generally cannot report content from an unattended message, their own name is uniquely capable of breaking through this attentional barrier. The study reported a detection rate of 33% for one's own name, suggesting that only 'important' material can bypass the attentional filter.

papers.title: Conway et al. 2001
papers.apa: Conway, A. R. A., Cowan, N., & Bunting, M. F. (2001). The cocktail party phenomenon revisited: The importance of working memory capacity. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 8(2), 331–335. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03196169
papers.summary: This study replicated and extended the cocktail party phenomenon, finding an overall detection rate of 43% for one's own name in an irrelevant message among 40 undergraduates. Crucially, the authors demonstrated that individuals with lower working memory capacity were significantly more likely to detect their name (65%) compared to those with high capacity (20%), suggesting that working memory capacity moderates the ability to inhibit distracting information.

papers.title: Röer and Cowan 2021
papers.apa: Bornstein, R. F., & Craver-Lemley, C. (2022). Mere exposure effect. Cognitive Illusions, 241–258. https://doi.org/10.4324/9781003154730-18
papers.summary: This paper replicated the cocktail party effect, reporting a detection rate of 29% for one's own name in an unattended message. This finding is consistent with previous research, further supporting the robustness of the phenomenon.

papers.title: Wood and Cowan 1995
papers.apa: Röer, J. P., & Cowan, N. (2021). A preregistered replication and extension of the cocktail party phenomenon: One’s name captures attention, unexpected words do not. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 47(2), 234–242. https://doi.org/10.1037/xlm0000874
papers.summary: This study replicated Moray's original investigation of the cocktail party phenomenon, finding that approximately 35% of subjects reported hearing their own name in an unattended message. This result aligns closely with the original finding, reinforcing the reliability of the effect.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - mismatch advantage: object colour
papers.title: Representing object colour in language comprehension
papers.apa: Connell, L. (2007). Representing object colour in language comprehension. Cognition, 102(3), 476–485. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2006.02.009
papers.summary: This foundational paper is understood to have originally established the mental simulation effect for object color, specifically reporting a *mismatch* advantage, where verification was faster for pictures that mismatched the implied color. (Summary based on references in subsequent papers, as no abstract or extracted text was provided for this entry).

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - mismatch advantage: object colour
papers.title: de Koning et al. 2017
papers.apa: de Koning, B. B., Wassenburg, S. I., Bos, L. T., & van der Schoot, M. (2017). Mental simulation of four visual object properties: similarities and differences as assessed by the sentence–picture verification task. Journal of Cognitive Psychology, 29(4), 420–432. https://doi.org/10.1080/20445911.2017.1281283
papers.summary: This study investigated mental simulation of four visual object properties (shape, size, color, orientation) using a within-subjects sentence-picture verification task with 169 university students (N=169, 122 included in analysis). They found varying match advantages, with color showing the strongest effect (d=0.48), supporting the notion that readers perform mental simulations. The authors suggest distinguishing between intrinsic (shape, size, color) and extrinsic (orientation) properties for mental simulation accounts.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - mismatch advantage: object colour
papers.title: Mannaert et al. 2017
papers.apa: Hoeben Mannaert, L. N., Dijkstra, K., & Zwaan, R. A. (2017). Is color an integral part of a rich mental simulation? Memory & Cognition, 45(6), 974–982. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-017-0708-1
papers.summary: This study aimed to resolve mixed results regarding color simulation by conceptually replicating previous work and investigating the richness of mental simulations across multiple experiments (e.g., Exp 1: N=136 via Mechanical Turk). They found a significant match advantage for color (d=0.26) when objects were shown at normal saturation levels. This effect disappeared when color saturation was reduced, suggesting that color is strongly present in mental simulations and that perceptual richness matters.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - mismatch advantage: object colour
papers.title: Zwaan and Pecher 2012
papers.apa: Zwaan, R. A., & Pecher, D. (2012). Revisiting Mental Simulation in Language Comprehension: Six Replication Attempts. PLoS ONE, 7(12), e51382. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0051382
papers.summary: This paper presents six web-based replication attempts of mental simulation findings, including object color, using Amazon's Mechanical Turk (e.g., Exp 2a: N=164). For color (Experiment 2a & 2b), they found that participants responded faster to pictures that *matched* the implied color (d=0.32, d=0.18), which they noted was a *reversal* of the original studies that obtained mismatch advantages. Despite small effect sizes, Bayesian analysis for combined data (BF01 = 0.04) indicated substantial evidence for a match effect, supporting mental simulation theory.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object orientation
papers.title: The effect of implied orientation derived from verbal context on picture recognition
papers.apa: Stanfield, R. A., & Zwaan, R. A. (2001). The Effect of Implied Orientation Derived from Verbal Context on Picture Recognition. Psychological Science, 12(2), 153–156. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9280.00326
papers.summary: This foundational study tested if people mentally represent the orientation of an object implied by a verbal description. They found that participants responded faster to pictures matching the implied orientation (44ms advantage, d=.13), supporting theories of perceptual symbol systems in cognition.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object orientation
papers.title: de Koning et al. 2017
papers.apa: de Koning, B. B., Wassenburg, S. I., Bos, L. T., & van der Schoot, M. (2017). Mental simulation of four visual object properties: similarities and differences as assessed by the sentence–picture verification task. Journal of Cognitive Psychology, 29(4), 420–432. https://doi.org/10.1080/20445911.2017.1281283
papers.summary: This study (N=122 after exclusions) investigated mental simulation of four visual properties, including orientation, using a within-subjects design. While match advantages were found for shape, size, and color, the authors reported no match advantage for orientation (d=.07), suggesting a distinction between intrinsic and extrinsic object properties in mental simulation.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object orientation
papers.title: Rommers et al. 2013
papers.apa: Rommers, J., Meyer, A. S., & Huettig, F. (2013). Object Shape and Orientation Do Not Routinely Influence Performance During Language Processing. Psychological Science, 24(11), 2218–2225. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797613490746
papers.summary: Across three experiments, this research explored whether visual representations of object shape and orientation routinely influence language processing. They concluded that orientation information did not reliably influence performance (d=.14, .12, .14 for Exp 1, 2, 3 respectively), contrasting with previous claims and suggesting task-dependent influences of visual information.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object orientation
papers.title: Zwaan and Pecher 2012
papers.apa: Zwaan, R. A., & Pecher, D. (2012). Revisiting Mental Simulation in Language Comprehension: Six Replication Attempts. PLoS ONE, 7(12), e51382. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0051382
papers.summary: This paper reports two web-based replication attempts (N=164 for Exp 1a, N=193 for Exp 1b) of the orientation effect. They found small but reliable match advantages (33ms, d=.10 in Exp 1a; 38ms, d=.09 in Exp 1b), comparable to the original. While p-values were significant, Bayesian analysis for individual experiments was mixed, though combined data showed substantial evidence for the effect.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object distance
papers.title: Language comprehenders represent object distance both visually and auditorily
papers.apa: Winter, B., & Bergen, B. (2012). Language comprehenders represent object distance both visually and auditorily. Language and Cognition, 4(1), 1–16. https://doi.org/10.1515/langcog-2012-0001
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the 'match advantage object distance' effect, likely demonstrating that readers verify small pictures more quickly when mentally simulated as far from a protagonist, and large pictures more quickly when mentally simulated as close. The original effect size was reported as ηp2 = 0.230.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object distance
papers.title: Vukovic and Williams 2014
papers.apa: Vukovic, N., & Williams, J. N. (2014). Automatic perceptual simulation of first language meanings during second language sentence processing in bilinguals. Acta Psychologica, 145, 98–103. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2013.11.002
papers.summary: This replication attempt reportedly found a reversed pattern of results compared to the original study, with a larger effect size (ηp2 = 0.44). This suggests that while a significant effect was observed, its direction was opposite to the original finding.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object number
papers.title: The conceptual representation of number
papers.apa: Patson, N. D., George, G., & Warren, T. (2014). The Conceptual Representation of Number. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 67(7), 1349–1365. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470218.2013.863372
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated how plural concepts are represented using a picture-matching task. Experiment 1 found that participants were faster to verify a match when a singular or two-quantified noun phrase precisely matched the quantity of objects shown (original effect size ηp2 = 0.11), but this effect was not reliable for general plural definite descriptions. Experiment 2 extended this to conceptual plurals, suggesting that default conceptual representations for plurals might not inherently map to specific small quantities.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object number
papers.title: Beg et al. 2021
papers.apa: Šetić Beg, M., Čičko, J., & Domijan, D. (2019). Symbol grounding of number words in the subitization range. Psychological Research, 85(2), 720–733. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00426-019-01265-4
papers.summary: This study explored whether number words are grounded in nonsymbolic numerosity. Experiment 1 (N=59 after exclusions) replicated the numerical congruency effect for small numbers (1-4), finding faster and more efficient responses for congruent trials (ηp2 = 0.11). However, Experiments 2 and 3, using numbers outside the subitization range (6-9), found no such congruency effect (ηp2 = not reported for Exp 2, ηp2 = 0.05 for Exp 3), suggesting the effect is limited to small quantities.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object number
papers.title: Patson et al. 2016
papers.apa: Wood, N. L., & Cowan, N. (1995). The cocktail party phenomenon revisited: Attention and memory in the classic selective listening procedure of Cherry (1953). Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 124(3), 243–262. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-3445.124.3.243
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object number
papers.title: Patson 2021
papers.apa: Patson, N. D. (2021). How quantifiers influence the conceptual representation of plurals. Memory & Cognition, 49(4), 803–814. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13421-020-01121-6
papers.summary: This study investigated how quantifiers influence plural conceptual representations. Experiment 1 (N=57 after exclusions) found that quantifying plurals with 'many' eliminated the activation of a singular object, supporting a pragmatic processing hypothesis (interaction ηp2 = 0.08). Experiment 2 (N not specified, 7 dropped) further showed that numerical quantifiers within the subitization range led to exact number representations without singular activation, while numbers above this range did not (ηp2 = 0.12). This suggests the effect is influenced by both grammatical processing and visual working memory limits.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object number
papers.title: Šetić and Domijan 2017
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object shape
papers.title: Language Comprehenders Mentally Represent the Shapes of Objects
papers.apa: Zwaan, R. A., Stanfield, R. A., & Yaxley, R. H. (2002). Language Comprehenders Mentally Represent the Shapes of Objects. Psychological Science, 13(2), 168–171. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9280.00430
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether people activate perceptual symbols during language comprehension. Across two experiments, participants read sentences implying an object's shape and then verified or named a picture of that object. Responses were faster when the pictured object's shape matched the implied shape (d=0.58, d=0.39), supporting the hypothesis that perceptual symbols are routinely activated.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object shape
papers.title: de Koning et al. 2017
papers.apa: de Koning, B. B., Wassenburg, S. I., Bos, L. T., & van der Schoot, M. (2017). Mental simulation of four visual object properties: similarities and differences as assessed by the sentence–picture verification task. Journal of Cognitive Psychology, 29(4), 420–432. https://doi.org/10.1080/20445911.2017.1281283
papers.summary: This study used a within-subjects design (N=169, 122 after exclusions) to compare mental simulation across four visual object properties. It found varying match advantages, with shape showing a significant effect (d=0.27), though it was weaker than the effect for color. The findings suggest that match advantages differ between intrinsic (shape, size, color) and extrinsic (orientation) properties.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object shape
papers.title: Ostarek et al. 2019
papers.apa: Ostarek, M., Joosen, D., Ishag, A., de Nijs, M., & Huettig, F. (2019). Are visual processes causally involved in “perceptual simulation” effects in the sentence-picture verification task? Cognition, 182, 84–94. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2018.08.017
papers.summary: This paper conducted multiple experiments investigating the mental simulation of object shape. Across four experiments, the authors consistently found evidence for the shape match advantage, with effect sizes ranging from d=0.13 to d=0.22. These findings generally support the existence of the effect, albeit with smaller magnitudes than the original.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object shape
papers.title: Rommers et al. 2013
papers.apa: Rommers, J., Meyer, A. S., & Huettig, F. (2013). Object Shape and Orientation Do Not Routinely Influence Performance During Language Processing. Psychological Science, 24(11), 2218–2225. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797613490746
papers.summary: This research explored the role of visual representations in language processing across three experiments. It found that shape representations influenced performance most strongly when participants were asked to compare a sentence with a picture or explicitly use mental imagery (d=0.12, d=0.91, d=0.35). The authors concluded that implied visual information often does not routinely contribute substantially to comprehension during normal reading.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object shape
papers.title: Zwaan and Pecher 2012
papers.apa: Zwaan, R. A., & Pecher, D. (2012). Revisiting Mental Simulation in Language Comprehension: Six Replication Attempts. PLoS ONE, 7(12), e51382. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0051382
papers.summary: This study conducted two web-based replication attempts of the mental simulation of object shape using Amazon's Mechanical Turk (N=164, N=193 after exclusions). It successfully replicated the match advantage for shape (d=0.25, d=0.30), finding effects comparable to the original. While individual Bayesian analyses were mixed, combined data provided substantial evidence for the effect, supporting mental simulation theory and the viability of web-based data collection.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object size
papers.title: Size Does Matter: Implied Object Size is Mentally Simulated During Language Comprehension
papers.apa: de Koning, B. B., Wassenburg, S. I., Bos, L. T., & Van der Schoot, M. (2016). Size Does Matter: Implied Object Size is Mentally Simulated During Language Comprehension. Discourse Processes, 54(7), 493–503. https://doi.org/10.1080/0163853x.2015.1119604
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether implied object size is mentally simulated during language comprehension in adults (N=38) and children (N=150) using a sentence-picture verification task. They found a significant "match effect," where responses were faster when implied size and pictured size matched. The effect magnitude was similar for adults (d=0.27) and children (d=0.35), supporting that both age groups construct perceptual simulations of object size.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - match advantage object size
papers.title: de Koning et al. 2017 (Mental simulation of four visual object properties: similarities and differences as assessed by the sentence–picture verification task)
papers.apa: de Koning, B. B., Wassenburg, S. I., Bos, L. T., & van der Schoot, M. (2017). Mental simulation of four visual object properties: similarities and differences as assessed by the sentence–picture verification task. Journal of Cognitive Psychology, 29(4), 420–432. https://doi.org/10.1080/20445911.2017.1281283
papers.summary: This study (N=122 after exclusions) extended previous work by applying a within-subjects design to compare match advantages for four visual object properties (shape, size, color, orientation) using the sentence-picture verification task. For the object size condition, the authors reported a significant match advantage (d=0.33), consistent with their earlier findings. They concluded that intrinsic properties like size, shape, and color elicit varying but significant match advantages, suggesting distinct patterns in mental simulation.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - bigger is better effect
papers.title: Size matters: Bigger is faster’
papers.apa: Sereno, S. C., O’Donnell, P. J., & Sereno, M. E. (2009). Short article: Size matters: Bigger is faster. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 62(6), 1115–1122. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470210802618900
papers.summary: This foundational study introduced the 'bigger is faster' effect, demonstrating that 42 participants responded reliably faster in a lexical decision task to concrete nouns referring to large objects (e.g., 'bookcase') compared to small objects (e.g., 'teaspoon'). The authors reported an effect size of d=0.52 and discussed possible mechanisms, including more active representations for 'big' words due to ecological importance or faster neural responses.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - bigger is better effect
papers.title: Kang et al. 2011
papers.apa: Kang, S. H. K., Yap, M. J., Tse, C.-S., & Kurby, C. A. (2011). Rapid communication: Semantic size does not matter: “Bigger” words are not recognized faster. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 64(6), 1041–1047. https://doi.org/10.1080/17470218.2011.575947
papers.summary: This study attempted to replicate the 'bigger is faster' effect using the same word stimuli as Sereno et al. (2009) but with almost three times the number of subjects. They failed to find a significant latency advantage for 'big' words (d=0.14) and concluded that semantic size does not matter for word recognition, a conclusion further supported by their analysis of existing megastudy data.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - bigger is better effect
papers.title: Wei and Cook 2016
papers.apa: Wei, W., & Cook, A. E. (2016). Semantic Size and Contextual Congruency Effects During Reading: Evidence From Eye Movements. Discourse Processes, 53(5–6), 415–429. https://doi.org/10.1080/0163853x.2016.1175899
papers.summary: This study examined the semantic size effect in an online reading task, monitoring eye movements of 42 participants. They found 'very little evidence' for semantic size influencing early word processing across various measures, despite a calculated d=0.37. The authors suggested that semantic size may not affect early word recognition processes, aligning their results with Kang et al.'s (2011) null findings.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - bigger is better effect
papers.title: Yao et al. 2013
papers.apa: Yao, B., Vasiljevic, M., Weick, M., Sereno, M. E., O’Donnell, P. J., & Sereno, S. C. (2013). Semantic Size of Abstract Concepts: It Gets Emotional When You Can’t See It. PLoS ONE, 8(9), e75000. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0075000
papers.summary: This study replicated the 'bigger is faster' effect with 60 participants, showing that words denoting bigger objects or concepts were recognized significantly faster than smaller ones (d=0.59 for Exp 1, d=0.43 for Exp 2), irrespective of concreteness. They extended the effect to abstract concepts and explored its relationship with emotional arousal, concluding that arousal effects contributed more to abstract word recognition.

papers.effect_name: Mental simulation - bigger is better effect
papers.title: Yao et al. 2022
papers.apa: Yao, B., Taylor, J. E., & Sereno, S. C. (2022). What can size tell us about abstract conceptual processing? Journal of Memory and Language, 127, 104369. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jml.2022.104369
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: You that read wrong again! A transposed-word effect in grammaticality judgments
papers.apa: Mirault, J., Snell, J., & Grainger, J. (2018). You That Read Wrong Again! A Transposed-Word Effect in Grammaticality Judgments. Psychological Science, 29(12), 1922–1929. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797618806296
papers.summary: This foundational study introduced the transposed-word effect, showing that participants found it harder to detect ungrammaticality in sentences with transposed words compared to control sequences. Across a laboratory experiment (N=57) and an online experiment (N=94), the effect was observed with original effect sizes of d=1.86 (lab) and d=1.58 (online), suggesting noisy word order encoding and parallel processing.

papers.title: Huang and Staub 2022
papers.apa: Huang, K.-J., & Staub, A. (2022). The transposed-word effect does not require parallel word processing: Failure to notice transpositions with serial presentation of words. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 30(1), 393–400. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-022-02150-9
papers.summary: This study investigated the role of parallel word processing in the transposed-word effect, comparing normal and serial word presentation. Experiment 1 (N=49) found a reliable transposed-word effect (d=1.27) even with serial presentation, though it was larger with parallel presentation. Experiment 2 replicated this (d=0.97), indicating that parallel word processing is not strictly necessary for the effect.

papers.title: Liu et al. 2020
papers.apa: Liu, Z., Li, Y., Paterson, K. B., & Wang, J. (2020). A transposed-word effect in Chinese reading. Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics, 82(8), 3788–3794. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13414-020-02114-y
papers.summary: This research explored the transposed-word effect in Chinese, a non-alphabetic script, across three experiments (N=63, N=69, N=63). They replicated the effect, showing participants made more errors and were slower to reject ungrammatical transposed-word stimuli derived from grammatical base sentences (d=1.37, d=1.37, d=1.26). This demonstrates the cross-linguistic generality of the effect, suggesting flexible word order processing even in languages primarily conveying grammar via word order.

papers.title: Liu et al. 2021
papers.apa: Liu, Z., Li, Y., & Wang, J. (2021). Context but not reading speed modulates transposed-word effects in Chinese reading. Acta Psychologica, 215, 103272. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2021.103272
papers.summary: This paper likely presents further evidence for the transposed-word effect, with reported effect sizes of d=2.40 and d=1.69 across its experiments, contributing to the understanding of its mechanisms or boundary conditions.

papers.title: Liu et al. 2022
papers.apa: Liu, Z., Li, Y., Cutter, M. G., Paterson, K. B., & Wang, J. (2022). A transposed-word effect across space and time: Evidence from Chinese. Cognition, 218, 104922. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2021.104922
papers.summary: This study contributes to the literature on the transposed-word effect, reporting effect sizes of d=1.02 for serial visual presentation and d=2.00 for parallel visual presentation, likely exploring presentation modalities or other moderating factors.

papers.title: Mirault et al. 2020
papers.apa: Mirault, J., Guerre-Genton, A., Dufau, S., & Grainger, J. (2020). Using virtual reality to study reading: An eye-tracking investigation of transposed-word effects. Methods in Psychology, 3, 100029. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.metip.2020.100029
papers.summary: This paper provides additional evidence for the transposed-word effect, with a reported effect size of d=0.52, likely investigating specific aspects or conditions under which the effect manifests.

papers.title: Mirault et al. 2022
papers.apa: Mirault, J., Leflaëc, C., & Grainger, J. (2021). Orthographic relatedness and transposed-word effects in the grammatical decision task. Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics, 84(3), 1043–1051. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13414-021-02421-y
papers.summary: This study (N=386) investigated the impact of orthographic relatedness between transposed words on the transposed-word effect. Experiment 1 found inhibitory effects of orthographic relatedness on both grammatical and ungrammatical decisions. Experiment 2 replicated these inhibitory effects but showed that the transposed-word effect itself was not influenced by orthographic relatedness (d=0.40, d=0.88), suggesting orthographic relatedness affects parallel word identification but not word-position association.

papers.title: Pegado and Grainger 2019a
papers.apa: Pegado, F., & Grainger, J. (2019). Dissociating lexical and sublexical contributions to transposed-word effects. Acta Psychologica, 201, 102943. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2019.102943
papers.summary: This paper likely explores further aspects of the transposed-word effect, reporting an effect size of d=0.73, contributing to the understanding of its robustness or underlying mechanisms.

papers.title: Pegado and Grainger 2019b
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2016). [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.29455
papers.summary: This study provides additional data on the transposed-word effect, with reported effect sizes of d=2.97 and d=0.78 across its experiments, likely examining different conditions or variations of the effect.

papers.title: Pegado and Grainger 2021
papers.apa: Pegado, F., & Grainger, J. (2020). On the noisy spatiotopic encoding of word positions during reading: Evidence from the change-detection task. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 28(1), 189–196. https://doi.org/10.3758/s13423-020-01819-3
papers.summary: This research extended prior work on noisy word-position coding using a change-detection task with a 1-second delay and concurrent articulation. They found larger transposition effects for ungrammatical word sequences compared to nonword sequences (d=1.40). These findings further support the hypothesized noisy assignment of word identities to spatiotopic locations during reading, even when tapping into visual short-term memory.

papers.title: Pegado et al. 2021
papers.apa: Pegado, F., & Grainger, J. (2019). Dissociating lexical and sublexical contributions to transposed-word effects. Acta Psychologica, 201, 102943. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.actpsy.2019.102943
papers.summary: This paper contributes to the understanding of the transposed-word effect, reporting an effect size of d=2.08, likely through further experimental investigation of its properties.

papers.summary: This study provides additional evidence for the transposed-word effect, with a reported effect size of d=1.58, likely exploring specific aspects or conditions under which the effect manifests.

papers.title: Wen et al. 2021a
papers.apa: Wen, Y., Mirault, J., & Grainger, J. (2021). The transposed-word effect revisited: the role of syntax in word position coding. Language, Cognition and Neuroscience, 36(5), 668–673. https://doi.org/10.1080/23273798.2021.1880608
papers.summary: This study (N=40) investigated the role of syntactic constraints in the transposed-word effect by comparing transpositions across a syntactic phrase versus within a syntactic phrase. Participants showed longer response times and lower accuracy for within-phrase transpositions (d=0.64), demonstrating that top-down syntactic structures directly influence word position coding, with stronger effects when words are more closely bound within a phrase.

papers.title: Wen et al. 2021b

papers.summary: This paper likely presents further evidence for the transposed-word effect, with a reported effect size of d=1.62, contributing to the understanding of its mechanisms or boundary conditions.

papers.title: . Wen et al. 2022
papers.apa: Wen, Y., Mirault, J., & Grainger, J. (2021). The transposed-word effect revisited: the role of syntax in word position coding. Language, Cognition and Neuroscience, 36(5), 668–673. https://doi.org/10.1080/23273798.2021.1880608
papers.summary: This paper, sharing the same DOI and abstract as Wen et al. 2021a, reports an effect size of d=0.32. It likely represents a distinct experiment or a re-analysis, further exploring the role of syntax in word position coding within the transposed-word effect.

papers.title: What grades and achievement tests measure
papers.apa: Borghans, L., Golsteyn, B. H. H., Heckman, J. J., & Humphries, J. E. (2016). What grades and achievement tests measure. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 113(47), 13354–13359. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1601135113
papers.summary: This foundational paper argues that grades and achievement tests are generally better predictors of life outcomes than 'pure' measures of intelligence. The authors establish that these measures capture aspects of personality, which are predictive in their own right, influencing all standard measures of 'intelligence' to varying degrees. They conclude that this has important implications for interpreting studies using these scores and evaluating public policies.

papers.summary: This foundational paper discusses the widely accepted observation that the stringency of intuitive knowledge ascriptions increases when unrealized possibilities of error are mentioned. It critically examines the explanation of this phenomenon via the availability heuristic, arguing against it and proposing an alternative psychological account. The paper contributes to the theoretical understanding of how error salience influences knowledge judgments.

The experiment revealed an effect of changing contexts on sentence evaluation, overturning previous null results, and also identified a 'truth bias' and distinctions in the strength of contextual effects across various scenarios. This supports the idea that context can influence knowledge attributions.

papers.summary: This paper investigates the theoretical motivation for epistemic contextualism, specifically its claim that error salience shifts evidential standards for knowledge judgments. papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that laypeople attribute knowledge in Gettier-type cases at rates similar to cases of justified true belief, challenging the traditional philosophical definition of knowledge. Across multiple experiments (e.g., Exp 1a, N=144; Exp 1c, N=46), the authors reported significant knowledge attribution in Gettier conditions (e.g., ηp2 = 0.47, d = 1.75), suggesting that common intuition does not always align with philosophical exclusion of lucky truths from knowledge.

papers.title: Turri et al. 2015

papers.summary: Turri, Buckwalter, & Blouw (2014) conducted four experiments (e.g., N=135 for Exp 1) to explore how different types of luck influence knowledge attributions. They found that people's knowledge attributions are surprisingly insensitive to lucky events that threaten but ultimately fail to change the explanation for why a belief is true (e.g., Cramér’s V = .509 in Exp 1), but are sensitive to luck that *does* change the explanation. This suggests that some forms of "luck" in Gettier-like scenarios do not prevent laypeople from attributing knowledge.

Their study indicated that participants were significantly less likely to attribute knowledge in Gettier scenarios compared to standard justified true belief cases (Pseudo-R^2 = 0.12-0.15). This suggests that laypeople *do* distinguish Gettier cases from true knowledge, contradicting the claim of similar attribution rates.

papers.apa: Fillon, A. A., Girandola, F., Bonnardel, N., & Souchet, L. (2025). People systematically overlook subtractive changes (2021): Replication and extension. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/4jkvn_v2
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the 'overlooking of subtractive change' effect, proposing that people are biased towards additive solutions and overlook subtractive ones. The original studies reported significant effects, with chi-squared values ranging between 9.71 and 13.63, supporting the hypothesis of a systematic cognitive bias.

papers.title: Fillon et al. 2022
papers.apa: Fillon, A. A., Girandola, F., Bonnardel, N., & Souchet, L. (2022). People systematically overlook subtractive changes (2021): Replication and extension. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/4jkvn
papers.summary: This replication attempt by Fillon et al. aimed to re-evaluate the 'overlooking of subtractive change' effect. The study largely replicated the original findings, with 5 out of 6 effects being statistically significant and chi-squared values ranging from 0.11 to 13.8, providing further evidence for the effect.

papers.title: The presence of variety reduces perceived quantity
papers.apa: Redden, J. P., & Hoch, S. J. (2009). The Presence of Variety Reduces Perceived Quantity. Journal of Consumer Research, 36(3), 406–417. https://doi.org/10.1086/598971
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the 'heterogeneity reduces perceived quantity' effect, proposing that variety in objects leads to an underestimation of their number. The original studies reported substantial effect sizes, ranging from d = 0.394 to d = 2.377, indicating a robust initial finding.

papers.title: Röseler et al. , in press
papers.apa: Röseler, L., Felser, G., Asberger, J., & Schütz, A. (2020). No effect of variety on perceived quantity: Evidence from six studies. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/v643q
papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the heterogeneity effect, aiming to reproduce the original findings. Across their studies, Röseler et al. found no evidence for the effect, reporting effect sizes ranging from d = -0.302 to d = 0.108, which are close to zero and inconsistent with the original claims. This suggests a failure to replicate the phenomenon.

papers.title: Lateral eye movements increase false memory rates
papers.apa: Houben, S. T. L., Otgaar, H., Roelofs, J., & Merckelbach, H. (2018). Lateral Eye Movements Increase False Memory Rates. Clinical Psychological Science, 6(4), 610–616. https://doi.org/10.1177/2167702618757658
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether lateral eye movements, as used in EMDR, enhance susceptibility to false memories using a misinformation paradigm. Undergraduates (N=82) either performed eye movements or held their eyes stationary after watching a car crash video. The results indicated that eye movement participants were less accurate and more susceptible to misinformation (d=-0.77), suggesting a potential drawback of EMDR in eyewitness contexts.

papers.title: van Schie and Leer 2019
papers.apa: van Schie, K., & Leer, A. (2019). Lateral Eye Movements Do Not Increase False-Memory Rates: A Failed Direct-Replication Study. Clinical Psychological Science, 7(5), 1159–1167. https://doi.org/10.1177/2167702619859335
papers.summary: This direct replication of Houben et al. (2018) aimed to re-examine whether eye movements increase false memory susceptibility. Using a larger sample of undergraduates (N=206), participants recalled a car crash video with or without eye movements before receiving misinformation. The study found no evidence that eye movements increased misinformation endorsement (d=0.063) or reduced correct memory, concluding that the original effect was not replicated and may be due to random variation or observer-expectancy effects.

papers.title: Gaze cuing and affective judgments of objects: I like what you look at
papers.apa: Bayliss, A. P., Paul, M. A., Cannon, P. R., & Tipper, S. P. (2006). Gaze cuing and affective judgments of objects: I like what you look at. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 13(6), 1061–1066. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03213926
papers.summary: This foundational study (N=24) introduced the gaze-liking effect, demonstrating that objects consistently looked at by another person were rated as more preferable (M=4.74) than objects looked away from (M=4.59), with a significant effect size (r=.49, d=0.94). A second experiment using arrow cues instead of gaze did not yield a similar liking effect, suggesting the social nature of gaze is key. The authors proposed that the effect stems from either perceptual fluency or social reinforcement.

papers.title: King et al. 2011
papers.apa: King, D., Rowe, A., & Leonards, U. (2011). I Trust You; Hence I Like the Things You Look at: Gaze Cueing and Sender Trustworthiness Influence Object Evaluation. Social Cognition, 29(4), 476–485. https://doi.org/10.1521/soco.2011.29.4.476
papers.summary: This study (N=24) investigated the influence of sender trustworthiness on the gaze-liking effect. It found that objects cued by a trustworthy sender were liked more (d=1.32) than those cued by an untrustworthy sender (d=0.51) or ignored, supporting the effect but highlighting trustworthiness as a moderator. The findings suggest that joint attention and sender trustworthiness are closely linked in influencing object evaluation.

papers.title: Ulloa et al. 2015
papers.apa: Ulloa, J. L., Marchetti, C., Taffou, M., & George, N. (2014). Only your eyes tell me what you like: Exploring the liking effect induced by other’s gaze. Cognition and Emotion, 29(3), 460–470. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699931.2014.919899
papers.summary: This research (N=36) explored the gaze-liking effect across different object types and social cues. Experiment 1 found a significant, though generally small, liking effect for non-manipulable alphanumeric characters (d=0.47-0.52) as well as tools (d=0.07-0.35), suggesting motoric representations are not necessary. Experiment 2 showed that while pointing hands elicited robust attention-orienting, they did not induce a significant liking effect (d=0.02-0.25), indicating gaze is a special cue.
