papers.summary: This study investigated the role of phonological precision in lexical competition using a masked priming experiment with 84 university students. The authors found an interaction between phonological precision and neighborhood density (NHD) on form priming, with high-precision readers showing facilitatory priming for sparse neighborhoods and inhibitory priming for dense ones. While the opposite pattern was observed for low-precision readers, this interaction was non-significant, suggesting phonological precision is linked to lexical competition. The reported replication effect sizes were d=0.28 and d=0.34.

papers.title: The powerful placebo
papers.apa: 3. “The Powerful Placebo.” (2014). Shadow Medicine. https://doi.org/10.7312/hall16904-005
papers.summary: Henry K. Beecher's 1955 paper is a seminal work that brought the placebo effect into the forefront of medical research. Based on a review of 15 studies, he reported that placebos produced satisfactory relief in an average of 32% of patients across various conditions, highlighting the significant psychological component in treatment outcomes. This work established the importance of controlling for placebo effects in clinical trials.

papers.title: Hróbjartsson 2001
papers.apa: Hróbjartsson, A., & Gøtzsche, P. C. (2001). Is the Placebo Powerless? New England Journal of Medicine, 344(21), 1594–1602. https://doi.org/10.1056/nejm200105243442106
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated the effect of placebo versus no treatment across various conditions. It found no significant effect of placebo on binary outcomes, but a beneficial effect on continuous outcomes (pooled standardized mean difference, MD = -0.28), particularly for pain relief (MD = -0.27). The findings suggest that while placebos may not always have a strong effect on objective measures, they can significantly impact subjective continuous outcomes like pain.

papers.title: Tang 2022
papers.apa: Tang, B., Barnes, K., Geers, A., Livesey, E., & Colagiuri, B. (2022). Choice and the Placebo Effect: A Meta-analysis. Annals of Behavioral Medicine, 56(10), 977–988. https://doi.org/10.1093/abm/kaab111
papers.summary: This meta-analysis found a significant effect size of _g_ = 0.298, indicating a replicated placebo effect. This suggests that across the studies included, placebos consistently produced a measurable beneficial outcome.

papers.title: Yeung 2018
papers.apa: Yeung, V., Sharpe, L., Glozier, N., Hackett, M. L., & Colagiuri, B. (2018). A systematic review and meta-analysis of placebo versus no treatment for insomnia symptoms. Sleep Medicine Reviews, 38, 17–27. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.smrv.2017.03.006
papers.summary: This meta-analysis specifically examined the placebo effect on sleep-related outcomes. It reported significant improvements in perceived sleep onset latency (_g_ = 0.272), total sleep time (_g_ = 0.322), and global sleep quality (_g_ = 0.581) following placebo treatment. These findings highlight the robust impact of placebos on subjective sleep measures.

papers.title: Placebo analgesia and its opioidergic regulation suggest that empathy for pain is grounded in self pain
papers.apa: Rütgen, M., Seidel, E.-M., Silani, G., Riečanský, I., Hummer, A., Windischberger, C., Petrovic, P., & Lamm, C. (2015). Placebo analgesia and its opioidergic regulation suggest that empathy for pain is grounded in self pain. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 112(41). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1511269112
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether inducing placebo analgesia in participants would reduce their empathy for others' pain. The authors found that placebo analgesia did indeed decrease pain empathy, which was associated with reduced activation in empathy-related brain areas. Furthermore, blocking placebo analgesia with an opioid antagonist also blocked its effects on pain empathy, suggesting an opioidergic mechanism and supporting the idea that pain empathy is grounded in first-hand pain experiences (d = 0.44 for unpleasantness ratings).

papers.title: Hartmann et al. 2021
papers.apa: Hartmann et al. review. (2021). https://doi.org/10.5194/acp-2020-1211-rc1
papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the "Placebo empathy analgesia" effect. The study found no evidence for the effect on unpleasantness ratings (ηp2 < 0.001 for intensity x group; ηp2 = 0.002 for pre-effort, ηp2 = 0.001 for post-effort unpleasantness ratings), indicating that placebo analgesia did not significantly dampen empathy for another person's pain. This suggests the original finding may not be robust.

papers.title: Hartmann et al. 2022
papers.apa: Hartmann, H., Forbes, P. A. G., Rütgen, M., & Lamm, C. (2022). Placebo Analgesia Reduces Costly Prosocial Helping to Lower Another Person’s Pain. Psychological Science, 33(11), 1867–1881. https://doi.org/10.1177/09567976221119727
papers.summary: This preregistered study explored whether placebo analgesia, while not replicating group differences in empathy, would reduce costly prosocial helping behavior. Across 90 Austrian adults, participants experiencing placebo analgesia made fewer prosocial choices and exerted less physical effort to reduce another person's pain compared to controls. Although direct empathy differences were not replicated, a mediation analysis suggested that self-reported empathic unpleasantness mediated the effect of placebo analgesia on prosocial choices.

papers.title: The nocebo reaction
papers.apa: Nocebo. (n.d.). SpringerReference. https://doi.org/10.1007/springerreference_122761
papers.summary: This foundational paper is credited with introducing or formally defining the 'nocebo effect,' describing how negative expectations can lead to adverse outcomes or side effects. It laid the groundwork for understanding the psychological mechanisms underlying such negative responses.

papers.title: Petersen et al. 2014
papers.apa: Petersen, G. L., Finnerup, N. B., Colloca, L., Amanzio, M., Price, D. D., Jensen, T. S., & Vase, L. (2014). The magnitude of nocebo effects in pain: A meta-analysis. Pain, 155(8), 1426–1434. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.pain.2014.04.016
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized evidence on the nocebo effect, quantifying its presence across various contexts. It reported effect sizes ranging from d=0.65 to d=1.07, indicating a robust and consistent negative impact of expectations on outcomes.

papers.title: Horváth et al. 2021
papers.apa: Horváth, Á., Köteles, F., & Szabo, A. (2021). Nocebo effects on motor performance: A systematic literature review. Scandinavian Journal of Psychology, 62(5), 665–674. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/sjop.12753
papers.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis examined nocebo effects on motor performance across 23 studies. It found that 14 studies reported a nocebo effect, with a mean effect size of d=0.60, suggesting a medium-to-large impact. The authors concluded that negative verbal information can evoke nocebo effects on motor performance, which may be more robust than placebo effects.

papers.title: Studies of interference in serial verbal reactions
papers.apa: Stroop, J. R. (1935). Studies of interference in serial verbal reactions. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 18(6), 643–662. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0054651
papers.summary: J. Ridley Stroop's foundational work established the classic Stroop effect, demonstrating that naming the color of incongruent words (e.g., 'red' in blue ink) takes significantly longer than naming the color of neutral stimuli or reading the words themselves. This seminal study highlighted the automaticity of reading and the resulting interference with color naming.

papers.title: Damen 2021
papers.summary: This study conducted a direct replication of the Stroop effect, finding a substantial effect size (ηp2 = 0.541 [0.369, 0.652]). The results strongly support the robustness and replicability of the classic Stroop interference phenomenon.

papers.title: Epp et al. 2012
papers.apa: Epp, A. M., Dobson, K. S., Dozois, D. J. A., & Frewen, P. A. (2012). A systematic meta-analysis of the Stroop task in depression. Clinical Psychology Review, 32(4), 316–328. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpr.2012.02.005
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated the emotional Stroop task in individuals with depression. It found a replicated effect, with significant interference for negative stimuli (g=.98) and positive stimuli (g=.87), indicating that emotional content can also interfere with color naming in specific populations.

papers.title: Homack and Riccio 2004
papers.apa: HOMACK, S. (2004). A meta-analysis of the sensitivity and specificity of the Stroop Color and Word Test with children. Archives of Clinical Neuropsychology, 19(6), 725–743. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.acn.2003.09.003
papers.summary: This meta-analysis examined Stroop performance in individuals with ADHD compared to controls. The findings consistently showed poorer performance in individuals with ADHD, with a mean weighted effect size of 0.50 or greater, suggesting the Stroop task is sensitive to attentional differences in this population.

papers.title: MacLeod 1991
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., Taylor, D., & Garrity, G. M. (2009). Exemplar Abstract for Parageobacillus thermantarcticus (Nicolaus et al. 2002) Aliyu et al. 2019, Bacillus thermoantarcticus (sic) Nicolaus et al. 2002, Bacillus thermantarcticus corrig. Nicolaus et al. 2002 and Geobacillus thermantarcticus (Nicolaus et al. 2002) Coorevits et al. 2012. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.4989
papers.summary: This paper is a highly cited review and theoretical analysis of the Stroop effect, synthesizing decades of research on its mechanisms, variants, and applications. It provides a comprehensive overview of the cognitive processes underlying the interference effect.

papers.title: Overcoming intuition: Metacognitive difficulty activates analytic reasoning
papers.apa: Alter, A., Oppenheimer, D., Epley, N., & Norwick, R. (2006). Overcoming Intuition: Metacognitive Difficulty Activates Analytic Reasoning [dataset]. In PsycEXTRA Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/e683342011-034
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the disfluency effect, demonstrating that participants answered more items on the Cognitive Reflection Test (CRT) correctly when presented in a disfluent font compared to a fluent font. Study 1 reported a significant effect with an effect size of η² = 0.056 (d = 0.71), suggesting that disfluency promotes deeper cognitive processing.

papers.title: Kühl and Eitel 2016
papers.apa: Kühl, T., & Eitel, A. (2016). Effects of disfluency on cognitive and metacognitive processes and outcomes. Metacognition and Learning, 11(1), 1–13. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11409-016-9154-x
papers.summary: This special issue compiled 13 experiments across six manuscripts with over 1000 participants, aiming to circumvent publication bias and investigate moderators of the disfluency effect. All 13 studies failed to show overall better performance due to disfluency, suggesting the effect is marginal or highly conditional. The authors concluded that disfluency interventions are not recommendable for educational settings.

papers.title: Meyer et al. 2015
papers.apa: MacLeod of Fuinary, Baron, (George Fielden MacLeod) (17 June 1895–27 June 1991). (2007). Who Was Who. https://doi.org/10.1093/ww/9780199540884.013.u174007
papers.summary: This paper reviewed 13 studies on the effect of disfluent font on Cognitive Reflection Test scores, reporting effect sizes ranging from d = -0.25 to d = 0.12, all of which were non-significant. A pooled analysis of 17 studies (including Thompson et al. and the original Alter et al. study) yielded a non-significant effect size of d = -0.01. The findings suggest a lack of robust evidence for the disfluency effect.

papers.title: Thompson et al. 2013
papers.apa: Thompson et al. review. (2016). https://doi.org/10.5194/acp-2015-1039-rc1
papers.summary: This paper presented three studies investigating the effect of disfluent font on Cognitive Reflection Test scores. The reported effect sizes ranged from d = -0.19 to d = 0.25, all of which were non-significant. These findings did not support the presence of a disfluency effect on cognitive reflection.

papers.title: Remembering can cause forgetting: Retrieval dynamics in long-term memory’
papers.apa: Anderson, M. C., Bjork, R. A., & Bjork, E. L. (1994). Remembering can cause forgetting: Retrieval dynamics in long-term memory. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 20(5), 1063–1087. https://doi.org/10.1037/0278-7393.20.5.1063
papers.summary: This foundational paper by Anderson, Bjork, and Bjork (1994) introduced the concept of retrieval-induced forgetting, demonstrating that retrieving some items from a category can impair the subsequent recall of other unpracticed items from the same category. The authors proposed that this forgetting occurs due to inhibitory mechanisms that suppress competing memories during retrieval practice. This work established the retrieval practice paradigm as a key method for studying memory inhibition.

papers.title: Jonker et al. 2013
papers.summary: This paper by Jonker et al. (2013) conducted a meta-analysis of retrieval-induced forgetting studies, synthesizing findings across numerous experiments. They reported a robust overall effect of RIF, with varying effect sizes depending on methodological factors. The meta-analysis provided strong evidence for the existence of RIF across different paradigms and populations.

papers.title: Rowland et al. 2014
papers.summary: This study attempted to replicate retrieval-induced forgetting across three experiments, using both traditional college undergraduate (N=72) and diverse internet (N=140) samples. Experiments 1 and 2 successfully replicated RIF using the traditional retrieval practice paradigm, reporting Cohen's d values of 0.31 and 0.38, respectively. However, Experiment 3 failed to observe RIF when using an independent cue procedure, suggesting the effect's sensitivity to methodological variations.

papers.title: Emotional mood as a context for learning and recall’
papers.summary: This foundational paper, by Bower, Monteiro, and Gilligan (1978), introduced the concept of mood-dependent retrieval. It reported evidence that memory recall is enhanced when an individual's mood at retrieval matches their mood at encoding, particularly in a two-list interference design.

papers.title: Bower and Mayer 1985
papers.apa: Bower, G. H., & Mayer, J. D. (1985). Failure to replicate mood-dependent retrieval. Bulletin of the Psychonomic Society, 23(1), 39–42. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03329773
papers.summary: This study attempted to replicate the mood-dependent retrieval effect using a two-list interference design, similar to the original Bower et al. (1978) experiment. The authors found no evidence for mood dependency in recall, with subjects performing similarly regardless of whether their learning and testing moods matched or mismatched. They concluded that mood-dependent retrieval is an unreliable laboratory phenomenon, though they did observe a mood-congruity effect.

papers.title: Eich 1995
papers.apa: Eich, E. (1995). Searching for Mood Dependent Memory. Psychological Science, 6(2), 67–75. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.1995.tb00309.x
papers.summary: This paper provides a theoretical review aiming to clarify the circumstances and mechanisms underlying mood-dependent memory (MDM). It focuses on four pivotal factors: the nature of encoded events, the type of retrieval task, the efficacy of mood induction, and the dimensionality of mood alterations. The author suggests that a detailed exploration of these factors can help resolve existing controversies and provide deeper insights into MDM's foundations.

papers.title: An experimental study of imagination
papers.apa: Perky, C. W. (1910). An Experimental Study of Imagination. The American Journal of Psychology, 21(3), 422. https://doi.org/10.2307/1413350
papers.summary: Perky's original study demonstrated that participants asked to visualize objects while faint images were projected onto a screen often reported only their mental image, failing to distinguish it from the external stimulus. This suggested that imagery could be mistaken for or interfere with perception. The study did not report specific effect sizes but noted consistent mistaking of perceptual for imaginative consciousness across experiments.

papers.title: Craver-Lemley and Reeves 1987
papers.apa: Craver-Lemley, C., & Reeves, A. (1987). Visual Imagery Selectively Reduces Vernier Acuity. Perception, 16(5), 599–614. https://doi.org/10.1068/p160599
papers.summary: This study investigated the Perky effect on vernier acuity, finding that imagining vertical lines near fixation reduced accuracy in reporting target offsets from 80% to 65%. The effect was localized, pattern-insensitive, and long-lasting, suggesting imagery causes a reduction in visual sensitivity equivalent to a 0.24 log unit reduction in target energy.

papers.title: Okada and Matsuoka 1992
papers.apa: Okada, H., & Matsuoka, K. (1992). Effects of Auditory Imagery on the Detection of a Pure Tone in White Noise: Experimental Evidence of the Auditory Perky Effect. Perceptual and Motor Skills, 74(2), 443–448. https://doi.org/10.2466/pms.1992.74.2.443
papers.summary: This research explored an auditory Perky effect, showing that imagining a pure tone interfered with detecting a physically presented tone only when their frequencies matched. This interference, with an effect size of ηp2 = 0.346, supports the idea that auditory imagery can assimilate or compete with external auditory signals.

papers.title: Reeves et al. 2020
papers.apa: Reeves et al. (2020). https://doi.org/10.5194/acp-2019-964-rc2
papers.summary: This paper found that visual imagery's interference with acuity depended on baseline performance: it interfered when performance was good but facilitated it when poor. For subjects scoring over 80% without imagery (N=47), the mean Perky effect was 21%, and a correlation of r=0.63 (N=111) was observed between the Perky effect and baseline accuracy.

papers.title: Segal and Fusella 1970
papers.apa: Table 2: Details of the NOS (Oh et al., 2023; Roderburg et al., 2021; Kurita et al., 2017; Musicco et al., 2013; Jørgensen et al., 2012; Khan et al., 2011; Baxter et al., 2009; Ren et al., 2022; Valentine et al., 2022; Ording et al., 2019; Heun et al., 2013; Ou et al., 2013; Attner et al., 2010). (n.d.). https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.18888/table-2
papers.summary: This study demonstrated that mental imagery significantly blocked the detection of both visual and auditory signals. In Experiment 1, sensitivity (d') was lower during visual (1.70) and auditory (2.13) imaging compared to control tasks (1.93, 1.72). Experiment 2 similarly showed reduced sensitivity during imaging (visual d'=1.48, auditory d'=1.68) compared to control (d'=2.64, 2.84), with all differences being statistically significant (p < .001).

papers.title: Segal and Gordon 1969
papers.apa: Segal, S. J., & Gordon, P.-E. (1969). The Perky Effect Revisited: Blocking of Visual Signals by Imagery. Perceptual and Motor Skills, 28(3), 791–797. https://doi.org/10.2466/pms.1969.28.3.791
papers.summary: This study confirmed the Perky effect, showing poorer detection of ambiguous signals during imagery. Perceptual sensitivity (d') was highest in a simple discrimination task (2.03-2.39), intermediate when participants were aware of signals during imaging (1.54), and lowest when they were unaware (0.74-1.19), indicating imagery blocks visual signal detection.

papers.apa: Segal, S. J. (1968). The Perky effect: Changes in reality judgments with changing methods of inquiry. Psychonomic Science, 12(8), 393–394. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03331370

papers.effect_name: Positive emotions broaden scope of attention
papers.title: Positive emotions broaden the scope of attention and thought‐action repertoire
papers.apa: Fredrickson, B. L., & Branigan, C. (2005). Positive emotions broaden the scope of attention and thought‐action repertoires. Cognition & Emotion, 19(3), 313–332. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699930441000238
papers.summary: This foundational paper tested the broaden-and-build theory, hypothesizing that positive emotions broaden attention and thought-action repertoires. Across two experiments with 104 college students, positive emotions (amusement, contentment) were found to broaden the scope of attention (Experiment 1, using a global-local visual processing task) and thought-action repertoires (Experiment 2) compared to neutral states. Negative emotions, conversely, narrowed these repertoires.

papers.effect_name: Positive emotions broaden scope of attention
papers.title: Bruyneel et al. 2013
papers.apa: Bruyneel, L., van Steenbergen, H., Hommel, B., Band, G. P. H., De Raedt, R., & Koster, E. H. W. (2012). Happy but still focused: failures to find evidence for a mood-induced widening of visual attention. Psychological Research, 77(3), 320–332. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00426-012-0432-1
papers.summary: This study investigated whether positive mood induction influences selective attention in a visual flanker task, aiming to replicate the broadening effect. Across three experiments (e.g., N=33 in Exp 1), the authors consistently found no evidence that positive affect impaired selective attention or increased processing of distant distractors (Exp 1: ηp2 = 0.04, Exp 2: ηp2 = 0.001, Exp 3: ηp2 = 0.01). The findings cast doubt on the reliability of a mood-induced widening of visual attention.

papers.effect_name: Positive emotions broaden scope of attention
papers.title: Huntsinger 2013
papers.apa: Huntsinger, J. R. (2013). Does Emotion Directly Tune the Scope of Attention? Current Directions in Psychological Science, 22(4), 265–270. https://doi.org/10.1177/0963721413480364
papers.summary: This paper reviews existing research on affect and attentional scope, challenging the idea of a fixed link where positive affect always broadens attention and negative affect always narrows it. The author proposes a more flexible model, suggesting that affective feelings might instead influence whether individuals act on momentarily dominant tendencies to focus broadly or narrowly. This implies that the effect of emotion on attention is not always direct or unidirectional.

papers.effect_name: Positive emotions broaden scope of attention
papers.title: Huntsinger et al. 2010
papers.apa: Segal, S. J., & Fusella, V. (1970). Influence of imaged pictures and sounds on detection of visual and auditory signals. Journal of Experimental Psychology, 83(3, Pt.1), 458–464. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0028840
papers.summary: This paper is cited as finding that positive affect empowers whatever focus is momentarily dominant, rather than having a fixed broadening effect. It reportedly found effect sizes of d=0.58 (Exp 1) and d=0.71 (Exp 2), suggesting that positive affect can either broaden or narrow attention depending on the context.

papers.title: Interactions between cognition and emotion during response inhibition’
papers.apa: Pessoa, L., Padmala, S., Kenzer, A., & Bauer, A. (2012). Interactions between cognition and emotion during response inhibition. Emotion, 12(1), 192–197. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0024109
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated how emotional stimuli affect response inhibition using a stop-signal task. In Experiment 1 (N=32), both fearful and happy faces, when used as stop signals, significantly improved response inhibition compared to neutral faces (fearful vs neutral: d=0.44; happy vs neutral: d=0.33). The authors concluded that emotion can either enhance or impair cognitive performance depending on the emotional potency of the stimuli involved.

papers.title: Pandey and Gupta 2022
papers.apa: Pandey, S., & Gupta, R. (2022). Irrelevant angry faces impair response inhibition, and the go and stop processes share attentional resources. Scientific Reports, 12(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-022-19116-5
papers.summary: This study investigated the impact of irrelevant emotional facial expressions (happy, angry, neutral) as stop signals on response inhibition. When the go signal was non-emotional, angry faces as stop signals impaired inhibitory control compared to happy and neutral faces (d=0.35 for angry vs happy). This suggests that emotion processing, particularly angry faces, can capture attention and impair inhibition, contradicting the original finding of facilitation by emotional faces.

papers.title: Williams et al. 2020
papers.apa: Williams, S. E., Lenze, E. J., & Waring, J. D. (2020). Positive information facilitates response inhibition in older adults only when emotion is task-relevant. Cognition and Emotion, 34(8), 1632–1645. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699931.2020.1793303
papers.summary: This series of three studies (N=80+ per study) investigated the impact of emotional facial expressions on response inhibition in younger and older adults. Studies 1 and 2 found no effect of emotional stop signals (fear, happy, neutral) on response inhibition when emotion was task-irrelevant (e.g., Study 1: d=0.03 for fearful vs neutral). Study 3 showed that emotional faces altered inhibition only when participants explicitly attended to emotional attributes, with happy faces leading to better inhibition than fearful faces, and negative information impairing inhibition in younger adults.

papers.title: When approach motivation and behavioral inhibition collide: Behavior regulation through stimulus devaluation’
papers.apa: Veling, H., Holland, R. W., & van Knippenberg, A. (2008). When approach motivation and behavioral inhibition collide: Behavior regulation through stimulus devaluation. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 44(4), 1013–1019. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2008.03.004
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of inhibition-induced devaluation, proposing that inhibiting responses to positive stimuli leads to their devaluation as an automatic behavior-regulation mechanism. Across three experiments (e.g., Exp 1: N=33), the authors found that participants evaluated no-go stimuli significantly lower than go stimuli (d=-0.49) and new stimuli (d=0.48). Experiments 2 and 3 further demonstrated that this devaluation effect is moderated by stimulus valence, occurring only for positive stimuli.

papers.title: Chen et al. 2016
papers.apa: Chen, Z., Veling, H., Dijksterhuis, A., & Holland, R. W. (2016). How does not responding to appetitive stimuli cause devaluation: Evaluative conditioning or response inhibition? Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 145(12), 1687–1701. https://doi.org/10.1037/xge0000236
papers.summary: This paper, comprising six experiments (five preregistered), investigated the mechanisms underlying inhibition-induced devaluation, replicating the effect and extending its theoretical understanding. Experiments 1-3 (e.g., Exp 1: N=41) consistently showed that not responding to appetitive stimuli in a go/no-go task caused devaluation (e.g., Exp 1: nogo vs go d=-0.39, nogo vs untrained d=-0.57), even without an explicit no-go cue. The findings suggest the devaluation is a result of response inhibition, contingent on the percentage of no-go trials, and specific to appetitive stimuli.

papers.title: Wessel et al. 2014
papers.apa:
Supplemental Material for Arbitrary Stimuli Are Not Devalued by
						Stopping Action: A Registered Replication of Wessel et al.
						(2014)\n. (2025). Journal of Experimental Psychology: General. https://doi.org/10.1037/xge0000964.supp
papers.summary: No abstract or extracted text was provided for this paper. Based on the provided replication effect sizes (Exp 1: η2= 0.25, Exp 2: η2= 0.24), this study appears to have found strong evidence for the inhibition-induced devaluation effect. However, without the paper's content, details on methodology, sample size, or specific findings cannot be accurately summarized.

papers.title: Inhibition-induced forgetting: when more control leads to less memory
papers.apa: Chiu, Y.-C., & Egner, T. (2014). Inhibition-Induced Forgetting. Psychological Science, 26(1), 27–38. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797614553945
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated inhibition-induced forgetting, proposing that memory is impaired for stimuli associated with inhibited responses due to attentional resource shunting. Across multiple experiments using go/no-go and stop-signal tasks, the authors consistently found impaired recognition memory for stimuli presented during no-go and stop trials compared to go trials, with effect sizes ranging from d=0.28 to d=0.45.

papers.title: Le and Cho 2020
papers.apa: Lee, H. J., & Cho, Y. S. (2019). The effect of threatening facial expressions on inhibition-induced forgetting depends on their task-relevance. Cognition and Emotion, 34(3), 526–538. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699931.2019.1650721
papers.summary: This replication study (N=40) explored whether inhibition-induced forgetting is modulated by threatening facial expressions. While Experiment 1 (d=0.06) showed no inhibition-induced forgetting, Experiment 2 (d=0.32) observed the effect for both angry and neutral faces when expressions were task-irrelevant. Experiment 3 (d=0.31) found the effect was negated only when responses to angry faces were inhibited, suggesting task-relevance plays a key role in the interaction between cognitive control and memory encoding.

papers.effect_name: Body-object interaction (BOI) effect in lexical-semantic processing
papers.title: Evidence for the activation of sensorimotor information during visual word recognition: The body–object interaction effect
papers.apa: Siakaluk, P. D., Pexman, P. M., Aguilera, L., Owen, W. J., & Sears, C. R. (2008). Evidence for the activation of sensorimotor information during visual word recognition: The body–object interaction effect. Cognition, 106(1), 433–443. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2006.12.011
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.effect_name: Body-object interaction (BOI) effect in lexical-semantic processing
papers.title: Siakaluk, P. D., Pexman, P. M., Sears, C. R., Wilson, K., Locheed, K., & Owen, W. J. (2008). The Benefits of Sensorimotor Knowledge: Body–Object Interaction Facilitates Semantic Processing. Cognitive Science, 32(3), 591–605.
papers.apa: Siakaluk, P. D., Pexman, P. M., Sears, C. R., Wilson, K., Locheed, K., & Owen, W. J. (2008). The Benefits of Sensorimotor Knowledge: Body–Object Interaction Facilitates Semantic Processing. Cognitive Science, 32(3), 591–605. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1080/03640210802035399
papers.summary: This study replicated and extended the BOI effect, finding faster and more accurate responses for high BOI words in semantic categorization tasks (Experiment 1, η2 = .38 for both tasks) and a semantic lexical decision task (Experiment 2, η2 = .57). The BOI effect was larger in the SLDT, suggesting it facilitates both semantic feedback and processing. These findings are consistent with embodied cognition theories.

papers.effect_name: Body-object interaction (BOI) effect in lexical-semantic processing
papers.title: Tousignant and Pexman 2012
papers.summary: This study investigated how task context modulates BOI effects in semantic categorization, using 159 participants across four conditions. They found facilitatory BOI effects (faster classification for high-BOI words) only when participants were explicitly expecting entity words (η2 = 0.69, 0.25, 0.16 for three conditions). Critically, no BOI advantage was observed when participants only expected action words (d = 0.05). This suggests a strong role for decision context in modulating BOI effects, supporting a dynamic view of conceptual knowledge.

papers.effect_name: Body-object interaction (BOI) effect in lexical-semantic processing
papers.title: Wellsby, M., Siakaluk, P. D., Owen, W. J., & Pexman, P. M. (2011). Embodied semantic processing: The body-object interaction effect in a non-manual task. Language and Cognition, 3(1), 1–14.
papers.apa: Wellsby, M., Siakaluk, P. D., Owen, W. J., & Pexman, P. M. (2011). Embodied semantic processing: The body-object interaction effect in a non-manual task. Language and Cognition, 3(1), 1–14. https://doi.org/10.1515/langcog.2011.001
papers.summary: This study investigated whether BOI effects are specific to button-press tasks, which could suggest motor priming rather than activation of stored motor information. Across three versions of a go/no-go semantic categorization task, including two with verbal responses, facilitatory BOI effects were consistently observed (η2 = .32, .32, .33). These findings contradict the motor priming hypothesis and support the view that stored motor information is a crucial component of the lexical semantic system.

papers.title: The formation of false memories
papers.apa: Loftus, E. F., & Pickrell, J. E. (1995). The Formation of False Memories. Psychiatric Annals, 25(12), 720–725. https://doi.org/10.3928/0048-5713-19951201-07
papers.summary: This foundational study demonstrated that individuals could be induced to form false memories for an event, such as being lost in a shopping mall as a child. Using suggestive techniques, the researchers found that approximately 25% of participants reported remembering the fabricated event, illustrating the malleability of human memory.

papers.title: Murphy et al. 2023
papers.apa: Murphy, G., Dawson, C. A., Huston, C., Ballantyne, L., Barrett, E., Cowman, C. S., Fitzsimons, C., Maher, J., Ryan, K. M., & Greene, C. M. (2023). Lost in the mall again: a preregistered replication and extension of Loftus & Pickrell (1995). Memory, 31(6), 818–830. https://doi.org/10.1080/09658211.2023.2198327
papers.summary: This preregistered replication and extension of Loftus & Pickrell (1995) aimed to re-evaluate the false memory implantation effect. The study successfully replicated the original finding, with approximately 35% of participants reporting false memories for the suggested event, thereby providing further evidence for the robustness of the phenomenon.

papers.title: Serial dependence in visual perception
papers.apa: Fischer, J., & Whitney, D. (2014). Serial dependence in visual perception. Nature Neuroscience, 17(5), 738–743. https://doi.org/10.1038/nn.3689
papers.summary: This foundational study introduced serial dependence, demonstrating that perceived orientation is systematically biased toward recently seen stimuli in an orientation judgment task. The effect was modulated by attention and tuned to spatial and temporal proximity, operating over several seconds. The authors proposed a 'continuity field' mechanism to explain how the visual system uses prior input to promote visual stability, reporting original effect sizes (a) of 8.19°, 6.76°, and 8.75°.

papers.title: Fritsche et al. 2017
papers.apa: Afghanistan health profile : statistics on health and disease in Afghanistan / Wessel Swart andhellip;[et al.]. (1997). https://doi.org/10.2458/azu_acku_pamphlet_ra541_a3_s83_1997
papers.summary: This paper investigated serial dependence, reporting effect sizes (a) ranging from 1.15° to 1.17°. While the effect was present, these magnitudes suggest a smaller bias compared to the original findings, indicating a replication of the phenomenon but potentially with a reduced strength.

papers.title: Czoschke et al. 2019
papers.apa: Czoschke, S., Fischer, C., Beitner, J., Kaiser, J., & Bledowski, C. (2018). Two types of serial dependence in visual working memory. British Journal of Psychology, 110(2), 256–267. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/bjop.12349
papers.summary: This study differentiated between two types of serial dependence in visual working memory: across-trial attraction and within-trial repulsion. They found that the current stimulus (S1) was attracted towards the preceding cued stimulus (S2) across trials (a=6.11°, 4.11°), but S2 was repulsed from S1 within the same trial. The findings suggest different mechanisms for integrating or segregating memory contents based on task relevance.

papers.title: Fischer et al. 2020
papers.apa: Fischer, C., Czoschke, S., Peters, B., Rahm, B., Kaiser, J., & Bledowski, C. (2020). Context information supports serial dependence of multiple visual objects across memory episodes. Nature Communications, 11(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-020-15874-w
papers.summary: This paper further explored the serial dependence effect, reporting an effect of the previous target on the current target with 'a' values of 2.99° and 2.00°. These findings corroborate the existence of serial dependence, showing a consistent bias towards prior stimuli, albeit with magnitudes that are generally lower than those reported in the foundational study.

papers.title: Verifying different-modality properties for concepts produces switching costs
papers.apa: Pecher, D., Zeelenberg, R., & Barsalou, L. W. (2003). Verifying Different-Modality Properties for Concepts Produces Switching Costs. Psychological Science, 14(2), 119–124. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9280.t01-1-01429
papers.summary: This foundational study introduced the modality-switching cost effect, demonstrating that verifying a concept's property is slower when the property's sensory modality differs from the preceding trial's property. Across two experiments, participants showed a significant switching cost (e.g., d=0.18, 29ms in Exp 1a), supporting the hypothesis that conceptual processing involves perceptual simulation rather than amodal representations. The effect was observed even without imagery instructions and was not due to associative priming.

papers.title: Vermeulen et al. 2007
papers.apa: Vermeulen, N., Niedenthal, P. M., & Luminet, O. (2007). Switching Between Sensory and Affective Systems Incurs Processing Costs. Cognitive Science, 31(1), 183–192. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1080/03640210709336990
papers.summary: This study replicated and extended the modality-switching cost effect, showing that processing costs arise when switching between sensory modalities and also when switching between sensory and affective systems for emotional concepts. Participants exhibited longer reaction times and higher error rates when switching modalities, consistent with the original findings (d=0.5 reported in the prompt). These results further support the embodied cognition view, particularly for emotional concepts.

papers.title: Lynott & Connell (2009)
papers.apa: Lynott, D., & Connell, L. (2009). Modality exclusivity norms for 423 object properties. Behavior Research Methods, 41(2), 558–564. https://doi.org/10.3758/brm.41.2.558
papers.summary: This paper developed modality exclusivity norms for 423 object properties, providing ratings of experiential strength across five perceptual modalities. The authors found that most properties are multimodal and that these norms can help researchers create cleaner stimulus sets for investigating modality-specific issues in conceptual representation. It is a resource paper, not a direct replication.

papers.title: Ambrosi et al. 2011
papers.apa: Ambrosi, S., Kalenine, S., Blaye, A., & Bonthoux, F. (2010). Modality switching cost during property verification by 7 years of age. International Journal of Behavioral Development, 35(1), 78–83. https://doi.org/10.1177/0165025410371603
papers.summary: This study investigated the modality-switching cost in 7-year-old children and adults using a property verification task involving visual and motor properties. Both age groups exhibited a significant modality-switching cost, with longer verification times in the different-modality condition (d=0.2 for adults, d=0.24 for children). The effect did not interact with age or property modality, providing further support for an embodied view of concepts in both children and adults.

papers.title: Look but don’t touch: Tactile disadvantage in processing modality-specific words
papers.apa: Pecher, D., Zeelenberg, R., & Barsalou, L. W. (2003). Verifying Different-Modality Properties for Concepts Produces Switching Costs. Psychological Science, 14(2), 119–124. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9280.t01-1-01429
papers.summary: This study investigated whether conceptual processing exhibits modality switching costs, similar to perceptual processing. Participants verified properties of concepts (e.g., BLENDER-loud) and showed slower reaction times when switching between modalities (e.g., auditory to taste) compared to staying within the same modality. This finding, observed without explicit imagery instructions, supports the hypothesis that perceptual simulation underlies conceptual processing.

papers.title: From detection to identification: Response to multiple targets in rapid serial visual presentation
papers.apa: Broadbent, D. E., & Broadbent, M. H. P. (1987). From detection to identification: Response to multiple targets in rapid serial visual presentation. Perception & Psychophysics, 42(2), 105–113. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03210498
papers.summary: This foundational study (N=20, 40, 12, 24 across experiments) demonstrated that identifying a first target in a rapid visual stream significantly impaired the identification of a second target for over half a second, particularly at 200-500ms intervals. The authors proposed that demanding identification processes for the first target interfere with those for the second, highlighting a limitation in human visual processing. The original effect size was calculated as ηp2=0.77.

papers.title: Raymond et al. 1992
papers.apa: Raymond, J. E., Shapiro, K. L., & Arnell, K. M. (1992). Temporary suppression of visual processing in an RSVP task: An attentional blink? Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 18(3), 849–860. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-1523.18.3.849
papers.summary: This study (N=5) investigated the attentional blink, asking participants to identify a target letter and detect a probe in an RSVP task. They found a significant reduction in probe detection when it appeared 180-270ms after the target, explicitly replicating previous findings of a post-target processing deficit. The authors suggested that interference from stimuli presented before target-identification processes are complete causes a temporary suppression of visual attention mechanisms, with a replication effect size of ηp2=0.33.

papers.title: Chun & Potter 1995
papers.apa: Chun, M. M., & Potter, M. C. (1995). A two-stage model for multiple target detection in rapid serial visual presentation. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 21(1), 109–127. https://doi.org/10.1037/0096-1523.21.1.109
papers.summary: This research (e.g., N=6 for Experiment 1) extended the understanding of the attentional blink (AB) using categorically defined targets in seven experiments. They replicated the AB deficit for a second target appearing 200-500ms after the first, and also found it for a third target, with a replication effect size of ηp2=0.73. The findings supported that AB is triggered by local interference and modulated by target-distractor discriminability, leading to a proposed two-stage model for multiple target detection.

papers.title: Implicit theories and their role in judgments and reactions: A word from two perspectives
papers.apa: Dweck, C. S., Chiu, C., & Hong, Y. (1995). Implicit Theories and Their Role in Judgments and Reactions: A Word From Two Perspectives. Psychological Inquiry, 6(4), 267–285. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327965pli0604_1
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduces the implicit theories model, proposing that beliefs about the fixedness (entity theory) or malleability (incremental theory) of attributes like intelligence shape judgments and reactions. Entity theorists tend to attribute failures to fixed ability and exhibit helplessness, while incremental theorists focus on effort/strategy and show mastery-oriented responses. The authors review multiple studies (N not specified for individual studies, but various populations) showing these theories influence attributions, goal choices, and social perceptions, with high reliability for measures (alpha .85-.98).

papers.title: Folioano et al. 2019
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., Taylor, D., & Garrity, G. M. (2009). Exemplar Abstract for Aquamicrobium lusatiense (Fritsche et al. 1999) Kämpfer et al. 2009, Defluvibacter lusatiensis corrig. Fritsche et al. 1999 and Defluvibacter lusatiae (sic) Fritsche et al. 1999. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.1409
papers.summary: This paper conducted a large-scale replication attempt of a growth mindset intervention or correlation, finding no evidence for the effect. It reported a meta-analytic effect size of d=0.00 [-0.02, 0.02], suggesting a lack of replication for the general effect.

papers.title: Sisk 2018
papers.apa: Sisk, V. F., Burgoyne, A. P., Sun, J., Butler, J. L., & Macnamara, B. N. (2018). To What Extent and Under Which Circumstances Are Growth Mind-Sets Important to Academic Achievement? Two Meta-Analyses. Psychological Science, 29(4), 549–571. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797617739704
papers.summary: This meta-analysis (k=273, N=365,915 for correlation; k=43, N=57,155 for interventions) examined the relationship between mind-set and academic achievement and the effectiveness of mind-set interventions. Overall effects were weak for both, with a non-experimental correlation of r=0.10 [0.08, 0.13] and an intervention effect of d=0.08 [0.02, 0.14]. However, the authors noted some support for the theory among students with low socioeconomic status or those academically at risk.

papers.title: The Role of Deliberate Practice in the Acquisition of Expert Performance
papers.apa: Ericsson, K. A., Krampe, R. T., & Tesch-Römer, C. (1993). The role of deliberate practice in the acquisition of expert performance. Psychological Review, 100(3), 363–406. https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-295x.100.3.363
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of deliberate practice, proposing that sustained, effortful practice focused on improving specific aspects of performance is the primary driver of expertise. It famously suggested that approximately 10,000 hours of deliberate practice are necessary to reach expert levels in complex domains.

papers.title: Macnamara et al. 2016
papers.apa: Macnamara, B. N., Moreau, D., & Hambrick, D. Z. (2016). The Relationship Between Deliberate Practice and Performance in Sports. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 11(3), 333–350. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691616635591
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated the relationship between deliberate practice and performance in sports, finding that deliberate practice accounted for only 18% of the variance in overall sports performance (ES=.43). Crucially, it explained only 1% of the variance among elite-level performers, challenging the idea that deliberate practice accounts for performance differences even at the highest levels. The study also found no evidence that higher-skilled athletes started their sport earlier.

papers.title: Learning style inventory: technical manual
papers.apa: Figure 17: Computational cost comparison (Srinivas et al., 2019; Wazid et al., 2019; Bera, Chattaraj & Das, 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Chaudhry et al., 2021; Tanveer et al., 2021; Li et al., 2022). (n.d.). https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj-cs.3399/fig-17
papers.summary: This paper is likely the technical manual for a learning style inventory, which would be foundational for the concept. Without an abstract or extracted text, specific details are unavailable.

papers.title: ‘Learning styles and learning spaces: enhancing experiential learning in higher education’
papers.apa: Kolb, A. Y., & Kolb, D. A. (2005). Learning Styles and Learning Spaces: Enhancing Experiential Learning in Higher Education. Academy of Management Learning & Education, 4(2), 193–212. https://doi.org/10.5465/amle.2005.17268566
papers.summary: This paper likely explores the conceptual link between learning styles and educational environments, potentially laying groundwork for the effect. Without an abstract or extracted text, specific details are unavailable.

papers.title: Husmann and O’Loughlin  2019
papers.apa: Husmann, P. R., & O’Loughlin, V. D. (2018). Another Nail in the Coffin for Learning Styles? Disparities among Undergraduate Anatomy Students’ Study Strategies, Class Performance, and Reported VARK Learning Styles. Anatomical Sciences Education, 12(1), 6–19. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ase.1777
papers.summary: This study investigated whether undergraduate anatomy students (N=426) utilized study strategies aligned with their VARK learning styles and if this alignment correlated with course outcomes. Results showed most students did not align strategies with VARK, and performance was not correlated with VARK categories. Some specific strategies (e.g., virtual microscope use, r=0.127) correlated with grades, but their alignment with VARK did not, providing further evidence against learning styles.

papers.title: Knoll et al. 2017
papers.apa: Knoll, A. R., Otani, H., Skeel, R. L., & Van Horn, K. R. (2016). Learning style, judgements of learning, and learning of verbal and visual information. British Journal of Psychology, 108(3), 544–563. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/bjop.12214
papers.summary: This paper likely presents an empirical investigation or critique of learning styles. While specific findings are not detailed in an abstract, the provided effect sizes (e.g., d=0.61 for pictures vs. words, ηp2=.51 for list types) suggest an analysis of instructional methods, potentially challenging the learning styles hypothesis.

papers.title: Pashler et al. 2009
papers.apa: Pashler, H., McDaniel, M., Rohrer, D., & Bjork, R. (2008). Learning Styles. Psychological Science in the Public Interest, 9(3), 105–119. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1539-6053.2009.01038.x
papers.summary: This review critically examined the scientific evidence for learning styles, defining specific experimental criteria for validation (a style-by-method interaction). The authors found ample evidence of individual preferences but virtually no evidence for the required interaction pattern. They concluded that there is no adequate evidence base to justify incorporating learning-styles assessments into general educational practice.

papers.title: literature review but no n reported, citations= 3422 (GS, March 2023
papers.apa: Pashler, H., McDaniel, M., Rohrer, D., & Bjork, R. (2008). Learning Styles. Psychological Science in the Public Interest, 9(3), 105–119. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1539-6053.2009.01038.x
papers.summary: This review critically examined the scientific evidence for learning styles, defining specific experimental criteria for validation (a style-by-method interaction). The authors found ample evidence of individual preferences but virtually no evidence for the required interaction pattern. They concluded that there is no adequate evidence base to justify incorporating learning-styles assessments into general educational practice.

papers.title: Willingham et al. 2015
papers.apa: Willingham, D. T., Hughes, E. M., & Dobolyi, D. G. (2015). The Scientific Status of Learning Styles Theories. Teaching of Psychology, 42(3), 266–271. https://doi.org/10.1177/0098628315589505
papers.summary: This paper reviewed theories of learning styles, noting their popularity despite a lack of scientific support. The authors concluded that there is insufficient evidence to validate these theories and suggested that educators' efforts would be better spent on other evidence-based instructional strategies.

papers.title: Meltzoff and Moore 1977
papers.apa: Meltzoff, A. N., & Moore, M. K. (1977). Imitation of Facial and Manual Gestures by Human Neonates. Science, 198(4312), 75–78. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.198.4312.75
papers.summary: This foundational study reported that infants aged 12 to 21 days could imitate facial and manual gestures, suggesting an innate capacity for intermodal matching. The authors argued this behavior could not be explained by conditioning or innate releasing mechanisms, implying neonates can equate observed behaviors with their own unseen actions.

papers.title: Oostenbroek et al. 2016
papers.apa: Meltzoff, A. N., Murray, L., Simpson, E., Heimann, M., Nagy, E., Nadel, J., Pedersen, E. J., Brooks, R., Messinger, D. S., Pascalis, L. D., Subiaul, F., Paukner, A., & Ferrari, P. F. (2017). Re‐examination of Oostenbroek et al. (2016): evidence for neonatal imitation of tongue protrusion. Developmental Science, 21(4). Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/desc.12609
papers.summary: This large-scale longitudinal study, involving 106 infants tested at multiple time points (1, 3, 6, and 9 weeks of age), investigated the presence of neonate imitation. The researchers found no statistical evidence for imitation of any of the 11 gestures tested, challenging the robustness of the original findings.

papers.title: ‘Transmission of aggression through imitation of aggressive models’
papers.apa: Bandura, A., Ross, D., & Ross, S. A. (1961). Transmission of aggression through imitation of aggressive models. The Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology, 63(3), 575–582. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0045925
papers.summary: This foundational study, often referred to as the Bobo Doll experiment, demonstrated that children exposed to an aggressive adult model were more likely to imitate aggressive behaviors towards a doll. The study provided early evidence for observational learning as a mechanism for the transmission of aggression.

papers.title: Anderson and& Bushman 2001
papers.apa: Anderson, C. A., & Bushman, B. J. (2001). Effects of Violent Video Games on Aggressive Behavior, Aggressive Cognition, Aggressive Affect, Physiological Arousal, and Prosocial Behavior: A Meta-Analytic Review of the Scientific Literature. Psychological Science, 12(5), 353–359. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9280.00366
papers.summary: This meta-analytic review concluded that violent video games increase aggressive behavior (meta-analytic r=0.27), aggressive cognition, aggressive affect, and physiological arousal in children and young adults. The authors found support across experimental and nonexperimental studies, and in both laboratory and field settings, also noting a decrease in prosocial behavior.

papers.title: Bushman 2016
papers.apa: Bushman, B. J. (2016). Violent media and hostile appraisals: A meta‐analytic review. Aggressive Behavior, 42(6), 605–613. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ab.21655
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 37 independent studies (N=10,410) found a small to moderate average correlation (r=0.20) between exposure to violent media and hostile appraisals. The effect was consistent across experimental, cross-sectional, and longitudinal designs, and showed no evidence of publication bias. The authors suggested that effects might accumulate over time, as the link was positively related to age.

papers.title: Drummond et al. 2020
papers.apa: Drummond, A., Sauer, J. D., & Ferguson, C. J. (2020). Do longitudinal studies support long-term relationships between aggressive game play and youth aggressive behaviour? A meta-analytic examination. Royal Society Open Science, 7(7), 200373. https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.200373
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 28 independent longitudinal studies (N=21,000 youth) found a very small overall effect size (r=0.059) for the link between aggressive game content and youth aggression, with no publication bias. The authors noted that effect sizes decreased with longer longitudinal periods and were lower in better-designed studies, suggesting that observed correlations might be due to methodological weaknesses and researcher expectancy effects rather than true long-term effects.

papers.title: Elson and Ferguson 2014
papers.apa: Young, R. K. (2009). Clinical, Technical, and Other Common Terms. In AMA Manual of Style. Oxford University Press. https://doi.org/10.1093/jama/9780195176339.021.215
papers.summary: This paper critically reviewed the literature on violent digital games and aggression, concluding that the evidence is, at best, mixed. The authors argued that the existing research cannot unambiguously support claims that such games are harmful or represent a public health crisis.

papers.title: Ferguson and Kilburn 2009
papers.apa: Ferguson, C. J., & Kilburn, J. (2009). The Public Health Risks of Media Violence: A Meta-Analytic Review. The Journal of Pediatrics, 154(5), 759–763. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpeds.2008.11.033
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated the link between violent media and aggression, reporting an overall effect size of r=0.08. The authors' findings contributed to the debate by suggesting a smaller effect than some previous meta-analyses.

papers.title: Greitemeyer and Mügge 2014
papers.apa: Greitemeyer, T., & Mügge, D. O. (2014). Video Games Do Affect Social Outcomes. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 40(5), 578–589. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167213520459
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 98 independent studies (N=36,965) found that violent video games significantly increase aggression and related variables (r=0.19), while decreasing prosocial outcomes. Conversely, prosocial video games had opposite effects. The authors concluded that these effects were reliable across experimental, correlational, and longitudinal designs, suggesting both short- and long-term causal impacts.

papers.title: Hilgard et al. 2016
papers.apa: Hilgard, J., Engelhardt, C. R., & Rouder, J. N. (2017). Overstated evidence for short-term effects of violent games on affect and behavior: A reanalysis of Anderson et al. (2010). Psychological Bulletin, 143(7), 757–774. https://doi.org/10.1037/bul0000074
papers.summary: This re-analysis of Anderson et al.'s (2010) meta-analysis (e.g., k=39, N=3,328 for aggressive behavior) detected substantial publication bias in experimental research on violent games and aggressive affect/behavior. After adjusting for this bias, the estimated effects on aggressive behavior were very small (r=0.02-0.15), and effects on aggressive affect were greatly reduced. The authors concluded that the evidence for causal effects of violent video games on aggressive outcomes has been overstated.

papers.title: Savage and Yancey 2008
papers.apa: Savage, J., & Yancey, C. (2008). The Effects of Media Violence Exposure On Criminal Aggression. Criminal Justice and Behavior, 35(6), 772–791. https://doi.org/10.1177/0093854808316487
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 26 independent samples found no positive association between media violence exposure and criminal aggression in aggregate and experimental studies, particularly when using original models controlling for trait aggression. While a small, statistically significant effect (r=0.07) was found for boys, the authors cautioned against strong conclusions due to potential biases, and longitudinal findings were considered ambiguous.

papers.title: Strasburger and Wilson 2014
papers.summary: This paper provided a review of the literature on media violence, concluding that there is a consistent link between exposure to violent media and aggression (review outcome r=0.3). The authors emphasized the importance of considering various media types and long-term effects.

papers.title: ‘Children’s use of mutual exclusivity to constrain the meanings of words’
papers.apa: Markman, E. M., & Wachtel, G. F. (1988). Children’s use of mutual exclusivity to constrain the meanings of words. Cognitive Psychology, 20(2), 121–157. https://doi.org/10.1016/0010-0285(88)90017-5
papers.summary: This foundational paper is credited with establishing the mutual exclusivity bias in word learning. It demonstrated that young children, when presented with a known and an unknown object, infer that a novel label refers to the unknown object, thereby constraining the possible meanings of new words.

papers.title: Lewis et al. 2020
papers.apa: Lewis, M., Cristiano, V., Lake, B. M., Kwan, T., & Frank, M. C. (2020). The role of developmental change and linguistic experience in the mutual exclusivity effect. Cognition, 198, 104191. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2020.104191
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized existing research on the mutual exclusivity bias, finding strong evidence for the effect. The authors reported a significant meta-analytic effect size of d=1.27 [0.99, 1.55], indicating robust support for children's use of mutual exclusivity in word learning.

papers.title: ‘Monolingual and bilingual children’s use of the mutual exclusivity constraint’
papers.apa:
DAVIDSON, D., JERGOVIC, D., IMAMI, Z., & THEODOS, V. (1997). Monolingual and bilingual children’s use of the 

mutual exclusivity constraint. Journal of Child Language, 24(1), 3–24. https://doi.org/10.1017/s0305000996002917
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether bilingual children's exposure to multiple languages influences their use of the mutual exclusivity constraint in word learning. The research likely compared monolingual and bilingual children's performance on tasks requiring them to map novel words to novel objects, finding that bilinguals exhibited a different pattern of mutual exclusivity use, potentially a delayed or reduced reliance on this bias. The original effect size was reported as a mean difference in proportion of 0.065.

papers.title: Lewis et al. 2020
papers.apa: Lewis, M., Cristiano, V., Lake, B. M., Kwan, T., & Frank, M. C. (2020). The role of developmental change and linguistic experience in the mutual exclusivity effect. Cognition, 198, 104191. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2020.104191
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings on the mutual exclusivity bias in bilinguals, aiming to provide a comprehensive overview of the effect's robustness and magnitude across various studies. The analysis likely confirmed the presence of the effect, indicating that bilinguals show differences in mutual exclusivity use compared to monolingual peers. A replication effect size of β=0.61 was reported, suggesting a significant relationship consistent with the original hypothesis.

papers.title: Rule learning by seven-month-old infants
papers.apa: Marcus, G. F., Vijayan, S., Bandi Rao, S., & Vishton, P. M. (1999). Rule Learning by Seven-Month-Old Infants. Science, 283(5398), 77–80. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.283.5398.77
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether 7-month-old infants could learn abstract algebraic rules. Across three experiments using an artificial language task, infants showed longer attention to novel sentences with unfamiliar structures compared to familiar ones, with an original effect size of η2p=0.647. The authors concluded that infants can represent, extract, and generalize abstract algebraic rules, ruling out explanations based on counting or transitional probabilities.

papers.title: Rabagliati et al. 2018
papers.apa: Rabagliati, H., Ferguson, B., & Lew‐Williams, C. (2018). The profile of abstract rule learning in infancy: Meta‐analytic and experimental evidence. Developmental Science, 22(1). Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/desc.12704
papers.summary: This paper presented a meta-analysis of 20 prior reports (1,318 infants across 63 experiments) on infants' learning of abstract repetition rules, alongside a new experiment. The meta-analysis found that infants were most likely to learn abstract patterns from meaningful stimuli, with a meta-analytic effect size of g=0.25 [0.09, 0.40]. The authors propose that the ability to detect and generalize simple patterns supports learning across domains, especially when the signal is meaningfully relevant.

papers.effect_name: Theory of mind in below-4 year olds
papers.title: ‘Beliefs about beliefs: Representation and constraining function of wrong beliefs in young children's understanding of deception’
papers.apa: Wimmer, H. (1983). Beliefs about beliefs: Representation and constraining function of wrong beliefs in young children’s understanding of deception. Cognition, 13(1), 103–128. https://doi.org/10.1016/0010-0277(83)90004-5
papers.summary: This foundational study introduced the false-belief task, a key paradigm for assessing Theory of Mind. It demonstrated that most children under four years of age struggle to correctly predict an agent's actions based on their false belief, suggesting a developmental milestone in understanding others' mental states.

papers.effect_name: Theory of mind in below-4 year olds
papers.title: Barone et al. 2019
papers.apa: Barone, P., Corradi, G., & Gomila, A. (2019). Infants’ performance in spontaneous-response false belief tasks: A review and meta-analysis. Infant Behavior and Development, 57, 101350. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.infbeh.2019.101350
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings on early theory of mind abilities, reporting a significant positive effect (d=0.57 [0.33, 0.89]) for children under four years of age on false-belief tasks. This finding suggests that children may demonstrate theory of mind competence earlier than traditionally assumed, challenging the strict age-four developmental milestone.

papers.title: ‘Beliefs about beliefs: Representation and constraining function of wrong beliefs in young children's understanding of deception’
papers.apa: Wimmer, H. (1983). Beliefs about beliefs: Representation and constraining function of wrong beliefs in young children’s understanding of deception. Cognition, 13(1), 103–128. https://doi.org/10.1016/0010-0277(83)90004-5
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the classic false-belief task (e.g., the Maxi task) to empirically investigate children's understanding of others' mental states. It demonstrated that most 4-year-olds could correctly attribute a false belief to a protagonist, while 3-year-olds typically failed, marking a significant developmental shift in theory of mind.

papers.title: Barone et al. 2019
papers.apa: Barone, P., Corradi, G., & Gomila, A. (2019). Infants’ performance in spontaneous-response false belief tasks: A review and meta-analysis. Infant Behavior and Development, 57, 101350. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.infbeh.2019.101350
papers.summary: This paper likely contributed to the understanding of the developmental trajectory of theory of mind, possibly through a meta-analysis or a large-scale study. The reported effect size (β=0.08 per month) suggests a continuous increase in false-belief understanding with age.

papers.title: Wellman et al. 2001
papers.apa: Wellman, H. M., Cross, D., & Watson, J. (2001). Meta-Analysis of Theory-of-Mind Development: The Truth about False Belief. Child Development, 72(3), 655–684. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-8624.00304
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 178 studies investigated inconsistencies in false-belief task findings, identifying age, country, and four task factors as significant predictors of performance (R² = 0.55). It confirmed a consistent developmental pattern where preschoolers transition from below-chance to above-chance performance, supporting genuine conceptual change in theory of mind during these years. The study's findings contradict "early competence" proposals.

papers.title: ‘Social evaluation by preverbal infants’
papers.apa: Hamlin, J. K., Wynn, K., & Bloom, P. (2007). Social evaluation by preverbal infants. Nature, 450(7169), 557–559. https://doi.org/10.1038/nature06288

papers.title: Margoni and Surian 2018

papers.title: Schlingloff et al. 2020

papers.summary: This study conducted a direct replication of Hamlin et al. (2007) with 14- to 16-month-old infants (N=32), benefiting from detailed procedural advice from the original authors. In contrast to the original findings, only 50% of infants reached for the helper, resulting in a log OR of 0.00, indicating a failure to replicate the preference for prosocial agents.

papers.title: The Mean IQ of Americans: Massive Gains 1932 to 1978

papers.summary: This foundational work by James Flynn first documented the significant rise in average IQ scores in the United States between 1932 and 1978, observing gains of approximately 3 IQ points per decade. This discovery, later termed the "Flynn effect," highlighted a pervasive generational increase in cognitive abilities as measured by standardized tests.

papers.title: Fletcher et al. 2010
papers.apa: Fletcher, J. M., Stuebing, K. K., & Hughes, L. C. (2010). IQ Scores Should Be Corrected for the Flynn Effect in High-Stakes Decisions. Journal of Psychoeducational Assessment, 28(5), 469–473. https://doi.org/10.1177/0734282910373341
papers.summary: This meta-analysis reviewed 14 studies from Flynn (2009) and calculated a weighted mean IQ gain of 2.80 points per decade (95% CI: 2.50, 3.09), confirming the magnitude of the Flynn effect. The authors argued for correcting IQ scores in high-stakes decisions to account for these generational gains, noting that the precision of the Flynn estimate supports an adjustment of about 3 points per decade.

papers.title: Trashon et al. 2014
papers.apa: Trahan, L. H., Stuebing, K. K., Fletcher, J. M., & Hiscock, M. (2014). The Flynn effect: A meta-analysis. Psychological Bulletin, 140(5), 1332–1360. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0037173

papers.effect_name: 2D:4D ratio of the fingers and its correlation with psychological traits

papers.summary: This study investigated the link between digit ratio (a proxy for prenatal testosterone) and consumer preferences for gender-imaged products. Across 588 consumers, a more masculine digit ratio predicted choices of masculine-imaged products and clothing colors for male consumers (β = -0.18 for beverages, β = -0.05 for clothing colors), but not for females. The authors replicated previous findings on prenatal testosterone affecting product preferences in males, but not females.

papers.effect_name: 2D:4D ratio of the fingers and its correlation with psychological traits
papers.title: Csathó, et al. 2003
papers.summary: This paper explored the relationship between digit ratio and sex-role orientation, specifically using the Bem Sex Role Inventory. While some associations were reported (e.g., Right hand b=0.29, Left hand b=0.26 (n.s.), Mean ratio b=0.31), subsequent meta-analyses and replication attempts have largely failed to confirm these findings, suggesting they may not be robust.

papers.effect_name: 2D:4D ratio of the fingers and its correlation with psychological traits
papers.title: Ferdenzi et al. 2011
papers.apa: Ferdenzi, C., Lemaître, J.-F., Leongómez, J. D., & Roberts, S. C. (2011). Digit ratio (2D:4D) predicts facial, but not voice or body odour, attractiveness in men. Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences, 278(1724), 3551–3557. https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2011.0544

Lower (more masculine) right 2D:4D and right-minus-left 2D:4D were associated with more attractive faces (r2 = [-0.25, -0.43]), but not with voice or body odor masculinity or attractiveness. papers.summary: This paper is listed as a critique of the 2D:4D effect, but no specific findings or details are provided in the extracted text or effect size information. Without further information, its specific contribution to the replication status of the effect cannot be determined.

A significant positive correlation was found between digit ratios and Celebrity Attitude Scale (CAS) scores for females (r = 0.51, p < 0.001), indicating that females with lower digit ratios were less likely to worship celebrities. No significant correlation was found for males (r = -0.13, p = .394).

papers.summary: This study investigated the association between digit ratio (2D:4D) as a proxy for prenatal androgenization and endurance running ability in 439 male and 103 female half-marathon runners. A significant positive correlation was found between digit ratio and half-marathon time in both males (right hand: r = 0.45, p<0.001; left hand: r = 0.42, p<0.001) and females (right hand: r = 0.26, p<0.01; left hand: r = 0.23, p = 0.02). This suggests that lower (more masculine) digit ratios are associated with faster running times, potentially signaling reproductive potential.

papers.effect_name: 2D:4D ratio of the fingers and its correlation with psychological traits
papers.title: Manning et al. 2021
papers.apa: Manning, J. T., Fink, B., Mason, L., & Trivers, R. (2021). Parental income inequality and children’s digit ratio (2D:4D): a ‘Trivers-Willard’ effect on prenatal androgenization? Journal of Biosocial Science, 54(1), 154–162. https://doi.org/10.1017/s0021932021000043
papers.summary: This large online survey (BBC Internet Study) examined the association between parental income inequality and children's digit ratio (2D:4D). Children of above-average income parents had lower 2D:4D (higher prenatal testosterone), while children of below-average income parents had higher 2D:4D. These significant effects were observed in the total sample (N > 100,000 for males and females), particularly among Whites and in the UK/USA, and were stronger for males than females.

papers.summary: This study, involving 176 adults, humorously explored whether 2D:4D ratio correlates with "good luck" (operationalized as poker hand rank) to highlight issues in biomarker research. While they found a significant correlation between men's right hand 2D:4D (measured by radiograph) and poker hand rank (r_s = 0.28, P=0.004), the authors explicitly state this is due to chance and serves as an example of the reproducibility crisis. They advocate for stronger methodology and transparent reporting.

papers.effect_name: 2D:4D ratio of the fingers and its correlation with psychological traits
papers.title: Voracek et al. 2011
papers.apa: Voracek, M., Pietschnig, J., Nader, I. W., & Stieger, S. (2011). Digit ratio (2D:4D) and sex-role orientation: Further evidence and meta-analysis. Personality and Individual Differences, 51(4), 417–422. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2010.06.009
papers.summary: This paper presents three new studies (N = 132, 623, 1112) that failed to find hypothesized associations between 2D:4D and sex-role orientation. A meta-analysis of 28 studies (N = 6311) found only a tiny (0.14% variance explained) and potentially non-robust positive correlation between men's left-hand 2D:4D and femininity. The authors conclude that cumulative evidence does not support systematic, robust, or noteworthy within-sex correlations between 2D:4D and masculinity/femininity personality dimensions, highlighting issues of underpowered designs and publication bias.

papers.effect_name: Personality traits and consequential life outcomes
papers.title: Personality and the prediction of consequential outcomes
papers.apa: Ozer, D. J., & Benet-Martínez, V. (2006). Personality and the Prediction of Consequential Outcomes. Annual Review of Psychology, 57(1), 401–421. https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev.psych.57.102904.190127
papers.summary: This review article synthesizes research demonstrating that personality traits, particularly the Big Five, are consistently linked to a wide array of important life outcomes. It identifies associations across individual (e.g., happiness, health), interpersonal (e.g., relationship quality), and social-institutional levels (e.g., occupational success, community involvement), highlighting the pervasive influence of personality.

papers.effect_name: Personality traits and consequential life outcomes
papers.title: Roberts et al. 2007
papers.apa: Roberts, B. W., Kuncel, N. R., Shiner, R., Caspi, A., & Goldberg, L. R. (2007).
