Perspectives on Psychological Science, 2(4), 313–345. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1745-6916.2007.00047.x
papers.summary: This study compared the predictive validity of personality traits against socioeconomic status (SES) and cognitive ability for outcomes like mortality, divorce, and occupational attainment, using prospective longitudinal data. The authors found that the magnitude of personality's effects was comparable to that of SES and cognitive ability, underscoring the significant influence of personality on important life outcomes.

papers.effect_name: Personality traits and consequential life outcomes
papers.title: Soto 2019
papers.apa: Soto, C. J. (2019). How Replicable Are Links Between Personality Traits and Consequential Life Outcomes? The Life Outcomes of Personality Replication Project. Psychological Science, 30(5), 711–727. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797619831612
papers.summary: The Life Outcomes of Personality Replication (LOOPR) Project conducted preregistered, high-powered (median N = 1,504) replications of 78 previously published personality-outcome associations. While 87% of the replications were statistically significant in the expected direction, the replication effects were, on average, 77% as strong as the original effects, indicating a significant decline in effect size. The study concluded that the literature is reasonably accurate but benefits from improved replicability efforts.

papers.title: Creative novation behaviour therapy as a prophylactic treatment for cancer and coronary heart disease: Part I—Description of treatment, Behaviour Research and Therapy’, Grossarth-Maticek and Eysenck 1991 (RETRACTED
papers.summary: This paper describes the 'Creative Novation Behavior Therapy' (CNBT), a specific behavioral intervention proposed as a prophylactic treatment for cancer and coronary heart disease. It details the therapeutic approach, which aimed to modify personality traits and coping mechanisms believed to be linked to disease susceptibility.

papers.title: Creative novation behaviour therapy as a prophylactic treatment for cancer and coronary heart disease: Part II—Effects of treatment, Behaviour Research and Therapy’, Eysenck and Grossarth-Maticek 1991 (RETRACTED
papers.summary: This paper presented the purported effects of 'Creative Novation Behavior Therapy' (CNBT) on the prevention and treatment of cancer and coronary heart disease. It reported astonishingly large effect sizes, claiming significant reductions in mortality rates for those receiving the therapy, such as 106 out of 600 deaths in the control group versus 27 out of 500 in the therapy group.

papers.title: Pelosi 2019
papers.apa: Pelosi, A. J. (2019). Personality and fatal diseases: Revisiting a scientific scandal. Journal of Health Psychology, 24(4), 421–439. https://doi.org/10.1177/1359105318822045
papers.summary: This article critically reviews the research program by Eysenck and Grossarth-Maticek on personality and fatal diseases, highlighting it as a major scientific scandal. It outlines the astonishingly large and unprecedented effect sizes reported, points to serious scientific and ethical criticisms published decades prior, and uses newly available confidential documents to provide further insights. The author calls for a formal inquiry into this research.

papers.title: ‘Graphological analysis and psychiatry: an experimental study’
papers.apa: EYSENCK, H. J. (1945). GRAPHOLOGICAL ANALYSIS AND PSYCHIATRY: AN EXPERIMENTAL STUDY. British Journal of Psychology. General Section, 35(3), 70–81. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2044-8295.1945.tb01093.x
papers.summary: This paper is identified as a foundational study in graphology, likely presenting early experimental evidence or theoretical underpinnings for the practice of inferring personality traits from handwriting. Without the abstract or full text, specific findings, sample sizes, or methodologies cannot be detailed.

papers.title: Dazzi & Pedrabissi 2009
papers.apa: Dazzi, C., & Pedrabissi, L. (2009). Graphology and Personality: An Empirical Study on Validity of Handwriting Analysis. Psychological Reports, 105(3_suppl), 1255–1268. https://doi.org/10.2466/pr0.105.f.1255-1268
papers.summary: This study investigated the validity of graphology to assess personality traits across two experiments. Study 1 (N=101) found no significant correlations between Big Five Questionnaire scores and graphologists' ratings of handwriting, with mean Spearman's rho values of .04 and .07. Study 2 (N=102) similarly found no evidence to validate graphology as a measure of personality, even when using autobiographical texts and comparing graphologists to laypersons, with mean Spearman's rho values of .05 and .03.

papers.title: Furnham and Gunter 1987
papers.apa: Furnham, A., & Gunter, B. (1987). Graphology and personality: Another failure to validate graphological analysis. Personality and Individual Differences, 8(3), 433–435. https://doi.org/10.1016/0191-8869(87)90045-6
papers.summary: This paper is classified as a critique or replication attempt of graphology. Without the abstract or full text, specific findings, sample sizes, or methodologies cannot be detailed, but it likely aimed to test the claims of graphological analysis regarding personality assessment.

papers.title: Do defaults save lives?
papers.apa: Johnson, E. J., & Goldstein, D. (2003). Do Defaults Save Lives? Science, 302(5649), 1338–1339. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1091721
papers.summary: This foundational paper investigated the default effect on organ donation rates through an online experiment (N=161), analysis of European consent laws, and a regression on actual donation data (1991-2001). It found that opt-out defaults significantly increased donation rates, with an online experiment showing a doubling of rates and real-world data indicating a 16.3% increase, challenging the view that defaults are inconsequential. The authors proposed that defaults influence choices by implying recommendations, reducing effort, and leveraging loss aversion.

papers.title: DellaVigna & Linos 2022
papers.apa: DellaVigna, S., & Linos, E. (2022). RCTs to Scale: Comprehensive Evidence From Two Nudge Units. Econometrica, 90(1), 81–116. https://doi.org/10.3982/ecta18709
papers.summary: This meta-analysis compared the effectiveness of nudge interventions reported in academic journals versus those implemented by Nudge Units (126 RCTs, N=23 million). It found that academic studies reported significantly larger effects (8.7 percentage point take-up, 33.4% increase) than Nudge Unit trials (1.4 percentage points, 8.0% increase). The authors concluded that selective publication, exacerbated by low statistical power in academic studies, accounted for about 70% of this discrepancy, with different nudge characteristics explaining the remainder.

papers.title: Chandrasehkar et al. 2022
papers.apa: Chandrashekar, S. P., Adelina, N., Zeng, S., Esther, C. Y. Y., Leung, G. Y. S., Henne, P., Cheng, B., & Feldman, G. (2022). Defaults versus framing: Revisiting Default Effect and Framing Effect with replications and extensions of Johnson and Goldstein (2003) and Johnson, Bellman, and Lohse (2002). https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/nymh2
papers.summary: This paper, without provided abstract, appears to be a replication or re-analysis of the default effect. The provided replication effect sizes (OR=1.38 to OR=1.67) suggest a smaller but still positive effect compared to the original findings.

papers.title: Adding asymmetrically dominated alternatives: violations of regularity and the similarity hypothesis
papers.apa: Huber, J., Payne, J. W., & Puto, C. (1982). Adding Asymmetrically Dominated Alternatives: Violations of Regularity and the Similarity Hypothesis. Journal of Consumer Research, 9(1), 90. https://doi.org/10.1086/208899
papers.summary: This foundational study introduced the decoy effect, demonstrating that adding an asymmetrically dominated alternative (decoy) to a choice set can increase the choice probability of the option that dominates it (target). Across within-subjects (N=93) and between-subjects designs (N=153 total), they found the target's choice share increased, averaging a 9.2% gain in the between-subjects analysis (p ≤ 0.05). The authors concluded this violates regularity in choice models and has implications for product line strategies.

papers.title: Heath and Chatterjee 1995
papers.apa: Heath, T. B., & Chatterjee, S. (1995). Asymmetric Decoy Effects on Lower-Quality Versus Higher-Quality Brands: Meta-Analytic and Experimental Evidence. Journal of Consumer Research, 22(3), 268. https://doi.org/10.1086/209449
papers.summary: This meta-analysis (92 control/experimental conditions) and experimental study investigated the asymmetric decoy effect, revealing that decoys reduce shares of lower-quality competitors more than higher-quality competitors. It found that regularity was violated only when higher-quality brands were targeted, with decoys increasing their shares but typically not those of lower-quality brands. The authors also conducted an experiment that replicated the meta-analytic pattern in a traditional population but reversed it in a non-traditional one, suggesting generalizability limits.

papers.title: Hu and Yu 2014
papers.summary: This fMRI study (N=16) investigated the neural mechanisms of the decoy effect using a gambling task. Behaviorally, participants showed a significant decoy effect, preferring target options over competitors (57.3% vs. 40.8%, t(15) = 4.316, p < 0.001). Neuroimaging revealed that choosing targets engaged greater activity in the left anterior insula, suggesting perceptual salience drives heuristic decision-making, and ACC activity predicted reduced susceptibility to the effect.

papers.title: Slaughter et al. 1999
papers.apa: Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., Wilson, S., & Wilson, S. (2010). papers.title: Nudge: Improving Decisions about Health, Wealth, and
papers.apa: Rooze, S. (2009). Review: Nudge: Improving Decisions about Health, Wealth and Happiness. Amsterdam Law Forum, 1(4), 89. https://doi.org/10.37974/alf.89
papers.summary: This seminal book introduced the concept of "nudges" as a form of choice architecture, proposing that subtle changes in the environment can predictably alter people's behavior without forbidding any options or significantly changing their economic incentives. It laid the theoretical groundwork for applying behavioral economics to public policy.

papers.title: Mertens et al. (2021)
papers.apa: Correction for Mertens et al., The effectiveness of nudging: A meta-analysis of choice architecture interventions across behavioral domains. (2022). Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 119(19). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2204059119
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings from numerous studies on nudging, reporting an overall effect size ranging from d=0.37 to d=0.46, suggesting that nudges are generally effective across various behavioral domains. A correction was later published, but the core finding of effectiveness remained.

papers.title: Maier et al. (2022)
papers.apa: Maier, M., Bartoš, F., Stanley, T. D., Shanks, D. R., Harris, A. J. L., & Wagenmakers, E.-J. (2022). No evidence for nudging after adjusting for publication bias. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 119(31). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2200300119
papers.summary: This meta-analysis re-evaluated the effectiveness of nudges, specifically addressing publication bias. After adjusting for such biases, the authors found a substantially reduced effect size, ranging from d=0.00 to d=0.14, leading them to conclude there is little to no evidence for nudging's effectiveness.

papers.title: The framing of decisions and the psychology of choice
papers.apa: Tversky, A., & Kahneman, D. (1985). The Framing of Decisions and the Psychology of Choice. Behavioral Decision Making, 25–41. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4613-2391-4_2
papers.summary: This foundational chapter, building on earlier work, elaborates on how the framing of decision problems, particularly in terms of gains or losses, systematically influences choices, often leading to deviations from rational economic models. It introduces the concept of framing as a key psychological principle affecting decision-making under risk and uncertainty.

papers.title: Kühberger, 1998
papers.apa: Kühberger, A. (1998). The Influence of Framing on Risky Decisions: A Meta-analysis. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 75(1), 23–55. https://doi.org/10.1006/obhd.1998.2781
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized 136 empirical papers with nearly 30,000 participants, calculating 230 effect sizes for risky choice framing. It found an overall framing effect of small to moderate size (d = 0.308), concluding that framing is a reliable phenomenon. The study also highlighted that the effect size varies significantly based on methodological features, particularly distinguishing between reference point and outcome salience manipulations.

papers.title: Rothman et al. (1999)
papers.apa: Rothman, A. J., Martino, S. C., Bedell, B. T., Detweiler, J. B., & Salovey, P. (1999). The Systematic Influence of Gain-and Loss-Framed Messages on Interest in and Use of Different Types of Health Behavior. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 25(11), 1355–1369. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167299259003
papers.summary: This foundational paper presented two experiments to directly test the hypothesis that message framing interacts with perceived risk of health behaviors. Experiment 1 found that willingness to act on a new disease was influenced by message frame and behavior type. Experiment 2 replicated these findings with gum disease, showing gain frames were more effective for a plaque-fighting rinse (prevention) and loss frames for a plaque-detecting rinse (detection).

papers.title: van Riet et al. (2016)
papers.apa: Van ’t Riet, J., Cox, A. D., Cox, D., Zimet, G. D., De Bruijn, G.-J., Van den Putte, B., De Vries, H., Werrij, M. Q., & Ruiter, R. A. C. (2016). Does perceived risk influence the effects of message framing? Revisiting the link between prospect theory and message framing. Health Psychology Review, 10(4), 447–459. https://doi.org/10.1080/17437199.2016.1176865
papers.summary: This review article critically re-examined the 'risk-framing hypothesis,' which posits that perceived risk influences the effectiveness of gain- vs. loss-framed health messages. The authors concluded that the hypothesis suffers from severe theoretical flaws and that the empirical evidence supporting it is weak and inconsistent. They argue that key aspects of prospect theory were lost in translation when applied to health promotion, and call for new methodology in message-framing research.

papers.title: Status quo bias in decision making
papers.apa: Samuelson, W., & Zeckhauser, R. (1988). Status quo bias in decision making. Journal of Risk and Uncertainty, 1(1), 7–59. https://doi.org/10.1007/bf00055564
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of status quo bias, demonstrating through a series of decision-making experiments that individuals disproportionately prefer to stick with existing options. The experiments showed that response rates for an alternative were consistently highest when it was presented as the status quo, lower in neutral conditions, and lowest when it was an alternative to the status quo. Real-world data on health plans and retirement programs further supported the substantial presence of this bias.

papers.title: Bostrom and Ord 2006
papers.apa: Bostrom, N., & Ord, T. (2006). The Reversal Test: Eliminating Status Quo Bias in Applied Ethics. Ethics, 116(4), 656–679. https://doi.org/10.1086/505233
papers.summary: This paper proposes the "Reversal Test" and "Double Reversal Test" as philosophical heuristics to diagnose and mitigate status quo bias in applied ethical judgments, particularly concerning human cognitive enhancement. By applying these tests, the authors argue that many common objections to enhancement are rooted in an irrational preference for the current state. They conclude that once status quo bias is accounted for, the case for developing and using genetic cognitive enhancements becomes much stronger.

papers.title: Godefroid et al. 2022
papers.apa: Godefroid, M.-E., Plattfaut, R., & Niehaves, B. (2022). How to measure the status quo bias? A review of current literature. Management Review Quarterly, 73(4), 1667–1711. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11301-022-00283-8
papers.summary: This literature review synthesizes research on the Status Quo Bias (SQB) across various scholarly fields, focusing on its measurement and countermeasures. The authors identify four distinct measurement approaches and 13 countermeasures, categorizing them by cognitive misperception, rational decision-making, and psychological commitment aspects of SQB. The paper aims to consolidate existing knowledge to help researchers combat this bias, particularly in contexts like the adoption of digital innovations.

papers.title: Johnson and Goldstein 2003
papers.apa: Johnson, E. J., & Goldstein, D. (2003). Do Defaults Save Lives? Science, 302(5649), 1338–1339. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1091721
papers.summary: This study investigated the significant impact of default options on organ donation rates, proposing that defaults influence choices through implied recommendations, reduced effort, and loss aversion. An online experiment and analysis of European consent rates showed that opt-out defaults led to significantly higher donation rates (e.g., nearly 60 percentage points difference in Europe) compared to opt-in. A regression analysis further indicated that defaults could increase actual cadaveric donation rates by 16.3%, demonstrating the powerful real-world effect of status quo bias.

papers.title: Xiao et al. 2021
papers.apa: Xiao, Q., Lam, C. S., Piara, M., & Feldman, G. (2021). Revisiting status quo bias. Meta-Psychology, 5. https://doi.org/10.15626/mp.2020.2470
papers.summary: This pre-registered replication study (N1=311, N2=316) revisited four decision scenarios from Samuelson and Zeckhauser (1988) to test the status quo bias. Strong empirical support for the bias was found in three out of four scenarios, including budget allocation, investment portfolios, and college jobs, with Cohen's h ranging from 0.45 to 0.62. However, the replication failed to find substantial support for the bias in the wagon color choice scenario, suggesting some boundary conditions or context dependency.

papers.title: The temporal pattern to the experience of regret
papers.apa: Gilovich, T., & Medvec, V. H. (1994). The temporal pattern to the experience of regret. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 67(3), 357–365. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.67.3.357
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed the temporal action-inaction effect, suggesting that people experience more regret for actions in the short term, but more regret for inactions in the long term. The authors posited that actions are more salient and mutable immediately, while inactions become more prominent as missed opportunities over time.

papers.title: Bonnefon and Zhang 2008
papers.apa: Bonnefon, J., & Zhang, J. (2007). The intensity of recent and distant life regrets: an integrated model and a large scale survey. Applied Cognitive Psychology, 22(5), 653–662. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/acp.1386
papers.summary: This study surveyed 957 French adults to investigate predictors of regret intensity, including action/inaction, consequence severity, and justification. It found that recent regret intensity was predicted by consequences (especially for actions), while distant regret intensity was predicted by consequences and justification (stronger for actions). The findings suggest a more nuanced temporal pattern of regret predictors.

papers.title: Feldman et al. 1999
papers.apa: Feldman, J., Miyamoto, J., & Loftus, E. F. (1999). Are Actions Regretted More Than Inactions? Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 78(3), 232–255. https://doi.org/10.1006/obhd.1999.2833
papers.summary: This paper, cited by later research, found that while inaction regrets were more frequently reported, they were not necessarily more intense than action regrets. This challenged the temporal theory's predictions regarding regret intensity over time.

papers.title: Towers et al. 2016
papers.summary: This postal survey of 500 New Zealand adults investigated multiple predictors of life regret intensity, including action/inaction. While inaction regrets were more frequent, the study found that actions were associated with slightly greater regret intensity, and no evidence supported the hypothesis that regret intensity for inactions increases over time while actions decrease. The authors concluded that their findings do not support the temporal theory of regret regarding intensity.

papers.title: Yeung and Feldman 2022
papers.apa: (1999). C. Narayanswamy v et al C.K. Jaffar Shariff et al. https://doi.org/10.14217/9781848596641-3-en
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Zeelenberg et al. 1998
papers.apa: Ordóñez, L. D., & Connolly, T. (2000). Regret and Responsibility: A Reply to Zeelenberg et al. (1998). Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 81(1), 132–142. https://doi.org/10.1006/obhd.1999.2834
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: ‘Effort for Payment: A Tale of Two Markets’
papers.apa: Heyman, J., & Ariely, D. (2004). Effort for Payment. Psychological Science, 15(11), 787–793. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.0956-7976.2004.00757.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that effort for payment operates under distinct monetary and social markets. Three experiments supported the hypothesis that monetary markets are highly sensitive to compensation magnitude, while social markets are not. The authors observed that people sometimes expend more effort for no payment than for low payment, and that mixed markets resemble monetary markets. Original effect sizes were reported as d = -0.59 for the money market and d = 0.25 for the social market.

papers.title: Imada et al. 2022
papers.apa: Yeung, S. K., & Feldman, G. (2022). Revisiting the Temporal Pattern of Regret in Action Versus Inaction: Replication of Gilovich and Medvec (1994) With Extensions Examining Responsibility. Collabra: Psychology, 8(1). https://doi.org/10.1525/collabra.37122
papers.summary: This replication study largely supported the original claim that sensitivity to compensation differs between money and social markets. For the money market, they replicated the finding that people expect more willingness to help with medium cash vs. small cash (Study 1: d = -1.25; Study 2: d = -1.30). However, contrary to the original, they found that people expect more willingness to help with medium goods vs. small goods in the social market (Study 1: d = -0.43; Study 2: d = -0.87), suggesting some sensitivity to compensation in this market as well, albeit with smaller effect sizes than the money market.

papers.title: The affect heuristic in judgments of risks and benefits
papers.apa: Finucane, M. L., Alhakami, A., Slovic, P., & Johnson, S. M. (2000). The affect heuristic in judgments of risks and benefits. Journal of Behavioral Decision Making, 13(1), 1–17. https://doi.org/10.1002/(sici)1099-0771(200001/03)13:1<1::aid-bdm333>3.0.co;2-s
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the affect heuristic, proposing that people's emotional reactions to stimuli guide their judgments of risks and benefits. It demonstrated an inverse relationship where increased perceived risks lead to lower perceived benefits, and vice versa, suggesting that affect serves as a mental shortcut in decision-making.

papers.title: Efendić et al. 2021
papers.apa: Efendić, E., Chandrashekar, S. P., Lee, C. S., Yeung, L. Y., Kim, M. J., Lee, C. Y., & Feldman, G. (2021). Risky Therefore Not Beneficial: Replication and Extension of Finucane et al.’s (2000) Affect Heuristic Experiment. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 13(7), 1173–1184. https://doi.org/10.1177/19485506211056761
papers.summary: This study replicated and extended Finucane et al.'s (2000) original finding on the affect heuristic in two well-powered studies. They observed a strong negative relationship between judgments of risks and benefits across three technologies (Study 1: r = -0.87; Study 2: r = -0.84), consistent with the original effect (r = -0.74). The authors also found that risk perceptions were more responsive to manipulation than benefit perceptions, and that incidental mood could moderate this relationship.

papers.title: A Wrinkle in Time: Asymmetric Valuation of Past and Future Events
papers.apa: Caruso, E. M., Gilbert, D. T., & Wilson, T. D. (2008). A Wrinkle in Time. Psychological Science, 19(8), 796–801. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02159.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the Temporal Value Asymmetry (TVA) effect, proposing that people experience stronger emotions when contemplating future events compared to equally distant past events. Across multiple studies, the authors demonstrated that future events evoke more intense affective responses than past events, particularly in between-subjects designs.

papers.title: Caruso 2010
papers.apa: Kawasaki, T., Ichikawa, J., Imada, H., Kanno, S., & Onohara, K. (2024). Comment on Manole et al. Primary Pericardial Synovial Sarcoma: A Case Report and Literature Review. Diagnostics 2022, 12, 158. Diagnostics, 14(10), 1012. https://doi.org/10.3390/diagnostics14101012
papers.summary: This paper, likely a response or further elaboration by Caruso, aimed to reinforce the original TVA findings or clarify the mechanisms underlying the asymmetry. It may have addressed criticisms or explored boundary conditions, potentially discussing the role of construal level or other cognitive factors.

papers.title: Kvam et al. 2022
papers.apa: Caruso, E. M., & Gilbert, D. T. (2007). The temporal value asymmetry [dataset]. In PsycEXTRA Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/e633982013-126
papers.summary: This paper likely presents a direct replication attempt and meta-analysis of the Temporal Value Asymmetry effect. It reportedly found a failure to replicate the original TVA effect across several studies, concluding that the evidence for TVA is weak or non-existent and challenging the robustness of the original finding.

papers.title: Norm Theory: Comparing Reality to Its Alternatives
papers.apa: Kahneman, D., & Miller, D. T. (1986). Norm theory: Comparing reality to its alternatives. Psychological Review, 93(2), 136–153. https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-295x.93.2.136
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced Norm Theory, proposing that people evaluate events by comparing them to easily imagined alternatives or 'norms.' It posits that events resulting from exceptional or abnormal circumstances are more likely to evoke counterfactual thoughts and stronger emotional responses (e.g., regret) than events resulting from routine circumstances, thereby laying the theoretical groundwork for the exceptionality effect.

papers.title: Fillon et al. 2020
papers.apa: Fillon, A., Kutscher, L., & Feldman, G. (2020). Impact of past behaviour normality: meta-analysis of exceptionality effect. Cognition and Emotion, 35(1), 129–149. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699931.2020.1816910
papers.summary: This pre-registered meta-analysis of 48 studies (N=4212) found strong support for the exceptionality effect. For 35 experimental studies (n=3332), a medium-to-strong effect was observed (g=0.60), while 13 one-sample comparison studies (n=1217) showed a very strong effect (g=1.98), though the authors note these designs may inflate effects. The study also identified moderators, such as the effect being stronger when routine aligned with status quo or action.

papers.title: Kutscher and Feldman 2019
papers.apa: Kutscher, L., & Feldman, G. (2018). The impact of past behaviour normality on regret: replication and extension of three experiments of the exceptionality effect. Cognition and Emotion, 33(5), 901–914. https://doi.org/10.1080/02699931.2018.1504747
papers.summary: This study conducted three direct replications of classic exceptionality effect experiments from Kahneman and Miller (1986) and Miller and McFarland (1986) using MTurk participants (N=342 for each of two experiments). It successfully replicated the hitchhiker and car accident scenarios, finding large effects, but failed to replicate the robbery scenario using the original compensation measure. However, the robbery scenario showed a medium effect when regret was measured, suggesting the effect's magnitude is sensitive to the dependent measure and study design (comparison vs. between-subjects).

papers.title: Temporal differences in trait self-ascription : When the self is seen as an other
papers.apa: Pronin, E., & Ross, L. (2006). Temporal differences in trait self-ascription: When the self is seen as an other. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 90(2), 197–209. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.90.2.197
papers.summary: This foundational paper established the effect of temporal differences in trait self-ascription. It reported that people attribute more dispositional traits to others compared to themselves (f=0.35), more positive traits in self-assessments (f=0.77), and that this self-enhancement ratio does not increase with temporal distance (f=0.16). Crucially, it found that people ascribe more dispositional traits to their temporally distant self compared to their present self (f=0.54).

papers.title: Adelina and Feldman 2021
papers.summary: This replication study investigated the temporal differences in trait self-ascription. It found a weaker social distance effect (f=0.10) and replicated the self-enhancement effect (f=0.88). However, it reversed the finding regarding self-enhancement over temporal distance, showing the ratio *increases* (f=0.33), and did not replicate the core hypothesis that people ascribe more dispositional traits to their temporally distant self (f=0.02).

papers.title: Perceptions of bias in self versus others
papers.apa: Pronin, E., Lin, D. Y., & Ross, L. (2002). The Bias Blind Spot: Perceptions of Bias in Self Versus Others. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 28(3), 369–381. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167202286008
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of the Bias Blind Spot, demonstrating through three studies that individuals perceive themselves as less susceptible to cognitive and motivational biases than others. Participants consistently rated themselves as less biased than various comparison groups, a phenomenon attributed to self-enhancement motives and availability biases. The original findings reported an effect size of d = -0.86 for biases, indicating a strong self-other asymmetry in perceived bias, but failed to find strong support for asymmetries in perceived personal shortcomings (d = 0.28).

papers.title: Chandrashekar et al. 2021
papers.apa: Chandrashekar, S. P., & Feldman, G. (2025). On the process and value of direct close replications: A rejoinder to Shafir and Cheek’s (2024) commentary on Chandrashekar et al. (2021). Judgment and Decision Making, 20. https://doi.org/10.1017/jdm.2025.10011
papers.summary: This replication study investigated self-other asymmetries in perceived biases and personal shortcomings. The authors found strong support for the Bias Blind Spot, reporting an effect size of d = -1.00 for biases, consistent with the original findings. Additionally, they found support for self-other asymmetries in perceived personal shortcomings (d = -0.34), which was not strongly supported in the original work.

papers.title: On the psychology of experimental surprise
papers.apa: Kvam, T.-M., Goksøyr, I. W., Rog, J., Autran, I., Berthold-Losleben, M., & Andreassen, O. A. (2026). MDMA-assisted psychotherapy in major depression: critical considerations for clinical translation: commentary, Kvam et al. The British Journal of Psychiatry, 1–2. https://doi.org/10.1192/bjp.2026.10551
papers.summary: This foundational paper by Baruch Fischhoff introduced and experimentally demonstrated the hindsight bias. Participants, informed of an outcome, consistently overestimated the probability they would have assigned to that outcome before knowing it, illustrating how knowledge of results distorts judgments of prior likelihood.

papers.title: Chen et al. 2021
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2021). Exemplar Abstract for Brachybacterium halotolerans Chen et al. 2021 and Brachybacterium halotolerans halotolerans (Chen et al. 2021) Kim et al. 2022. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.40695
papers.summary: This replication study investigated the hindsight bias, finding evidence for the effect but with a considerably smaller magnitude than previously reported. The study reported effect sizes ranging from d=0.05 to d=0.32, suggesting that while the bias exists, its strength might be less pronounced than initially estimated.

papers.title: The Disjunction Effect in Choice under Uncertainty
papers.apa: Tversky, A., & Shafir, E. (1992). The Disjunction Effect in Choice under Uncertainty. Psychological Science, 3(5), 305–310. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.1992.tb00678.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the Disjunction Effect, demonstrating violations of Savage's sure-thing principle. The authors proposed that uncertainty makes decision-makers reluctant to fully consider the implications of each outcome, leading to different choices than when outcomes are known. They also observed that the sure-thing principle is satisfied when participants are made aware of their preferences given each outcome.

papers.title: Kühberger et al. 2001
papers.apa: Kühberger, A., Komunska, D., & Perner, J. (2001). The Disjunction Effect: Does It Exist for Two-Step Gambles? Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 85(2), 250–264. https://doi.org/10.1006/obhd.2000.2942
papers.summary: This paper reported a failure to replicate the 'choice under risk' problem, a key paradigm for the Disjunction Effect, across four experiments. Specific effect sizes and sample sizes were not provided in the prompt.

papers.title: Lambdin and Burdsal 2007
papers.apa: Lambdin, C., & Burdsal, C. (2007). The disjunction effect reexamined: Relevant methodological issues and the fallacy of unspecified percentage comparisons. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 103(2), 268–276. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.obhdp.2006.04.001
papers.summary: This study reported a failure to replicate the Disjunction Effect. Specific effect sizes and sample sizes were not provided in the prompt.

papers.title: Ziano et al. 2021
papers.apa: Ziano, I., Kong, M. F., Kim, H. J., Liu, C. Y., Wong, S. C., Cheng, B. L., & Feldman, G. (2021). Replication: Revisiting Tversky and Shafir’s (1992) Disjunction Effect with an extension comparing between and within subject designs. Journal of Economic Psychology, 83, 102350. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joep.2020.102350
papers.summary: This paper investigated the replicability of the Disjunction Effect across different paradigms. It failed to replicate the 'choice under risk' problem (Cramer’s V = 0.11 [-0.07, 0.20]) but successfully replicated the 'paying-to-know' problem (Cramer’s V = 0.30 [0.24, 0.37]). The paper also reported original effect sizes for these paradigms.

papers.title: ‘Money Illusion
papers.apa: Maguire, R., Maguire, P., & Keane, M. T. (2011). Making sense of surprise: An investigation of the factors influencing surprise judgments. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 37(1), 176–186. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0021609
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced and demonstrated the phenomenon of money illusion through a series of experiments. Participants consistently failed to distinguish between nominal and real changes in monetary value, making decisions based on nominal values even when real values suggested a different course of action. The authors proposed that this cognitive bias arises from a tendency to anchor on nominal values and a difficulty in fully adjusting for inflation or deflation.

papers.title: Ziano et al. 2021
papers.apa: Howitt, P. (1989). Money Illusion. Money, 244–247. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-349-19804-7_29
papers.summary: This study aimed to replicate the money illusion effect across several problems. The findings largely confirmed the presence of money illusion, with effect sizes for Problem 1 (V=0.28), Problem 2 (70%), and Problem 3 (buy: 47%, sell: 43%) being comparable to or even larger than the original findings. While Problem 4 showed a slightly smaller effect (V=0.17), the overall pattern of results supported the robustness of the money illusion phenomenon.

papers.title: Choosing versus rejecting: Why some options are both better and worse than others
papers.apa: Shafir, E. (1993). Choosing versus rejecting: Why some options are both better and worse than others. Memory & Cognition, 21(4), 546–556. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03197186
papers.summary: This foundational study introduced the "choosing versus rejecting" effect, demonstrating that an "enriched" option (with more positive and negative features) is often both chosen and rejected more frequently than an "impoverished" option. Across multiple experiments with Princeton undergraduates (N=170-398 for between-subject, N=248 for within-subject), the enriched option was consistently chosen and rejected more often (e.g., custody problem: 64% chosen, 55% rejected; sum 119%, z=2.48, p<.02). The findings challenge procedure invariance and are explained by the compatibility principle, where positive features are weighted more in choosing and negative in rejecting.

papers.title: Chandrashekar et al. 2021
papers.apa: Chandrashekar, S. P., & Feldman, G. (2025). On the process and value of direct close replications: A rejoinder to Shafir and Cheek’s (2024) commentary on Chandrashekar et al. (2021). Judgment and Decision Making, 20. https://doi.org/10.1017/jdm.2025.10011
papers.summary: This paper is a replication attempt of the choosing versus rejecting effect. It reported a very small effect size (d=0.01), suggesting a failure to replicate the original finding.

papers.title: Ganzach 1995
papers.apa: Ganzach, Y. (1995). Attribute Scatter and Decision Outcome: Judgment versus Choice. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 62(1), 113–122. https://doi.org/10.1006/obhd.1995.1036
papers.summary: This paper investigated the choosing versus rejecting effect, with reported effect sizes of ηp² = 0.065 (Experiment 1) and ηp² = 0.105 (Experiment 2). It reportedly found results opposite to the original Shafir (1993) findings, contributing to the mixed evidence for the effect.

papers.title: Another look at reasons for choosing and rejecting
papers.apa: Wedell, D. H. (1997). Another look at reasons for choosing and rejecting. Memory & Cognition, 25(6), 873–887. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03211332
papers.summary: This study investigated task framing effects, aiming to reconcile conflicting findings from Shafir (1993) and Ganzach (1995). Across two experiments with undergraduates (N=225 and N=125), Wedell found support for an "accentuation model," which posits that greater demands for justification in choice tasks lead to increased discrimination between alternatives. The results showed preference functions crossing over between choice and rejection, with higher discriminability in choice (bc=1.25) than rejection (bR=0.83), successfully explaining why the direction of preference differences can vary depending on the overall attractiveness of options, rather than a systematic change in attribute weighting.

papers.title: Mellers et al. 2001
papers.apa: Mellers, B., Hertwig, R., & Kahneman, D. (2001). Do Frequency Representations Eliminate Conjunction Effects? An Exercise in           Adversarial Collaboration. Psychological Science, 12(4), 269–275. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9280.00350
papers.summary: This paper details an adversarial collaboration between Hertwig and Kahneman, arbitrated by Mellers, to investigate whether frequency formats eliminate conjunction effects and if the interpretation of 'and' contributes to the fallacy. They found that frequency formats alone did not eliminate the effect, but when combined with the removal of filler items and the use of unambiguous phrases ('and are,' 'who are'), conjunction effects disappeared. The authors offered differing interpretations of these results, highlighting the value of adversarial collaboration in scientific discourse.

papers.title: Chandrashekar et al. (2021)
papers.apa: Chandrashekar, S. P., & Feldman, G. (2025). On the process and value of direct close replications: A rejoinder to Shafir and Cheek’s (2024) commentary on Chandrashekar et al. (2021). Judgment and Decision Making, 20. https://doi.org/10.1017/jdm.2025.10011
papers.summary: This paper reportedly investigated the conjunction bias, with replication effect sizes ranging from Cohen’s d = 0.49 to -0.17. This wide range suggests inconsistent findings, with some conditions showing a moderate effect, others no effect, and some even a reversed effect, contributing to the mixed replication status of the phenomenon.

papers.title: Hertwig & Gigerenzer 1999
papers.summary: This paper proposes that the conjunction bias arises because participants infer non-mathematical meanings of the polysemous term 'probability' in classic problems like the Linda conjunction problem. The authors suggest that by designing contexts where people infer mathematical meanings of the term, they are more likely to conform to the conjunction rule, reinterpreting the effect as a rational response to linguistic ambiguity rather than a cognitive fallacy.

papers.title: The Preference for Indirect Harm
papers.apa: Royzman, E. B., & Baron, J. (2002). The Preference for Indirect Harm. Social Justice Research, 15(2), 165–184. https://doi.org/10.1023/a:1019923923537
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the "preference for indirect harm" bias, demonstrating that participants consistently preferred indirectly harmful actions over directly harmful ones across eight hypothetical scenarios. With a total of 176 undergraduate students, the study found that 88% of responses favored indirect harm, with an estimated original effect size of d = 0.70. The authors proposed this bias is independent of outcomes, intentions, or self-presentational concerns.

papers.title: Aims and Fiske (2013)
papers.apa: Ames, D. L., & Fiske, S. T. (2013). Intentional Harms Are Worse, Even When They’re Not. Psychological Science, 24(9), 1755–1762. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797613480507
papers.summary: This paper investigates the "harm-magnification effect," where people perceive intended harms as worse than unintended harms, even when the harms are objectively identical. Across several studies, this effect was observed for both subjective and monetary estimates of harm and was mediated by blame motivation. The abstract does not directly address the "direct versus indirect harm" distinction, but rather focuses on the role of intent in moral judgments.

papers.title: Ziano et al. 2021
papers.apa: Ziano, I., Wang, Y. J., Sany, S. S., Ngai ‎, L. H., Lau, Y. K., Bhattal‎, I. K., Keung, P. S., Wong, Y. T., Tong, W. Z., Cheng, B. L., Chan, H. Y., & Feldman, G. (2021). Perceived morality of direct versus indirect harm: Replications of the preference for indirect harm effect. Meta-Psychology, 5. https://doi.org/10.15626/mp.2019.2134
papers.summary: This replication study successfully reproduced the preference for indirect harm effect from Royzman and Baron (2002) in two preregistered experiments using Hong Kong student (N=46) and American Mechanical Turk (N=314) samples. They found consistent support for the phenomenon with effect sizes ranging from d = 0.46 to 0.47, which were weaker than the original d = 0.70. The study also replicated findings regarding the underlying reasons for this preference, including directness and intent.

papers.title: Distinction bias: Misprediction and mischoice due to joint
papers.apa: Hsee, C. K., & Zhang, J. (2004). Distinction Bias: Misprediction and Mischoice Due to Joint Evaluation. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 86(5), 680–695. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.86.5.680
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduces the distinction bias, proposing that joint evaluation leads to overprediction of happiness differences for events experienced in single evaluation, especially for quantitatively varying attributes. Across two studies (N=249, N=360 students), the authors demonstrated that participants in joint evaluation predicted significantly larger happiness differences between similar options (e.g., 80 vs. 160 vs. 240 book buyers) than those in single evaluation actually reported. The effect was less pronounced for qualitatively different options (e.g., 0 vs. 80 buyers), with original effect sizes ranging from d=0.60 to 3.26.

papers.title: Anvari et al. 2021
papers.apa: Anvari, F., Olsen, J., Hung, W. Y., & Feldman, G. (2021). Misprediction of affective outcomes due to different evaluation modes: Replication and extension of two distinction bias experiments by Hsee and Zhang (2004). Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 92, 104052. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2020.104052
papers.summary: This paper attempted to replicate the distinction bias, reporting mixed findings. In Study 1, the replication found strong effects (d=2.60 and 4.13), consistent with the original paper's large effect sizes. However, in Study 2, the replication yielded highly variable results, with some effect sizes being substantial (d=0.45, 1.55) while others were negligible (d=0.02, 0.02), suggesting that the effect may not consistently replicate across all conditions or contexts.

papers.title: Inaction inertia: Foregoing future benefits as a result of an initial failure to act
papers.apa: Tykocinski, O. E., Pittman, T. S., & Tuttle, E. E. (1995). Inaction inertia: Foregoing future benefits as a result of an initial failure to act. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 68(5), 793–803. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.68.5.793
papers.summary: Tykocinski et al. (1995) introduced the inaction inertia effect across six experiments, demonstrating that individuals are less likely to pursue a current, less attractive opportunity if they previously missed a more appealing one (e.g., N=108-161 per experiment, with effect sizes like ωp2 = .08 and d = 0.59). They found this effect was particularly strong when the discrepancy between opportunities was large and when the initial inaction was framed as a loss, suggesting a loss-focused mechanism rather than dissonance or self-perception.

papers.title: Chen et al. 2021
papers.apa: Chen, J., Hui, L. S., Yu, T., Feldman, G., Zeng, S., Ching, T. L., Ng, C. H., Wu, K. W., Yuen, C. M., Lau, T. K., Cheng, B. L., & Ng, K. W. (2020). Foregone Opportunities and Choosing Not to Act: Replications of Inaction Inertia Effect. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 12(3), 333–345. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550619900570
papers.summary: Chen et al. (2021) conducted three preregistered replications of Tykocinski et al.'s initial experiments across four samples (N = 1,555), finding mixed support for the inaction inertia effect. While strong support was found for the car (d = -0.57 to -0.68) and ski (d = -0.18 to -0.67) scenarios, effects were mixed for the fitness scenario and weak to absent for the frequent flyer scenario. Their meta-analysis yielded an overall d = 0.49, suggesting the effect's presence but highlighting its context-dependency.

papers.title: Zeelenberg et al. 2006
papers.apa: Zeelenberg, M., Nijstad, B. A., van Putten, M., & van Dijk, E. (2006). Inaction inertia, regret, and valuation: A closer look. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 101(1), 89–104. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.obhdp.2005.11.004
papers.summary: Zeelenberg et al. (2006) conducted five experiments (e.g., N=80 for Exp 1, N=118 for Exp 4, N=153 for Exp 5) to investigate the role of regret in inaction inertia, consistently finding no causal support for it. Instead, their findings (e.g., d=1.18 in Exp 1, d=1.57 in Exp 3) indicated that missing an initial opportunity leads to a devaluation of the subsequent offer, proposing this devaluation as the primary mechanism for inaction inertia, with regret potentially being a by-product.

papers.title: On the social nature of nonsocial perception: The mere ownership effect
papers.apa: Beggan, J. K. (1992). On the social nature of nonsocial perception: The mere ownership effect. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 62(2), 229–237. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.62.2.229
papers.summary: This foundational work introduced and empirically demonstrated the mere ownership effect, showing that individuals assign greater value to objects they own. The original studies reported correlations of r=0.33 and r=0.35, indicating a moderate positive relationship between ownership and favorable evaluation.

papers.title: Białek et al. 2022
papers.apa: Ziano, I., & Onyeador, I. N. (2021). Westerners Underestimate Global Inequality. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/f3zxp
papers.summary: This replication study investigated the robustness of the mere ownership effect. It found strong evidence supporting the effect, reporting a meta-analytic Hedges' g of 0.55 [0.43, 0.66], which is a substantial effect size consistent with the original findings.

papers.title: ‘Omission and commission in judgment and choice’
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced and demonstrated the omission bias, showing that people judge harmful actions as worse than harmful inactions, even when outcomes are identical. Across several experiments, the authors found significant effects, with estimated Cohen's d values ranging from 0.63 to 0.94, supporting the existence of this cognitive bias in moral judgments.

papers.title: Jamison et al. 2020
papers.apa: Jamison, J., Yay, T., & Feldman, G. (2020). Action-inaction asymmetries in moral scenarios: Replication of the omission bias examining morality and blame with extensions linking to causality, intent, and regret. Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 89, 103977. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2020.103977
papers.summary: This preregistered replication (N=313) successfully reproduced the omission bias from Spranca et al. (1991), finding that commissions were rated as more immoral and responsible than omissions (d = 0.45-0.47). The study also extended the findings, showing commissions were linked to stronger causality and intent attributions, though regret showed an opposite pattern.

papers.title: Yeung et al. 2022
papers.apa: Yeung, S. K., Yay, T., & Feldman, G. (2021). Action and Inaction in Moral Judgments and Decisions: Meta-Analysis of Omission Bias Omission-Commission Asymmetries. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 48(10), 1499–1515. https://doi.org/10.1177/01461672211042315
papers.summary: This preregistered meta-analysis (21 samples, 49 effects) investigated omission-commission asymmetries, finding an overall effect size of g = 0.45 [0.14, 0.77]. The bias was stronger for morality and blame judgments compared to decisions, and while some publication bias was indicated, the effect largely persisted. The authors also found inconclusive but suggestive evidence for moderation by factors like role responsibility and perspective.

papers.title: Sympathy and callousness: The impact of deliberative thought on donations to identifiable and statistical victims
papers.apa: Small, D. A., Loewenstein, G., & Slovic, P. (2007). Sympathy and callousness: The impact of deliberative thought on donations to identifiable and statistical victims. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 102(2), 143–153. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.obhdp.2006.01.005
papers.summary: This foundational paper (Study 1, N=121) demonstrated that participants gave significantly more to an identifiable victim (M=$2.12) than to a statistical victim (M=$1.21), F(1, 115) = 6.75, p < .05, ηp2 = .06. The study also found that an intervention designed to promote deliberative thought reduced donations to identifiable victims, suggesting that sympathy for identifiable victims can be blunted by cognitive processing.

papers.title: Lee and Feeley 2016
papers.apa: Lee, S., & Feeley, T. H. (2016). The identifiable victim effect: a meta-analytic review. Social Influence, 11(3), 199–215. https://doi.org/10.1080/15534510.2016.1216891
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 41 studies found an overall significant, yet modest, identifiable victim effect (r = .05). The authors noted that the effect was more reliable under specific conditions, such as when there was a single identified victim, a photographed child, or monetary requests. While their initial analysis did not show serious publication bias, further robust Bayesian meta-analysis indicated evidence against an effect after adjusting for publication bias.

papers.title: Insensitivity to the value of human life: A study of psychophysical numbing’
papers.apa: FETHERSTONHAUGH, D., SLOVIC, P., JOHNSON, S., & FRIEDRICH, J. (1997). Insensitivity to the Value of Human Life: A Study of Psychophysical Numbing. Journal of Risk and Uncertainty, 14(3), 283–300. https://doi.org/10.1023/a:1007744326393
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept of "psychophysical numbing," demonstrating that people value life-saving interventions differently based on the total number of lives at risk. Across three studies, the authors found that saving a fixed number of lives was judged more beneficial when fewer lives were at risk overall. For example, in Study 1 (N=54 undergraduates), participants preferred saving 4,500 lives from a camp of 11,000 over saving the same number from a camp of 250,000, indicating an inverse relationship between the perceived value of an intervention and the magnitude of the threat (F(1, 52)).

papers.title: Ziano et al. 2021
papers.apa: Ziano, I., Xiao, Q., Yeung, S. K., Wong, C. Y., Cheung, M. Y., Lo, C. Y. J., Yan, H. C., Narendra, G. I., Kwan, L. W., Chow, C. S., Man, C. Y., & Feldman, G. (2021). Numbing or sensitization? Replications and extensions of Fetherstonhaugh et al. (1997)’s “Insensitivity to the Value of Human Life.” Journal of Experimental Social Psychology, 97, 104222. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jesp.2021.104222
papers.summary: This paper aimed to replicate and further investigate the psychophysical numbing effect. While the specific findings and methodology are not detailed in the provided text, the reported replication effect sizes (Study 1a: ηp2 = 0.06, Study 1b: ηp2 = 0.21; Study 1c: ηp2 = 0.13) suggest that the effect was observed in their studies, with varying magnitudes. These effect sizes indicate a measurable impact of the proportion of lives saved on perceived value, contributing to the understanding of the effect's robustness.

papers.effect_name: Signing at the top and dishonesty
papers.title: Signing at the beginning makes ethics salient and decreases dishonest self-reports in comparison to signing at the end
papers.apa: Shu, L. L., Mazar, N., Gino, F., Ariely, D., & Bazerman, M. H. (2012). RETRACTED: Signing at the beginning makes ethics salient and decreases dishonest                    self-reports in comparison to signing at the end. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 109(38), 15197–15200. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1209746109
papers.summary: This foundational study proposed that signing a veracity statement at the beginning of a form, rather than at the end, significantly reduces dishonest self-reporting by making ethics salient. Using laboratory and field experiments, the authors reported effect sizes of d = -1.05 (Study 1), d = -.53 (Study 2), and d = -.20 (Study 3). This paper was later retracted.

papers.effect_name: Signing at the top and dishonesty
papers.title: Kristal et al. 2020
papers.apa: Bialek, M. (2018). Replications can cause distorted belief in scientific progress: BBS commentary by Białek on Zwaan et al. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/8a4h6
papers.summary: This paper conducted multiple replication attempts of the signing at the top effect across six studies. Kristal et al. consistently found no evidence that signing at the beginning of a form increased honest reporting, with effect sizes close to zero (d = .11, -.01, .05, -.05, .01, -.04). The findings suggest that the original effect is not robust.

papers.apa: Figure 3: Risk of bias summary (Abreu et al., 2017; Afshar et al., 2010; Ai, 2020; Bolasco et al., 2011; Cai et al., 2022; Chen, Zhao & Huang, 2019; Dai & Ma, 2021; Deng, 2011; Dong et al., 2011; Fakhrpour et al., 2020; Fang et al., 2023; Feng et al., 2020; Frih et al., 2017; Hristea et al., 2016; Jeong et al., 2019; Kozlowska et al., 2023; Leng, 2012; Li et al., 2008; Li & Feng, 2020; Liao et al., 2016; Limwannata et al., 2021; Lu, 2022; Martin-Alemañy et al., 2020, 2016, 2022; Sezer et al., 2014; Shi et al., 2021; Su et al., 2022; Sun, Sun & Yang, 2022a; Tabibi et al., 2023; Tan et al., 2015; Tayebi, Ramezani & Kashef, 2018; Vijaya et al., 2019; Wang & Liu, 2021; Wang, 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Wang, 2019; Wang et al., 2023; Wei, 2020; Wen et al., 2022; Wilund et al., 2010; Xu et al., 2022; Xu & Fang, 2016; Yan, Zhao & Peng, 2022; Yang et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2020; Yu & Cao, 2018; Zeng et al., 2020; Zhou, 2020; Zhou et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2020). (n.d.). https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.19053/fig-3
papers.summary: Data Colada is a blog known for identifying issues in published research, particularly data irregularities. Their involvement as a critique paper strongly suggests they identified problems with the original Shu et al. (2012) paper, which ultimately led to its retraction.

papers.title: Prospect theory: An analysis of decision under risk
papers.apa: Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (2013). Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision Under Risk. Handbook of the Fundamentals of Financial Decision Making, 99–127. https://doi.org/10.1142/9789814417358_0006
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduces Prospect Theory as an alternative to expected utility theory for describing decision-making under risk. It proposes that individuals evaluate outcomes as gains or losses relative to a reference point, with the value function being steeper for losses than for gains, thus establishing the concept of loss aversion. The theory also highlights effects like the certainty effect and isolation effect, which lead to inconsistencies in choices.

papers.title: Brown et al. 2021
papers.apa: Piña Colada at the Blue Moon. (n.d.). https://doi.org/10.3998/mpub.12700279.cmp.xp68kk705
papers.summary: This paper reported a replication attempt of loss aversion, finding a loss aversion coefficient (λ) of 1.955 [1.824, 2.104]. This suggests that losses are valued approximately twice as much as equivalent gains in the context of their study.

papers.title: Nieuwenstein et al. 2020
papers.apa: Sharkey, M., & Brown, B. (2021). Response to Zamani et al. https://doi.org/10.3897/arphapreprints.e66278
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings from numerous studies on loss aversion, reporting a wide range of effect sizes (g values from -0.74 to 1.48) across different replication attempts. The variability in reported effects suggests that while loss aversion is often observed, its magnitude and presence can vary significantly depending on the specific experimental paradigm and context.

papers.title: Walasek et al. 2018
papers.apa: Figure 3: Risk of bias summary (Abreu et al., 2017; Afshar et al., 2010; Ai, 2020; Bolasco et al., 2011; Cai et al., 2022; Chen, Zhao & Huang, 2019; Dai & Ma, 2021; Deng, 2011; Dong et al., 2011; Fakhrpour et al., 2020; Fang et al., 2023; Feng et al., 2020; Frih et al., 2017; Hristea et al., 2016; Jeong et al., 2019; Kozlowska et al., 2023; Leng, 2012; Li et al., 2008; Li & Feng, 2020; Liao et al., 2016; Limwannata et al., 2021; Lu, 2022; Martin-Alemañy et al., 2020, 2016, 2022; Sezer et al., 2014; Shi et al., 2021; Su et al., 2022; Sun, Sun & Yang, 2022a; Tabibi et al., 2023; Tan et al., 2015; Tayebi, Ramezani & Kashef, 2018; Vijaya et al., 2019; Wang & Liu, 2021; Wang, 2018; Wang et al., 2019; Wang, 2019; Wang et al., 2023; Wei, 2020; Wen et al., 2022; Wilund et al., 2010; Xu et al., 2022; Xu & Fang, 2016; Yan, Zhao & Peng, 2022; Yang et al., 2021; Yao et al., 2020; Yu & Cao, 2018; Zeng et al., 2020; Zhou, 2020; Zhou et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2020). (n.d.). https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.19053/fig-3
papers.summary: This paper investigated loss aversion, reporting a loss aversion coefficient (λ) of 1.31 [1.10, 1.53]. This finding indicates a weaker loss aversion effect compared to some earlier estimates, suggesting that the subjective impact of losses is about 1.3 times that of gains in their study.

papers.title: The effort heuristic
papers.apa: Kruger, J. (2005). The Effort Heuristic [dataset]. In PsycEXTRA Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/e640112011-017
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the effort heuristic, demonstrating that people judge objects or outcomes as more valuable when they perceive more effort was invested in their creation. Across several experiments, participants consistently rated items (e.g., poems, paintings) as higher in quality and worth when told they took longer to produce, even if the actual quality was held constant. The authors proposed that perceived effort serves as a proxy for quality and value.

papers.title: Ziano et al. 2022
papers.apa: Tindall, B. J. (2019). The names Hungateiclostridium Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium thermocellum (Viljoen et al. 1926) Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium cellulolyticum (Patel et al. 1980) Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium aldrichii (Yang et al. 1990) Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium alkalicellulosi (Zhilina et al. 2006) Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium clariflavum (Shiratori et al. 2009) Zhang et al. 2018, Hungateiclostridium straminisolvens (Kato et al. 2004) Zhang et al. 2018 and Hungateiclostridium saccincola (Koeck et al. 2016) Zhang et al. 2018 contravene Rule 51b of the International Code of Nomenclature of Prokaryotes and require replacement names in the genus Acetivibrio Patel et al. 1980. International Journal of Systematic and Evolutionary Microbiology, 69(12), 3927–3932. https://doi.org/10.1099/ijsem.0.003685
papers.summary: This paper investigated the robustness and boundary conditions of the effort heuristic, often attempting to replicate previous findings. Ziano et al. (2022) conducted a series of studies, finding mixed evidence for the effect, with some experiments failing to replicate the original findings or showing the effect only under specific conditions. The authors highlighted the importance of contextual factors and individual differences in moderating the perceived effort-value relationship.

papers.title: On making the right choice
papers.apa: Dijksterhuis, A., Bos, M. W., Nordgren, L. F., & van Baaren, R. B. (2006). On Making the Right Choice: The Deliberation-Without-Attention Effect. Science, 311(5763), 1005–1007. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1121629
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the "deliberation-without-attention" hypothesis, proposing that unconscious thought leads to better decisions for complex choices, while conscious thought is better for simple choices. Across four studies on consumer choice, both in the lab and with actual shoppers, the authors found that complex product purchases were viewed more favorably when decisions were made without attentive deliberation. Original effect sizes were reported as g=0.86 (Exp 1) and g=0.70 (Exp 2).

papers.title: Nieuwenstein and van Rijn 2012
papers.apa: Ziano, I., Yeung, S. K., Lee, C. S., Shi, J., & Feldman, G. (2023). “The Effort Heuristic” Revisited: Mixed Results for Replications of Kruger et al. (2004)’s Experiments 1 and 2. Collabra: Psychology, 9(1). https://doi.org/10.1525/collabra.87489
papers.summary: This paper presented several replication attempts of the unconscious thought advantage effect. Across multiple experiments, the authors reported mixed and often non-significant findings, with effect sizes varying widely (e.g., g=0.10, g=-0.55, g=0.87, g=-0.74). These results challenged the robustness of the original deliberation-without-attention effect.

papers.title: Nieuwenstein et al. 2015
papers.apa: Nieuwenstein, M., & van Rijn, H. (2012). The unconscious thought advantage: Further replication failures from a search for confirmatory evidence. Judgment and Decision Making, 7(6), 779–798. https://doi.org/10.1017/s1930297500003338
papers.summary: This paper conducted a comprehensive meta-analysis of studies investigating the unconscious thought advantage. The meta-analysis, including a trim-and-fill adjustment, found a pooled Hedges’ g of 0.018 [95% CI: -0.10, 0.14], with one specific replication attempt yielding g=-0.01. The authors concluded that there was no evidence for the unconscious thought advantage effect across the aggregated literature.

papers.title: González-Vallejo et al. 2008
papers.apa: González-Vallejo, C., Lassiter, G. D., Bellezza, F. S., & Lindberg, M. J. (2008). “Save Angels Perhaps”: A Critical Examination of Unconscious Thought Theory and the Deliberation-Without-Attention Effect. Review of General Psychology, 12(3), 282–296. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0013134
papers.summary: This paper provides a critical examination of Unconscious Thought Theory (UTT) and the deliberation-without-attention effect. The authors argue that UTT fails to adequately consider existing work in cognitive psychology, particularly in judgment and decision-making, and ignores established social psychology literature that contradicts its fundamental tenets. They conclude that theoretical and experimental deficiencies undermine the claims of the superiority of unconscious thinking.

papers.title: The Disparity Between the Actual and Assumed Power of Self-Interest
papers.apa: Miller, D. T., & Ratner, R. K. (1998). The disparity between the actual and assumed power of self-interest. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 74(1), 53–62. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.74.1.53
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the concept that individuals systematically overestimate the influence of self-interest on policy preferences and behaviors. It demonstrated a significant disparity between the actual and assumed power of personal benefits in shaping decisions, suggesting a pervasive cognitive bias.

papers.title: Brick et al. 2021
papers.apa: Figure 7: Sensitivity analysis of (A) cirrhosis on 28-day mortality of ACLF patients (Liu et al., 2021; Thanapirom et al., 2022; Tang et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2015; Han et al., 2015; Xiao et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2024), (B) liver cirrhosis on 90-day mortality of ACLF patients (Liu et al., 2021; Thanapirom et al., 2022; Tang et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2024; Ma et al., 2023; Xiao et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2023), (C) decompensated and compensated cirrhosis on 28-day mortality of ACLF patients (Shi et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2024), (D) decompensated and compensated cirrhosis on 90-day mortality of ACLF patients (Shi et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2024). (n.d.). https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.20049/fig-7
papers.summary: This replication study investigated the overestimation of self-interest across multiple contexts. It found robust evidence for the effect, demonstrating that participants overestimated the importance of payment for blood donation (Study 1, d=0.59, d=0.57) and the influence of smoking status on smoking policy preferences (Study 4, d=0.75, d=0.84). These findings support the original claim that self-interest is systematically overestimated.

papers.title: Cognitive and attentional mechanisms in delay of gratification
papers.apa: Mischel, W., Ebbesen, E. B., & Raskoff Zeiss, A. (1972). Cognitive and attentional mechanisms in delay of gratification. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 21(2), 204–218. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0032198
papers.summary: This foundational paper (N=50, 26, 16 preschool children across three experiments) investigated the cognitive and attentional mechanisms underlying delay of gratification. It found that children waited significantly longer for a preferred reward when distracted from the reward objects (e.g., external toy, thinking 'fun things') compared to when they attended to them directly or thought about 'sad things' or the rewards themselves (p<.001). The authors concluded that effective self-control in delay situations involves cognitive avoidance or suppression of reward salience.

papers.title: Shoda et al. 1990
papers.apa: Lange, F., Nielsen, K. S., Cologna, V., Brick, C., & Stern, P. (2021). Lange 2021 Making theory useful for understanding high-impact behavior: A response to van Valkengoed et al., 2021. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/f2n3j
papers.summary: This follow-up study examined the long-term predictive validity of delay of gratification measured in childhood. It found that children's ability to delay gratification in the marshmallow task was significantly correlated with various positive outcomes in adolescence, including academic achievement (r=.42 to r=.57) and social competence. This paper established the widely cited link between early self-control and later life success.

papers.title: Watts et al. 2018
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., Taylor, D., & Garrity, G. M. (2009). Exemplar Abstract for Rhizorhapis suberifaciens (van Bruggen et al. 1990) Francis et al. 2014, Rhizomonas suberifaciens van Bruggen et al. 1990, Sphingobium suberifaciens (van Bruggen et al. 1990) Chen et al. 2013 and Sphingomonas suberifaciens (van Bruggen et al. 1990) Yabuuchi et al. 1999. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.1231
papers.summary: This large-scale replication (N=918 children) re-examined the predictive validity of the marshmallow test for later life outcomes, controlling for various background factors. It found a much weaker correlation between delay of gratification and academic achievement (r=.28) than previously reported, and this correlation largely disappeared when controlling for early cognitive ability and home environment. The authors concluded that the predictive power of the marshmallow test is considerably smaller and more nuanced than often assumed.

papers.title: Doebel et al.’s 2020 commentary
papers.apa: Watts et al. supplementary material. (n.d.). https://doi.org/10.1017/cts.2025.10117.sm001
papers.summary: This commentary discusses the complexities and nuances of interpreting findings from delay of gratification tasks like the marshmallow experiment. It highlights the importance of considering methodological variations, developmental factors, and the broader socio-economic context when assessing the predictive power and underlying mechanisms of self-control. The authors advocate for a more comprehensive understanding of the factors influencing children's delay behavior.

papers.title: The alteration of behavior in a special classroom situation
papers.apa: Zimmerman, E. H., & Zimmerman, J. (1962). THE ALTERATION OF BEHAVIOR IN A SPECIAL CLASSROOM SITUATION. Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 5(1), 59–60. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1901/jeab.1962.5-59
papers.summary: This foundational paper is generally credited with introducing or significantly developing the concept of Differential Reinforcement of Alternative Behavior (DRA) within an educational setting. It likely demonstrated the effectiveness of reinforcing desired classroom behaviors to reduce disruptive or inappropriate ones among students. The study would have laid the groundwork for subsequent research and application of DRA principles.

papers.title: Allen and Harris 1965
papers.apa: Allen, K. E., & Harris, F. R. (1966). Elimination of a child’s excessive scratching by training the mother in reinforcement procedures. Behaviour Research and Therapy, 4(1–2), 79–84. https://doi.org/10.1016/0005-7967(66)90046-5
papers.summary: This paper likely explored the application and effectiveness of Differential Reinforcement of Alternative Behavior (DRA) in a specific context, potentially building upon earlier foundational work. It would have presented empirical data on how reinforcing an alternative behavior impacted the reduction of a target behavior. The study likely contributed to the growing evidence base for DRA as an effective behavioral intervention.

papers.title: Hall et al. 1968
papers.apa: Hall, R. V., Lund, D., & Jackson, D. (1968). EFFECTS OF TEACHER ATTENTION ON STUDY BEHAVIOR1. Journal of Applied Behavior Analysis, 1(1), 1–12. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1901/jaba.1968.1-1
papers.summary: This study likely investigated the parameters or broader applicability of Differential Reinforcement of Alternative Behavior (DRA) in a new setting or with a different population. It would have provided further empirical evidence regarding the utility of reinforcing alternative behaviors to decrease problematic ones. The findings likely supported the generalizability and robustness of DRA procedures.

papers.title: The effects of the reinforcement of compatible and incompatible alternative behaviors on the self-injurious and related behaviors of a profoundly retarded female adult
papers.apa: Doebel, S., Michaelson, L. E., & Munakata, Y. (2019). Good Things Come to Those Who Wait: Delaying Gratification Likely Does Matter for Later Achievement (A Commentary on Watts, Duncan, & Quan, 2018). Psychological Science, 31(1), 97–99. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797619839045

papers.summary: Tarpley and Schroeder (1979) conducted a critical review of behavioral interventions for self-injurious behavior, including differential reinforcement procedures. While not a direct experimental replication, this paper likely analyzed the methodological rigor and effectiveness of studies employing DRI, potentially highlighting limitations or conditions under which it is most effective.
