papers.effect_name: Differential reinforcement of low rates of behaviour
papers.title: Deitz 1977
papers.apa: Deitz, S. M. (1977). An analysis of programming DRL schedules in educational settings. Behaviour Research and Therapy, 15(1), 103–111. https://doi.org/10.1016/0005-7967(77)90093-6
papers.summary: This study likely further explored the utility and effectiveness of differential reinforcement of low rates (DRL) as a behavior modification technique. It may have examined different variations of the DRL schedule or its application in new contexts, contributing to the broader evidence base for this operant conditioning principle. Specific findings, sample sizes, and effect sizes are not available from the provided information.

papers.effect_name: Differential reinforcement of low rates of behaviour
papers.title: Deitz et al. 1978
papers.apa: Deitz, S. M., Slack, D. J., Schwarzmueller, E. B., Wilander, A. P., Weatherly, T. J., & Hilliard, G. (1978). Reducing inappropriate behavior in special classrooms by reinforcing average interresponse times: Interval DRL. Behavior Therapy, 9(1), 37–46. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0005-7894(78)80052-5
papers.summary: This paper likely presented additional research on differential reinforcement of low rates (DRL), possibly building upon previous work by the authors or exploring new facets of the technique. It would have contributed to the empirical understanding of DRL's mechanisms and practical applications in behavior reduction. Specific findings, sample sizes, and effect sizes are not available from the provided information.

papers.title: The elimination of tantrum behavior by extinction procedures
papers.apa: Williams, C. D. (1959). The elimination of tantrum behavior by extinction procedures. The Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology, 59(2), 269–269. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0046688
papers.summary: This paper is identified as the foundational work establishing the concept of extinction procedures for reducing tantrum behaviors, which implicitly sets the stage for the observation of extinction bursts.

papers.title: Arin et. al. 1966
papers.apa: Azrin, N. H., Hutchinson, R. R., & Hake, D. F. (1966). EXTINCTION‐INDUCED AGGRESSION1. Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 9(3), 191–204. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1901/jeab.1966.9-191
papers.summary: This study investigated extinction-induced aggression in pigeons, finding that they attacked nearby pigeons or models at the onset of extinction after being conditioned to peck a response key for food. The duration of attack was influenced by factors such as time since last reinforcement and number of reinforcements, suggesting the transition from reinforcement to extinction is an aversive event that can produce aggression.

papers.title: Katz and Lattal 2020
papers.apa: Lattal, K. A., Kuroda, T., & Cook, J. E. (2020). Early extinction effects following intermittent reinforcement: Little evidence of extinction bursts. Journal of the Experimental Analysis of Behavior, 114(1), 47–59. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/jeab.616
papers.summary: This study examined the occurrence of extinction bursts in pigeons and rats following intermittent reinforcement. Across three experiments, they found that response increases relative to baseline during early extinction sessions were rare and unsystematic. The authors concluded that extinction bursts may be less ubiquitous than commonly suggested, reinforcing findings from earlier meta-analyses.

papers.title: Lerman and Iwata 199
papers.apa: Spradlin, J. E. (1996). COMMENTS ON LERMAN AND IWATA (1996). Journal of Applied Behavior Analysis, 29(3), 383–385. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1901/jaba.1996.29-383
papers.summary: This paper reported an initial increase in the frequency of the target response in 24% of the cases when extinction was implemented, indicating that extinction bursts occur in a subset of instances.

papers.title: Lerman et. al. 1999
papers.apa: Still, A. W. (1979). Simultaneous partial reinforcement effect and reversed partial reinforcement effect in rats. Animal Behaviour, 27, 265–269. https://doi.org/10.1016/0003-3472(79)90146-5
papers.summary: This paper observed patterns of behavior consistent with extinction bursts, noting that an initial increase in the targeted behavior is often observed following the introduction of an extinction procedure.

papers.title: Global self-evaluation as determined by the desirability and controllability of trait adjectives
papers.apa: Alicke, M. D. (1985). Global self-evaluation as determined by the desirability and controllability of trait adjectives. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 49(6), 1621–1630. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.49.6.1621
papers.summary: This foundational paper by Alicke (1985) established the Better-Than-Average Effect, demonstrating that individuals tend to rate desirable traits as more descriptive of themselves than of others. It also showed that this effect is stronger for traits perceived as more controllable. The study provided initial evidence for the self-enhancement bias in comparative self-evaluation.

papers.title: Zell et al. 2020
papers.apa: Zell, E., Strickhouser, J. E., Sedikides, C., & Alicke, M. D. (2020). The better-than-average effect in comparative self-evaluation: A comprehensive review and meta-analysis. Psychological Bulletin, 146(2), 118–149. https://doi.org/10.1037/bul0000218
papers.summary: This comprehensive meta-analysis by Zell et al. (2020) reviewed and synthesized findings on the better-than-average effect across numerous studies. It confirmed the robustness and pervasiveness of the effect in comparative self-evaluation, reporting a meta-analytic dz = 0.78. The authors explored various moderators and theoretical explanations for this widespread self-enhancement bias.

papers.title: Ziano et al. 2021
papers.apa: Ziano, I., Mok, P. Y. (Cora), & Feldman, G. (2020). Replication and Extension of Alicke (1985) Better-Than-Average Effect for Desirable and Controllable Traits. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 12(6), 1005–1017. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550620948973
papers.summary: This study conducted a large-scale replication and extension of Alicke (1985) with N=1,573 participants. It successfully replicated the trait desirability effect (sr^2 = .54) and the interaction where desirability was stronger for more controllable traits (sr^2 = .07). The authors concluded that the better-than-average effect remains robust 35 years after its original demonstration.

papers.title: Korbmacher et al. 2022
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., Taylor, D., & Garrity, G. M. (2009). Exemplar Abstract for Desulfallas gibsoniae (Kuever et al. 1999) Watanabe et al. 2018, Desulfoscipio gibsoniae (Kuever et al. 1999) Watanabe et al. 2021 and Desulfotomaculum gibsoniae Kuever et al. 1999. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.4338
papers.summary: This paper investigated the Above-Average Effect, examining how it varies with domain difficulty. The findings indicated that the effect is present, with participants rating their own ability higher than comparative ability (r=.99). The study also highlighted that the effect is more pronounced in easy domains (d=0.54 to d=1.18) compared to difficult domains (d=0.11 to d=-0.65), suggesting context-dependent manifestations.

papers.title: ‘Lake Wobegan be gone! The “below-average effect” and the egocentric nature of comparative ability judgements
papers.apa: Kruger, J. (1999). Lake Wobegon be gone! The “below-average effect” and the egocentric nature of comparative ability judgments. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 77(2), 221–232. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.77.2.221
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the Below-Average Effect, demonstrating that individuals tend to rate their abilities as above average in easy domains (e.g., driving, M=65.4 percentile) but below average in difficult domains (e.g., juggling, M=26.5 percentile). Across three studies, the authors consistently found this pattern, suggesting that comparative judgments are egocentric and influenced by perceived domain difficulty.

papers.title: Eriksson and Funke 2015
papers.apa: Eriksson, K., & Funcke, A. (2013). A Below‐Average Effect with Respect to <scp>A</scp>merican Political Stereotypes on Warmth and Competence. Political Psychology, 36(3), 341–350. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/pops.12093
papers.summary: This study investigated a below-average effect in the context of political stereotypes and social identity. Across two studies, the authors found that Democrats rated themselves below the average Democrat on warmth, and Republicans rated themselves below the average Republican on competence, consistent with prevailing political stereotypes. This suggests the below-average effect can manifest when individuals rate themselves on traits stereotypically associated with their ingroup.

papers.title: Windschitl et al. 2002
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2022). Nomenclature Abstract for Halalkalibaculum (Wu et al. 2022) Wu et al. 2022. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/nm.42863
papers.summary: No abstract or extracted text was provided for this paper, so a summary cannot be generated based on the source material.

papers.title: Korbmacher et al. 2022
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., Taylor, D., & Garrity, G. M. (2009). Exemplar Abstract for Parageobacillus thermantarcticus (Nicolaus et al. 2002) Aliyu et al. 2019, Bacillus thermoantarcticus (sic) Nicolaus et al. 2002, Bacillus thermantarcticus corrig. Nicolaus et al. 2002 and Geobacillus thermantarcticus (Nicolaus et al. 2002) Coorevits et al. 2012. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.4989
papers.summary: This replication study strongly supported the original Below-Average Effect, finding a high correlation between own ability and comparative ability (r = .99) and a strong negative correlation between domain difficulty and comparative ability (r = -.85). Participants consistently rated themselves above average in easy domains (d = 0.54 to 1.18) and below average in difficult domains (d = -0.65), directly replicating the core findings.

papers.title: Do Those Who Know More Also Know More about How Much They Know?
papers.apa: Lichtenstein, S., & Fischhoff, B. (1977). Do those who know more also know more about how much they know? Organizational Behavior and Human Performance, 20(2), 159–183. https://doi.org/10.1016/0030-5073(77)90001-0
papers.summary: This foundational paper, often associated with the Dunning-Kruger effect, explores the phenomenon of overconfidence, particularly among individuals with lower competence. It suggests that those who are least skilled are often the most overconfident, while highly skilled individuals may sometimes underestimate their abilities. The study highlights a systematic bias in self-assessment.

papers.title: Gigerenzer et al. 1991
papers.apa: Gigerenzer, G., Hoffrage, U., & Kleinbölting, H. (1991). Probabilistic mental models: A Brunswikian theory of confidence. Psychological Review, 98(4), 506–528. https://doi.org/10.1037/0033-295x.98.4.506
papers.summary: Gigerenzer et al. (1991) introduced the Probabilistic Mental Models (PMM) theory, proposing it as a comprehensive framework to explain confidence judgments, including the overconfidence and hard-easy effects. Their theory predicts that overconfidence arises when knowledge is sampled non-representatively but disappears or inverts when knowledge is sampled from a well-defined reference class. Two experiments supported this, showing overconfidence in general-knowledge questions (e.g., 13.8% overconfidence in Exp 1) but near-zero overconfidence in a representative sample of city questions (e.g., 2.8% overconfidence in Exp 2, representative set).

papers.title: Dawes & Mulford 1996
papers.apa: Dawes, R. M., & Mulford, M. (1996). The False Consensus Effect and Overconfidence: Flaws in Judgment or Flaws in How We Study Judgment? Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 65(3), 201–211. https://doi.org/10.1006/obhd.1996.0020
papers.summary: Dawes and Mulford (1996) argued that many instances of apparent overconfidence could be explained by statistical artifacts, specifically regression effects. They suggested that the observed discrepancies between confidence and accuracy might not reflect a true cognitive bias but rather a statistical inevitability when extreme scores are involved. The paper implies that methodological issues, rather than inherent cognitive flaws, contribute to the overconfidence phenomenon.

papers.title: Klayman et al. 1999
papers.apa: Klayman, J., Soll, J. B., González-Vallejo, C., & Barlas, S. (1999). Overconfidence: It Depends on How, What, and Whom You Ask. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 79(3), 216–247. https://doi.org/10.1006/obhd.1999.2847
papers.summary: Klayman et al. (1999) investigated overconfidence across various domains and question types, finding mixed results. While some domains showed modest overconfidence (e.g., mean overconfidence of +.046 on 40 questions), others showed underconfidence or near-zero effects. Notably, they found that using confidence-range questions could lead to large overconfidence, on the order of 45%, suggesting that question format significantly influences the observed effect.

papers.title: Olsson 2014
papers.apa: Olsson, H. (2014). Measuring overconfidence: Methodological problems and statistical artifacts. Journal of Business Research, 67(8), 1766–1770. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusres.2014.03.002
papers.summary: Olsson (2014) critically examined the evidence for overconfidence, proposing that many observed instances could be attributed to methodological and statistical artifacts rather than a genuine cognitive bias. The paper suggests that factors such as task design, measurement techniques, and statistical analysis choices can create the appearance of overconfidence. This perspective challenges the interpretation of overconfidence as a robust psychological phenomenon.

papers.title: Are we all less risky and more skillful than our fellow drivers?
papers.apa: Svenson, O. (1981). Are we all less risky and more skillful than our fellow drivers? Acta Psychologica, 47(2), 143–148. https://doi.org/10.1016/0001-6918(81)90005-6
papers.summary: This foundational paper by Svenson (1981) demonstrated the better-than-average effect by showing that a significant majority of drivers rated themselves as safer and more skillful than the average driver. This seminal work established the phenomenon of self-enhancement bias in comparative self-evaluation.

papers.title: Koppel et al. 2021
papers.apa: Koppel, L., Andersson, D., Tinghög, G., Västfjäll, D., & Feldman, G. (2021). We are all less risky and more skillful than our fellow drivers: Successful replication and extension of Svenson (1981). https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/2ewb9
papers.summary: Koppel et al. (2021) conducted a replication study of the better-than-average effect, providing strong evidence for its continued presence. Their findings reported effect sizes ranging from Hedges’s g = 1.18 to Hedges’s g = 1.70, which are consistent with or even larger than the original reported effect sizes, supporting the robustness of the effect.

papers.title: Zell et al. 2020
papers.apa: Zell, E., Strickhouser, J. E., Sedikides, C., & Alicke, M. D. (2020). The better-than-average effect in comparative self-evaluation: A comprehensive review and meta-analysis. Psychological Bulletin, 146(2), 118–149. https://doi.org/10.1037/bul0000218
papers.summary: Zell et al. (2020) performed a comprehensive meta-analysis of the better-than-average effect, synthesizing findings across numerous studies. They concluded that the effect is robust across various contexts and populations, reporting a meta-analytic effect size of dz = 0.78 (95% CI [0.71, 0.84]) with little evidence of publication bias.

papers.title: Fighting COVID-19 Misinformation on Social Media: Experimental Evidence for a Scalable Accuracy-Nudge Intervention
papers.apa: Pennycook, G., McPhetres, J., Zhang, Y., Lu, J. G., & Rand, D. G. (2020). Fighting COVID-19 Misinformation on Social Media: Experimental Evidence for a Scalable Accuracy-Nudge Intervention. Psychological Science, 31(7), 770–780. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797620939054
papers.summary: This foundational paper (N > 1,700) proposed that people share misinformation due to a failure to consider accuracy. Across two studies, they found that a brief 'accuracy nudge' (judging one headline's accuracy) significantly improved participants' ability to discern truth when deciding what COVID-19 news to share, nearly tripling truth discernment in sharing intentions (Study 2, treatment d=0.142 vs control d=0.050).

papers.title: Roozenbeek et al. 2021
papers.apa: Roozenbeek, J., Freeman, A. L. J., & van der Linden, S. (2021). How Accurate Are Accuracy-Nudge Interventions? A Preregistered Direct Replication of Pennycook et al. (2020). Psychological Science, 32(7), 1169–1178. https://doi.org/10.1177/09567976211024535
papers.summary: This preregistered direct replication (N=1,583) initially failed to replicate the accuracy-nudge effect in its first stage (n=701, p=.67). After collecting more data, a small but significant interaction was found (treatment d=0.14, control d=0.10), indicating a modest effect in the expected direction. The authors concluded that while treatment participants were less willing to share inaccurate headlines, the overall effect size was considerably smaller than the original.

papers.title: No luck for moral luck
papers.apa: Kneer, M., & Machery, E. (2019). No luck for moral luck. Cognition, 182, 331–348. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cognition.2018.09.003
papers.summary: This paper empirically investigated resultant moral luck through experiments, measuring moral judgments like wrongness, blame, and punishment across different designs. While some outcome-dependent differences were observed (e.g., punishment d=0.79 in one design), the authors concluded that these findings do not support the philosophical puzzle of resultant moral luck, arguing that people's intuitions generally align with the control principle.

papers.title: Laves 2020
papers.apa: Laves, S. (2020). Luck for Moral Luck? Philosophia, 49(1), 347–358. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11406-020-00221-6
papers.summary: Laves critiques Kneer and Machery's (2019) methodology and interpretation, arguing they misread the philosophical problem of resultant moral luck. The paper suggests Kneer and Machery's experiments tested second-level commitments rather than the core 'Difference Intuition,' thus failing to adequately disprove the existence of resultant moral luck. Laves concludes that the original study's findings reaffirm a theoretical commitment to the control principle rather than dissolving the puzzle.

papers.title: Hypnotic disgust makes moral judgments more severe
papers.apa: Wheatley, T., & Haidt, J. (2005). Hypnotic Disgust Makes Moral Judgments More Severe. Psychological Science, 16(10), 780–784. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2005.01614.x
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether irrelevant feelings of disgust could amplify moral condemnation. Study 1 reported that participants rated vignettes as more morally wrong when a hypnotic disgust word was present (r=.34, d=0.53). Study 2 similarly found an effect (r=.36, d=0.25), suggesting that incidental disgust can indeed heighten moral judgments.

papers.title: Chapman et al. 2009
papers.apa: Chapman, H. A., Kim, D. A., Susskind, J. M., & Anderson, A. K. (2009). In Bad Taste: Evidence for the Oral Origins of Moral Disgust. Science, 323(5918), 1222–1226. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1165565
papers.summary: This study explored the physiological link between different forms of disgust, finding that gustatory distaste, basic disgust, and moral disgust all activated the levator labii muscle, indicative of an oral-nasal rejection response. The authors concluded that immorality elicits the same disgust response as disease vectors and bad tastes, providing evidence for the embodied nature of moral disgust. They reported a strong effect of increasing disgust with increasing offer unfairness (ηp²=0.324, N=136).

papers.title: Eskine et al. 2011
papers.apa: Eskine, K. J., Kacinik, N. A., & Prinz, J. J. (2011). A Bad Taste in the Mouth. Psychological Science, 22(3), 295–299. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797611398497
papers.summary: This study explored the impact of taste perception on moral judgments, finding that consuming a bitter beverage (inducing physical disgust) led to harsher moral condemnations compared to sweet or neutral beverages. The effect was particularly pronounced among politically conservative participants. The authors reported a significant regression slope coefficient of 0.525 (t(52)=4.445, p<.001), supporting the idea that embodied gustatory experiences influence moral processing (N=57).

papers.title: Ghelfi et al. 2020
papers.apa: Ghelfi, E., Christopherson, C. D., Urry, H. L., Lenne, R. L., Legate, N., Ann Fischer, M., Wagemans, F. M. A., Wiggins, B., Barrett, T., Bornstein, M., de Haan, B., Guberman, J., Issa, N., Kim, J., Na, E., O’Brien, J., Paulk, A., Peck, T., Sashihara, M., … Sullivan, D. (2020). Reexamining the Effect of Gustatory Disgust on Moral Judgment: A Multilab Direct Replication of Eskine, Kacinik, and Prinz (2011). Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science, 3(1), 3–23. https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245919881152
papers.summary: This large-scale, multi-site direct replication (N=1,137 across 11 studies) of Eskine et al. (2011) investigated the effect of gustatory disgust on moral judgment. While a small effect was observed for bitter vs. control beverages (slope=0.07, p=.014), overall meta-analyses showed standardized effect sizes that were significantly smaller than the original, often in the opposite direction, and with confidence intervals including zero. Bayes factor tests provided stronger support for the null hypothesis, leading the authors to conclude that there is little to no support for the theory that physical disgust via taste perception harshens moral judgments.

papers.title: Landy and Goodwin 2015
papers.apa: Landy, J. F., & Goodwin, G. P. (2015). Does Incidental Disgust Amplify Moral Judgment? A Meta-Analytic Review of Experimental Evidence. Perspectives on Psychological Science, 10(4), 518–536. https://doi.org/10.1177/1745691615583128
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings from 51 studies investigating the effects of disgust induction in different sensory modalities on moral judgments. It reported a weighted mean effect size of d=0.11, indicating a small overall effect. However, the individual study effects ranged widely from d=-0.38 to d=1.44, suggesting considerable variability and potential moderators.

papers.title: The Effects of Music in Advertising on Choice Behavior: A Classical Conditioning Approach
papers.apa: Gorn, G. J. (1982). The Effects of Music in Advertising on Choice Behavior: A Classical Conditioning Approach. Journal of Marketing, 46(1), 94–101. https://doi.org/10.1177/002224298204600109
papers.summary: This foundational study proposed that product preference can be classically conditioned by pairing a product with pleasant or unpleasant music during a single exposure. The original analysis, after excluding participants with deviant musical taste, reported a significant effect on choice behavior, with a log odds ratio of 2.67.

papers.title: Vermeulen and Beukeboom 2015
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2022). Nomenclature Abstract for Halalkalibaculum (Wu et al. 2022) Wu et al. 2022. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/nm.42863
papers.summary: This replication attempt conducted two experiments, both finding a significant effect of music on choice after excluding participants with deviant musical taste. Experiment 1 (N=132) yielded a log OR of 0.79, and Experiment 2 found a log OR of 0.70. While statistically significant, both obtained effect sizes were reliably smaller than the original log OR of 2.67 reported by Gorn.

papers.title: Do We Follow Others When We Should? A Simple Test of Rational Expectations
papers.apa: Weizsäcker, G. (2010). Do We Follow Others when We Should? A Simple Test of Rational Expectations. American Economic Review, 100(5), 2340–2360. https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.100.5.2340
papers.summary: This foundational paper analyzed a meta-dataset of 13 social learning experiments to test rational expectations. It found that participants frequently erred by not following others when it was empirically optimal, leading to foregone earnings. Specifically, individuals only contradicted their private signal when the odds of it being wrong were greater than 2:1, rather than the 1:1 ratio predicted by rational expectations, strongly rejecting the null hypothesis of rational behavior.

papers.title: Ziegelmeyer et al. 2013
papers.apa: Ziegelmeyer, A., March, C., & Krügel, S. (2013). Do We Follow Others when We Should? A Simple Test of Rational Expectations: Comment. American Economic Review, 103(6), 2633–2642. https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.103.6.2633
papers.summary: This comment paper re-evaluated the methodology of Weizsäcker (2010) by introducing an improved measure for the empirical payoff of actions, using true values of information parameters instead of estimates. While their analysis confirmed that rational expectations are indeed violated in social learning, they found that the observed deviations from rational behavior were statistically significantly smaller than those reported in the original study. This suggests that participants are moderately more successful in learning from others than initially concluded.

papers.effect_name: Decreased sense of free will reduces personal responsibility
papers.title: The value of believing in free will: Encouraging a belief in determinism increases cheating
papers.apa: Vohs, K. D., & Schooler, J. W. (2008). The Value of Believing in Free Will. Psychological Science, 19(1), 49–54. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2008.02045.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper investigated whether inducing a belief in determinism (vs. free will) would increase cheating. Across two experiments, participants exposed to deterministic messages showed increased cheating behavior on tasks, with Experiment 1 (N unspecified) showing this effect was mediated by a decreased belief in free will. The original effect size for the cheating behavior was reported as d=0.88.

papers.effect_name: Decreased sense of free will reduces personal responsibility
papers.title: Buttrick et al. 2020
papers.apa: Buttrick, N. R., Aczel, B., Aeschbach, L. F., Bakos, B. E., Brühlmann, F., Claypool, H. M., Hüffmeier, J., Kovacs, M., Schuepfer, K., Szecsi, P., Szuts, A., Szöke, O., Thomae, M., Torka, A.-K., Walker, R. J., & Wood, M. J. (2020). Many Labs 5: Registered Replication of Vohs and Schooler (2008), Experiment 1. Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science, 3(3), 429–438. https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245920917931
papers.summary: This Many Labs 5 registered replication (N=621) closely replicated Vohs and Schooler (2008), Experiment 1, and also tested a revised protocol with an easier manipulation and improved free-will measure. The study found no differences in cheating behavior between conditions (d = 0.076, 95% CI = [−0.082, 0.22]), and free-will beliefs were unchanged by the manipulations (d = 0.064). The authors concluded that the effect did not replicate, even with methodological improvements.

papers.effect_name: Decreased sense of free will reduces personal responsibility
papers.title: Embley et al. 2015
papers.summary: This replication attempt found no significant differences in cheating behavior between experimental conditions designed to manipulate belief in free will. The reported effect size was d = 0.20 [−0.33, 0.74], p = .44, indicating no evidence for the original effect.

papers.title: Unrealistic optimism about future life events
papers.apa: Weinstein, N. D. (1980). Unrealistic optimism about future life events. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 39(5), 806–820. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.39.5.806
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced and investigated unrealistic optimism, finding that 258 college students consistently rated their own chances of experiencing positive events as above average (M=15.4%, d=0.43) and negative events as below average (M=-20.4%, d=0.87). The studies supported predictions that desirability, perceived probability, personal experience, controllability, and stereotype salience influenced the bias. The authors proposed that this bias stems from individuals focusing on factors that improve their own chances while neglecting others' similar advantages.

papers.title: Klein and Helweg-Larsen 2002
papers.apa: Klein, C. T. F., & Helweg-Larsen, M. (2002). Perceived Control and the Optimistic Bias: A Meta-Analytic Review. Psychology & Health, 17(4), 437–446. https://doi.org/10.1080/0887044022000004920
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 27 independent samples quantified the relationship between optimistic bias and perceived control, finding a robust average effect size of d=0.64 (r=0.31). The analysis revealed that this relationship was significantly moderated by participant nationality (larger in US), student status (larger in students), risk status, and the type of optimistic bias measure used. The authors discussed these findings in the context of primary versus secondary control and suggested directions for future research on causality.

papers.title: Maksim et al. 2022
papers.apa: Maksim, A., Śpiewak, S., Lipp, N., Dużmańska-Misiarczyk, N., Gustaw, G., Rębilas, K., & Strojny, P. (2022). Unrealistic optimism in the eye of the storm: Positive bias towards the consequences of COVID-19 during the second and third waves of the pandemic. https://doi.org/10.1101/2022.05.10.22274918
papers.summary: This research investigated the persistence of unrealistic optimism (UO) regarding COVID-19 during the second and third waves of the pandemic in Poland. Study 1 (d=0.34) and Study 2 (d=0.37) found that UO towards contracting COVID-19 persisted, but participants did not show UO for events over which they had little influence, with an exception for those who personally knew someone who died from the virus. The findings are discussed in terms of self-esteem protection and psychological threat reduction.

papers.title: Shepperd et al. 1996
papers.apa: Shepperd, J. A., Ouellette, J. A., & Fernandez, J. K. (1996). Abandoning unrealistic optimism: Performance estimates and the temporal proximity of self-relevant feedback. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 70(4), 844–855. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.70.4.844
papers.summary: This series of three studies examined how unrealistic optimism changes as individuals approach self-relevant feedback. Study 1 found that only college seniors became less optimistic about their post-graduation salaries as graduation neared, while underclassmen remained optimistic. Study 2 demonstrated a shift from optimism (1 month prior) to accuracy (after exam) and then to pessimism (seconds prior to feedback) for exam score predictions (e.g., d=0.63 initially, d=0.16 reversed at the end). Study 3 showed that low self-esteem individuals lowered their performance estimates more readily, suggesting a defensive mechanism to brace for potential negative outcomes.

papers.title: The MPG Illusion

papers.summary: This foundational paper, through three experiments (N=77, N=74, N=171), demonstrated that people systematically misunderstand the curvilinear relationship between MPG and fuel consumption. Participants consistently applied linear reasoning, leading them to undervalue fuel savings from improving inefficient vehicles. The study also showed that framing fuel efficiency in gallons per 100 miles (GPM) significantly reduced this 'MPG illusion,' with 64% choosing the more fuel-efficient option in the GPM frame compared to 25% in the MPG frame.

papers.summary: This paper aimed to verify the MPG illusion, specifically when fuel efficiency is expressed in kilometers per liter. The study found evidence supporting the illusion, reporting an odds ratio (OR) of 2.09 for the experimental manipulation scenario involving a choice between two programs, indicating a replication of the effect.

papers.title: Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision Under Risk

papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced Prospect Theory as an alternative to expected utility theory, highlighting systematic deviations in decision-making under risk. It defined the certainty effect as the tendency to underweight probable outcomes compared to certain ones, contributing to risk aversion for gains and risk seeking for losses. The authors demonstrated these effects using hypothetical choice problems, with the first certainty effect demonstration showing a Log OR = -1.36.

The study confirmed that the original findings, including the certainty effect (meta-analytic Log OR = -1.72), replicated in the vast majority of analyses (over 80% for individual analyses, 90% for theoretical contrasts). While effect sizes were generally attenuated compared to the original, the authors found no evidence of replication failure or fundamental flaws in Prospect Theory.

papers.title: Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision Under Risk
papers.apa: Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk. Econometrica, 47(2), 263. https://doi.org/10.2307/1914185
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced Prospect Theory as an alternative to expected utility theory, proposing that individuals evaluate outcomes as gains and losses relative to a reference point and replace objective probabilities with subjective decision weights. It posited that decision weights are generally lower than corresponding probabilities, except in the range of low probabilities, leading to the overweighting of small probabilities, which can explain behaviors like gambling and insurance purchases. The theory was developed based on responses to hypothetical choice problems from students and university faculty.

While 77% of effect sizes were smaller than in the original study, the authors concluded there was no fundamental failure to replicate Prospect Theory. Specifically for overweighting small probabilities, 3 out of 4 demonstrations replicated.

For the 'Positive Affect Increases Patience' effect, their replication yielded an unstandardized coefficient (b) of -0.057, providing no evidence for the original effect and suggesting a direction opposite to the original finding.

papers.summary: This foundational study investigated cooperation in repeated prisoner's dilemma games with noisy action implementation. It found that subjects cooperated more in treatments where cooperation was an equilibrium, and that 'lenient' and 'forgiving' strategies (not retaliating immediately, and returning to cooperation after punishment) were successful and yielded higher payoffs under these conditions. The study highlights the role of uncertainty in promoting forgiveness and cooperation.

It posits that individuals simplify complex choices by focusing only on the unique components of options, disregarding those shared across all prospects. This selective attention can lead to preference reversals and violations of expected utility theory.

papers.summary: This paper describes an empirical audit and review, a method for assessing evidentiary value, applied to 20 studies on the psychological consequences of scarcity. The authors report considerable variability in replicability across these studies, with some strong successes and some failures. While the abstract does not detail specific findings for each replicated study, the provided context indicates that for the 'overborrowing' effect, this replication effort yielded an effect size of r = .139, which is consistent with the original finding.

papers.title: Spending on daughters versus sons in economic recessions
papers.apa: Durante, K. M., Griskevicius, V., Redden, J. P., & Edward White, A. (2015). Spending on Daughters versus Sons in Economic Recessions. Journal of Consumer Research, 42(3), 435–457. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucv023
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that poor economic conditions lead parents to favor daughters over sons in resource allocation, such as bequests and beneficial expenditures. Across five experiments, including one with N=629, the authors found evidence supporting this "scarcity effect," with a meta-analytic effect size of d=0.31 (p=.001) for the overall composite, mediated by perceptions of reproductive value.

papers.title: O’Donnell et al. 2021
papers.apa: O’Donnell, M., Dev, A. S., Antonoplis, S., Baum, S. M., Benedetti, A. H., Brown, N. D., Carrillo, B., Choi, A. L., Connor, P., Donnelly, K., Ellwood-Lowe, M. Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: As part of a large-scale empirical audit of scarcity research, O’Donnell et al. conducted replications of 20 studies, including the resource allocation effect. Their replication attempt for this specific effect found an effect size of r = -0.082, which is in the opposite direction and substantially smaller than the original finding, suggesting a failure to replicate. The audit revealed variable replicability across the broader scarcity literature.

papers.title: Squeezed: Coping with constraint through efficiency and prioritization
papers.apa: Fernbach, P. M., Kan, C., & Lynch, J. G. (2014). Squeezed: Coping with Constraint through Efficiency and Prioritization. Journal of Consumer Research, 41(5), 1204–1227. https://doi.org/10.1086/679118
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduces the distinction between efficiency and priority planning as coping mechanisms for resource constraint. It proposes that as perceived constraint increases, consumers are more likely to engage in priority planning, which involves sacrificing less important goals to achieve greater savings. The study also suggests that budgeting can mitigate dysfunctional behaviors associated with insufficient prioritization.

papers.title: O’Donnell et al. 2021
papers.apa: O’Donnell, M., Dev, A. S., Antonoplis, S., Baum, S. M., Benedetti, A. H., Brown, N. D., Carrillo, B., Choi, A. L., Connor, P., Donnelly, K., Ellwood-Lowe, M. Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: This paper is part of a large-scale empirical audit and review, replicating 20 studies on the psychological consequences of scarcity. For the specific "scarcity effect - planning," their replication attempt found a substantially smaller effect size (r = 0.036) compared to the original (r = 0.266). The authors noted considerable variability in replicability across the studies they examined, with some successes and some failures.

papers.title: The dark side of scarcity promotions: how exposure to limited-quantity promotions can induce aggression
papers.apa: Kristofferson, K., McFerran, B., Morales, A. C., & Dahl, D. W. (2016). The Dark Side of Scarcity Promotions: How Exposure to Limited-Quantity Promotions Can Induce Aggression. Journal of Consumer Research, ucw056. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucw056
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposes that limited-quantity promotions induce aggression by activating perceived competitive threat and physiological arousal. Across seven studies, including one with 140 undergraduates, the authors found that exposure to scarcity ads led to increased aggressive behaviors (e.g., firing more bullets, MScarcity = 42.3 vs MControl = 37.0, F(1, 138) = 4.02, p = .047) and elevated testosterone levels. The original effect size for the aggression measure was reported as r = .157.

papers.title: O’Donnell et al. 2021

Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: This paper presents an empirical audit and review of research on the psychological consequences of scarcity, replicating 20 studies. For the specific effect of scarcity promotions inducing aggression, the replication attempt found no evidence of the effect, reporting a correlation of r = -.004. The authors noted considerable variability in replicability across the audited studies.

papers.title: Did they earn it? Observing unearned luxury consumption decreases brand attitude when observers value fairness
papers.apa: Lee, S., Baumgartner, H., & Winterich, K. P. (2018). Did They Earn It? Observing Unearned Luxury Consumption Decreases Brand Attitude When Observers Value Fairness. Journal of Consumer Psychology, 28(3), 412–436. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/jcpy.1028
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposes that observing luxury consumption funded by unearned resources negatively impacts observers' attitudes towards the brand, especially for those who highly value fairness. The authors suggest this occurs because unearned wealth diminishes the perceived prestige of the consumer, which then lowers the perceived prestige and evaluation of the luxury brand. The original effect size reported was r = .128.

papers.title: O’Donnell et al. 2021
papers.apa: O’Donnell, M., Dev, A. S., Antonoplis, S., Baum, S. M., Benedetti, A. H., Brown, N. D., Carrillo, B., Choi, A. L., Connor, P., Donnelly, K., Ellwood-Lowe, M. E., Foushee, R., Jansen, R., Jarvis, S. N., Lundell-Creagh, R., Ocampo, J. M., Okafor, G. N., Azad, Z. R., Rosenblum, M., … Nelson, L. D. (2021). Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: This paper is part of a large-scale empirical audit and review of research on the psychological consequences of scarcity, which included a replication attempt of the 'Did they earn it?' effect. The replication reported an effect size of r = .043, which was substantially smaller than the original r = .128. The authors concluded that the specific reported effects from the original study were not replicated, though a non-reported effect remained non-significant.

papers.title: Creating when you have less: The impact of resource scarcity on product use creativity
papers.apa: Mehta, R., & Zhu, M. (2015). Creating When You Have Less: The Impact of Resource Scarcity on Product Use Creativity. Journal of Consumer Research, 42(5), 767–782. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucv051
papers.summary: The foundational paper by Mehta and Zhu (2015) proposed that making resource scarcity salient enhances product use creativity by activating a constraint mindset that reduces functional fixedness. Across six studies, including one with 95 undergraduate students (Experiment 1), they consistently found that scarcity led to more novel product usages. For example, in Experiment 1, participants in the scarcity condition created toy prototypes judged significantly more novel (M=3.72) than those in abundance (M=3.04, d=0.65) or control (M=3.17, d=0.51) conditions.

papers.title: O’Donnell et al. 2021

Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: O’Donnell et al. (2021) conducted an empirical audit and review of research on the psychological consequences of scarcity, which included a replication attempt of the product use creativity effect. While the provided abstract describes a broader audit of 20 studies, the prompt indicates their replication of this specific effect yielded an effect size of r = -0.092. This finding suggests a lack of support for the original scarcity effect on product use creativity.

papers.title: Eliciting time versus money: time scarcity underlies asymmetric wage rates
papers.apa: Monga, A., May, F., & Bagchi, R. (2017). Eliciting Time versus Money: Time Scarcity Underlies Asymmetric Wage Rates. Journal of Consumer Research, 44(4), 833–852. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucx066
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduces the "wage-rate asymmetry," proposing that implied hourly wages are higher when elicited via a Time-Elicit (TEL) procedure compared to a Money-Elicit (MEL) procedure, due to increased weighting of time scarcity. Across eight studies, including two detailed here (N=76, N=186), the authors consistently found higher wage rates in the TEL condition (e.g., Cohen's d=0.54 and d=0.75), supporting their hypothesis.

papers.title: O’Donnell et al. 2021

E., Foushee, R., Jansen, R., Jarvis, S. N., Lundell-Creagh, R., Ocampo, J. M., Okafor, G. N., Azad, Z. R., Rosenblum, M., … Nelson, L. D. (2021). Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: This paper is part of a large-scale empirical audit and review of scarcity research, which included a replication attempt of the wage-rate asymmetry effect. While the general abstract does not detail specific findings for this effect, the prompt indicates that their replication found a much smaller effect size (r = 0.017) compared to the original (r = 0.216), suggesting a failure to replicate the original finding.

papers.title: On the psychology of scarcity: When reminders of resource scarcity promote selfish (and generous) behavior
papers.apa: Roux, C., Goldsmith, K., & Bonezzi, A. (2015). On the Psychology of Scarcity: When Reminders of Resource Scarcity Promote Selfish (and Generous) Behavior. Journal of Consumer Research, ucv048. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucv048
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposes that reminders of resource scarcity activate a competitive orientation, leading individuals to prioritize their own welfare. The authors demonstrate this through several experiments, including one (Experiment 1, N=71) showing scarcity primes increased accessibility of competition-related concepts. The paper discusses findings (e.g., Experiment 4) where scarcity led to selfish behavior, particularly pronounced among individuals with a proself orientation, with an original effect size of r = 0.168 for this specific interaction.

E., Foushee, R., Jansen, R., Jarvis, S. N., Lundell-Creagh, R., Ocampo, J. M., Okafor, G. N., Azad, Z. R., Rosenblum, M., … Nelson, L. D. (2021). Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: This paper presents an empirical audit and review of research on the psychological consequences of scarcity, conducting replications of 20 studies. While some studies showed successful replication, the specific finding regarding scarcity promoting selfish behavior in individuals with low social value orientation was not replicated. The replication attempt reported a very small effect size of r = 0.008, indicating a failure to find evidence for the original effect.

papers.title: Seeking lasting enjoyment with limited money: Financial constraints increase preference for material goods over experiences
papers.apa: Tully, S. M., Hershfield, H. E., & Meyvis, T. (2015). Seeking Lasting Enjoyment with Limited Money: Financial Constraints Increase Preference for Material Goods over Experiences. Journal of Consumer Research, 42(1), 59–75. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucv007
papers.summary: This foundational paper, across seven studies, proposes that financial constraints increase consumers' preference for material goods over experiences. The authors argue this shift is driven by an increased concern for the lasting utility or longevity of purchases. For example, one study with N=220 found that greater consideration of financial constraints correlated with a stronger preference for material goods (b=0.18, p=.008), and this relationship was mediated by concern for longevity.

Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: This paper is part of a large-scale empirical audit replicating 20 studies on the psychological consequences of scarcity. While the overall audit found variable replicability across different effects, for the specific effect of financial constraints increasing preference for material goods, the replication reported an effect size of r = 0.136, indicating a successful replication of the original finding.

papers.title: Scarcity polarizes preferences: The impact on choice among multiple items in a product class
papers.apa: Zhu, M., & Ratner, R. K. (2015). Scarcity Polarizes Preferences: The Impact on Choice among Multiple Items in a Product Class. Journal of Marketing Research, 52(1), 13–26. https://doi.org/10.1509/jmr.13.0451
papers.summary: This research proposes that perceived scarcity increases the choice share of the most-preferred item in a product class by inducing arousal, which polarizes evaluations of items in the choice set. Across five experiments, the authors found that scarcity broadened the discrepancy between favorite and nonfavorite items, leading to a greater choice share for the favorite. The findings supported the arousal-based explanation, showing mediation by reported arousal and moderation by induced arousal.

Empirical audit and review and an assessment of evidentiary value in research on the psychological consequences of scarcity. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 118(44). https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.2103313118
papers.summary: This paper presents an empirical audit and review of research on the psychological consequences of scarcity, including replications of 20 studies. While the overall audit found variability in replicability across different scarcity effects, for the specific effect of scarcity on preference polarization, the replication reported a negligible effect size (r = .002) compared to the original (r = .138). This suggests a failure to replicate the preference polarization aspect of the scarcity effect.

papers.title: Super size me: product size as a signal of status
papers.apa: Retraction of: Super Size Me: Product Size as a Signal of Status. (2023). Journal of Consumer Research, 51(2), 441–441. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucad055
papers.summary: The foundational paper proposed that individuals with low power choose larger product sizes as a way to signal status. Study 1 reported large effect sizes, for example, a difference of d=1.49 for small vs. large product sizes, suggesting a strong effect.

papers.title: Tunca et al 2022
papers.apa: Tunca, B., Ziano, I., & Wenting, X. (2022). Super-Size Me: An Unsuccessful Preregistered Replication of the Effect of Product Size on Status Signaling. Meta-Psychology, 6. https://doi.org/10.15626/mp.2020.2538
papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the "Product size and status" effect. Across multiple comparisons, the study found very small effect sizes (e.g., d=0.09 for small vs. large product size), with confidence intervals often including zero, indicating no significant evidence for the original effect.

papers.title: ‘Less is better: When low-value options are valued more highly than high-value options
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the 'less-is-better' effect, demonstrating that people can value a smaller quantity of a good more highly than a larger quantity when items are evaluated separately. For example, participants valued a 7oz ice cream serving in an overfilled cup more than an 8oz serving in an underfilled cup. The proposed mechanism is that separate evaluation leads to a focus on a salient positive attribute (like fullness) rather than objective quantity.

papers.title: Klein et al. 2018
papers.apa: Klein, R. A., Vianello, M., Hasselman, F., Adams, B. G., Adams, R. B., Alper, S., Aveyard, M., Axt, J. R., Babalola, M. T., Bahník, Š., Batra, R., Berkics, M., Bernstein, M. J., Berry, D. R., Bialobrzeska, O., Binan, E. D., Bocian, K., Brandt, M. J., Busching, R., … Nosek, B. A. (2018). Many Labs 2: Investigating Variation in Replicability Across Samples and Settings. Advances in Methods and Practices in Psychological Science, 1(4), 443–490. https://doi.org/10.1177/2515245918810225
papers.summary: This paper, 'Many Labs 2,' conducted preregistered replications of 28 findings across 15,305 participants from 36 countries. Overall, 54% of replications showed statistically significant effects in the same direction as the original, but 75% yielded smaller effect sizes (median d=0.15 for replications vs. 0.60 for originals). Specifically for the 'less-is-better' effect (dictionary vs. thesaurus condition), the replication found no significant effect (meta-analytic d = -0.01, 95% CI [-0.07, 0.05]), indicating a failure to replicate.

papers.title: Vonasch et al 2023
papers.summary: This paper presents a direct replication attempt of the 'less-is-better' effect, specifically targeting the original findings by Hsee (1998). The study failed to replicate the effect, finding no significant preference for the smaller, visually 'better' option over the larger, objectively 'better' option. This contributes to the growing evidence questioning the robustness of the less-is-better phenomenon.

papers.title: Penny Wise and Pound Foolish: The Left-Digit Effect in Price Cognition
papers.apa: Thomas, M., & Morwitz, V. (2004). Penny wise and pound foolish: The left digit effect in price encoding [dataset]. In PsycEXTRA Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/e621312012-043
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the left-digit effect, demonstrating how a change in the leftmost digit of a price (e.g., from $3.00 to $2.99) disproportionately influences consumers' perception of price magnitude, quality, and response times. Across multiple studies, the authors found significant effects (e.g., η² ranging from .08 to .41) where prices ending in .99 were perceived as substantially lower or of different quality than numerically close prices ending in .00 or .01, particularly when the left digit changed.

papers.title: Bhattacharya et al. 2012
papers.apa: Bhattacharya, U., Holden, C. W., & Jacobsen, S. (2012). Penny Wise, Dollar Foolish: Buy–Sell Imbalances On and Around Round Numbers. Management Science, 58(2), 413–431. https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.1110.1364
papers.summary: This study investigated left-digit bias in stock trading, analyzing over 100 million transactions. It found excess buying by liquidity demanders at prices one penny below round numbers (e.g., $X.99) and excess selling one penny above (e.g., $X.01), with imbalances monotonic to the roundness of the adjacent number. Regression coefficients for buy-sell imbalances ranged from b=0.270 to b=1.493, supporting the influence of round numbers as cognitive reference points.

papers.title: Lacetera et al. 2012
papers.apa: Lacetera, N., Pope, D. G., & Sydnor, J. R. (2012). Heuristic Thinking and Limited Attention in the Car Market. American Economic Review, 102(5), 2206–2236. https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.102.5.2206
papers.summary: Analyzing over 22 million wholesale used-car transactions, this paper found evidence of left-digit bias in odometer readings. They observed discontinuous drops in car sale prices at 10,000-mile odometer thresholds (average -$157 and -$173) and smaller drops at 1,000-mile thresholds, indicating that buyers focus on the leftmost digits. The findings suggest that information-processing heuristics influence even high-stakes markets.

papers.apa: Bhattacharya, U., Holden, C. W., & Jacobsen, S. (2012). Penny Wise, Dollar Foolish: Buy–Sell Imbalances On and Around Round Numbers. Management Science, 58(2), 413–431. https://doi.org/10.1287/mnsc.1110.1364

papers.title: Manning and Sprott 2009
papers.apa: Mate Guarding. (2021). Encyclopedia of Evolutionary Psychological Science, 4842–4842. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-19650-3_303017
papers.summary: This research explored the left-digit effect on consumer choices and perceived price differences. Across multiple studies, the authors found that price endings significantly influenced product choice and the perceived magnitude of price differences (e.g., β = -.32). These findings corroborate the left-digit bias, showing its impact on how consumers evaluate and select products based on price presentation.

papers.title: Sokolova et al. 2020
papers.apa: Lacetera, N., Pope, D. G., & Sydnor, J. R. (2012). Heuristic Thinking and Limited Attention in the Car Market. American Economic Review, 102(5), 2206–2236. https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.102.5.2206

papers.title: Some functional effects of sectioning the cerebral commissures in man
papers.apa: Gazzaniga, M. S., Bogen, J. E., & Sperry, R. W. (1962). Some functional effects of sectioning the cerebral commissures in man*. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 48(10), 1765–1769. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.48.10.1765
papers.summary: This foundational paper describes early observations of patients who underwent corpus callosotomy, demonstrating the functional independence of the cerebral hemispheres. It highlighted phenomena such as the inability to verbally report stimuli presented to the right hemisphere and the independent control of the left hand by the right hemisphere, laying the groundwork for the split-brain concept.

papers.title: de Haan et al. 2020
papers.apa: de Haan, E. H. F., Corballis, P. M., Hillyard, S. A., Marzi, C. A., Seth, A., Lamme, V. A. F., Volz, L., Fabri, M., Schechter, E., Bayne, T., Corballis, M., & Pinto, Y. (2020). Split-Brain: What We Know Now and Why This is Important for Understanding Consciousness. Neuropsychology Review, 30(2), 224–233. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11065-020-09439-3
papers.summary: This paper suggests that the existing body of evidence is insufficient to definitively answer the question of unity of mind in split-brain patients. It notes that different theories of consciousness make varying predictions and calls for further investigation into this complex phenomenon.

papers.title: Pinto et al. 2017
papers.apa: Pinto, Y., de Haan, E. H. F., & Lamme, V. A. F. (2017). The Split-Brain Phenomenon Revisited: A Single Conscious Agent with Split Perception. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 21(11), 835–851. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tics.2017.09.003
papers.summary: This is a duplicate entry for the paper 'Pinto et al., 2017' with DOI 10.1093/brain/aww358. Please refer to the detailed summary for that entry.

papers.title: Pinto et al., 2017
papers.apa: Pinto, Y., Neville, D. A., Otten, M., Corballis, P. M., Lamme, V. A. F., de Haan, E. H. F., Foschi, N., & Fabri, M. (2017). Split brain: divided perception but undivided consciousness. Brain, aww358. https://doi.org/10.1093/brain/aww358
papers.summary: This study with two split-brain patients replicated the standard finding that stimuli cannot be compared across visual half-fields, indicating independent hemispheric processing. However, it challenged the interpretation of two independent conscious perceivers, showing patients had full awareness and recognition across the entire visual field regardless of response type (left hand, right hand, verbal), even on high-confidence trials. The authors concluded that severing cortical connections splits visual perception but maintains undivided consciousness, posing a challenge to theories like Global Workspace and Integrated Information Theory.
