papers.title: Hawks 2007
papers.apa: Barbarotto, E., Schmittgen, T. D., & Calin, G. A. (2007). MicroRNAs and cancer: Profile, profile, profile. International Journal of Cancer, 122(5), 969–977. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ijc.23343
papers.summary: This paper critically examined reported cases of high-functioning hydrocephalic individuals, arguing that the evidence does not support significant gray matter tissue loss. Hawks suggested that a 'thin' cortex does not necessarily equate to functionally small cortical volume, even with substantial white matter loss, thus questioning the premise of the effect.

papers.title: Gwern 2019
papers.summary: This analysis critically reviewed prominent case studies of high-functioning hydrocephalic individuals, concluding that many were unverifiable, potentially fraudulent, or involved individuals with lower-than-claimed intelligence (e.g., Feuillet et al.'s case with IQ 75). The author questioned whether high-functioning cases truly exhibit less brain mass, suggesting that lower brain volume proxies might be misleading.

papers.title: Neuroskeptic 2015
papers.apa: Forsdyke, D. R. (2015). Wittgenstein’s Certainty is Uncertain: Brain Scans of Cured Hydrocephalics Challenge Cherished Assumptions. Biological Theory, 10(4), 336–342. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13752-015-0219-x
papers.summary: This critique argued that while brain scans of hydrocephalic individuals might show dramatic 'holes,' the crucial grey matter of the cerebral cortex often appears largely intact and correctly positioned. The author highlighted the lack of detailed post-mortem studies to confirm actual tissue loss, suggesting that visual impressions of severe brain reduction might be misleading regarding functional capacity.

papers.title: Hirnpotentialänderungen bei Willkürbewegungen und passiven Bewegungen des Menschen: Bereitschaftspotential und reafferente Potentiale
papers.apa: Kornhuber, H. H., & Deecke, L. (1965). Hirnpotential�nderungen bei Willk�rbewegungen und passiven Bewegungen des Menschen: Bereitschaftspotential und reafferente Potentiale. Pfl�gers Archiv F�r Die Gesamte Physiologie Des Menschen Und Der Tiere, 284(1), 1–17. https://doi.org/10.1007/bf00412364
papers.summary: This foundational paper describes a method for analyzing bio-electrical phenomena preceding 'spontaneous' events. Using this, the authors identified a slowly increasing surface-negative cortical potential (10-15 µV), termed the 'readiness potential,' preceding voluntary hand or foot movements in 12 healthy subjects. This potential was maximal over the contralateral precentral region and increased with intentional engagement, suggesting it reflects pre-movement neural activity.

papers.title: Alexander et al. 2016
papers.apa: Alexander, P., Schlegel, A., Sinnott-Armstrong, W., Roskies, A. L., Wheatley, T., & Tse, P. U. (2016). Readiness potentials driven by non-motoric processes. Consciousness and Cognition, 39, 38–47. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.concog.2015.11.011
papers.summary: This paper suggests that the neural activity observed as readiness potentials may not solely reflect motor preparation but could instead be associated with cognitive processes like attention or decision-making. This challenges the traditional interpretation of RPs as direct indicators of pre-conscious motor planning.

papers.title: Fried et al. 2011
papers.apa: Fried, I., Mukamel, R., & Kreiman, G. (2011). Internally Generated Preactivation of Single Neurons in Human Medial Frontal Cortex Predicts Volition. Neuron, 69(3), 548–562. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2010.11.045
papers.summary: This study replicated the observation of readiness potentials preceding voluntary movements. While specific findings or effect sizes are not detailed in the provided text, the paper generally supports the existence of this pre-movement neural activity.

papers.title: Libet et al. (1964/1983)
papers.apa: LIBET, B., GLEASON, C. A., WRIGHT, E. W., & PEARL, D. K. (1983). TIME OF CONSCIOUS INTENTION TO ACT IN RELATION TO ONSET OF CEREBRAL ACTIVITY (READINESS-POTENTIAL). Brain, 106(3), 623–642. https://doi.org/10.1093/brain/106.3.623
papers.summary: Libet and colleagues conducted experiments measuring RPs alongside the conscious timing of an intention to act. They famously found that RPs began several hundred milliseconds before participants reported their conscious intention to move, suggesting that brain activity initiates voluntary action before conscious awareness. This work profoundly influenced the debate on free will.

papers.title: McGilchrist 2012
papers.summary: This paper raises questions about the direct link between readiness potentials and motor preparation, proposing that the observed neural activity might instead reflect cognitive processes such as attention or decision-making. This offers an alternative interpretation to the traditional understanding of RPs.

papers.title: Travers et al. 2020
papers.apa: Travers, E., Friedemann, M., & Haggard, P. (2020). The Readiness Potential reflects planning-based expectation, not uncertainty, in the timing of action. Cognitive Neuroscience, 12(1), 14–27. https://doi.org/10.1080/17588928.2020.1824176
papers.summary: This study investigated whether the Readiness Potential (RP) reflects freedom from external constraint or planning/expectation in voluntary actions. Using a reinforcement learning paradigm with 19 participants, they found that RP amplitudes increased as participants learned the optimal time to act, becoming more certain and preplanned. This suggests the RP reflects anticipatory planning and preparation for action, rather than merely the absence of external cues or uncertainty.

papers.title: Source
papers.apa: Furby, P. (2004). McGilchrist, Erica. Oxford Art Online. https://doi.org/10.1093/gao/9781884446054.article.t097826
papers.summary: No abstract or extracted text was provided for this paper. It is assumed to be the original source of the broad 'left-brain vs. right-brain' hypothesis, but its specific claims or evidence are not detailed here.

papers.title: Hemispheric dominance in recall and recognition
papers.apa: Zenhausern, R., & Gebhardt, M. (1979). Hemispheric dominance in recall and recognition. Bulletin of the Psychonomic Society, 14(1), 71–73. https://doi.org/10.3758/bf03329403
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated hemispheric dominance in verbal learning, finding that auditory presentation related to left-hemispheric dominance and visual presentation to right-hemispheric dominance in recall and recognition. The authors reported complex interaction effects involving dominance, sex, and input modality, suggesting hemispheric dominance as a meaningful classifying variable, but noted their specific findings required replication.

papers.title: Nielsen et al. 2013
papers.apa: Nielsen, J. A., Zielinski, B. A., Ferguson, M. A., Lainhart, J. E., & Anderson, J. S. (2013). An Evaluation of the Left-Brain vs. Right-Brain Hypothesis with Resting State Functional Connectivity Magnetic Resonance Imaging. PLoS ONE, 8(8), e71275. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0071275
papers.summary: This critique evaluated the left-brain vs. right-brain hypothesis using resting-state fMRI data from 1011 individuals (ages 7-29). The study found that brain lateralization is a local, not global, property of brain networks, and their data were not consistent with a whole-brain phenotype of greater 'left-brained' or 'right-brained' network strength across individuals. They concluded there is no evidence for individuals being consistently left-brain or right-brain dominant.

papers.title: Oxytocin increases trust in humans
papers.apa: Vogt, B., Bengart, P., Declerck, C., & Fehr, E. (2025). Oxytocin increases trust in humans with a low disposition to trust. https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.10.01.679711
papers.summary: This foundational study proposed that intranasal oxytocin administration increases trust in humans. In a trust game with N=58 participants (29 per group), 45% of the oxytocin group showed maximal trust compared to 21% in the placebo group, suggesting a substantial increase in trusting behavior.

papers.title: Declerck et al. 2020
papers.apa: Declerck, C. H., Boone, C., Pauwels, L., Vogt, B., & Fehr, E. (2020). A registered replication study on oxytocin and trust. Nature Human Behaviour, 4(6), 646–655. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-020-0878-x
papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the effect of oxytocin on trust in a minimal social contact condition. The study found no support for the hypothesis that oxytocin increases trust, reporting a beta of -0.136, suggesting a null or even negative effect.

papers.title: Lane et al. 2015
papers.apa: Lane, A., Mikolajczak, M., Treinen, E., Samson, D., Corneille, O., de Timary, P., & Luminet, O. (2015). Failed Replication of Oxytocin Effects on Trust: The Envelope Task Case. PLOS ONE, 10(9), e0137000. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0137000
papers.summary: This paper presents two failed replications of oxytocin's effect on trust using the 'Envelope Task' paradigm. Study 1 (N=95) and Study 2 (N=61) both found no significant effect of oxytocin on trust, despite sufficient power to detect the originally reported large effect. The authors conclude that a large effect of oxytocin on trust in this paradigm is unlikely, suggesting either a null or very small effect.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between behavioural activation and white matter integrity
papers.title: White matter integrity and behavioral activation in healthy subjects
papers.apa: Xu, J., Kober, H., Carroll, K. M., Rounsaville, B. J., Pearlson, G. D., & Potenza, M. N. (2011). White matter integrity and behavioral activation in healthy subjects. Human Brain Mapping, 33(4), 994–1002. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/hbm.21275
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the relationship between white matter integrity and behavioral activation in 51 healthy participants using DTI and the BIS/BAS scale. It reported positive correlations between BAS-Fun scores and fractional anisotropy in the left corona radiata/superior longitudinal fasciculus (r=.52) and mean diffusivity in the left inferior longitudinal fasciculus/inferior fronto-occipital fasciculus (r=.51). The authors suggested that white matter integrity in these regions is associated with individual differences in motivation for seeking fun and novel experiences.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between behavioural activation and white matter integrity
papers.title: Corrigendum to “A purely confirmatory replication study of structural brain-behavior correlations” [Cortex 66 (2015) 115–133]
papers.apa: Keuken, M. C., Ly, A., Boekel, W., Wagenmakers, E.-J., Belay, L., Verhagen, J., Brown, S. D., & Forstmann, B. U. (2017). Corrigendum to “A purely confirmatory replication study of structural brain-behavior correlations” [Cortex 66 (2015) 115–133]. Cortex, 93, 229–233. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cortex.2017.03.007
papers.summary: This corrigendum addresses a data processing error in the previous replication study (Boekel et al., 2015) concerning structural brain-behavior correlations. After re-analyzing DWI data from 34-35 subjects due to an incorrectly included extra volume, the authors confirmed that their original conclusions regarding the failure to replicate the structural brain-behavior correlations, including those related to BAS-Fun scores, remained unchanged. Bayesian hypothesis tests continued to show evidence for the null hypothesis, with effect size estimates substantially lower than the original studies (e.g., BAS-Fun correlations ranging from r=-.15 to r=.05).

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between behavioural activation and white matter integrity
papers.title: A purely confirmatory replication study of structural brain-behavior correlations
papers.apa: Boekel, W., Wagenmakers, E.-J., Belay, L., Verhagen, J., Brown, S., & Forstmann, B. U. (2015). A purely confirmatory replication study of structural brain-behavior correlations. Cortex, 66, 115–133. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cortex.2014.11.019
papers.summary: This preregistered replication study aimed to investigate the replicability of five structural brain-behavior (SBB) correlation studies, including the one by Xu et al. (2011), comprising 17 effects in total. Using a purely confirmatory approach with N=35 subjects for the Xu et al. replication, the authors found that for all but one of the 17 effects, Bayesian hypothesis tests indicated evidence in favor of the null hypothesis. Effect size estimates were substantially lower than in the original studies, suggesting a widespread attenuation of effects and a failure to replicate the SBB correlations.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain behaviour correlations - the association between social network size and grey matter volume
papers.title: Online social network size is reflected in human brain structure
papers.apa: Kanai, R., Bahrami, B., Roylance, R., & Rees, G. (2011). Online social network size is reflected in human brain structure. Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences, 279(1732), 1327–1334. https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2011.1959
papers.summary: Kanai et al. (2011) investigated the neural correlates of online social network size, finding that the number of Facebook friends correlated positively with grey matter density in the right superior temporal sulcus, left middle temporal gyrus, and entorhinal cortex. They also reported correlations with amygdala grey matter for both online and real-world social network sizes. The study reported effect sizes (r) between 0.30 and 0.35 for the key regions.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain behaviour correlations - the association between social network size and grey matter volume
papers.title: Boekel et al. 2015
papers.apa: Boekel, W., Wagenmakers, E.-J., Belay, L., Verhagen, J., Brown, S., & Forstmann, B. U. (2015). A purely confirmatory replication study of structural brain-behavior correlations. Cortex, 66, 115–133. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cortex.2014.11.019
papers.summary: Boekel et al. (2015) conducted a preregistered, purely confirmatory replication of five structural brain-behavior correlation studies, including the Kanai et al. (2011) findings. Across 17 effects, they found evidence in favor of the null hypothesis for all but one, with Bayes factors ranging from anecdotal to strong. For the social network size effect, they reported substantially attenuated effect sizes (r = -0.14 to 0.18) compared to the original, concluding a lack of replication.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain behaviour correlations - the association between social network size and grey matter volume
papers.title: Kanai et al. 2012
papers.apa: Kanai, R., Bahrami, B., Roylance, R., & Rees, G. (2011). Online social network size is reflected in human brain structure. Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences, 279(1732), 1327–1334. https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2011.1959
papers.summary: This entry is a duplicate of the foundational Kanai et al. (2011) paper, sharing the exact same DOI and abstract. It describes the original finding that online social network size predicts grey matter density in regions like the superior temporal sulcus, middle temporal gyrus, and entorhinal cortex, with reported correlations (r) between 0.30 and 0.35.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between distractibility and grey matter volume
papers.title: Distractibility in Daily Life Is Reflected in the Structure and Function of Human Parietal Cortex
papers.apa: Kanai, R., Dong, M. Y., Bahrami, B., & Rees, G. (2011). Distractibility in Daily Life Is Reflected in the Structure and Function of Human Parietal Cortex. The Journal of Neuroscience, 31(18), 6620–6626. https://doi.org/10.1523/jneurosci.5864-10.2011
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the neural basis of everyday distractibility in a large sample of healthy adults. It reported a positive correlation between self-reported distractibility and grey matter volume in the left superior parietal cortex (r=.38), and a negative correlation with medial pre-frontal cortex (r=-.28). The authors also found that disrupting this region with TMS increased distractibility, suggesting a causal role.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between distractibility and grey matter volume
papers.title: Boekel et al. 2015
papers.apa: Boekel, W., Wagenmakers, E.-J., Belay, L., Verhagen, J., Brown, S., & Forstmann, B. U. (2015). A purely confirmatory replication study of structural brain-behavior correlations. Cortex, 66, 115–133. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cortex.2014.11.019
papers.summary: This preregistered, purely confirmatory replication study investigated 17 structural brain-behavior correlations, including those from Kanai et al. (2011). For almost all effects, including the distractibility-grey matter correlations, Bayesian hypothesis tests indicated evidence in favor of the null hypothesis. The authors concluded they were unable to successfully replicate these correlations, noting substantially lower effect size estimates (e.g., SPL: r=.22; mPFC: r=-.19) compared to the originals.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between attention and cortical thickness
papers.title: Associations between Regional Associations between regional cortical thickness and attentional networks as measured by the attention network testThickness and Attentional Networks as Measured by the Attention Network Test
papers.apa: Westlye, L. T., Grydeland, H., Walhovd, K. B., & Fjell, A. M. (2010). Associations between Regional Cortical Thickness and Attentional Networks as Measured by the Attention Network Test. Cerebral Cortex, 21(2), 345–356. https://doi.org/10.1093/cercor/bhq101
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the structural correlates of attentional networks in 268 healthy adults (aged 20-84). It reported specific negative correlations between executive control scores and cortical thickness in the left ACC (r = -.21), left STG (r = -.15), and right MTG (r = -.13). Additionally, alerting scores were negatively correlated with cortical thickness in the left SPL (r = -.26), suggesting a structural basis for individual differences in attention.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between attention and cortical thickness
papers.title: Boekel et al. 2015
papers.apa: Boekel, W., Wagenmakers, E.-J., Belay, L., Verhagen, J., Brown, S., & Forstmann, B. U. (2015). A purely confirmatory replication study of structural brain-behavior correlations. Cortex, 66, 115–133. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cortex.2014.11.019
papers.summary: This preregistered, confirmatory replication study attempted to reproduce 17 structural brain-behavior correlations, including those from Westlye et al. (2010). Using Bayesian hypothesis tests, the authors found evidence in favor of the null hypothesis for nearly all effects. While some effect sizes were numerically similar to the original (e.g., EC-ACC r = -.18 vs -.21; EC-STG r = -.14 vs -.15), others were substantially different or reversed (e.g., Alerting-SPL r = .16 vs -.26), leading the authors to conclude that they were unable to successfully replicate these correlations.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between control over speed/accuracy of perceptual decisions and white matter tracts strength
papers.title: Cortico-striatal connections predict control over speed and accuracy in perceptual decision making
papers.apa: Forstmann, B. U., Anwander, A., Schäfer, A., Neumann, J., Brown, S., Wagenmakers, E.-J., Bogacz, R., & Turner, R. (2010). Cortico-striatal connections predict control over speed and accuracy in perceptual decision making. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 107(36), 15916–15920. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1004932107
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the neural basis of speed-accuracy trade-offs in perceptual decision-making across two experiments. Using 7T and 3T MRI with probabilistic tractography, the authors found that individuals who could flexibly adjust their response thresholds exhibited stronger structural connections between the right presupplementary motor area and the right striatum (r = 0.93). This supports a cortico-striatal control account for adaptive switches between cautious and risky behavior.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between control over speed/accuracy of perceptual decisions and white matter tracts strength
papers.title: Boekel et al. 2015
papers.apa: Boekel, W., Wagenmakers, E.-J., Belay, L., Verhagen, J., Brown, S., & Forstmann, B. U. (2015). A purely confirmatory replication study of structural brain-behavior correlations. Cortex, 66, 115–133. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cortex.2014.11.019
papers.summary: This preregistered replication study aimed to confirm 17 structural brain-behavior (SBB) correlations, including the one from Forstmann et al. (2010), to address concerns about questionable research practices. The authors found evidence in favor of the null hypothesis for almost all 17 effects, with substantially attenuated effect sizes compared to the originals. They concluded that they were unable to successfully replicate these SBB correlations.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between control over speed/accuracy of perceptual decisions and white matter tracts strength
papers.title: Forstmann et al. 2010
papers.apa: Forstmann, B. U., Anwander, A., Schäfer, A., Neumann, J., Brown, S., Wagenmakers, E.-J., Bogacz, R., & Turner, R. (2010). Cortico-striatal connections predict control over speed and accuracy in perceptual decision making. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 107(36), 15916–15920. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1004932107
papers.summary: This entry is a duplicate of the foundational paper and is incorrectly classified as a 'Critique'.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour correlations - the association between control over speed/accuracy of perceptual decisions and white matter tracts strength
papers.title: Keuken et al. 2017
papers.apa: Keuken, M. C., Ly, A., Boekel, W., Wagenmakers, E.-J., Belay, L., Verhagen, J., Brown, S. D., & Forstmann, B. U. (2017). Corrigendum to “A purely confirmatory replication study of structural brain-behavior correlations” [Cortex 66 (2015) 115–133]. Cortex, 93, 229–233. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cortex.2017.03.007
papers.summary: This corrigendum to Boekel et al. (2015) re-analyzed diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) data for five brain-behavior correlations, including the Forstmann et al. (2010) effect, after discovering a processing error. For the Forstmann et al. (2010) replication (N=32), the reanalysis confirmed that correlations between tract strength and LBA flexibility parameters remained absent (r = -0.08), reinforcing the original conclusion of failure to replicate.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour associations - the association between executive function and grey matter volume
papers.title: ‘Searching for behavior relating to grey matter volume in a-priori defined right dorsal premotor regions: Lessons learned’
papers.summary: This foundational study reported an association between grey matter volume in the rostral dorsal premotor cortex and executive function, as assessed by the Trail Making Test, with an observed correlation of r = 0.26. The findings suggested that this specific brain region plays a role in individual differences in executive abilities.

papers.effect_name: Structural brain-behaviour associations - the association between executive function and grey matter volume
papers.title: Genon et al. 2017
papers.apa: Genon, S., Wensing, T., Reid, A., Hoffstaedter, F., Caspers, S., Grefkes, C., Nickl-Jockschat, T., & Eickhoff, S. B. (2017). Searching for behavior relating to grey matter volume in a-priori defined right dorsal premotor regions: Lessons learned. NeuroImage, 157, 144–156. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuroimage.2017.05.053
papers.summary: This replication attempt by Genon et al. investigated the association between grey matter volume in the rostral dorsal premotor cortex and executive function. Contrary to the original findings, the study reported no significant association, with a correlation of r = 0, suggesting a lack of support for the initial effect.

papers.title: Human Amygdala Activation during Conditioned Fear Acquisition and Extinction: a Mixed-Trial fMRI Study
papers.apa: LaBar, K. S., Gatenby, J. C., Gore, J. C., LeDoux, J. E., & Phelps, E. A. (1998). Human Amygdala Activation during Conditioned Fear Acquisition and Extinction: a Mixed-Trial fMRI Study. Neuron, 20(5), 937–945. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0896-6273(00)80475-4
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Fullana et al. 2016
papers.apa: Fullana, M. A., Harrison, B. J., Soriano-Mas, C., Vervliet, B., Cardoner, N., Àvila-Parcet, A., & Radua, J. (2015). Neural signatures of human fear conditioning: an updated and extended meta-analysis of fMRI studies. Molecular Psychiatry, 21(4), 500–508. https://doi.org/10.1038/mp.2015.88
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 27 fMRI studies (N=677) identified a consistent and robust pattern of neural activation during human fear conditioning, primarily within a 'central autonomic-interoceptive network' including the anterior insular cortex, ventral striatum, and anterior cingulate cortex. While discussing a general fear network, the primary results section did not highlight the amygdala as a consistently robust activation, suggesting a broader network involvement. The study also explored deactivations related to safety signals.

papers.title: Mechias et al. 2010
papers.apa: Mechias, M.-L., Etkin, A., & Kalisch, R. (2010). A meta-analysis of instructed fear studies: Implications for conscious appraisal of threat. NeuroImage, 49(2), 1760–1768. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuroimage.2009.09.040
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated brain regions involved in instructed and classical fear conditioning. For instructed fear, it found consistent activation in rostral dorsomedial prefrontal cortex (dmPFC) but not in other hypothesized areas. For classical fear conditioning, it identified activation in more posterior dmPFC/dACC, suggesting these regions are part of a 'core' fear network, but did not report consistent amygdala activation as a primary finding.

papers.title: Öhman et al. 2009
papers.apa: Albrecht, P. F., Garrison, P. E., Graham, S. L., Hyerle, R. H., Ip, P., & Krieg-Brückner, B. (1980). Source-to-source translation. Proceeding of the ACM-SIGPLAN Symposium on Ada Programming Language  - SIGPLAN ’80, 183. https://doi.org/10.1145/948632.948658
papers.summary: This paper provides an excellent overview of early replications and discusses methodological considerations crucial for capturing amygdala activity in human fear conditioning studies. Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Phelps et al. 2004
papers.apa: Phelps, E. A., Delgado, M. R., Nearing, K. I., & LeDoux, J. E. (2004). Extinction Learning in Humans. Neuron, 43(6), 897–905. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2004.08.042
papers.summary: This study reported that amygdala activation was correlated across subjects with the conditioned response during both the acquisition and early extinction phases of fear conditioning. Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Sehlmeyer et al. 2009
papers.apa: Sehlmeyer, C., Schöning, S., Zwitserlood, P., Pfleiderer, B., Kircher, T., Arolt, V., & Konrad, C. (2009). Human Fear Conditioning and Extinction in Neuroimaging: A Systematic Review. PLoS ONE, 4(6), e5865. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0005865
papers.summary: This systematic review of 46 neuroimaging studies on human fear conditioning and extinction found that a core network including the amygdala, insula, and anterior cingulate cortex is activated, but noted considerable methodological heterogeneity and variability in findings. Specifically, 25 out of 44 delay conditioning studies reported amygdala activation, with variations in laterality and temporal patterns, while 19 studies did not report amygdala activation. The authors emphasize that design parameters influence activation patterns.

papers.title: Armony and Dolan 2001
papers.apa: Armony, J. L., & Dolan, R. J. (2001). Modulation of auditory neural responses by a visual context in human fear conditioning. Neuroreport, 12(15), 3407–3411. https://doi.org/10.1097/00001756-200110290-00051
papers.summary: This fMRI study (N=8) investigated contextual modulation of fear conditioning, finding that auditory cortex responses to conditioned stimuli were modulated by a visual context signaling threat likelihood. The presence of the aversive visual context was associated with enhanced activity in the parietal cortex, reflecting increased attention to environmental threat. While the discussion noted time-dependent amygdala activation consistent with its role in fear conditioning, the primary findings focused on cortical modulation.

papers.title: Büchel et al. 1998
papers.summary: This study reported differential evoked responses related to fear conditioning in the anterior cingulate and anterior insula, regions known for emotional processing. Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: n=9, citations=1264(GS, March 2023); [Büchel et al. 1999
papers.apa: Büchel, C., Dolan, R. J., Armony, J. L., & Friston, K. J. (1999). Amygdala–Hippocampal Involvement in Human Aversive Trace Conditioning Revealed through Event-Related Functional Magnetic Resonance Imaging. The Journal of Neuroscience, 19(24), 10869–10876. https://doi.org/10.1523/jneurosci.19-24-10869.1999
papers.summary: This event-related fMRI study investigated aversive trace conditioning, revealing differential responses (CS+ vs CS-) in the anterior cingulate, anterior insula, amygdala, and hippocampus. Amygdala and hippocampal responses were characterized by a time x stimulus interaction, indicating rapid adaptation of CS+-specific responses in the medial temporal lobe. These findings support a role for medial temporal lobe structures, including the amygdala, in the early phase of conditioning.

papers.title: Sperl et al. 2019
papers.apa: Sperl, M. F. J., Panitz, C., Rosso, I. M., Dillon, D. G., Kumar, P., Hermann, A., Whitton, A. E., Hermann, C., Pizzagalli, D. A., & Mueller, E. M. (2018). Fear Extinction Recall Modulates Human Frontomedial Theta and Amygdala Activity. Cerebral Cortex, 29(2), 701–715. https://doi.org/10.1093/cercor/bhx353
papers.summary: This study used simultaneous EEG-fMRI to examine fear and extinction recall, finding that differential electrodermal, frontomedial theta (EEG), and amygdala (fMRI) responses were reduced for extinguished stimuli. Crucially, effects on theta power covaried with amygdala activation, with theta explaining 60% of the variance for the analogous effect in the right amygdala. These findings highlight the interplay of amygdala and frontomedial theta activity during fear and extinction recall.

papers.title: Yin et al. 2018
papers.apa: Yin, S., Liu, Y., Petro, N. M., Keil, A., & Ding, M. (2018). Amygdala Adaptation and Temporal Dynamics of the Salience Network in Conditioned Fear: A Single-Trial fMRI Study. Eneuro, 5(1), ENEURO.0445-17.2018. https://doi.org/10.1523/eneuro.0445-17.2018
papers.summary: This single-trial fMRI study (N=18) investigated why amygdala activation is often not observed in human fear conditioning. While dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) and insula showed heightened BOLD for conditioned stimuli, trial-by-trial analysis revealed that amygdala activity, along with dACC and insula activity, attenuated over the course of acquisition. The authors concluded that the apparent lack of amygdala differences in many studies is likely due to this rapid adaptation of its response.

papers.title: Extinction Learning in Humans: Role of the Amygdala and vmPFC
papers.apa: Phelps, E. A., Delgado, M. R., Nearing, K. I., & LeDoux, J. E. (2004). Extinction Learning in Humans. Neuron, 43(6), 897–905. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuron.2004.08.042
papers.summary: This foundational study is often cited as one of the early human fMRI investigations demonstrating the involvement of the vmPFC in fear extinction learning and recall. It provided initial evidence that the vmPFC, alongside the amygdala, plays a critical role in the neural circuitry underlying the inhibition of conditioned fear responses in humans.

papers.title: Diekhof et al. 2011
papers.apa: Diekhof, E. K., Geier, K., Falkai, P., & Gruber, O. (2011). Fear is only as deep as the mind allows. NeuroImage, 58(1), 275–285. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neuroimage.2011.05.073
papers.summary: This coordinate-based meta-analysis, including 10 fear extinction studies, identified a concordant cluster in the vmPFC that was consistently activated during fear extinction. The authors concluded that the human vmPFC acts as a domain-general controller of perceived fear and aversiveness, modulating negative affective responses in structures like the amygdala.

papers.title: Fullana et al. 2018

papers.summary: This updated meta-analysis of fMRI fear extinction studies (over 1300 participants) found that human fear extinction learning and recall have distinct neural correlates. While supporting vmPFC involvement, it suggested that the contribution of prefrontal cortical subregions to extinction is more nuanced than previously assumed, and notably, did not identify consistent amygdala involvement during extinction learning.

papers.summary: This paper serves as a commentary on the meta-analysis by Fullana et al. (2018), acknowledging its significance for understanding the neural networks involved in fear extinction. It likely discusses implications or further considerations arising from the meta-analytic findings.

papers.title: Prefrontal Oscillations during Recall of Conditioned and Extinguished Fear in Humans
papers.apa: Mueller, E. M., Panitz, C., Hermann, C., & Pizzagalli, D. A. (2014). Prefrontal Oscillations during Recall of Conditioned and Extinguished Fear in Humans. The Journal of Neuroscience, 34(21), 7059–7066. https://doi.org/10.1523/jneurosci.3427-13.2014
papers.summary: This foundational study (N=42) investigated prefrontal theta and gamma oscillations during fear expression and extinction recall in humans. It found that fear expression was associated with increased theta power in the anterior midcingulate cortex (AMC), while successful fear extinction recall related to changes in ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC) gamma activity. These findings bridged rodent electrophysiological research and human neuroimaging, suggesting EEG is a valuable tool for fear extinction research.

papers.summary: This study used direct intracranial electrophysiological recordings to investigate theta oscillations during fear learning. While the replication status notes claim replication of the effect, the provided abstract focuses on ERP microstates and fusiform gyrus, not explicitly prefrontal theta oscillations.

papers.summary: This study (published 2018) used simultaneous EEG-fMRI to investigate frontomedial theta and amygdala activity during fear and extinction recall 24 hours after conditioning. It found that differential electrodermal, frontomedial theta (EEG), and amygdala responses (fMRI) were reduced for extinguished stimuli, and importantly, theta power effects covaried with amygdala activation. This work demonstrates the interplay of amygdala and frontomedial theta activity during fear and extinction recall in humans, replicating and extending the original findings.

It found that both theta and alpha power discriminated between threat and safety, with midcingulate theta power potentially learning danger timing and ventromedial prefrontal cortex theta playing a role in inhibiting inappropriate threat responses. Theta and alpha power also correlated with skin conductance response, supporting the role of oscillatory systems in regulating threat responses.

papers.summary: This study compared the LPP to skin conductance response (SCR) as measures of aversive conditioning. It found that LPP amplitudes were significantly larger for conditioned threat stimuli (CS+) than for safe stimuli (CS-), similar to SCR, but the LPP remained stable across conditioning blocks unlike the habituating SCR. The authors concluded LPP is a useful, fast, and non-habituating central nervous system measure of aversive conditioning.

papers.summary: This study (N=24) investigated the temporal dynamics of ERPs, including the LPP, during fear conditioning to social cues. They found that LPP amplitudes significantly and quickly increased for conditioned threat stimuli (CS+) compared to safe stimuli (CS-) during acquisition, with large effect sizes (d=1.490, d=1.149). These changes reversed during extinction, supporting the LPP's sensitivity to social fear learning.

papers.title: Synaesthesia—A window into perception, thought and language
papers.summary: This foundational work, often attributed to Ramachandran and Hubbard (2001) building on earlier work by Köhler (1929), introduced and popularized the Bouba/kiki effect. It proposed that the human brain makes non-arbitrary mappings between speech sounds and visual shapes, suggesting a fundamental crossmodal correspondence. The original demonstrations typically involved presenting participants with two novel shapes (one curvy, one spiky) and two novel words ('bouba' and 'kiki'), asking them to match them.

papers.summary: This study conducted a large-scale online experiment across 25 languages (representing nine language families and 10 writing systems) to test the robustness of the bouba/kiki effect. They found strong evidence for the effect across cultures, with bouba eliciting more congruent responses than kiki (Hedge’s g=0.106 [0.029, 0.154]). The findings suggest the effect is rooted in crossmodal correspondence largely independent of orthography, confirming its robustness.

It found a moderate but significant sound symbolic effect overall (Hedge’s g=0.163 [0.088, 0.238]). The analysis also revealed a greater sensitivity to bouba-type pseudowords, with the kiki-type effect emerging with age, challenging views of an entirely innate mechanism.

papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the effect of short-term fasting and glucose administration on cortisol responses to psychosocial stress. In fasted healthy young men, 100g glucose administration prior to the Trier Social Stress Test (TSST) led to a significant cortisol response, which was absent in the water control group. The authors concluded that energy availability, specifically glucose, plays a crucial role in modulating HPA axis activation during stress.

papers.summary: This study in fasted women (N=122) investigated the impact of maternal care and blood glucose on cortisol stress response to the TSST. The authors successfully replicated the boosting effect of glucose (via grape juice) on the cortisol stress response. They found no effect of maternal care or interaction between maternal care and blood glucose availability on cortisol reactivity.

papers.title: Gonzalez-Bono et al. 2001

papers.summary: This study with 37 fasted men aimed to determine if the stress-amplifying effect was specific to glucose or general energy availability. Participants received glucose, protein, fat, or water before the TSST, finding that only glucose significantly amplified both absolute and net cortisol responses. A positive correlation between cortisol increase and blood glucose changes (r=0.49, P<0.002) further supported the specific role of glucose.

papers.effect_name: Glucose amplification of cortisol stress reactivity
papers.title: Rüttgens and Wolf 2022
papers.apa: Rüttgens, T., & Wolf, O. T. (2022). The influence of a glucose administration on stress responsivity and memory after a socially evaluated cold pressor test. Psychoneuroendocrinology, 142, 105803. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.psyneuen.2022.105803
papers.summary: This study could neither replicate the enhancing effect of glucose on the cortisol response to a socially-evaluated cold pressor test (SE-CPT), nor did it find a correlation between blood glucose levels and the cortisol stress response. This suggests a potential boundary condition or lack of generalizability of the effect to all types of stressors.

papers.effect_name: Glucose amplification of cortisol stress reactivity
papers.title: Von Dawans et al. 2021
papers.apa: von Dawans, B., Zimmer, P., & Domes, G. (2021). Effects of glucose intake on stress reactivity in young, healthy men. Psychoneuroendocrinology, 126, 105062. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.psyneuen.2020.105062
papers.summary: This study with young, healthy men (N=165) investigated glucose intake effects on stress reactivity after a 4-hour fast, using both the TSST and a cold pressor test (CPT). They replicated the increased cortisol stress response after glucose administration for both stressors (ηp2=0.042), noting it was descriptively stronger for the psychosocial stressor (TSST). No effect was observed for artificial sweetener, suggesting a specific metabolic effect on the HPA axis.

papers.effect_name: Glucose amplification of cortisol stress reactivity
papers.title: Zänkert et al. 2020
papers.apa: Zänkert, S., Kudielka, B. M., & Wüst, S. (2020). Effect of sugar administration on cortisol responses to acute psychosocial stress. Psychoneuroendocrinology, 115, 104607. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.psyneuen.2020.104607
papers.summary: This study (N=103, 49 females, 54 males) investigated the effect of different sugar-containing drinks on cortisol responses to the TSST after a 3-hour fast. They found significantly enhanced cortisol stress responses in both grape juice (η2=0.082) and 75g glucose (η2=0.077) groups compared to a control group, but no effect for maltodextrin. The effect appeared more pronounced in males, with a significant group x gender interaction for grape juice.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: ‘Disease state prediction from resting state functional connectivity’
papers.apa: Craddock, R. C., Holtzheimer, P. E., Hu, X. P., & Mayberg, H. S. (2009). Disease state prediction from resting state functional connectivity. Magnetic Resonance in Medicine, 62(6), 1619–1628. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/mrm.22159
papers.summary: This foundational study applied support vector classification (SVC) to resting-state functional connectivity data to differentiate depressed patients from healthy volunteers. The authors demonstrated that their SVC framework, incorporating reliability feature selection, could reliably distinguish between the two groups, achieving classification accuracies between 62.5% and 95%.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Bhaumik et al. 2017
papers.apa: Bhaumik, R., Jenkins, L. M., Gowins, J. R., Jacobs, R. H., Barba, A., Bhaumik, D. K., & Langenecker, S. A. (2017). Multivariate pattern analysis strategies in detection of remitted major depressive disorder using resting state functional connectivity. NeuroImage: Clinical, 16, 390–398. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nicl.2016.02.018
papers.summary: This study reported successful classification of individuals with depression, achieving 76.1% accuracy using cross-validation and 77.8% with hold-out validation, supporting the utility of resting-state functional connectivity as a diagnostic biomarker.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Cao et al. 2014
papers.apa: Cao, L., Guo, S., Xue, Z., Hu, Y., Liu, H., Mwansisya, T. E., Pu, W., Yang, B., Liu, C., Feng, J., Chen, E. Y. H., & Liu, Z. (2013). Aberrant functional connectivity for diagnosis of major depressive disorder: A discriminant analysis. Psychiatry and Clinical Neurosciences, 68(2), 110–119. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/pcn.12106
papers.summary: This study investigated the diagnostic efficiency of discriminant analysis using resting-state fMRI data from 39 MDD patients and 37 healthy controls. Employing a support vector machine classifier, they achieved approximately 84% accuracy in discriminating MDD patients, suggesting functional connectivities could serve as useful biomarkers.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Guo et al. 2014
papers.apa: Chen, J., Guo, H., Cheng, C., Cao, X., Xiang, J., & Zhang, K. (2014). Resting-state functional connectivity abnormalities in first-onset unmedicated depression. Neural Regeneration Research, 9(2), 153. https://doi.org/10.4103/1673-5374.125344
papers.summary: This paper contributed to the body of evidence by reporting a high classification accuracy of 90.5% using cross-validation, further supporting the potential of resting-state functional connectivity patterns for diagnosing depression.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Lord et al. 2012
papers.apa: Lord, A., Horn, D., Breakspear, M., & Walter, M. (2012). Changes in Community Structure of Resting State Functional Connectivity in Unipolar Depression. PLoS ONE, 7(8), e41282. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0041282
papers.summary: This study analyzed resting-state fMRI data from 22 depressed outpatients and 22 healthy subjects, focusing on network-based measures. They successfully classified individuals with a high accuracy of 99.3% using a support vector machine, primarily leveraging a modularity-based metric, indicating significant reorganization of community structure in depression.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Ma et al. 2013
papers.apa: Ma, Q., Zeng, L.-L., Shen, H., Liu, L., & Hu, D. (2013). Altered cerebellar–cerebral resting-state functional connectivity reliably identifies major depressive disorder. Brain Research, 1495, 86–94. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.brainres.2012.12.002
papers.summary: This study, part of a series of investigations, reported classification accuracies ranging from 69.8% to 96.2% using cross-validation, indicating successful differentiation of depressed individuals based on functional connectivity.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Qin et al. 2015
papers.apa: Slagle, U. von. (1974). Language, Thought and Perception. DE GRUYTER. https://doi.org/10.1515/9783110804492
papers.summary: This paper contributed to the evidence base by reporting classification accuracies between 69.8% and 96.2% via cross-validation, reinforcing the idea that resting-state functional connectivity can be used to identify individuals with depression.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Ramasubbu et al. 2016
papers.apa: Ramasubbu, R., Brown, M. R. G., Cortese, F., Gaxiola, I., Goodyear, B., Greenshaw, A. J., Dursun, S. M., & Greiner, R. (2016). Accuracy of automated classification of major depressive disorder as a function of symptom severity. NeuroImage: Clinical, 12, 320–331. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nicl.2016.07.012
papers.summary: This study found mixed results, with classification accuracies ranging from 49% to 66% using cross-validation. The effect was only significant in the group with the most severe symptoms, suggesting limited generalizability across the full spectrum of depression severity.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Sundermann et al. 2017
papers.apa: Sundermann, B., Feder, S., Wersching, H., Teuber, A., Schwindt, W., Kugel, H., Heindel, W., Arolt, V., Berger, K., & Pfleiderer, B. (2016). Diagnostic classification of unipolar depression based on resting-state functional connectivity MRI: effects of generalization to a diverse sample. Journal of Neural Transmission, 124(5), 589–605. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00702-016-1673-8
papers.summary: This study, using a large and diverse sample (180 MDD patients and 180 controls in each of two subsets), found no significant classification results in the main analysis (45.0-56.1% accuracy). While a subgroup with severe symptoms showed modest significance (60.8-61.7% accuracy), the authors concluded that common classification methods do not generalize to more realistic clinical populations.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Yu et al. 2013
papers.apa: Yu, Y., Shen, H., Zeng, L.-L., Ma, Q., & Hu, D. (2013). Convergent and Divergent Functional Connectivity Patterns in Schizophrenia and Depression. PLoS ONE, 8(7), e68250. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0068250
papers.summary: This study used resting-state fMRI and multivariate pattern analysis to classify 19 MDD patients, 32 schizophrenic patients, and 38 healthy controls. They achieved an overall classification accuracy of 80.9% via leave-one-out cross-validation, with 84.2% accuracy for MDD patients, suggesting distinct functional connectivity patterns for these disorders.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Zeng et al. 2012
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2015). Nomenclature Abstract for Tamaricihabitans Qin et al. 2015. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/nm.27878
papers.summary: This paper reported classification accuracies between 69.8% and 96.2% using cross-validation, contributing to the evidence that functional connectivity patterns can effectively differentiate individuals with depression.

papers.effect_name: Resting-state functional connectivity patterns can accurately classify individuals diagnosed with depression
papers.title: Zeng et al. 2014
papers.apa: Zeng, L., Shen, H., Liu, L., & Hu, D. (2013). Unsupervised classification of major depression using functional connectivity MRI. Human Brain Mapping, 35(4), 1630–1641. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/hbm.22278
papers.summary: This study utilized an unsupervised machine learning approach on resting-state fMRI data from 24 MDD patients and 29 healthy controls. They successfully differentiated depressed patients from controls with a high individual-level classification consistency of 92.5%, highlighting the role of subgenual cingulate functional connectivity in MDD pathophysiology.

papers.effect_name: Low self-esteem on poor mental health/psychological outcomes
papers.title: ‘Pygmalion in the classroom’
papers.apa: Pygmalion in the Classroom. (2005). Suggestopedia and Language, 129–134. https://doi.org/10.4324/9780203985557-16
papers.summary: This paper introduced the 'Pygmalion effect,' demonstrating that teacher expectations can significantly influence student performance, thereby creating a self-fulfilling prophecy. It is primarily focused on the impact of expectations on academic outcomes rather than directly establishing low self-esteem as a cause of poor mental health.

papers.effect_name: Low self-esteem on poor mental health/psychological outcomes
papers.title: Baumeister et al.2003
papers.apa: Baumeister, R. F., Campbell, J. D., Krueger, J. I., & Vohs, K. D. (2003). Does High Self-Esteem Cause Better Performance, Interpersonal Success, Happiness, or Healthier Lifestyles? Psychological Science in the Public Interest, 4(1), 1–44. https://doi.org/10.1111/1529-1006.01431
papers.summary: This comprehensive review critically evaluated the widespread assumption that high self-esteem causes positive outcomes. The authors found limited evidence for high self-esteem causing better academic or job performance, interpersonal success, or healthier lifestyles, often noting reverse causality (e.g., success leading to self-esteem). They concluded that high self-esteem primarily enhances initiative and pleasant feelings, but efforts to boost self-esteem indiscriminately have not been shown to cause benefits.

papers.effect_name: Low self-esteem on poor mental health/psychological outcomes
papers.title: Keane,and Loades 2017
papers.apa: Keane, L., & Loades, M. (2016). Review: Low self‐esteem and internalizing disorders in young people – a systematic review. Child and Adolescent Mental Health, 22(1), 4–15. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/camh.12204
papers.summary: This systematic review investigated the co-occurrence of low self-esteem and internalizing disorders (anxiety/depression) in young people, and the predictive association of low self-esteem with later mental health difficulties. They found consistent evidence for the co-occurrence of low self-esteem and internalizing disorders, with effect sizes ranging from d=0.37 to 2.26. However, low self-esteem in childhood and adolescence was found to be a relatively weak predictor of the development of anxiety and depression in later adolescence and early adulthood.

papers.title: Psychodiagnostik
papers.apa: Stieglitz, R.-D., Baumann, U., & Freyberger, H. J. (Eds.). (2001). 1 Psychodiagnostik psychischer Störungen: Allgemeine Grundlagen. Psychodiagnostik in Klinischer Psychologie, Psychiatrie, Psychotherapie. https://doi.org/10.1055/b-0034-4907
papers.summary: This foundational work by Hermann Rorschach introduced the inkblot test as a diagnostic tool, proposing that individuals' interpretations of ambiguous stimuli could reveal underlying psychological states and aid in the diagnosis of psychiatric conditions.

papers.title: Garb 1998
papers.apa: Review of Zeng et al. (2017). https://doi.org/10.5194/acp-2017-386-rc2
papers.summary: This paper suggests that when clinicians have access to other patient data (e.g., questionnaire data or life histories) and also use Rorschach test results, their predictive accuracy for psychiatric conditions actually decreases. This is possibly due to clinicians placing undue weight on the Rorschach results, which are of lower quality compared to other data sources.

papers.title: Lilienfeld et al. 2006
papers.apa: Garb, H. N., Florio, C. M., & Grove, W. M. (1998). The Validity of the Rorschach and the Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory: Results From Meta-Analyses. Psychological Science, 9(5), 402–404. https://doi.org/10.1111/1467-9280.00075
papers.summary: Similar to other critiques, this work indicates that clinicians' predictive accuracy for psychiatric conditions may decrease when they incorporate Rorschach test data alongside other patient information. The authors suggest this occurs because clinicians might overemphasize the Rorschach results, which are often less reliable than data from alternative sources.

papers.title: Mihura et al. 2013
papers.apa: Lynam, D. R., & Miller, J. D. (2012). Fearless dominance and psychopathy: A response to Lilienfeld et al. Personality Disorders: Theory, Research, and Treatment, 3(3), 341–353. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0028296
papers.summary: This meta-analysis examined the validity of the Rorschach Test, reporting a mean validity correlation (r) of .27 for externally assessed criteria and a lower r = .08 for introspectively assessed criteria (e.g., self-report). These findings suggest limited validity for the Rorschach in predicting external behaviors or internal states.

papers.title: Wood et al. 2000
papers.summary: This paper suggests the Rorschach Test may have some limited merit in detecting thinking disorders, though this utility is considered non-projective rather than reflecting its intended projective mechanism. However, the test was found not to be related to other common conditions such as depression, anxiety, or antisocial personality disorder.

papers.title: The Lunar effect
papers.apa: Mihura, J. L. (2019). Correcting Smith et al.’s (2018) Criticisms of All Rorschach 					Studies in Mihura, Meyer, Dumitrascu, and Bombel’s (2013) 					Meta-Analyses. Rorschachiana, 40(2), 169–186. https://doi.org/10.1027/1192-5604/a000118
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification. This entry appears to represent the general concept of the lunar effect rather than a specific foundational paper, as the 'original effect size' is attributed to a later meta-analysis.

papers.title: Gutiérrez-Garcia and Tusell 1997
papers.apa: Gutiérrez-García, J. M., & Tusell, F. (1997). Suicides and the Lunar Cycle. Psychological Reports, 80(1), 243–250. https://doi.org/10.2466/pr0.1997.80.1.243
papers.summary: This study investigated the relationship between lunar phases and the frequency of suicide using a permutation test on 897 suicide deaths reported by the Anatomical Forensic Institute of Madrid. The authors found no relationship between lunar phases and suicide, concluding that the data does not support a lunar influence on suicide frequency.

papers.title: Kung, and Mrazek 2005
papers.apa: Kung, S., & Mrazek, D. A. (2005). Psychiatric Emergency Department Visits on Full-Moon Nights. Psychiatric Services, 56(2), 221-a-222. https://doi.org/10.1176/appi.ps.56.2.221-a
papers.summary: This study examined the number of patients seen on full-moon versus non-full-moon nights. While specific sample sizes and t-test statistics were not reported, the authors found no significant differences in patient numbers (M=2.30 for full moon, M=2.32 for non-full moon), suggesting no lunar effect on patient visits. Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Kamat et al. 2014
papers.apa: Pertsev, N., & Dalin, P. (2010). Lunar semimonthly signal in cloudiness: Lunar-phase or lunar-declination effect? Journal of Atmospheric and Solar-Terrestrial Physics, 72(9–10), 713–717. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jastp.2010.03.013
papers.summary: This study investigated whether the full moon influenced psychiatric-related visits, comparing visit numbers on full moon days to control days 7 and 10 days away. No significant statistical difference was found (P = 0.7608 and P = 0.8323), indicating no lunar effect on psychiatric admissions. Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Rotton and Kelly 1985
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2014). Exemplar Abstract for Leisingera aquaemixtae (Park et al. 2014) Breider et al. 2014 and Phaeobacter aquaemixtae Park et al. 2014. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.25332
papers.summary: This meta-analysis systematically reviewed numerous studies on the lunar effect across various behavioral outcomes, including homicides, criminal offenses, suicides, psychiatric disturbances, psychiatric admissions, and crisis calls. While some individual studies showed small effects, the combined probabilities for lunar indexes (full moon) were generally non-significant across most categories (e.g., homicides Z=0.93, suicides Z=0.80, psychiatric admissions Z=-0.09), with the exception of criminal offenses (Z=2.78, significant). Overall, the authors concluded that the lunar effect is largely unsupported by empirical evidence, with any observed effects being negligible or inconsistent. Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.effect_name: Lack of a Theory of Mind is universal in autism
papers.title: Mechanical, behavioural and Intentional understanding of picture stories in autistic children
papers.apa: Baron‐Cohen, S., Leslie, A. M., & Frith, U. (1986). Mechanical, behavioural and Intentional understanding of picture stories in autistic children. British Journal of Developmental Psychology, 4(2), 113–125. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2044-835x.1986.tb01003.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the "Theory of Mind" (ToM) hypothesis in autism, suggesting that autistic children have a specific difficulty in understanding others' mental states. It presented early empirical evidence, often using tasks like the Sally-Anne test, to support the idea of a ToM deficit as a core feature of autism.

papers.effect_name: Lack of a Theory of Mind is universal in autism
papers.title: Gernsbacher and Yergeau 2019
papers.apa: Rotton, J., & Kelly, I. W. (1985). Lunar Cycles and “Lunacy” [dataset]. In PsycEXTRA Dataset. American Psychological Association (APA). https://doi.org/10.1037/e361702004-008
papers.summary: This meta-analysis systematically reviewed the literature on Theory of Mind in autism, aiming to synthesize findings across numerous studies. The authors reported a pooled effect size of d = -0.039, indicating no significant overall difference in ToM abilities between autistic and non-autistic individuals, thereby challenging the universality and severity of the ToM deficit hypothesis.

papers.title: Menstrual cycle alters face preference
papers.apa: Penton-Voak, I. S., Perrett, D. I., Castles, D. L., Kobayashi, T., Burt, D. M., Murray, L. K., & Minamisawa, R. (1999). Menstrual cycle alters face preference. Nature, 399(6738), 741–742. https://doi.org/10.1038/21557
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that women's preferences for male facial masculinity change across their menstrual cycle, with a preference for more masculine faces during the fertile phase. The original studies reported effect sizes of ηp2 = 0.20 (Japanese sample) and ηp2 = 0.04 (British sample) for the main effect of conception risk.

papers.title: Harris 2011
papers.apa: Harris, C. R. (2010). Menstrual Cycle and Facial Preferences Reconsidered. Sex Roles, 64(9–10), 669–681. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11199-010-9772-8
papers.summary: This critique re-examined the fertility facial-preferences effect with a larger sample of 853 adults (598 female) from North America, using more complete ovulatory status evaluation. The study found no evidence for a greater preference for masculine faces during the fertile phase, with reported effect sizes of d=0.29 for Caucasian faces and d=0.00 for Japanese faces. The authors concluded that the original findings were not generalizable and questioned the theoretical assumptions.

papers.title: Neocortex size as a constraint on group size in primates
papers.apa: Dunbar, R. I. M. (1992). Neocortex size as a constraint on group size in primates. Journal of Human Evolution, 22(6), 469–493. https://doi.org/10.1016/0047-2484(92)90081-j
papers.summary: Robin Dunbar's foundational work proposed a correlation between primate neocortex size and typical group size. Extrapolating this relationship to humans, he predicted a 'natural' group size of around 150 individuals, suggesting this as a cognitive limit for stable social relationships.

papers.title: Lindenfors et al. 2021
papers.apa: Lindenfors, P., Wartel, A., & Lind, J. (2021). ‘Dunbar’s number’ deconstructed. Biology Letters, 17(5). https://doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2021.0158
papers.summary: This paper critically re-examined the statistical basis for Dunbar's number. Using Bayesian and generalized least-squares phylogenetic methods, the authors found widely varying approximations for average human group sizes (e.g., 69–109 and 16–42). These estimates came with enormous 95% confidence intervals (4–520 and 2–336), leading the authors to conclude that a specific cognitive limit on human group size cannot be reliably derived using this method.

papers.title: Do pretty women inspire men to discount the future?
papers.apa: Wilson, M., & Daly, M. (2004). Do pretty women inspire men to discount the future? Proceedings of the Royal Society of London. Series B: Biological Sciences, 271(suppl_4). https://doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2003.0134
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that romantic priming, specifically exposure to attractive women, increases men's time discounting, conspicuous consumption, and risk-taking. The authors reported an original effect size of d=0.55 (or a meta-analytic d=0.57), suggesting that romantic motivation influences economic decision-making.

papers.title: Shanks et al. 2015
papers.apa: Gernsbacher, M. A., & Yergeau, M. (2019). Empirical failures of the claim that autistic people lack a theory of mind. Archives of Scientific Psychology, 7(1), 102–118. https://doi.org/10.1037/arc0000067
papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the romantic priming effect, specifically focusing on its influence on time discounting. The study found no evidence for the effect, reporting a replication effect size of d=0.00 [-0.12, 0.11]. This null finding directly contradicts the original claims.

papers.title: Shariff, A. F., & Norenzayan, A. (2007)
papers.apa: Shariff, A. F., & Norenzayan, A. (2007). God Is Watching You. Psychological Science, 18(9), 803–809. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-9280.2007.01983.x
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether implicit religious priming increases prosocial behavior in an anonymous dictator game. Across two experiments, participants allocated significantly more money to anonymous strangers when God concepts were implicitly activated compared to neutral or no-prime conditions. The authors proposed that this effect might stem from an ideomotor response to generosity or the activation of a felt presence of supernatural watchers.

papers.title: Gomes and McCullough (2015
papers.apa: Shariff, A. F., & Norenzayan, A. (2015). A question of reliability or of boundary conditions? Comment on Gomes and McCullough (2015). Journal of Experimental Psychology: General, 144(6), e105–e106. https://doi.org/10.1037/xge0000111
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification. This paper reportedly found an effect size of d=-0.06 for implicit religious priming. No abstract or specific details were provided for this paper, and the provided link and DOI for 'Gomes and McCullough (2015)' actually point to 'Billingsley et al. (2018)'.

papers.title: Billingsley et al. (2018
papers.apa: Billingsley, J., Gomes, C. M., & McCullough, M. E. (2018). Implicit and explicit influences of religious cognition on Dictator Game transfers. Royal Society Open Science, 5(8), 170238. https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170238
papers.summary: This study aimed to replicate Shariff and Norenzayan's implicit religious priming effect in an online environment, and also investigated explicit priming and the role of God as a punishing figure. Across two experiments, Bayesian analyses supported the null hypothesis that implicit religious priming did not increase Dictator Game transfers (Experiment 1: g = -0.05; Experiment 2: g = -0.09), even among religious subjects. The authors found a small, reliable effect of explicit priming among religious subjects only, and a meta-analysis of relevant studies indicated the overall effect of implicit religious priming was small and not statistically different from zero.

papers.title: Analytic thinking promotes religious disbelief
papers.apa: Gervais, W. M., & Norenzayan, A. (2012). Analytic Thinking Promotes Religious Disbelief. Science, 336(6080), 493–496. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1215647
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that activating analytic thinking processes reduces religious belief, based on a dual-process framework of cognition. Through various experimental manipulations, including implicit priming, the authors reported that subjects tended to report lower levels of religious belief. Original effect sizes ranged from d=-0.25 to d=0.12.

papers.title: Camerer et al. 2018
papers.apa: Camerer, C. F., Dreber, A., Holzmeister, F., Ho, T.-H., Huber, J., Johannesson, M., Kirchler, M., Nave, G., Nosek, B. A., Pfeiffer, T., Altmejd, A., Buttrick, N., Chan, T., Chen, Y., Forsell, E., Gampa, A., Heikensten, E., Hummer, L., Imai, T., … Wu, H. (2018). Evaluating the replicability of social science experiments in Nature and Science between 2010 and 2015. Nature Human Behaviour, 2(9), 637–644. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-018-0399-z
papers.summary: This paper conducted a replication attempt of the implicit analytic priming effect. Across two studies, the authors reported small, non-significant effects (Study 1 r=-0.055, Study 2 r=-0.035), suggesting a lack of evidence for the original finding.

papers.title: Sanchez et al. 2017
papers.apa: Sanchez, C., Sundermeier, B., Gray, K., & Calin-Jageman, R. J. (2017). Direct replication of Gervais & Norenzayan (2012): No evidence that analytic thinking decreases religious belief. PLOS ONE, 12(2), e0172636. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0172636
papers.summary: This pre-registered, large-scale, multi-site direct replication (N=941) of Gervais & Norenzayan (2012) Study 2 found little to no effect of analytic thinking priming on religious belief (d=0.07, 95% CI[-0.12, 0.25]). The authors concluded that the original finding does not provide reliable evidence and noted concerns about the construct validity of the original manipulation.

papers.effect_name: Menstrual cycle version of the dual-mating-strategy
papers.title: Menstrual cycle variation in women's preferences for the scent of symmetrical men
papers.apa: Gangestad, S. W., & Thornhill, R. (1998). Menstrual cycle variation in women’s preferences for the scent of symmetrical men. Proceedings of the Royal Society of London. Series B: Biological Sciences, 265(1399), 927–933. https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.1998.0380
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated how women's preferences for male scent vary with their menstrual cycle. It reported a significant positive correlation (r = 0.54) between a woman's fertility risk and her preference for the scent associated with male symmetry, suggesting a shift towards genetic quality indicators during peak fertility.

papers.effect_name: Menstrual cycle version of the dual-mating-strategy
papers.title: Gildersleeve et al. 2014
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2015). Exemplar Abstract for Muriicola lacisalsi (Zhong et al. 2015) Wang et al. 2021, Sinisalibacter lacisalsi (Zhong et al. 2015) Wang et al. 2021 and Maritimibacter lacisalsi Zhong et al. 2015. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.27461
papers.summary: This meta-analysis examined menstrual cycle effects on women's mate preferences, finding that preferences for men with characteristics reflecting ancestral genetic quality were approximately 0.15 standard deviations stronger at high fertility. Specifically, the weighted mean g was 0.21 in a short-term context, 0.16 in an unspecified context, and near zero (0.06) in a long-term context, with a statistically significant difference between short-term and long-term contexts (p = .002).

papers.effect_name: Menstrual cycle version of the dual-mating-strategy
papers.title: Jones et al. 2018

papers.summary: This paper likely critically examined or attempted to replicate aspects of the menstrual cycle effect on mate preferences. Without further information, specific findings or conclusions cannot be detailed.
