papers.title: Wood et al. 2014
papers.apa: Wood, W., Kressel, L., Joshi, P. D., & Louie, B. (2014). Meta-Analysis of Menstrual Cycle Effects on Women’s Mate Preferences. Emotion Review, 6(3), 229–249. https://doi.org/10.1177/1754073914523073
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 58 independent reports largely did not support the hypothesis that fertile women especially desire high genetic quality men (e.g., high testosterone, masculinity, dominance, symmetry) for short-term relationships. The authors concluded that the few observed significant preference shifts were likely research artifacts, noting that effects declined over time, were limited to less precise fertility definitions, and were found only in published work.

papers.title: Lunar Influences on the Reproductive Cycle in Women
papers.apa: Morfeld, P. (2015). Buchanich et al (2014). Journal of Occupational & Environmental Medicine, 57(2), e13. https://doi.org/10.1097/jom.0000000000000381
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed a synchronization between the human menstrual cycle and the lunar cycle. The authors presented data suggesting that menstruation in women with cycles close to 29.5 days was significantly more likely to occur during the full moon phase, indicating a non-uniform distribution of menstrual onset relative to lunar phases.

papers.title: Komada et al. 2021
papers.apa: Aguirre, A. A., Palomares, R. A., De Ondiz, A. D., Soto, E. R., Perea, M. S., Hernández-Fonseca, H. J., & Perea, F. P. (2021). Lunar Cycle Influences Reproductive Performance of Crossbred Brahman Cows Under Tropical Conditions. Journal of Biological Rhythms, 36(2), 160–168. https://doi.org/10.1177/0748730420983638
papers.summary: This replication study investigated the proposed lunar influence on the menstrual cycle. The authors found no significant evidence supporting a synchronization between menstruation and the full moon, reporting a z-score of -0.58. This finding suggests that the previously reported lunar effect on the menstrual cycle is not robust.

papers.title: ‘Big and tall parents have more sons:Further generalizations of the Trivers–Willard hypothesis’
papers.apa: Kanazawa, S. (2005). Big and tall parents have more sons: Further generalizations of the Trivers–Willard hypothesis. Journal of Theoretical Biology, 235(4), 583–590. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtbi.2005.02.010
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that larger and taller parents are more likely to have sons, extending the Trivers-Willard hypothesis. The authors reported positive regression coefficients for the effects of weight on the number of boys (b = 0.0183) and height on the number of male fetuses (b = 0.0182), and negative coefficients for female offspring.

papers.title: Denny 2007
papers.apa: Leizhang. (2021). zhang et al 2021 (Version V1) [Data set]. Science Data Bank. https://doi.org/10.57760/SCIENCEDB.00882
papers.summary: This critique paper attempted to replicate the 'Large parents have more sons' effect. Across multiple regression models, the study found no significant effects of father's height, mother's height, father's BMI, or mother's BMI on the likelihood of having male children, with all Z-scores ranging from 0.010 to 0.440, indicating a lack of evidence for the original claim.

papers.effect_name: Men’s strength in particular predicts opposition to egalitarianism
papers.title: The Ancestral Logic of Politics: Upper Body Strength Regulates Men’s Assertion of Self-Interest Over Economic Redistribution’
papers.apa: Petersen, M. B., Sznycer, D., Sell, A., Cosmides, L., & Tooby, J. (2013). The Ancestral Logic of Politics. Psychological Science, 24(7), 1098–1103. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797612466415
papers.summary: This foundational study proposed that men's upper-body strength influences their political views on economic redistribution, leading them to endorse self-beneficial positions. Across studies in Argentina, Denmark, and the US, stronger men of lower socioeconomic status supported redistribution, while stronger men of higher socioeconomic status opposed it. The authors suggest this reflects an evolved psychology from small-scale groups.

papers.effect_name: Men’s strength in particular predicts opposition to egalitarianism
papers.title: Gelman and Loken 2019
papers.apa: Denny, K. (2008). Big and tall parents do not have more sons. Journal of Theoretical Biology, 250(4), 752–753. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtbi.2007.11.004
papers.summary: This critique re-examined the original findings, suggesting that the reported effect of upper-body strength on political egalitarianism disappears once participant age is included as a covariate. The authors imply that the original finding might be spurious or confounded by age.

papers.effect_name: Men’s strength in particular predicts opposition to egalitarianism
papers.title: Petersen and Laustsen 2019
papers.apa: Petersen, M. B., & Laustsen, L. (2018). Upper‐Body Strength and Political Egalitarianism: Twelve Conceptual Replications. Political Psychology, 40(2), 375–394. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/pops.12505
papers.summary: This paper presents 12 conceptual replications across various samples and countries to re-examine the link between upper-body strength and support for inequality. While not every measure replicated, the authors conclude that the overall data pattern supports a positive correlation between upper-body strength and support for inequality in males (overall male effect b=0.17), but not in females (overall female effect b=0.11), though the gender difference was not statistically significant (p=0.09).

papers.title: Sex differences in human mate preferences: Evolutionary hypotheses tested in 37 cultures
papers.apa: Sex dieeferences in human mate preferences: Evolutionary hypotheses tested in 37 cultures. (2004). Close Relationships, 176–198. https://doi.org/10.4324/9780203311851-19
papers.summary: This foundational study proposed that men and women exhibit universal sex differences in mate preferences, reflecting distinct evolutionary pressures. Across 33 cultures, the research found that men tend to prefer attractive, young mates, while women tend to prefer older mates with financial prospects.

papers.title: Walter et al. 2021
papers.apa: Walter, K. V., Conroy-Beam, D., Buss, D. M., Asao, K., Sorokowska, A., Sorokowski, P., Aavik, T., Akello, G., Alhabahba, M. M., Alm, C., Amjad, N., Anjum, A., Atama, C. S., Atamtürk Duyar, D., Ayebare, R., Batres, C., Bendixen, M., Bensafia, A., Bizumic, B., … Zupančič, M. (2020). Sex Differences in Mate Preferences Across 45 Countries: A Large-Scale Replication. Psychological Science, 31(4), 408–423. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797620904154
papers.summary: This large-scale replication study, involving 14,399 participants across 45 countries, aimed to re-examine classic findings on mate preferences. The authors found robust support for universal sex differences, confirming that men prefer attractive, young mates and women prefer older mates with financial prospects (e.g., a standardized preference for older mates with financial prospects, b = -0.30). The study also noted that both sexes tend to have mates closer to their own ages as gender equality increases, but neither pathogen prevalence nor gender equality robustly predicted other sex differences in preferences.

papers.title: Do women shy away from competition? Do men compete too much?
papers.summary: This foundational paper established a significant gender difference in competitive preferences, finding that men were more likely to select competitive tournament settings than women. The study reported probit regression coefficients ranging from -0.38 to -0.16, indicating a robust gender gap in competitive behavior.

papers.title: Price 2020
papers.apa: Gelman, A., & Loken, E. (2016). The Statistical Crisis in Science. The Best Writing on Mathematics 2015, 305–318. https://doi.org/10.2307/j.ctvc778jw.30
papers.summary: This replication study investigated the gender gap in competitive preferences, finding that females in the replication had a 22.4 percentage point higher probability of entering a tournament compared to females in the original study, after controlling for task performance. The probit regression coefficient for the NV*Female interaction term shifted from non-significant to significant, indicating a failure to replicate the original gender difference.

papers.title: The Incidental Orgasm: The Presence of Clitoral Knowledge and the Absence of Orgasm for Women
papers.apa: Wade, L. D., Kremer, E. C., & Brown, J. (2005). The Incidental Orgasm: The Presence of Clitoral Knowledge and the Absence of Orgasm for Women. Women & Health, 42(1), 117–138. https://doi.org/10.1300/j013v42n01_07
papers.summary: This study surveyed 883 undergraduate students (657 women, 226 men) to investigate clitoral knowledge and its relationship to orgasm frequency. It found that while respondents had adequate clitoral knowledge, the most frequently cited sources (school, friends) were least correlated with actual knowledge, whereas self-exploration (most correlated) was rarely cited. The paper explores factors contributing to women's difficulties in achieving orgasm, linking knowledge to orgasm frequency in both masturbation and partnered sex.

papers.title: Mahar et al. 2020
papers.apa: Mahar, E. A., Mintz, L. B., & Akers, B. M. (2020). Orgasm Equality: Scientific Findings and Societal Implications. Current Sexual Health Reports, 12(1), 24–32. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11930-020-00237-9
papers.summary: This literature review synthesizes recent research on the gendered orgasm gap, consistently finding its persistence in heterosexual sexual encounters. The authors highlight sociocultural factors, such as the cultural prioritization of penile-vaginal intercourse, women's lack of entitlement to pleasure, and societal scripts, as key contributors to this disparity. They note one study where orgasm rates converged under specific conditions, suggesting the gap is a consequence of social organization rather than inevitability.

papers.effect_name: Sympathetic nervous system activity predicts political ideology
papers.title: Political Attitudes Vary with Physiological Traits
papers.apa: Oxley, D. R., Smith, K. B., Alford, J. R., Hibbing, M. V., Miller, J. L., Scalora, M., Hatemi, P. K., & Hibbing, J. R. (2008). Political Attitudes Vary with Physiological Traits. Science, 321(5896), 1667–1670. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1157627
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the biological basis of political attitudes in 46 adult participants with strong political beliefs. It found that individuals with lower physical sensitivities to sudden noises and threatening images were more likely to support liberal policies, while those with higher physiological reactions favored conservative policies. The authors concluded that physiological responsiveness to threat indicates the degree to which individuals advocate for policies protecting the social structure, reporting an effect of physiological reactions on support for socially protective policies of β = 0.377.

papers.effect_name: Sympathetic nervous system activity predicts political ideology
papers.title: Osmundsen et al. 2022
papers.apa: Osmundsen, M., Hendry, D. J., Laustsen, L., Smith, K. B., & Petersen, M. B. (2022). The Psychophysiology of Political Ideology: Replications, Reanalyses, and Recommendations. The Journal of Politics, 84(1), 50–66. https://doi.org/10.1086/714780
papers.summary: This paper presents a large-scale empirical evaluation of the psychophysiological correlates of political ideology, conducting two large replications in Danish and American samples and re-analyzing all published studies. While they replicated the original finding in their US sample (b = .33 for Wilson-Patterson, b = .33 for Social Conservatism Scale), they found no significant association in the Danish sample or overall (b = .13 and b = .11, respectively). The authors concluded there is limited empirical support for the claim due to significant reliability and validity problems with electrodermal activity as a measure of threat-sensitivity.

papers.title: Influence of Life Stress on Depression: Moderation by a Polymorphism in the 5-HTT Gene
papers.apa: Caspi, A., Sugden, K., Moffitt, T. E., Taylor, A., Craig, I. W., Harrington, H., McClay, J., Mill, J., Martin, J., Braithwaite, A., & Poulton, R. (2003). Influence of Life Stress on Depression: Moderation by a Polymorphism in the 5-HTT Gene. Science, 301(5631), 386–389. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1083968
papers.summary: Caspi et al. (2003) conducted a prospective-longitudinal study on a representative birth cohort to investigate how a polymorphism in the serotonin transporter (5-HTT) gene might moderate the influence of stressful life events on depression. They reported that individuals with one or two copies of the short allele of the 5-HTT promoter polymorphism exhibited more depressive symptoms, diagnosable depression, and suicidality in relation to stressful life events than individuals homozygous for the long allele, providing initial evidence for this gene-by-environment interaction.

papers.title: Chabris et al. 2012
papers.apa: Chabris, C. F., Hebert, B. M., Benjamin, D. J., Beauchamp, J., Cesarini, D., van der Loos, M., Johannesson, M., Magnusson, P. K. E., Lichtenstein, P., Atwood, C. papers.summary: The provided text for Chabris et al. (2012) describes an attempt to replicate associations between general cognitive ability and 12 specific genetic variants, finding only 1 out of 32 tests nominally significant. This summary does not directly address the 5-HTT GxE interaction for depression, so its specific contribution to this effect cannot be determined from the provided content.

papers.title: Collaborative meta-analysis finds no evidence of a strong interaction between stress and 5-HTTLPR genotype contributing to the development of depression
papers.apa: Culverhouse, R. C., Saccone, N. L., Horton, A. C., Ma, Y., Anstey, K. J., Banaschewski, T., Burmeister, M., Cohen-Woods, S., Etain, B., Fisher, H. L., Goldman, N., Guillaume, S., Horwood, J., Juhasz, G., Lester, K. J., Mandelli, L., Middeldorp, C. M., Olié, E., Villafuerte, S., … Bierut, L. J. (2017). Collaborative meta-analysis finds no evidence of a strong interaction between stress and 5-HTTLPR genotype contributing to the development of depression. Molecular Psychiatry, 23(1), 133–142. https://doi.org/10.1038/mp.2017.44
papers.summary: Culverhouse et al. (2017) conducted a large-scale collaborative meta-analysis of 31 datasets, including 38,802 subjects of European ancestry, to rigorously test the 5-HTTLPR gene-by-environment interaction hypothesis for depression. They found no statistically significant evidence for an interaction between stress and 5-HTTLPR genotype across various subgroups or variable definitions (OR = 1.05 [0.94 - 1.16]). The study concluded that if such an interaction exists, it must be of modest effect size and not broadly generalizable.

This summary does not directly address the 5-HTT GxE interaction for depression, so its specific contribution to this effect cannot be determined from the provided content.

papers.title: Motivation, vision and gender
papers.apa: You, C. (Julia), Dörnyei, Z., & Csizér, K. (2015). Motivation, Vision, and Gender: A Survey of Learners of English in China. Language Learning, 66(1), 94–123. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/lang.12140
papers.summary: This foundational study surveyed over 10,000 Chinese secondary school and university students to investigate the role of vision and imagery in second language (L2) motivation. It found that the capacity for vision significantly contributes to learners' overall motivational setup, with an original effect size of η² = 0.08, suggesting that vivid mental imagery of an Ideal L2 Self enhances motivation. The study also explored gender differences and changes in imagery capacity over time.

papers.title: Hiver and Al-Hoorie 2020
papers.apa: Hiver, P., & Al‐Hoorie, A. H. (2019). Reexamining the Role of Vision in Second Language Motivation: A Preregistered Conceptual Replication of You, Dörnyei, and Csizér (2016). Language Learning, 70(1), 48–102. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/lang.12371
papers.summary: This preregistered conceptual replication and extension of You, Dörnyei, and Csizér (2015) used a South Korean sample of 1,297 secondary school English learners. The study found support for an alternative structural model where "Intended Effort" functioned better as a predictor of motivation rather than an outcome variable, challenging the original model's structure. While some relationships were observed (e.g., Ideal L2 self on grades β=0.38, on intended effort β=0.75), the authors emphasized the need for greater precision in modeling and open science practices, suggesting the original model's specific claims were not directly replicated.

papers.title: Flip Your Classroom: Reach Every Student in Every Class Every Day
papers.apa: Lopez, S. (2025). Flip Your Classroom -Reach Every Student in Every Class Every Day. SSRN Electronic Journal. https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.5074342
papers.summary: This foundational book by Bergmann and Sams is widely credited with popularizing the 'flipped classroom' model. It outlines the pedagogical approach of delivering instructional content outside of class (e.g., via video lectures) and using in-class time for active, hands-on learning and personalized guidance.

papers.title: Cheng et al. 2019
papers.apa: Cheng, L., Ritzhaupt, A. D., & Antonenko, P. (2018). Effects of the flipped classroom instructional strategy on students’ learning outcomes: a meta-analysis. Educational Technology Research and Development, 67(4), 793–824. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11423-018-9633-7
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated the effectiveness of flipped learning, reporting a Hedges' g of 0.19 [0.11, 0.27] across various disciplines, indicating a small but positive effect in favor of flipped classrooms.

papers.title: Låg and Sæle 2019
papers.apa: Låg, T., & Sæle, R. G. (2019). Does the Flipped Classroom Improve Student Learning and Satisfaction? A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. AERA Open, 5(3). https://doi.org/10.1177/2332858419870489
papers.summary: This meta-analysis compared flipped classroom teaching with traditional methods, finding a small effect in favor of flipped classrooms on learning (Hedges’ g = 0.35). When restricted to sufficiently powered studies, the estimate was g = 0.24. Effects on pass rates (OR = 1.55) and student satisfaction (g = 0.16) were also small and potentially influenced by publication bias.

papers.title: Lo and Hew 2017
papers.apa: Lo, C. K., & Hew, K. F. (2017). A critical review of flipped classroom challenges in K-12 education: possible solutions and recommendations for future research. Research and Practice in Technology Enhanced Learning, 12(1). https://doi.org/10.1186/s41039-016-0044-2
papers.summary: This critical review examined 15 K-12 flipped classroom studies, finding a neutral to positive impact on student achievement and mixed results for student attitude. It identified and categorized challenges related to students, faculty, and operational aspects, proposing a model and guidelines to address them.

papers.title: Lo and Hew
papers.apa: Lo, C. K., & Hew, K. F. (2019). The impact of flipped classrooms on student achievement in engineering education: A meta‐analysis of 10 years of research. Journal of Engineering Education, 108(4), 523–546. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/jee.20293
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized 29 comparative studies (N=5,329 students) on flipped classrooms in engineering education. It found a significant overall effect favoring flipped classrooms over traditional lecturing (Hedges' g = 0.289 [0.165, 0.414]), with the effect enhanced when instructors provided a brief in-class review.

papers.title: Shi et al.
papers.apa: Shi, Y., Ma, Y., MacLeod, J., & Yang, H. H. (2019). College students’ cognitive learning outcomes in flipped classroom instruction: a meta-analysis of the empirical literature. Journal of Computers in Education, 7(1), 79–103. https://doi.org/10.1007/s40692-019-00142-8
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 33 studies investigated the effectiveness of flipped classroom instructions on college students' cognitive learning outcomes. It concluded that flipped classrooms positively influence cognitive learning compared to traditional lectures, with pedagogical approach identified as a significant moderator variable.

papers.title: Strelan et al.
papers.apa: Strelan, P., Osborn, A., & Palmer, E. (2020). The flipped classroom: A meta-analysis of effects on student performance across disciplines and education levels. Educational Research Review, 30, 100314. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.edurev.2020.100314
papers.summary: This meta-analysis examined the impact of flipped learning across various disciplines, reporting a Hedges' g of 0.50 [0.42, 0.52], indicating a moderate to large positive effect.

papers.title: van Altren et al.
papers.apa: van Alten, D. C. D., Phielix, C., Janssen, J., & Kester, L. (2019). Effects of flipping the classroom on learning outcomes and satisfaction: A meta-analysis. Educational Research Review, 28, 100281. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.edurev.2019.05.003
papers.summary: This meta-analysis explored the effectiveness of flipped learning, finding a Hedges' g of 0.36 [0.28, 0.44] across various disciplines, suggesting a moderate positive impact.

papers.title: Vitta & Al-Hoorie
papers.apa: Vitta, J. P., & Al-Hoorie, A. H. (2020). The flipped classroom in second language learning: A meta-analysis. Language Teaching Research, 27(5), 1268–1292. https://doi.org/10.1177/1362168820981403
papers.summary: This meta-analysis of 56 language learning reports (N=4,220) found flipped classrooms outperformed traditional ones (g = 0.99). After adjusting for publication bias using Trim and Fill, the effect remained sizable (g = 0.58). The effect varied by proficiency level, with higher proficiency linked to larger effects.

papers.title: Xu et al.
papers.apa: Xu, P., Chen, Y., Nie, W., Wang, Y., Song, T., Li, H., Li, J., Yi, J., & Zhao, L. (2019). The effectiveness of a flipped classroom on the development of Chinese nursing students’ skill competence: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Nurse Education Today, 80, 67–77. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nedt.2019.06.005
papers.summary: This meta-analysis focused on flipped learning in nursing education in China, reporting a large effect size (Cohen's d = 1.79 [1.32, 2.27]) favoring the flipped classroom approach.

papers.title: Implicit Theories of Intelligence Predict Achievement Across an Adolescent Transition
papers.apa: Blackwell, L. S., Trzesniewski, K. H., & Dweck, C. S. (2007). Implicit Theories of Intelligence Predict Achievement Across an Adolescent Transition: A Longitudinal Study and an Intervention. Child Development, 78(1), 246–263. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-8624.2007.00995.x
papers.summary: This foundational study explored the role of implicit theories of intelligence in adolescent mathematics achievement. In Study 1 (N=373 7th graders), an incremental theory predicted an upward trajectory in grades, while an entity theory predicted a flat trajectory, mediated by learning goals and effort beliefs. Study 2 (N=48 experimental, N=43 control 7th graders) showed that an intervention teaching an incremental theory promoted positive changes in classroom motivation and reversed a decline in grades for the experimental group.

papers.summary: This study reported no effect of growth mindset on grades across all three of its investigations. Further details on methodology or sample sizes were not provided.

papers.title: Macnamara and Burgoyne 2022
papers.apa: Macnamara, B. N., & Burgoyne, A. P. (2023). Do growth mindset interventions impact students’ academic achievement? A systematic review and meta-analysis with recommendations for best practices. Psychological Bulletin, 149(3–4), 133–173. https://doi.org/10.1037/bul0000352
papers.summary: This meta-analysis systematically reviewed the impact of growth mindset interventions on students' academic achievement. It reported a wide range of effect sizes (d=0.033-1.51 for positive, d=-0.98- -0.68 for negative, d=-0.56-1.39 for non-significant), but concluded that the overall effect was non-significant after correcting for publication bias.

papers.title: Sisk et al. 2018
papers.apa: Sisk, V. F., Burgoyne, A. P., Sun, J., Butler, J. L., & Macnamara, B. N. (2018). To What Extent and Under Which Circumstances Are Growth Mind-Sets Important to Academic Achievement? Two Meta-Analyses. Psychological Science, 29(4), 549–571. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797617739704
papers.summary: This paper presented two meta-analyses on mindsets and academic achievement. The first (k=273, N=365,915) found a weak relationship between mindset and achievement, with only 37% of studies showing a positive link. The second (k=43, N=57,155) found weak overall effectiveness for mindset interventions, with only 12% showing a positive effect. However, some results suggested that students with low socioeconomic status or who are academically at risk might benefit from interventions.

papers.title: Counterfactual thinking and the first instinct fallacy
papers.apa: Kruger, J., Wirtz, D., & Miller, D. T. (2005). Counterfactual Thinking and the First Instinct Fallacy. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 88(5), 725–735. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.88.5.725
papers.summary: This foundational study (N=1,561) demonstrated that on multiple-choice tests, answer changes from wrong to right (51%) significantly outnumbered changes from right to wrong (25%), leading to improved scores for 54% of test-takers. The authors proposed that the widespread belief in the 'first instinct fallacy' stems from counterfactual thinking, where the regret of changing a correct answer to an incorrect one is more salient and memorable than the regret of not changing an incorrect answer.

papers.title: Couchman et al. 2016
papers.apa: Li, Y., & Bates, T. C. (2017). Does growth mindset improve children’s IQ, educational attainment or response to setbacks?  Active-control interventions and data on children’s own mindsets. https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/tsdwy_v1
papers.summary: This study (d=0.58) investigated the role of metacognition in answer changing, finding that students confident in their first answers were usually correct, while those less confident benefited from revising. It concluded that while initial confidence is a strong predictor of correctness, changing answers can still improve performance, especially when initial confidence is low, thus refining the understanding of the first instinct fallacy.

papers.summary: This psychoanalytic case study of a 21-year-old mathematician (N=1) with 'strephosymbolia' (dyslexia/dysgraphia) interpreted his writing errors as oscillations between visual and phonetic reproduction. The authors proposed that strephosymbolia stems from a failure to synthesize ordinal and visual aspects of words, linking it to primal scene fantasies and precocious ego development. During analysis, the patient showed significant improvement in reading speed and proofreading ability.

papers.title: The Pen Is Mightier Than the Keyboard: Advantages of Longhand Over Laptop Note Taking

papers.summary: In three studies, Mueller and Oppenheimer (2014) investigated the impact of note-taking medium on learning. They found that students taking notes longhand performed significantly better on conceptual questions than those using laptops, attributing this to deeper processing and less verbatim transcription. Original effect sizes for conceptual questions ranged from d = 0.38 to 0.64.

papers.title: Urry et al. 2021
papers.apa: Urry, H. L., Crittle, C. S., Floerke, V. A., Leonard, M. Z., Perry, C. S., Akdilek, N., Albert, E. R., Block, A. J., Bollinger, C. A., Bowers, E. M., Brody, R. S., Burk, K. C., Burnstein, A., Chan, A. K., Chan, P. C., Chang, L. J., Chen, E., Chiarawongse, C. P., Chin, G., … Zarrow, J. E. (2021). Don’t Ditch the Laptop Just Yet: A Direct Replication of Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) Study 1 Plus Mini Meta-Analyses Across Similar Studies. Psychological Science, 32(3), 326–339. https://doi.org/10.1177/0956797620965541
papers.summary: This direct replication of Mueller and Oppenheimer’s (2014) Study 1 (N=142) found no evidence that longhand note-taking improved immediate learning, with a negligible effect in the opposite direction (Hedges’s g = -0.13). While laptop participants did transcribe more verbatim, this did not translate to worse quiz performance. An exploratory mini-meta-analysis of eight similar studies also found a negligible, non-significant effect (Hedges’s g = 0.04).

papers.title: The breaking of a habit by suggestion during sleep
papers.apa: Leshan, L. (1942). The breaking of a habit by suggestion during sleep. The Journal of Abnormal and Social Psychology, 37(3), 406–408. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0057703
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether habits could be broken through suggestions delivered during sleep. The authors reported that 40% of boys who listened to a message about bitter-tasting fingernails during 54 nights of sleep successfully stopped biting their nails, compared to 0% in two control groups. This suggests that information presented during sleep could influence behavior, implying a form of sleep-assisted learning.

papers.title: Aarons 1976
papers.apa: Shoch, D. (1981). On Writing, Reading and Dyslexia. American Journal of Ophthalmology, 91(2), 280–281. https://doi.org/10.1016/0002-9394(81)90202-6
papers.summary: This paper explored the efficacy of sleep-assisted learning, noting that its effectiveness remains unclear. Preliminary findings suggested that individual differences such as age, sex, health, wake learning capacity, suggestibility, and motivation to learn might be crucial factors influencing whether learning during sleep occurs. No specific effect sizes were reported, and the study did not provide a definitive conclusion on the generalizability of sleep learning.

papers.title: Arzi et al. 2012
papers.apa: Arzi, A., Shedlesky, L., Ben-Shaul, M., Nasser, K., Oksenberg, A., Hairston, I. S., & Sobel, N. (2012). Humans can learn new information during sleep. Nature Neuroscience, 15(10), 1460–1465. https://doi.org/10.1038/nn.3193
papers.summary: This study demonstrated that sleeping subjects could learn novel associations between tones and odors. Participants showed differential sniff volumes during sleep, responding more to pleasant odors (d=1.39) and to tones previously paired with pleasant odors (d=1.29), indicating implicit learning and action during sleep. The findings suggest that the brain can process and form new associations during sleep, challenging previous notions about the limitations of sleep learning.

papers.title: Simon and Emmons 1955
papers.apa: Aarons, L. (1976). Research of Interest. Journal of Learning Disabilities, 9(10), 648–650. https://doi.org/10.1177/002221947600901007
papers.summary: This critical analysis reviewed ten existing sleep-learning studies, concluding that there was insufficient acceptable evidence to support the possibility of learning during sleep. The authors argued that even when positive results were reported, they could not be definitively attributed to 'sleep-learning' due to methodological issues or the possibility of subjects being in a light, waking state. This paper casts significant doubt on the validity of earlier sleep-learning claims.

papers.title: Wood et al. 1992
papers.apa: Simon, C. W., & Emmons, W. H. (1955). Learning during sleep? Psychological Bulletin, 52(4), 328–342. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0043733
papers.summary: This study investigated implicit learning during sleep using homophone and category-instance tasks, as well as recognition tests. The results consistently showed that presentation of items affected implicit memory and recognition in waking controls but not in sleeping subjects, indicating an absence of implicit learning during sleep (e.g., ηp²=0.85 for homophone test, ηp²=0.77 for category-instance task). The findings suggest that complex associative learning and explicit memory formation do not occur during sleep.

papers.title: ‘The Doctor Fox lecture: A paradigm of educational seduction’
papers.apa: Naftulin, D. H., Ware, J. E., & Donnelly, F. A. (1973). The Doctor Fox Lecture. Academic Medicine, 48(7), 630–635. https://doi.org/10.1097/00001888-197307000-00003
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced the Dr. Fox effect, demonstrating that a charismatic lecturer delivering a lecture devoid of meaningful content could still receive highly favorable student evaluations. The authors proposed that students were 'seduced' into believing they had learned something significant, highlighting the influence of presentation style over substance in educational settings.

papers.title: Peer and Babad 2014
papers.apa: Peer, E., & Babad, E. (2014). The Doctor Fox research (1973) rerevisited: “Educational seduction” ruled out. Journal of Educational Psychology, 106(1), 36–45. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0033827
papers.summary: This study replicated the Dr. Fox effect, finding that students (N=247) consistently gave favorable evaluations to the charismatic lecturer despite the lack of content (χ²(1) = 5.57, p < .05, Cohen's w = 0.35). However, the authors ruled out the 'educational seduction' hypothesis, demonstrating that while students enjoyed the lecture, they were not seduced into believing they had learned significantly. The study explored alternative explanations like acquiescence and status bias, finding they did not account for the favorable ratings.

papers.title: Ware and Williams 1975
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., Taylor, D., & Garrity, G. M. (2010). Exemplar Abstract for Gluconacetobacter europaeus corrig. (Sievers et al. 1992) Yamada et al. 1998, Gluconoacetobacter europaeus (sic) (Sievers et al. 1992) Yamada et al. 1998, Komagataeibacter europaeus (Sievers et al. 1992) Yamada et al. 2013 emend. Hördt et al. 2020 and Acetobacter europaeus Sievers et al. 1992. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.867
papers.summary: This replication study (N=201) confirmed the Dr. Fox effect, showing that presentation style significantly influenced student ratings (ωp² = 0.27). However, it also identified an interaction where medium-sized content combined with positive characteristics led to the highest overall ratings (ωp² = 0.03), suggesting that content is not entirely irrelevant. This indicates a more nuanced relationship between presentation, content, and student evaluations.

papers.effect_name: Stress as the main/sole cause of peptic ulcers
papers.title: Chronic stress and peptic ulcer
papers.apa: Ware, J. E., & Williams, R. G. (1975). The Dr. Fox effect. Academic Medicine, 50(2), 149–156. https://doi.org/10.1097/00001888-197502000-00006
papers.summary: This foundational work proposed that chronic emotional and physical stress is the main cause of peptic ulcers. It suggested a hormonal mechanism involving the adrenal gland and the hypothalamic-pituitary-gastric pathway as the means by which stress induces gastrointestinal ulceration.

papers.effect_name: Stress as the main/sole cause of peptic ulcers
papers.title: Skillman et al. 1969
papers.apa: Skillman, J. J., Bushnell, L. S., Goldman, H., & Silen, W. (1969). Respiratory failure, hypotension, sepsis, and jaundice. The American Journal of Surgery, 117(4), 523–530. https://doi.org/10.1016/0002-9610(69)90011-7
papers.summary: This paper described stress ulceration and suggested that increased secretion of acid may be an important cause of this disease. While acknowledging stress, it highlights a physiological mechanism (acid secretion) as a key factor.

papers.effect_name: Stress as the main/sole cause of peptic ulcers
papers.title: Marshall and Warren 1983
papers.apa: Gray, S. J. (1951). CHRONIC STRESS AND PEPTIC ULCER. Journal of the American Medical Association, 147(16), 1529. https://doi.org/10.1001/jama.1951.03670330021007
papers.summary: This seminal paper identified the bacterium _Helicobacter pylori_ as strongly correlated with peptic ulcers. The authors found _H. pylori_ in almost all patients with active chronic gastritis, duodenal ulcer, or gastric ulcer, suggesting it is a major etiological factor, thereby challenging stress as the *sole* cause.

papers.effect_name: Stress as the main/sole cause of peptic ulcers
papers.title: Gough et al. 1984
papers.apa: Tulchinsky, T. H. (2018). Warren, Marshall, Helicobacter Pylori, Peptic Ulcers and Gastric Cancer. Case Studies in Public Health, 543–560. https://doi.org/10.1016/b978-0-12-804571-8.00006-8
papers.summary: This study provided further evidence for the bacterial cause of ulcers by demonstrating that treating ulcers with antibiotics significantly reduced recurrence rates by approximately 90-95%. This finding strongly supported the role of _H. pylori_ over stress as the primary cause.

papers.effect_name: Stress as the main/sole cause of peptic ulcers
papers.title: Levenstein et al. 1997
papers.apa: Erratum: Gough et al (2017). (2018). International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism, 28(2), 220–220. https://doi.org/10.1123/ijsnem.2018-0038
papers.summary: This research investigated the association between psychological factors and incident ulcers. It found that five baseline psychological measures (depression, hostility, ego resiliency, social alienation, and personal uncertainty) had significant age-adjusted associations with incident ulcer, with odds ratios ranging from 1.8 to 2.6.

papers.effect_name: Stress as the main/sole cause of peptic ulcers
papers.title: Levenstein et al. 1999
papers.apa: Langdon, D. (2000). Re: Levenstein et al.—ulcerative colitis in remission and stress. The American Journal of Gastroenterology, 95(12), 3653. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0002-9270(00)02188-2
papers.summary: This paper concluded that in most ulcer cases where stress is involved, _H. pylori_ is likely to be present as well. The authors suggested that the impact of stress and _H. pylori_ on ulcer development may be additive, indicating a more complex etiology than stress alone.

papers.effect_name: Stress as the main/sole cause of peptic ulcers
papers.title: Levenstein et al. 2015
papers.apa: Levenstein, S., Rosenstock, S., Jacobsen, R. K., & Jorgensen, T. (2015). Psychological Stress Increases Risk for Peptic Ulcer, Regardless of Helicobacter pylori Infection or Use of Nonsteroidal Anti-inflammatory Drugs. Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology, 13(3), 498-506.e1. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cgh.2014.07.052
papers.summary: This study found that ulcer incidence was significantly higher among subjects in the highest tertile of stress scores (3.5%) compared to the lowest tertile (1.6%), with an adjusted odds ratio of 2.2 (95% CI, 1.2–3.9; P < .01). Importantly, stress had similar effects on ulcers associated with _H. pylori_ infection and those unrelated to either _H. pylori_ or NSAID use, suggesting an independent role for stress.

papers.title: Cigarette graphic warning labels and smoking prevalence in Canada: a critical examination and reformulation of the FDA regulatory impact analysis
papers.apa: Huang, J., Chaloupka, F. J., & Fong, G. T. (2013). Cigarette graphic warning labels and smoking prevalence in Canada: a critical examination and reformulation of the FDA regulatory impact analysis. Tobacco Control, 23(suppl 1), i7–i12. https://doi.org/10.1136/tobaccocontrol-2013-051170
papers.summary: This foundational paper provided initial significant estimates for the effect of graphic warning labels (GWL) on smoking prevalence, reporting reductions between 12.1% and 19.6% (β=−0.13 to β=−0.22). It likely established these figures through a critical examination and reformulation of existing regulatory impact analyses, serving as a key reference for the effect's magnitude.

papers.title: Harris et al. 2015
papers.apa: Harris, J. E., Balsa, A. I., & Triunfo, P. (2015). Tobacco control campaign in Uruguay: Impact on smoking cessation during pregnancy and birth weight. Journal of Health Economics, 42, 186–196. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhealeco.2015.04.002
papers.summary: This study investigated the impact of graphic warning labels on the probability of quitting smoking during pregnancy. It reported estimated effects ranging from β=0.028 (non-significant) to β=0.038, indicating some positive but mixed findings for this specific population and outcome.

papers.title: Shang et al. 2017
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2017). Nomenclature Abstract for Serratia bozhouensis Shang et al. 2017. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/nm.30445
papers.summary: This paper examined the association between graphic warnings and cigarette smoking prevalence, finding a significant 10.0% lower prevalence (OR = 0.89 [0.81, 0.97], p ≤ 0.01) among adults with less than a secondary education or no formal education, thus replicating the effect for this specific demographic.

papers.title: Beleche et al. 2018
papers.apa: Langdon, D. (2000). Re: Levenstein et al.—ulcerative colitis in remission and stress. The American Journal of Gastroenterology, 95(12), 3653. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0002-9270(00)02188-2
papers.summary: This study explored the effects of graphic warning labels, reporting mixed results across different regression models. While some models showed significant reductions in smoking prevalence (up to β=−0.22), others did not (β=−0.03), leading to a conclusion of partial replication.

papers.effect_name: Stereotype threat on gender differences in political knowledge
papers.title: Gender Differences in Political Knowledge: Bringing Situation Back In
papers.apa: Ihme, T. A., & Tausendpfund, M. (2017). Gender Differences in Political Knowledge: Bringing Situation Back In. Journal of Experimental Political Science, 5(1), 39–55. https://doi.org/10.1017/xps.2017.21
papers.summary: This foundational paper investigated whether stereotype threat contributes to observed gender differences in political knowledge. Across two studies, the authors found that when gender stereotypes about political knowledge were made salient, women performed significantly worse on political knowledge tests compared to control conditions, while men's performance was unaffected. This suggested that situational factors, specifically stereotype threat, could explain a portion of the gender gap in political knowledge, with reported effect sizes of partial η2 = 0.12 and 0.03.

papers.title: Not for All the Tea in China!” Political Ideology and the Avoidance of Dissonance-Arousing Situations
papers.apa: Nam, H. H., Jost, J. T., & Van Bavel, J. J. (2013). “Not for All the Tea in China!” Political Ideology and the Avoidance of Dissonance-Arousing Situations. PLoS ONE, 8(4), e59837. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0059837
papers.summary: This foundational paper investigated whether conservatives are more motivated to avoid dissonance-arousing tasks than liberals. Across two experiments (Study 1 N=180), the authors found that supporters of conservative presidents (Bush, Reagan) were less likely than supporters of liberal presidents (Obama, Clinton) to comply with requests to write counter-attitudinal essays under high perceived choice, specifically for political topics (e.g., Study 1 _b_ = -2.36, _b_ = -.31). This difference was not observed for non-political topics or under low perceived choice, suggesting ideological differences in dissonance avoidance specific to the political domain.

papers.title: Brandt and Crawford 2013
papers.apa: Ryals, B. M. (2018). Response to Iliadou et al. 2018 and Keith et al. 2018. Ear & Hearing, 39(6), 1243–1244. https://doi.org/10.1097/aud.0000000000000654
papers.summary: This replication attempt investigated the ideological asymmetry in dissonance avoidance. The authors found non-significant effects for ideology on compliance behavior (e.g., _b_ = -0.02) and its interactions with essay topic or type (e.g., _b_ = 0.06, _b_ = 0.05, _b_ = -0.002). These findings suggest that the original effect of ideological differences in dissonance avoidance was not replicated.

papers.title: Sex, age, and equity behavior
papers.apa: Leventhal, G. S., & Lane, D. W. (1970). Sex, age, and equity behavior. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 15(4), 312–316. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0029611
papers.summary: This foundational paper is credited with establishing the depressed-entitlement effect, proposing that women pay themselves less than men for equivalent work in the absence of clear performance standards.

papers.title: Callahan-Levy and Messe 1979
papers.apa: Nam, H. H., Jost, J. T., & Van Bavel, J. J. (2013). “Not for All the Tea in China!” Political Ideology and the Avoidance of Dissonance-Arousing Situations. PLoS ONE, 8(4), e59837. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0059837
papers.summary: This study replicated the depressed-entitlement effect, showing that women allocated less pay to themselves than men did for the same work. Study 1 found Target X Sex of Target interaction effects on actual pay (ηp2 = 0.16, d = 0.43) and fair pay estimates (ηp2 = 0.16, d = 0.41), while Study 2 found main effects of sex on pay allocation (ηp2 = 0.18, d = 0.47) and fair pay estimates (ηp2 = 0.11, d = 0.35).

papers.title: Jost 1997
papers.apa: Jost, J. T. (1997). An Experimental Replication of the Depressed-Entitlement Effect Among Women. Psychology of Women Quarterly, 21(3), 387–393. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1471-6402.1997.tb00120.x
papers.summary: This experimental study replicated the depressed-entitlement effect in a sample of 132 university students (68 men, 64 women), finding that women paid themselves less than men for objectively similar work (ηp2 = 0.044, d = 0.21). The findings are interpreted within a system-justification framework, suggesting that members of disadvantaged groups may internalize justifications for their own disadvantage.

papers.title: Hogue and Yoder 2003
papers.apa: Hogue, M., & Yoder, J. D. (2003). The Role of Status in Producing Depressed Entitlement in Women’s and Men’s Pay Allocations. Psychology of Women Quarterly, 27(4), 330–337. https://doi.org/10.1111/1471-6402.00113
papers.summary: This study with 180 undergraduates confirmed the depressed entitlement effect in a control condition (ηp2 = 0.165, d = 0.87), where women paid themselves less than men. It further demonstrated that enhancing women's status through legitimation of their task abilities eliminated this pay gap, suggesting that status subordination plays a role in the effect.

papers.title: Lane and Messe 1971
papers.apa: Mierzowski, K. (1979). Hannover 1979 - Ein Messe-Kaleidoskop. It - Information Technology, 21(4), 198–203. https://doi.org/10.1515/itit-1979-0409
papers.summary: This replication study found evidence for the depressed-entitlement effect, observing main effects of sex on self-interest responses (ηp2 = 0.099, d = 0.33). The results align with the original finding that women tend to reward themselves less than men for comparable work.

papers.title: Major et al. 1984
papers.apa: Lane, I. M., & Messe, L. A. (1971). Equity and the distribution of rewards. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 20(1), 1–17. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0031684
papers.summary: This research replicated the depressed-entitlement effect across two studies. Study 1 showed a Sex X Social Comparison Condition interaction (ηp2 = 0.13, d = 0.38), while Study 2 found main effects of Sex on the amount of work for a fixed amount of money (ηp2 = 0.18, d = 0.47), both supporting the original finding.

papers.title: When White Voters Evaluate Black Candidates: The Processing Implications of Candidate Skin Color, Prejudice, and Self-Monitoring
papers.apa: Terkildsen, N. (1993). When White Voters Evaluate Black Candidates: The Processing Implications of Candidate Skin Color, Prejudice, and Self-Monitoring. American Journal of Political Science, 37(4), 1032. https://doi.org/10.2307/2111542
papers.summary: This foundational paper proposed that white voters evaluate Black political candidates less favorably than white candidates, with both light-skinned and dark-skinned Black candidates receiving lower ratings (0.7 and 0.6 points lower, respectively, on an 11-point scale, p<0.05). The study suggested that candidate skin color, voter prejudice, and self-monitoring might influence these evaluations.

papers.title: van Oosten et al. 2023
papers.apa: van Oosten, S., Mügge, L., & van der Pas, D. (2023). Race/Ethnicity in Candidate Experiments: a Meta-Analysis and the Case for Shared Identification. Acta Politica, 59(1), 19–41. https://doi.org/10.1057/s41269-022-00279-y
papers.summary: This meta-analysis synthesized findings from candidate experiments to investigate the role of race/ethnicity in voter evaluations. Across a broad range of studies, it found no significant evidence of discrimination against ethnic minority candidates by the general electorate (e.g., Black candidate coefficient: 0.007, not significant). Interestingly, it identified a slight preference for Asian candidates (coefficient: 0.008, p<0.05) and a significant same-race preference among minority voters (b = 0.079, p<0.05).

papers.title: Why Don´t Democracies Fight Each Other? An Experimental Assessment of the ”Political Incentive" Explanation.
papers.apa: Mintz, A., & Geva, N. (1993). Why Don’t Democracies Fight Each Other? Journal of Conflict Resolution, 37(3), 484–503. https://doi.org/10.1177/0022002793037003004
papers.summary: This foundational paper experimentally investigated the "political incentive" explanation for the democratic peace, proposing that citizens in democracies are less willing to approve war against other democracies. Across three experiments, the study found significantly lower approval for using force against democracies compared to autocracies (e.g., -1.73 to -3.06 on a 0-10 scale), supporting the hypothesis.

papers.title: Tomz and Weeks 2013
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., Wigley, S., & Garrity, G. M. (2008). Exemplar Abstract for Leifsonia xyli (Davis et al. 1984) Evtushenko et al. 2000, Leifsonia xyli xyli (Davis et al. 1984) Evtushenko et al. 2000, Clavibacter xyli Davis et al. 1984 and Clavibacter xyli xyli Davis et al. 1984. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.6114
papers.summary: This replication study by Tomz and Weeks (2013) re-examined the experimental findings on citizen approval for war against democracies. Across three experiments, they consistently replicated the original effect, showing significantly lower approval to use force against democracies compared to autocracies (e.g., -13.3% to -11.4% approval), confirming the robustness of the initial findings.

papers.effect_name: Moral foundations across the political spectrum
papers.title: Liberals and conservatives rely on different sets of moral foundations
papers.apa: Graham, J., Haidt, J., & Nosek, B. A. (2009). Liberals and conservatives rely on different sets of moral foundations. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 96(5), 1029–1046. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0015141
papers.summary: This foundational paper introduced or significantly developed Moral Foundations Theory, proposing that human morality is based on a limited set of innate, universal moral foundations. It posits that liberals primarily emphasize Care and Fairness, while conservatives value all five foundations (Care, Fairness, Loyalty, Authority, Purity) more equally, explaining differences in political moral reasoning.

papers.effect_name: Moral foundations across the political spectrum
papers.title: Curry 2019
papers.apa: Graham, J., Nosek, B. A., Haidt, J., Iyer, R., Koleva, S., & Ditto, P. H. (2011). Mapping the moral domain. Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 101(2), 366–385. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0021847
papers.summary: This paper critically examines Moral Foundations Theory, arguing that it suffers from several methodological and conceptual flaws. Curry points out issues such as an ad hoc selection of moral values, the omission of key types of cooperation relevant to morality, and a flawed questionnaire that confounds moral relevance with agreement, thus questioning the validity of MFT's empirical measures.

papers.effect_name: Moral foundations across the political spectrum
papers.title: Davis et al. 2019
papers.apa: Review of Davis et al. (2019). (2019). https://doi.org/10.5194/gmd-2019-238-rc2
papers.summary: This study investigated the applicability of Moral Foundations Theory to Black Americans, finding that the theory's structure did not replicate well in Black samples (β = 0.19) compared to White participants (β = 0.47). The authors concluded that MFT, particularly its six foundations, may be biased towards White American morality and fail to adequately capture the moral concerns of Black individuals.

papers.title: When Corrections Fail: The Persistence of Political Misconceptions
papers.apa: Nyhan, B., & Reifler, J. (2010). When Corrections Fail: The Persistence of Political Misperceptions. Political Behavior, 32(2), 303–330. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11109-010-9112-2
papers.summary: This foundational study conducted four experiments using mock news articles to test the efficacy of corrections on political misperceptions. The authors found that corrections frequently failed to reduce misperceptions among targeted ideological groups and, in several instances, actually increased misperceptions (the 'backfire effect') among those most committed to the original belief. The study suggests motivated reasoning as a mechanism for this persistence, with original effect sizes for backfire ranging from b = 0.198 to b = 0.359.

papers.title: Wood and Porter 2019
papers.apa: Wood, T., & Porter, E. (2018). The Elusive Backfire Effect: Mass Attitudes’ Steadfast Factual Adherence. Political Behavior, 41(1), 135–163. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11109-018-9443-y
papers.summary: This large-scale replication study (N=3127) investigated the backfire effect across various political issues using Amazon's Mechanical Turk. The authors found no evidence of a backfire effect; instead, the average corrected subject increased their agreement with the facts, regardless of ideology. They conclude that backfire is 'stubbornly difficult to induce' and suggest that previous findings might be due to sample selection issues, such as university students being more prone to cognitive effort.

papers.title: Political extremism predicts belief in conspiracy theories
papers.apa: van Prooijen, J.-W., Krouwel, A. P. M., & Pollet, T. V. (2015). Political Extremism Predicts Belief in Conspiracy Theories. Social Psychological and Personality Science, 6(5), 570–578. https://doi.org/10.1177/1948550614567356
papers.summary: This foundational study empirically tested whether extreme political ideologies (left and right) are associated with increased conspiracy beliefs. Across four studies in the US and Netherlands (N=207, 1010, 1297, 268), a quadratic relationship between political ideology strength and conspiracy beliefs was found (β = .58, .35, .53, .70). The authors concluded that political extremism and conspiracy beliefs are strongly associated due to a structured thinking style.

papers.title: Bartlett and Miller
papers.apa: TOMZ, M. R., & WEEKS, J. L. P. (2013). Public Opinion and the Democratic Peace. American Political Science Review, 107(4), 849–865. https://doi.org/10.1017/s0003055413000488
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Imhoff
papers.summary: This large-scale, cross-sectional study across 26 countries (N=104,253) investigated the relationship between political orientation and conspiracy mentality. The authors found a significant quadratic relationship (Study 1: β=0.062, p=0.001; Study 2: β=0.220, p<0.001), providing support for the predicted quadratic effect.

papers.title: Van der Linden
papers.apa: van der Linden, S., Panagopoulos, C., Azevedo, F., & Jost, J. T. (2020). The Paranoid Style in American Politics Revisited: An Ideological Asymmetry in Conspiratorial Thinking. Political Psychology, 42(1), 23–51. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/pops.12681
papers.summary: This paper investigated the relationship between political ideology and conspiratorial thinking in the US, challenging the idea of equal intensity across the spectrum. Across four large studies (total N=5049), results showed that US conservatives were more likely than liberals to endorse specific conspiracy theories and espouse general conspiratorial worldviews (r=.27). Extreme conservatives were significantly more likely to engage in conspiratorial thinking than extreme liberals (Hedges' g=.77), with distrust of officialdom mediating this relationship.

papers.title: Van et al. 2015
papers.apa: van Prooijen, J.-W., Krouwel, A. P. M., Boiten, M., & Eendebak, L. (2015). Fear Among the Extremes. Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin, 41(4), 485–497. https://doi.org/10.1177/0146167215569706
papers.summary: This study, by the original lead author, investigated whether political extremes (left and right) are more likely to experience fear and derogate outgroups than moderates. A large-scale sample revealed a quadratic effect on socio-economic fear and derogation of various societal categories, mediated by socio-economic fear. While related to the psychology of political extremes, this paper focuses on fear and outgroup derogation rather than directly replicating or critiquing the conspiracy mentality effect.

papers.effect_name: Voters elect rather than affect policies
papers.title: Do Voters Affect or Elect Policies? Evidence from the U.S. House
papers.apa: Button, P. (2017). A Replication of “Do Voters Affect or Elect Policies? Evidence from the U.S. House” (Quarterly Journal of Economics, 2004). Public Finance Review, 46(5), 886–893. https://doi.org/10.1177/1091142117721739
papers.summary: This foundational study introduced the 'Voters Elect, Not Affect Policies' effect, proposing that voters primarily elect politicians with aligned policy preferences rather than influencing the policies of incumbents. Using U.S. House data, the authors found a significant 'elect' component (22.84, SE: 2.2) but no significant 'affect' component (-1.64, SE: 2.0), supporting their hypothesis.

papers.effect_name: Voters elect rather than affect policies
papers.title: Button 2017
papers.apa: Button, P. (2017). A Replication of “Do Voters Affect or Elect Policies? Evidence from the U.S. House” (Quarterly Journal of Economics, 2004). Public Finance Review, 46(5), 886–893. https://doi.org/10.1177/1091142117721739
papers.summary: This paper replicated the original 2004 study using updated regression discontinuity design methodologies, including nonparametric methods with optimal bandwidths and bias correction, and extended data up to the 2012 election. Across various model specifications, the replication consistently found results identical to the original, confirming the significant 'elect' component and the non-significant 'affect' component of voter influence.

papers.title: The nature of the relationship between personality traits and political attitudes
papers.apa: Verhulst, B., Hatemi, P. K., & Martin, N. G. (2010). The nature of the relationship between personality traits and political attitudes. Personality and Individual Differences, 49(4), 306–316. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2009.11.013
papers.summary: This entry appears to describe the general findings of the original research, specifically the correlations between general ideology (higher scores indicating more liberal) and personality traits like Psychoticism (r = -.495, indicating more conservative), Extraversion (r = -.061), Neuroticism (r = -.008), and Social Desirability (r = .261, indicating more liberal). These findings were later identified as containing a coding error in the foundational paper.

papers.title: Verhulst et al. 2012
papers.apa: Verhulst, B., Eaves, L. J., & Hatemi, P. K. (2011). Correlation not Causation: The Relationship between Personality Traits and Political Ideologies. American Journal of Political Science, 56(1), 34–51. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1540-5907.2011.00568.x
papers.summary: This foundational paper originally reported correlations between personality traits and political attitudes. Key findings, as later described in a correction, included that higher Eysenck’s Psychoticism was associated with more conservative views, and higher Neuroticism and Social Desirability were associated with more liberal views. These findings were subsequently identified to contain a coding error.

papers.title: Ludeke and Rasmussen 2016
papers.apa: Ludeke, S. G., & Rasmussen, S. H. R. (2016). Personality correlates of sociopolitical attitudes in the Big Five and Eysenckian models. Personality and Individual Differences, 98, 30–36. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.paid.2016.03.079
papers.summary: This paper reviewed the literature on personality and political attitudes, concluding that conservatives are typically higher in Conscientiousness and behavioral constraint, and lower in Openness. For Eysenckian measures, the authors found that higher Psychoticism correlated negatively with conservative self-placement (r = -.22), indicating more liberal views, and the Lie scale (social desirability) correlated positively with conservative self-placement (r = .10), indicating more conservative views. These findings reversed several of the originally reported relationships.

papers.title: Verhulst et al. 2016
papers.apa: BARTLETT v. BARTLETT. (1876). Victorian Reports, 21 VLR, 366–368. https://doi.org/10.25291/vr/21-vlr-366
papers.summary: This paper served as a correction to the authors' own 2012 foundational manuscript, identifying a coding error. The corrected analysis revealed that individuals higher in Eysenck’s Psychoticism are actually more liberal (reversing the original report), and those higher in Neuroticism and Social Desirability are, in fact, more conservative (also reversing the original reports). This paper directly confirmed the 'reversed' status of the original findings.

papers.title: Use of experimenter-given cues during object-choice tasks by capuchin monkeys
papers.apa: Anderson, J. R., Sallaberry, P., & Barbier, H. (1995). Use of experimenter-given cues during object-choice tasks by capuchin monkeys. Animal Behaviour, 49(1), 201–208. https://doi.org/10.1016/0003-3472(95)80168-5
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether capuchin monkeys could use human gaze cues to locate hidden food in an object-choice task. The findings indicated that capuchins generally failed to follow the experimenter's gaze to choose the correct container, suggesting a limited ability to spontaneously use human gaze as an informative cue in this context.

papers.title: Anderson et al. 1996
papers.apa: Anderson, J. R., Montant, M., & Schmitt, D. (1996). Rhesus monkeys fail to use gaze direction as an experimenter-given cue in an object-choice task. Behavioural Processes, 37(1), 47–55. https://doi.org/10.1016/0376-6357(95)00074-7

papers.title: Emery et al. 1997
papers.apa: Review of Verhulst et al. (2017). (2017). https://doi.org/10.5194/acp-2016-850-rc2
papers.summary: This study investigated gaze following in rhesus macaques, demonstrating that these monkeys could follow the gaze of a human experimenter to a specific location, even when the target was out of their immediate visual field. Unlike some findings with capuchins, rhesus macaques showed evidence of sophisticated gaze following, particularly to distant targets.

papers.title: ‘Use of experimenter-given cues during object-choice tasks by capuchin monkeys’
papers.apa: Anderson, J. R., Sallaberry, P., & Barbier, H. (1995). Use of experimenter-given cues during object-choice tasks by capuchin monkeys. Animal Behaviour, 49(1), 201–208. https://doi.org/10.1016/0003-3472(95)80168-5
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Kumashiro et al. 2003
papers.apa: Kumashiro, M., Ishibashi, H., Uchiyama, Y., Itakura, S., Murata, A., & Iriki, A. (2003). Natural imitation induced by joint attention in Japanese monkeys. International Journal of Psychophysiology, 50(1–2), 81–99. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0167-8760(03)00126-0
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Miklosi & Soproni 2005
papers.apa: Miklósi, Á., & Soproni, K. (2005). A comparative analysis of animals’ understanding of the human pointing gesture. Animal Cognition, 9(2), 81–93. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-005-0008-1
papers.summary: This review paper systematically compares experimental methods and results on animal comprehension of human pointing gestures across various species. It evaluates different hypotheses for observed species differences, such as competitive lifestyles, domestication, and conditioning, arguing against simplistic explanations. The authors advocate for more systematic comparative research to understand the complex communicative and representational abilities underlying pointing comprehension.

papers.title: Chimpanzee gaze following in an object-choice task
papers.apa: Call, J., Hare, B. A., & Tomasello, M. (1998). Chimpanzee gaze following in an object-choice task. Animal Cognition, 1(2), 89–99. https://doi.org/10.1007/s100710050013
papers.summary: This study with six adult female chimpanzees found they followed a human's gaze to distal locations (75% of trials), often checking back to the human's face when nothing was found. However, in an object-choice task, chimpanzees failed to use the human's gaze to choose food hidden under bowls (56.9% correct, not above chance, t(5)=1.19, p=0.14), though they succeeded with tubes/barriers where the experimenter could see. The authors concluded that simple orientation or a full understanding of 'seeing-knowing' were insufficient explanations for their findings.

papers.title: Itakura (2004)
papers.apa: ITAKURA, S. (2004). Gaze‐following and joint visual attention in nonhuman animals. Japanese Psychological Research, 46(3), 216–226. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1468-5584.2004.00253.x
papers.summary: This paper reviews studies on gaze-following and joint visual attention in nonhuman animals. It suggests that while monkeys and apes can follow gaze, only apes demonstrate an understanding of the 'seeing-knowing' relationship in competitive scenarios. The review also notes that domestic animals may acquire gaze-following abilities with humans due to selective breeding, emphasizing the need to clarify social gaze parameters across species.

papers.title: Kano et al. 2018
papers.apa: Kano, F., Moore, R., Krupenye, C., Hirata, S., Tomonaga, M., & Call, J. (2018). Human ostensive signals do not enhance gaze following in chimpanzees, but do enhance object-oriented attention. Animal Cognition, 21(5), 715–728. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-018-1205-z
papers.summary: Across three eye-tracking experiments (N=15, 12, 19 chimpanzees), this study investigated if human ostensive signals enhance gaze following. Chimpanzees followed the actor's gaze in both ostension and control conditions, but ostensive signals did not significantly enhance gaze following (e.g., Exp 1: F(1,14)=3.06, p=0.10 for first-look). However, ostensive signals did enhance subsequent attention to both target and distractor objects. The authors suggest chimpanzees have limited communicative expectations from ostensive signals, unlike human infants and dogs.

papers.title: Production and comprehension of referential pointing by orangutans (Pongo pygmaeus)
papers.apa: Call, J., & Tomasello, M. (1994). Production and comprehension of referential pointing by orangutans (Pongo pygmaeus). Journal of Comparative Psychology, 108(4), 307–317. https://doi.org/10.1037/0735-7036.108.4.307
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification. This paper is presumed to be the original study that established some form of pointing comprehension or production in orangutans, laying the groundwork for further research into primate understanding of human gestures.

papers.title: Miklosi & Soproni 2005
papers.apa: Miklósi, Á., & Soproni, K. (2005). A comparative analysis of animals’ understanding of the human pointing gesture. Animal Cognition, 9(2), 81–93. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-005-0008-1
papers.summary: This paper provides a comprehensive review and comparative analysis of studies on animal comprehension of human pointing gestures, evaluating experimental methods and results across various species. The authors discuss different hypotheses explaining species differences in this ability, arguing against simplistic views and advocating for more systematic comparative research to understand the underlying communicative and representational abilities.

papers.title: Clark et al. 2019
papers.apa: Clark, H., Elsherif, M. M., & Leavens, D. A. (2019). Ontogeny vs. phylogeny in primate/canid comparisons: A meta-analysis of the object choice task. Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews, 105, 178–189. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neubiorev.2019.06.001
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification. This paper is a meta-analysis synthesizing findings on pointing comprehension in nonhuman primates, reporting effect sizes for temporal cue properties (r=.30) and ipsilateral pointing cues (r=.28).

papers.title: ‘Domestic dogs (canis familiaris) use human and conspecific social cues to locate hidden food’
papers.apa: Hare, B., & Tomasello, M. (1999). Domestic dogs (Canis familiaris) use human and conspecific social cues to locate hidden food. Journal of Comparative Psychology, 113(2), 173–177. https://doi.org/10.1037/0735-7036.113.2.173
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: Itakura 2004
papers.apa: ITAKURA, S. (2004). Gaze‐following and joint visual attention in nonhuman animals. Japanese Psychological Research, 46(3), 216–226. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1468-5584.2004.00253.x
papers.summary: This paper reviews existing studies on gaze-following and joint visual attention in nonhuman animals, including domestic dogs. It discusses findings suggesting that domestic animals like dogs and horses have acquired the ability to follow human gaze, potentially due to selective breeding. The author emphasizes the need to clarify social gaze parameters across species to better understand the evolution of attention and mental state processing.

papers.title: Teglas et al. 2012
papers.apa: Bates, P. (2019). Review of Emery et al. https://doi.org/10.5194/hess-2019-242-rc3
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.title: ’Use of experimenter-given cues in dogs’
papers.apa: Scheider, L., Grassmann, S., Kaminski, J., & Tomasello, M. (2011). Domestic Dogs Use Contextual Information and Tone of Voice when following a Human Pointing Gesture. PLoS ONE, 6(7), e21676. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0021676
papers.summary: This study (N=48 dogs) investigated how contextual information and tone of voice influence dogs' responses to human pointing gestures. They found that dogs searched longer when a pointing gesture was present, especially in a previously established food-related context (F(1,44) = 5.087, p = 0.029), and searched more in informative (high-pitched) trials compared to imperative (low-pitched) ones (F(1,44) = 6.753, p = 0.013). The findings suggest dogs' interpretation of pointing is flexible and influenced by communicative context.

papers.title: McKinley & Sambrook 2000
papers.apa: McKinley, J., & Sambrook, T. D. (2000). Use of human-given cues by domestic dogs ( Canis familiaris ) and horses ( Equus caballus ). Animal Cognition, 3(1), 13–22. https://doi.org/10.1007/s100710050046
papers.summary: This study tested 16 domestic dogs and 4 horses on their ability to use human manual and facial cues in an object-choice task. It found that 12 of the 16 dogs successfully used pointing as a cue, performing significantly better in experimental conditions than control trials. The authors also noted that working gundogs and gundog breeds performed better, suggesting cognitive ability, domestication, and enculturation influence this skill.

papers.title: Miklosi and Soproni 2005
papers.apa: Miklósi, Á., & Soproni, K. (2005). A comparative analysis of animals’ understanding of the human pointing gesture. Animal Cognition, 9(2), 81–93. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-005-0008-1
papers.summary: This paper provides a comprehensive review and 3-step analysis of studies on animals' understanding of human pointing gestures. It compares experimental methods and results across species, evaluating current hypotheses regarding the function, evolution, and mechanisms of this ability. The authors argue against simplistic explanations like competitive ape lifestyles or simple conditioning, advocating for more systematic comparative research.

papers.title: Schneider et al. 2011
papers.apa: Scheider, L., Grassmann, S., Kaminski, J., & Tomasello, M. (2011). Domestic Dogs Use Contextual Information and Tone of Voice when following a Human Pointing Gesture. PLoS ONE, 6(7), e21676. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0021676
papers.summary: This study (N=48 dogs) investigated how contextual information and tone of voice influence dogs' responses to human pointing gestures. They found that dogs searched longer when a pointing gesture was present, especially in a previously established food-related context (F(1,44) = 5.087, p = 0.029), and searched more in informative (high-pitched) trials compared to imperative (low-pitched) ones (F(1,44) = 6.753, p = 0.013). The findings suggest dogs' interpretation of pointing is flexible and influenced by communicative context.

papers.title: Clark et al. 2019
papers.apa: Clark, H., Elsherif, M. M., & Leavens, D. A. (2019). Ontogeny vs. phylogeny in primate/canid comparisons: A meta-analysis of the object choice task. Neuroscience & Biobehavioral Reviews, 105, 178–189. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neubiorev.2019.06.001
papers.summary: This meta-analysis investigated factors influencing dogs' use of pointing cues. It found that dogs categorized as “close” (N=174) scored significantly higher (Mdn Z=1.26) than “seldom” (N=14) for within-dog pointing cues (U=13.97, p<.001). For contralateral pointing, “occasional” (N=95) outperformed “close” (N=6) (U=136.5, p=0.029), and overall “close” (N=356) scored higher than “seldom” (N=22) (U=1235.5, p<.001).

papers.title: Human-Socialized Wolves Follow Diverse Human Gestures... And They May Not Be Alone
papers.apa: Udell, M. A. R., Spencer, J. M., Dorey, N. R., & Wynne, C. D. L. (2012). Human-Socialized Wolves Follow Diverse Human Gestures… And They May Not Be Alone. International Journal of Comparative Psychology, 25(2). https://doi.org/10.46867/ijcp.2012.25.02.04
papers.summary: This foundational paper likely established the methodology for testing canids, including coyotes, in an object-choice task to assess their ability to follow human pointing gestures. The specific findings regarding coyotes are not detailed in the provided text, but it served as a methodological basis for subsequent research.

papers.title: Floyd and Young 2021
papers.apa: Floyd, N., & Young, J. K. (2020). Testing coyotes in an object choice task following a human gesture. Ethology Ecology & Evolution, 33(4), 394–405. https://doi.org/10.1080/03949370.2020.1837966
papers.summary: This study tested 12 captive coyotes (5 hand-reared, 7 coyote-reared) on their ability to follow human distal-pointing gestures in an object-choice task. Only one hand-reared male coyote performed better than chance (8/10 trials), and overall performance declined over time. The authors concluded that most coyotes do not respond to human gestures, suggesting that early human socialization does not significantly improve this ability in coyotes, possibly due to neophobia or anxiety.

papers.title: Cues to food location that domestic dogs (Canis familiaris) of different ages do and do not use
papers.apa: Agnetta, B., Hare, B., & Tomasello, M. (2000). Cues to food location that domestic dogs (Canis familiaris) of different ages do and do not use. Animal Cognition, 3(2), 107–112. https://doi.org/10.1007/s100710000070
papers.summary: This foundational study primarily investigated domestic dogs' ability to use human cues to locate hidden food. In a small pilot study involving two arctic wolves (N=2), the authors reported that these wolves were unable to use human cues to locate hidden food in an object-choice task, suggesting a unique specialization in dogs.

papers.title: Lampe et al. 2017
papers.apa: Lampe, M., Bräuer, J., Kaminski, J., & Virányi, Z. (2017). The effects of domestication and ontogeny on cognition in dogs and wolves. Scientific Reports, 7(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-017-12055-6
papers.summary: This study compared captive wolves (n=12), captive dogs (n=14), and pet dogs (n=12) on various cognitive tasks, including communicative and behavioral cues in object-choice tasks. It found that all three groups performed similarly in the communicative and behavioral conditions, implying that wolves, like dogs, can utilize these types of human cues.

papers.title: Range and Virányi 2011
papers.apa: Range, F., & Virányi, Z. (2011). Development of Gaze Following Abilities in Wolves (Canis Lupus). PLoS ONE, 6(2), e16888. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0016888
papers.summary: This study investigated the development of gaze following in nine hand-raised wolves (N=9). It found that wolves could successfully follow both conspecific and human gaze cues, even around barriers, with this ability reliably appearing at 6 months of age, directly challenging the earlier suggestion that wolves are unable to use human gaze cues.

papers.title: The Domestication of Social Cognition in Dogs
papers.apa: Hare, B., Brown, M., Williamson, C., & Tomasello, M. (2002). The Domestication of Social Cognition in Dogs. Science, 298(5598), 1634–1636. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1072702
papers.summary: This foundational study compared the social-cognitive abilities of human-raised wolves and domestic dog puppies in using human pointing gestures to find hidden food. The authors found that dog puppies, even with little human contact, successfully followed these cues, while similarly raised wolves did not. This led to the conclusion that domestication selected for unique social-cognitive skills in dogs, enabling special human-dog communication.

papers.title: Gácsi et al. 2009
papers.apa: Gácsi, M., Gyoöri, B., Virányi, Z., Kubinyi, E., Range, F., Belényi, B., & Miklósi, Á. (2009). Explaining Dog Wolf Differences in Utilizing Human Pointing Gestures: Selection for Synergistic Shifts in the Development of Some Social Skills. PLoS ONE, 4(8), e6584. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0006584
papers.summary: This study investigated dog-wolf differences in utilizing human pointing gestures, finding age-dependent variations. While 8-week-old dogs and wolves performed similarly with proximal pointing, 4-month-old pet dogs outperformed same-aged hand-reared wolves with distal pointing.
