However, socialized adult wolves eventually performed as well as dogs in distal momentary pointing, suggesting that while dogs show earlier emergence of this skill, adult wolves can also acquire it with sufficient socialization and willingness to cooperate.

papers.title: Lampe et al. 2017
papers.apa: Lampe, M., Bräuer, J., Kaminski, J., & Virányi, Z. (2017). The effects of domestication and ontogeny on cognition in dogs and wolves. Scientific Reports, 7(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-017-12055-6
papers.summary: This study compared captive wolves (n=12), pack dogs (n=14), and pet dogs (n=12) on object-choice tasks using communicative, behavioral, and causal cues. It observed that all three groups performed similarly in the communicative and behavioral conditions (including pointing), suggesting higher ontogenetic flexibility in these social domains. Interestingly, wolves outperformed dogs in following causal cues, indicating domestication might have altered specific skills in physical cognition.

papers.title: Miklósi et al. 2003
papers.apa: Miklósi, Á., Kubinyi, E., Topál, J., Gácsi, M., Virányi, Z., & Csányi, V. (2003). A Simple Reason for a Big Difference. Current Biology, 13(9), 763–766. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0960-9822(03)00263-x
papers.summary: This paper is often cited as foundational work on dog-wolf differences in social cognition, generally supporting the idea that dogs are better at following human cues than wolves. It contributes to the understanding of how domestication may have shaped canine social-cognitive abilities.

papers.title: Udell et al. 2008
papers.apa: Udell, M. A. R., Dorey, N. R., & Wynne, C. D. L. (2008). Wolves outperform dogs in following human social cues. Animal Behaviour, 76(6), 1767–1773. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.anbehav.2008.07.028
papers.summary: This study challenged the idea that domestication is a prerequisite for human-like social cognition in canids. It found that human-socialized wolves (N=8) tested outdoors successfully used momentary distal human pointing cues to find food without training, performing at or above the level of pet dogs tested indoors (N=8). In contrast, pet dogs tested outdoors and shelter dogs (N=8) did not follow the same points, suggesting that rearing environment and socialization play a crucial role, and wolves can indeed follow human pointing.

papers.title: Udell et al. 2012
papers.apa: Udell, M. A. R., Spencer, J. M., Dorey, N. R., & Wynne, C. D. L. (2012). Human-Socialized Wolves Follow Diverse Human Gestures… And They May Not Be Alone. International Journal of Comparative Psychology, 25(2). https://doi.org/10.46867/ijcp.2012.25.02.04
papers.summary: This paper further explored the social-cognitive abilities of human-socialized wolves, demonstrating their capacity to follow a diverse range of human gestures. It reinforced the argument that the ability to understand human cues is not exclusive to domestic dogs and is significantly influenced by individual rearing conditions and experiences.

papers.title: Virányi et al. 2008
papers.apa: Virányi, Z., Gácsi, M., Kubinyi, E., Topál, J., Belényi, B., Ujfalussy, D., & Miklósi, Á. (2008). Comprehension of human pointing gestures in young human-reared wolves (Canis lupus) and dogs (Canis familiaris). Animal Cognition, 11(3), 373–387. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-007-0127-y
papers.summary: This study compared human-reared wolf and dog pups, finding that 4-month-old dogs readily used momentary distal pointing to find hidden food, while wolves did not attend to this subtle cue. However, through extensive formal training over several months, the wolves were able to reach the same level of success as dogs in following momentary distal pointing. This suggests an initial species difference in spontaneous attention to cues, but also that wolves can acquire this skill with sufficient experience.

papers.title: Visual Cues Given by Humans Are Not Sufficient for Asian Elephants (Elephas maximus) to Find Hidden Food
papers.apa: Plotnik, J. M., Pokorny, J. J., Keratimanochaya, T., Webb, C., Beronja, H. F., Hennessy, A., Hill, J., Hill, V. J., Kiss, R., Maguire, C., Melville, B. L., Morrison, V. M. B., Seecoomar, D., Singer, B., Ukehaxhaj, J., Vlahakis, S. K., Ylli, D., Clayton, N. S., Roberts, J., … Getz, D. (2013). Visual Cues Given by Humans Are Not Sufficient for Asian Elephants (Elephas maximus) to Find Hidden Food. PLoS ONE, 8(4), e61174. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0061174
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether captive Asian elephants (n=7) could follow human visual cues (pointing, body orientation) to locate hidden food in an object-choice task. The elephants, as a group, failed to follow these visual cues, though they did respond to familiar vocal commands. The authors suggested this might support the domestication hypothesis or indicate a reliance on other sensory modalities, also noting a significant side bias in the elephants' choices.

papers.title: Ketchaisri et al. 2019
papers.apa: Ketchaisri, O., Siripunkaw, C., & Plotnik, J. M. (2019). The use of a human’s location and social cues by Asian elephants in an object-choice task. Animal Cognition, 22(6), 907–915. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-019-01283-0
papers.summary: This replication study (n=7 Asian elephants) adapted previous methodology to investigate human cue-following, specifically considering the experimenter's position. As a group, elephants failed to follow pointing or gazing cues when the experimenter was equidistant or closer to the incorrect bucket, but did follow cues when the experimenter was positioned closer to the correct bucket, suggesting local enhancement is a crucial complementary cue. The study also noted significant individual variability and side biases, calling for larger sample sizes.

papers.title: African Elephants Can Use Human Pointing Cues to Find Hidden Food
papers.apa: Smet, A. F., & Byrne, R. W. (2013). African Elephants Can Use Human Pointing Cues to Find Hidden Food. Current Biology, 23(20), 2033–2037. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2013.08.037
papers.summary: This foundational paper is reported to have first established that African elephants can follow human pointing cues to find hidden food. Specific details regarding methodology, sample size, and effect sizes are not available in the provided text.

papers.title: Smet and Byrne 2014
papers.apa: Smet, A. F., & Byrne, R. W. (2014). Interpretation of human pointing by African elephants: generalisation and rationality. Animal Cognition, 17(6), 1365–1374. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-014-0772-x
papers.summary: This study (N=8 African elephants) replicated and extended previous findings, demonstrating that elephants successfully follow human pointing cues to locate hidden food, even when the cues are unsustained or novel (e.g., foot pointing). Elephants chose the correct container significantly above chance in all conditions (e.g., M=0.80, t(7)=5.06, p=0.001 for sustained pointing), though performance was slightly reduced with unsustained cues. The authors concluded that elephants generalize their understanding of pointing, but do not evaluate the 'rationality' of the gesture.

papers.title: The use of human-given cues by domestic horses, Equus caballus, during an object choice task
papers.apa: Proops, L., Walton, M., & McComb, K. (2010). The use of human-given cues by domestic horses, Equus caballus, during an object choice task. Animal Behaviour, 79(6), 1205–1209. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.anbehav.2010.02.015
papers.summary: This foundational study tested 28 horses on their ability to use five human-given cues in an object choice task. Horses successfully used distal sustained pointing and marker placement cues (P < 0.0001, P = 0.001), but did not perform above chance with momentary tapping, body orientation, or gaze alternation cues (P > 0.3). The authors concluded that horses likely use stimulus enhancement rather than understanding communicative intent, positioning their ability as inferior to dogs but similar to goats.

papers.title: Proops et al. 2013
papers.apa: Proops, L., Rayner, J., Taylor, A. M., & McComb, K. (2013). The Responses of Young Domestic Horses to Human-Given Cues. PLoS ONE, 8(6), e67000. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0067000
papers.summary: This study investigated the ontogeny of human cue-reading skills in young domestic horses, comparing them to adults. In object choice tasks, young horses performed similarly to adults, successfully using marker placement, pointing, and touching cues, but failing to use body orientation, gaze, elbow pointing, or tapping cues. The findings suggest no innate predisposition for human cue-reading, with attentional skills requiring experience and communicative cue use reflecting general learning mechanisms like stimulus enhancement.

papers.title: Use of human-given cues by domestic dogs (Canis familiaris) and horses (Equus caballus)
papers.apa: McKinley, J., & Sambrook, T. D. (2000). Use of human-given cues by domestic dogs ( Canis familiaris ) and horses ( Equus caballus ). Animal Cognition, 3(1), 13–22. https://doi.org/10.1007/s100710050046
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the ability of 16 domestic dogs and 4 horses to use human manual and facial cues in an object-choice task. While dogs showed strong performance, only one of the four horses successfully used pointing. The authors suggested that animals' use of human signals depends on cognitive ability, domestication, and individual enculturation.

papers.title: Dorey et al. 2014
papers.apa: Dorey, N. R., Conover, A. M., & Udell, M. A. R. (2014). Interspecific communication from people to horses (Equus ferus caballus) is influenced by different horsemanship training styles. Journal of Comparative Psychology, 128(4), 337–342. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0037255
papers.summary: This study examined how prior training methods influenced 20 horses' ability to follow a momentary distal point in an object-choice task. Initially, neither group (Parelli natural horsemanship vs. traditional) could follow the point, but Parelli-trained horses learned significantly faster with more experience. The findings suggest that individual experience and training history, rather than domestication alone, are crucial for this ability.

papers.title: Maros et al. 2008
papers.apa: Maros, K., Gácsi, M., & Miklósi, Á. (2008). Comprehension of human pointing gestures in horses (Equus caballus). Animal Cognition, 11(3), 457–466. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-008-0136-5
papers.summary: This study tested 20 domestic horses (14 horses, 13 ponies) on their ability to use different human pointing gestures in a two-way object choice task. Horses successfully used distal dynamic-sustained, proximal momentary, and proximal dynamic-sustained pointing gestures, but failed to perform above chance with distal momentary pointing. The results suggest horses rely on cues with stimulus or local enhancement effects, with the comprehension of distal momentary pointing remaining unclear.

papers.title: Prendergast et al. (2016)
papers.apa: Prendergast, A. (Kit), Nansen, C., & Blache, D. (2016). Responses of Domestic Horses and Ponies to Single, Combined and Conflicting Visual and Auditory Cues. Journal of Equine Veterinary Science, 46, 40–46. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jevs.2016.06.080
papers.summary: This study investigated 20 domestic horses and ponies' responses to single, combined, and conflicting visual and auditory cues in an object-choice task. Subjects were equally successful at using single visual (proportion of correct choices: 0.63), auditory (0.61), and combined cues (0.64), with ponies outperforming horses using visual cues (P=.044). In conflicting cue tests, 70% responded randomly, suggesting horses can use both modalities but don't necessarily benefit from combined cues.

papers.title: Proops et al. 2010
papers.apa: Proops, L., Walton, M., & McComb, K. (2010). The use of human-given cues by domestic horses, Equus caballus, during an object choice task. Animal Behaviour, 79(6), 1205–1209. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.anbehav.2010.02.015
papers.summary: This study tested 28 horses on their ability to use five different human-given cues in an object-choice task. Horses spontaneously used distal sustained pointing and marker placement cues (P < 0.0001, P = 0.001 respectively) but not momentary tapping, body orientation, or gaze alternation. The authors concluded that horses likely use stimulus enhancement rather than understanding communicative intent, positioning their ability below dogs but similar to goats.

papers.title: Proops et al. 2013
papers.apa: Proops, L., Rayner, J., Taylor, A. M., & McComb, K. (2013). The Responses of Young Domestic Horses to Human-Given Cues. PLoS ONE, 8(6), e67000. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0067000
papers.summary: This study investigated the ontogeny of human cue-reading skills in young domestic horses, comparing them to adults. Across two object-choice experiments, young horses performed similarly to adults, using marker placement, pointing, and touching cues, but not body orientation, gaze, elbow pointing, or tapping. The findings suggest that horses' limited use of communicative cues in object-choice tasks reflects a general learning ability related to stimulus enhancement, rather than an innate predisposition.

papers.title: Ringhofer et al. 2021
papers.apa: Ringhofer, M., Trösch, M., Lansade, L., & Yamamoto, S. (2021). Horses with sustained attention follow the pointing of a human who knows where food is hidden. Scientific Reports, 11(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-95727-8
papers.summary: This study investigated whether 36 horses could evaluate the credibility of human pointing by manipulating informants' attentional states during food-hiding. Results showed that horses with sustained high attention levels could evaluate credibility and followed the pointing of an informant who knew where food was hidden (Z = -2.281, P = 0.002). This suggests horses are sensitive to human attentional states and perceive pointing as a communicative cue, highlighting the importance of motivation.

papers.title: The effect of domestication and ontogeny in swine cognition (Sus scrofa scrofa and S. s. domestica)
papers.apa: Albiach-Serrano, A., Bräuer, J., Cacchione, T., Zickert, N., & Amici, F. (2012). The effect of domestication and ontogeny in swine cognition (Sus scrofa scrofa and S. s. domestica). Applied Animal Behaviour Science, 141(1–2), 25–35. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.applanim.2012.07.005
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the effects of domestication and ontogeny on swine cognition using an object choice paradigm with physical and social cues (touching, pointing, gazing). For domestic pigs, the study found no evidence that they performed above chance level in social tasks, including pointing, suggesting they did not utilize human social cues to find hidden food. The authors concluded that specific experience with stimuli, rather than general experience, explained performance.

papers.title: Nawroth et al. 2014
papers.apa: Nawroth, C., Ebersbach, M., & von Borell, E. (2013). Juvenile domestic pigs (Sus scrofa domestica) use human-given cues in an object choice task. Animal Cognition, 17(3), 701–713. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-013-0702-3
papers.summary: This study investigated the use of human-given cues by 17 young domestic pigs in an object choice task. Pigs successfully used proximal pointing cues (e.g., PDS-G: t(16)=9.53, P<0.001) and were able to utilize human body and head orientation to locate a hidden reward. However, they failed with distal pointing and when gaze was directed into distant space, suggesting some constraints on their abilities.

papers.title: Nawroth et al. 2016
papers.apa: Nawroth, C., Ebersbach, M., & von Borell, E. (2016). Are domestic pigs (Sus scrofa domestica) able to use complex humangiven cues to find a hidden reward? Animal Welfare, 25(2), 185–190. https://doi.org/10.7120/09627286.25.2.185
papers.summary: This study, with a small sample of four domestic pigs, explored their ability to use complex human-given cues in an object-choice paradigm. All four pigs successfully used a long cross pointing gesture, and one individual demonstrated the ability to use head orientation to find a hidden reward. These findings provide further evidence for pigs' capacity to utilize certain human social cues.

papers.title: The effect of domestication and ontogeny in swine cognition (Sus scrofa scrofa and S. s. domestica)
papers.apa: Albiach-Serrano, A., Bräuer, J., Cacchione, T., Zickert, N., & Amici, F. (2012). The effect of domestication and ontogeny in swine cognition (Sus scrofa scrofa and S. s. domestica). Applied Animal Behaviour Science, 141(1–2), 25–35. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.applanim.2012.07.005
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the cognitive abilities of wild boars and domestic pigs in object-choice tasks using various cues, including human pointing. It found that while wild boars with specific experience could use human pointing gestures to locate food, domesticated pigs did not perform above chance level in the pointing task. The authors concluded that specific experience, rather than general domestication, explains success in such tasks.

papers.title: Bensoussan et al. 2016
papers.apa: Bensoussan, S., Cornil, M., Meunier-Salaün, M.-C., & Tallet, C. (2016). Piglets Learn to Use Combined Human-Given Visual and Auditory Signals to Find a Hidden Reward in an Object Choice Task. PLOS ONE, 11(10), e0164988. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0164988
papers.summary: This study investigated whether piglets (N=16) could use human pointing gestures, voice, or a combination of both to find a hidden reward. Initially, piglets did not spontaneously use these signals, but they learned to use a combination of auditory and visual signals (pointing and voice) over successive tests. The authors concluded that a combination of signals is necessary for piglets to succeed, highlighting a learning component.

papers.title: Gerencsér et al. 2019
papers.apa: Gerencsér, L., Pérez Fraga, P., Lovas, M., Újváry, D., & Andics, A. (2019). Comparing interspecific socio-communicative skills of socialized juvenile dogs and miniature pigs. Animal Cognition, 22(6), 917–929. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-019-01284-z
papers.summary: This comparative study assessed the socio-communicative skills of highly socialized juvenile miniature pigs (N=9) and dogs (N=9) in an object-choice task. It found that only dogs spontaneously relied on a human distal dynamic-sustained pointing gesture to locate a hidden food reward, while pigs developed a side bias and did not follow the pointing gesture. The results suggest species predispositions influence the success of spontaneously responding to human pointing.

papers.title: Nawroth et al. 2014
papers.apa: Nawroth, C., Ebersbach, M., & von Borell, E. (2013). Juvenile domestic pigs (Sus scrofa domestica) use human-given cues in an object choice task. Animal Cognition, 17(3), 701–713. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-013-0702-3
papers.summary: This study investigated the ability of young domestic pigs (N=17) to use human-given cues in an object-choice task. It found that pigs were able to use proximal pointing gestures (both momentary and dynamic-sustained) and, with some limitations, distal pointing, performing significantly above chance in these conditions (e.g., PDS-G: t16 = 9.53; P < 0.001). The authors concluded that domestic pigs are skillful in utilizing various human cues, contrasting with earlier findings that suggested they could not.

papers.title: Nawroth et al. 2016
papers.apa: Nawroth, C., Ebersbach, M., & von Borell, E. (2016). Are domestic pigs (Sus scrofa domestica) able to use complex humangiven cues to find a hidden reward? Animal Welfare, 25(2), 185–190. https://doi.org/10.7120/09627286.25.2.185
papers.summary: This study further explored the ability of domestic pigs (N=4) to use complex human-given cues, including various pointing gestures. It found that pigs could successfully use certain types of pointing, such as a long cross pointing, and in a second experiment, all four pigs used a pointing gesture to find a hidden reward behind them. The findings provide additional evidence for pigs' ability to use novel human-given cues, while also highlighting limitations.

papers.title: Domestic goats, Capra hircus, follow gaze direction and use social cues in an object choice task
papers.apa: Kaminski, J., Riedel, J., Call, J., & Tomasello, M. (2005). Domestic goats, Capra hircus, follow gaze direction and use social cues in an object choice task. Animal Behaviour, 69(1), 11–18. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.anbehav.2004.05.008
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated goats' ability to use social cues. In an object choice task, goats successfully used human communicative cues like touching and pointing to find hidden food, with 14 subjects above chance in Touch and 5 in Point, but did not use gaze alone. The authors suggested this ability, similar to dogs, might be a side-effect of domestication.

papers.title: Nawroth et al. 2015
papers.apa: Nawroth, C., von Borell, E., & Langbein, J. (2014). ‘Goats that stare at men’: dwarf goats alter their behaviour in response to human head orientation, but do not spontaneously use head direction as a cue in a food-related context. Animal Cognition, 18(1), 65–73. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-014-0777-5
papers.summary: This study (N=11 female Nigerian dwarf goats) investigated goats' use of human cues in a food-related context. In an object choice task, goats successfully used 'Touch' and 'Point' cues to find hidden food, mirroring Kaminski et al. (2005), but did not perform above chance with 'Head only' cues. Unlike the original, they found performance in the 'Point' condition increased over time, suggesting learning, and no side bias.

papers.title: Nawroth et al. 2020
papers.summary: This study investigated the use of different human pointing gestures by goats in an object choice task. Goats succeeded in following proximal and crossed pointing gestures, where the experimenter's finger was closer to the target, but not asymmetric pointing where the experimenter was further away. The authors suggest this indicates reliance on stimulus/local enhancement rather than referential information, and found no evidence of learning over time.

papers.title: Domestic goats, Capra hircus, follow gaze direction and use social cues in an object choice task
papers.apa: Kaminski, J., Riedel, J., Call, J., & Tomasello, M. (2005). Domestic goats, Capra hircus, follow gaze direction and use social cues in an object choice task. Animal Behaviour, 69(1), 11–18. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.anbehav.2004.05.008
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated gaze following in domestic goats. It found that goats successfully followed the gaze of conspecifics to locate objects. However, in an object choice task with a human experimenter, goats used human communicative cues like touching and pointing to find food, but did not use human gaze direction alone (only 1 out of 23 subjects performed above chance in the gaze condition).

papers.title: Schaffer et al. 2020
papers.apa: Schaffer, A., Caicoya, A. L., Colell, M., Holland, R., Ensenyat, C., & Amici, F. (2020). Gaze Following in Ungulates: Domesticated and Non-domesticated Species Follow the Gaze of Both Humans and Conspecifics in an Experimental Context. Frontiers in Psychology, 11. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2020.604904
papers.summary: This study investigated gaze following in four ungulate species, including domestic goats, using an experimental approach. It found that all tested species, including goats, followed the gaze of both human experimenters and conspecifics, although they were more likely to follow conspecific gaze. This study provided evidence for gaze following abilities in both domesticated and non-domesticated ungulates, despite a relatively low number of study subjects.

papers.title: The role of cat eye narrowing movements in cat–human communication
papers.apa: Vonk, J. (2022). Faculty Opinions recommendation of The role of cat eye narrowing movements in cat-human communication. [dataset]. In Faculty Opinions – Post-Publication Peer Review of the Biomedical Literature. H1 Connect. https://doi.org/10.3410/f.738798370.793596909
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether cats respond to human slow blinking with their own eye narrowing movements. Across two experiments, the authors found that cats were significantly more likely to perform slow blinks towards humans who were slow blinking compared to a neutral gaze, suggesting this behavior facilitates positive cat-human communication (Experiment 1: d=0.56; Experiment 2: d=0.75, d=0.90).

papers.title: Humphreys et al. 2020b
papers.apa: Humphrey, T., Stringer, F., Proops, L., & McComb, K. (2020). Slow Blink Eye Closure in Shelter Cats Is Related to Quicker Adoption. Animals, 10(12), 2256. https://doi.org/10.3390/ani10122256
papers.summary: This replication study aimed to further investigate the slow blink effect in a controlled setting. The authors found that cats did indeed respond with increased eye narrowing to human slow blinks compared to control conditions, largely replicating the original findings with similar effect sizes (slow blinking vs. control trials: d=0.71; slow blinking vs. neutral trials: d=0.52).

papers.title: [n = 24
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2012). Exemplar Abstract for Burkholderia schumannianae (sic) Lemaire et al. 2012, Burkholderia schumanniana corrig. Lemaire et al. 2012, Paraburkholderia schumanniana corrig. (Lemaire et al. 2012) Sawana et al. 2014 and Paraburkholderia schumannianae (sic) (Lemaire et al. 2012) Sawana et al. 2014. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.22800
papers.summary: No information provided for this paper beyond a partial title and sample size; cannot summarize its contribution.

papers.title: ‘A Comparative Study of the Use of Visual Communicative Signals in Interactions Between Dogs (Canis familiaris) and Humans and Cats (Felis catus) and Humans’
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated the ability of domestic cats and dogs to follow human communicative cues, specifically pointing gestures, in an object choice task. It established that while dogs readily follow pointing, cats generally do not, indicating a species-specific difference in socio-cognitive abilities. The original effect size for cats was reported as W = 0.37, suggesting a weak or absent response to pointing.

papers.title: Mäses and Wascher 2022 [preprint, n =
papers.apa: 24. Lecture 24. (1998). Afternotes Goes to Graduate School, 205–211. https://doi.org/10.1137/1.9781611971422.ch24
papers.summary: This replication study aimed to re-examine the ability of felines to follow human pointing gestures. The authors reported strong effect sizes (ipsilateral pointing: h = 1.287, cross-body pointing: h = 0.823), which, in line with the original finding, are interpreted as confirming that cats do not reliably follow human pointing cues in object choice tasks.

papers.title: ‘Mirror-induced behavior in the magpie (Pica pica): evidence of self-recognition’
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated mirror-induced behavior in magpies, reporting that 2 out of 5 tested animals showed self-recognition during a mark test. These findings were interpreted as evidence for self-awareness in magpies, suggesting a capacity for self-representation.

papers.title: Gallup and Anderson 2020
papers.apa: Sharma, S. (2022). This is test 9 Nov 2022 (Preprint). https://doi.org/10.2196/preprints.44138
papers.summary: This replication attempt by Gallup and Anderson (2020) aimed to reproduce the original findings of mirror-self recognition in magpies. Their study found no reproducible evidence that magpies can recognize themselves in the mirror, casting doubt on the original claim.

papers.title: Soler et al. 2020
papers.apa: Freeberg, T. M. (2020). On mark-test replication and mirror self-recognition in magpies. Journal of Comparative Psychology, 134(4), 361–362. https://doi.org/10.1037/com0000256
papers.summary: Soler et al. (2020) conducted another replication of the magpie mirror-self recognition effect, specifically focusing on the mark test. Their descriptive results indicated that no animals exhibited self-directed behavior in front of the mirror, further failing to support the original finding.

papers.effect_name: Right-bias in hand use in chimpanzees
papers.title: Chimpanzee Hand Preference in Throwing and Infant Cradling: Implications for the Origin of Human Handedness
papers.apa: Hopkins, W. D., Bard, K. A., Jones, A., & Bales, S. L. (1993). Chimpanzee Hand Preference in Throwing and Infant Cradling: Implications for the Origin of Human Handedness. Current Anthropology, 34(5), 786–790. https://doi.org/10.1086/204224
papers.summary: This foundational paper likely presented initial evidence for a population-level right-hand bias in chimpanzees, possibly focusing on specific tasks like throwing or infant cradling, and drawing parallels to human handedness, with reported original effect sizes ranging from d = 0.69 to 0.97.

papers.effect_name: Right-bias in hand use in chimpanzees
papers.title: Corballis 2003
papers.apa: Parker, C. T., & Garrity, G. M. (2020). Exemplar Abstract for Alkalihalobacillus caeni (Mo et al. 2020) Gupta et al. 2020, Bacillus caeni Mo et al. 2020 and Pseudalkalibacillus caeni (Mo et al. 2020) Joshi et al. 2022. [dataset]. In C. T. Parker & G. M. Garrity (Eds.), The NamesforLife Abstracts. NamesforLife, LLC. https://doi.org/10.1601/ex.36742
papers.summary: This paper likely argues against the concept of population-level handedness in chimpanzees, proposing instead that such population-level asymmetries in handedness are a uniquely human feature, thereby directly challenging the core claim of the effect.

papers.effect_name: Right-bias in hand use in chimpanzees
papers.title: Hopkins et al. 1994
papers.apa: Hopkins, W. D. (1994). Hand preferences for bimanual feeding in 140 captive chimpanzees (Pan troglodytes): Rearing and ontogenetic determinants. Developmental Psychobiology, 27(6), 395–407. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/dev.420270607
papers.summary: This study assessed hand preference for bimanual feeding in 140 captive chimpanzees (Pan troglodytes). It found that adults and mother-reared chimpanzees were more lateralized, and among those with a significant hand preference, a larger proportion exhibited a right-hand preference. The study reported an effect size of d = 0.52 for this preference.

papers.effect_name: Right-bias in hand use in chimpanzees
papers.title: Palmer 2002
papers.apa: Palmer, A. R. (2002). Chimpanzee right‐handedness reconsidered: Evaluating the evidence with funnel plots. American Journal of Physical Anthropology, 118(2), 191–199. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1002/ajpa.10063
papers.summary: Palmer (2002) re-examined evidence for population-level right-handedness in chimpanzees, including the Hopkins (1994) study, using funnel plots. The analysis suggested that reported right-sided asymmetries might be statistical artifacts, particularly from animals with fewer observations, and that statistical support for population-level right-handedness became marginal or disappeared with more robust analysis. The paper concluded that current evidence for population-level right-handedness in chimpanzees remains equivocal.

papers.effect_name: Cache protection in Eurasian jays (_Garrulus glandarius_)
papers.title: Eurasian jays (Garrulus glandarius) conceal caches from onlookers
papers.apa: Legg, E. W., & Clayton, N. S. (2014). Eurasian jays (Garrulus glandarius) conceal caches from onlookers. Animal Cognition, 17(5), 1223–1226. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-014-0743-2
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether Eurasian jays would strategically cache food behind an opaque barrier when observed by a conspecific, compared to caching in private. The authors found that jays cached a significantly greater proportion of food in out-of-view locations when an observer was present, suggesting a strategy to prevent pilfering. The exact number of cacher birds (N) and effect sizes were not explicitly reported in the provided text.

papers.effect_name: Cache protection in Eurasian jays (_Garrulus glandarius_)
papers.title: Amodio et al. 2022
papers.apa: Corballis, M. C. (2003). From mouth to hand: Gesture, speech, and the evolution of right-handedness. Behavioral and Brain Sciences, 26(2), 199–208. https://doi.org/10.1017/s0140525x03000062
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.effect_name: Desire-state attribution may govern food sharing in Eurasian jays (_Garrulus glandarius_)
papers.title: Evidence suggesting that desire-state attribution may govern food sharing in Eurasian jays  (Garrulus glandarius)
papers.apa: Ostojić, L., Shaw, R. C., Cheke, L. G., & Clayton, N. S. (2013). Evidence suggesting that desire-state attribution may govern food sharing in Eurasian jays. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 110(10), 4123–4128. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1209926110
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated whether male Eurasian jays share food with their female partners based on the female's current desire state. The authors found that males flexibly fed their mates according to the female's food preference, but only if they had observed what the female had previously eaten. This suggests that males might be capable of attributing desire to their mates, rather than simply responding to overt behavioral cues or their own desires.

papers.effect_name: Reasoning about hidden causal agents in New Caledonian Crows (_Corvus moneduloides_)
papers.title: New Caledonian crows reason about hidden causal agents
papers.apa: Taylor, A. H., Miller, R., & Gray, R. D. (2012). New Caledonian crows reason about hidden causal agents. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 109(40), 16389–16391. https://doi.org/10.1073/pnas.1208724109
papers.summary: Taylor et al. (2012) investigated whether eight New Caledonian crows could reason about hidden causal agents. They found that crows inspected a hide and abandoned probing for food more frequently when a stick moved without an observable human agent, compared to when a human was visibly present or absent. This difference was interpreted as evidence that crows infer the presence of a hidden causal agent.

papers.effect_name: Reasoning about hidden causal agents in New Caledonian Crows (_Corvus moneduloides_)
papers.title: O’Neill et al. 2021
papers.apa: O’Neill, L., Linder, G., van Buuren, M., & von Bayern, A. M. P. (2021). New Caledonian Crows and Hidden Causal Agents Revisited. Animal Behavior and Cognition, 8(2), 166–189. https://doi.org/10.26451/abc.08.02.06.2021
papers.summary: O'Neill et al. (2021) conducted a replication attempt of the original study. The paper reportedly found no evidence for the effect, and no effect size was reported.

papers.effect_name: Owners predict spatial impulsivity in dogs
papers.title: A spatial discounting test to assess impulsivity in dogs
papers.apa: Brady, K., Hewison, L., Wright, H., Zulch, H., Cracknell, N., & Mills, D. (2018). A spatial discounting test to assess impulsivity in dogs. Applied Animal Behaviour Science, 202, 77–84. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.applanim.2018.01.003
papers.summary: This foundational study developed a Spatial Discounting Task (SDT) to measure impulsivity in dogs. It found that in adult dogs, owner ratings of impulsivity (DIAS) significantly correlated with the maximum distance traveled in the SDT (r = -0.46 in Lab 1, r = -0.61 in a simplified field study), indicating convergent validity. However, no such relationship was found in young dogs (r = -0.05 in Lab 2).

papers.effect_name: Owners predict spatial impulsivity in dogs
papers.title: Mongillo et al. 2019
papers.apa: Mongillo, P., Scandurra, A., Eatherington, C. J., D’Aniello, B., & Marinelli, L. (2019). Development of a Spatial Discount Task to Measure Impulsive Choices in Dogs. Animals, 9(7), 469. https://doi.org/10.3390/ani9070469
papers.summary: This study developed a spatial discount task to assess impulsive choices in dogs (N=48), where dogs chose between smaller, closer and larger, more distant rewards. While it validated its own task, the study reported no significant correlation (r = 0.01) between owner ratings of impulsivity and the dogs' choices in the spatial discounting task. This finding did not support the link between owner perception and behavioral measures of impulsivity.

papers.effect_name: Owners predict spatial impulsivity in dogs
papers.title: Stevens et al. 2022
papers.apa: Stevens, J., Mathias, M., Herridge, M., Hughes-Duvall, K., Wolff, L., & Yohe, M. (2022). Do Owners Know How Impulsive Their Dogs Are? Animal Behavior and Cognition, 9(3), 261–286. https://doi.org/10.26451/abc.09.03.02.2022
papers.summary: This study directly aimed to replicate previous findings by correlating owner DIAS scores with dogs' performance in a spatial impulsivity task across two studies. They found no significant correlation between owner ratings and the maximum distance traveled (Study 1: r = -0.10; Study 2: r = 0.04), with a meta-analysis yielding r = -0.11. This result failed to replicate the original Brady et al. effect, aligning instead with Mongillo et al.'s null finding and questioning the generalizability of owner reports.

papers.effect_name: Numerical ratio effects on quantity discrimination in elephants
papers.title: Relative quantity judgment by Asian elephants (Elephas maximus)
papers.apa: Irie-Sugimoto, N., Kobayashi, T., Sato, T., & Hasegawa, T. (2008). Relative quantity judgment by Asian elephants (Elephas maximus). Animal Cognition, 12(1), 193–199. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-008-0185-9
papers.summary: This study investigated relative quantity judgment in 9 Asian elephants, presenting them with simultaneous and sequential quantities of bait. The authors found that elephants could discriminate quantities but, notably, did not exhibit disparity or magnitude effects, meaning performance was not predicted by the numerical ratio. Individual correlations for the ratio effect were very low (r = 0.23, 0.01, 0.02), leading them to suggest elephants might use a different mechanism for quantity comparison than other species.

papers.effect_name: Numerical ratio effects on quantity discrimination in elephants
papers.title: Perdue et al. 2012
papers.apa: Perdue, B. M., Talbot, C. F., Stone, A. M., & Beran, M. J. (2012). Putting the elephant back in the herd: elephant relative quantity judgments match those of other species. Animal Cognition, 15(5), 955–961. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-012-0521-y
papers.summary: This replication study tested two African elephants to re-examine relative quantity judgment, addressing methodological concerns from the original study. In contrast to the previous findings, the authors found clear evidence of ratio effects for both visible and nonvisible sequentially presented food sets, with performance decreasing as the ratio between quantities increased. Individual correlations were strong and negative (r = -0.64, -0.96), suggesting elephants process quantities similarly to other species, consistent with an accumulator model.

papers.title: Dogs catch human yawns
papers.apa: Joly-Mascheroni, R. M., Senju, A., & Shepherd, A. J. (2008). Dogs catch human yawns. Biology Letters, 4(5), 446–448. https://doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2008.0333
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated contagious yawning in dogs, finding that 21 out of 29 dogs (72%) yawned after observing a human yawn, whereas none yawned after control mouth movements. The authors proposed this cross-species phenomenon suggests a rudimentary form of empathy in dogs and may help coordinate dog-human interaction.

papers.title: Harr et al. 2009
papers.apa: Harr, A. L., Gilbert, V. R., & Phillips, K. A. (2009). Do dogs (Canis familiaris) show contagious yawning? Animal Cognition, 12(6), 833–837. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-009-0233-0
papers.summary: This study with 15 dogs investigated contagious yawning using video stimuli of humans and dogs yawning or making open-mouth expressions. Only one dog (6%) showed significantly more yawns during or after yawning videos, primarily to human videos, with no significant effects at the group level. The authors concluded that contagious yawning in dogs, if present, occurs at a much lower rate than previously reported.

papers.title: O’Hara and Reeve 2010
papers.apa: O’Hara, S. J., & Reeve, A. V. (2011). A test of the yawning contagion and emotional connectedness hypothesis in dogs, Canis familiaris. Animal Behaviour, 81(1), 335–340. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.anbehav.2010.11.005
papers.summary: This study with 22 dogs attempted to replicate contagious yawning and test the emotional connectedness hypothesis, controlling for stress. While five of 19 dogs yawned in response to an unfamiliar human, no statistically significant differences in yawning contagion were found based on familiarity, similarity, or emotional closeness. The findings cast doubt on empathy-based contagious yawning in dogs, suggesting any observed contagion is likely due to less cognitively stringent mechanisms.

papers.title: Madsen and Persson 2012
papers.apa: Madsen, E. A., & Persson, T. (2012). Contagious yawning in domestic dog puppies (Canis lupus familiaris): the effect of ontogeny and emotional closeness on low-level imitation in dogs. Animal Cognition, 16(2), 233–240. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10071-012-0568-9
papers.summary: This study with 35 dog puppies (4-14 months) confirmed that dogs yawn contagiously after observing a human yawn, with 69% of dogs above 7 months evidencing contagion. However, emotional closeness to the model did not affect the strength of contagion. The study supports a developmental increase in susceptibility to yawn contagion in dogs, similar to humans, and suggests the original findings were not due to tension yawns.

papers.effect_name: Temporal preferences in chimpanzees and bonobos
papers.title: The evolutionary origins of human patience: temporal preferences in chimpanzees, bonobos, and human adults
papers.apa: Rosati, A. G., Stevens, J. R., Hare, B., & Hauser, M. D. (2007). The Evolutionary Origins of Human Patience: Temporal Preferences in Chimpanzees, Bonobos, and Human Adults. Current Biology, 17(19), 1663–1668. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cub.2007.08.033
papers.summary: This foundational study investigated temporal preferences in 5 chimpanzees and 5 bonobos at the Wolfgang Köhler Primate Research Center. It found that chimpanzees were more patient than bonobos, waiting longer for larger, delayed food rewards, with an estimated effect size of d=1.89. The authors suggested that this capacity for patience evolved before the human lineage split and might be influenced by ecological factors like food availability.

papers.effect_name: Temporal preferences in chimpanzees and bonobos
papers.title: Rosati and Hare 2013
papers.apa: Rosati, A. G., & Hare, B. (2013). Chimpanzees and Bonobos Exhibit Emotional Responses to Decision Outcomes. PLoS ONE, 8(5), e63058. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0063058
papers.summary: This replication study examined temporal and risk preferences in a larger sample of 23 chimpanzees and 15 bonobos at sanctuaries in the Republic of Congo. It confirmed the original finding that chimpanzees were more patient than bonobos, with an estimated effect size of d=0.68. The study also explored emotional responses to decision outcomes, noting that chimpanzees exhibited more intense negative reactions while waiting, suggesting an interplay between cognition and emotion in their decision-making.

papers.effect_name: Risk preferences in chimpanzees and bonobos
papers.title: A fruit in the hand or two in the bush? Divergent risk preferences in chimpanzees and bonobos
papers.apa: Heilbronner, S. R., Rosati, A. G., Stevens, J. R., Hare, B., & Hauser, M. D. (2008). A fruit in the hand or two in the bush? Divergent risk preferences in chimpanzees and bonobos. Biology Letters, 4(3), 246–249. https://doi.org/10.1098/rsbl.2008.0081
papers.summary: This foundational study experimentally examined risk-sensitive behavior in chimpanzees and bonobos, predicting greater risk tolerance in chimpanzees due to their exploitation of riskier food sources. Results from a sample at the Leipzig Zoo confirmed this, showing chimpanzees significantly preferred the risky option while bonobos preferred the fixed option. The authors concluded that ecological pressures can shape economic decision-making, providing an example of risk-prone behavior in the context of gains.

papers.effect_name: Risk preferences in chimpanzees and bonobos

papers.summary: This study investigated temporal and risk preferences, alongside emotional responses, in 38 semi-free-ranging apes (23 chimpanzees, 15 bonobos) at sanctuaries in Congo. Consistent with previous work, they found that chimpanzees were more risk-prone than bonobos. The study also revealed that both species exhibit affective and motivational responses to decision outcomes, suggesting that emotional processes play a role in ape decision-making, similar to humans.

papers.summary: This study experimentally tested the empirical claims of interest-relative invariantism, specifically Stanley's "bank cases," using 241 and 298 university students across two experiments. While they found a significant effect of stakes on participants' confidence in knowledge attribution (F(1,237)=4.36, p=.04), they concluded that raising stakes did not typically lead people to deny knowledge. Their findings challenged the common-sense judgments predicted by both Stanley and Schaffer, suggesting practical interests affect confidence but not outright knowledge attribution.

papers.title: Sripada and Stanley (2012)
papers.apa: Sripada, C. S., & Stanley, J. (2012). EMPIRICAL TESTS OF INTEREST-RELATIVE INVARIANTISM. Episteme, 9(1), 3–26. https://doi.org/10.1017/epi.2011.2
papers.summary: This study aimed to empirically test Interest-Relative Invariantism (IRI), which posits that knowledge attribution is influenced by the practical costs of being wrong. The authors presented their own experimental results, which they claim provide strong support for IRI. They reported effect sizes (Cohen's d) of 0.34 for the Implicit/Explicit vignette, 0.36 for the Ignorant vignette, and 0.03 for the Basic vignette, indicating varying degrees of support for the stakes effect across different scenarios.

papers.effect_name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait anxiety/neuroticism on conditioning
papers.title: Eysenck, 1962
papers.apa: EYSENCK, H. J. (1962). CONDITIONING AND PERSONALITY. British Journal of Psychology, 53(3), 299–305. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2044-8295.1962.tb00835.x
papers.summary: This paper is a theoretical discussion on the application of learning theory to personality, responding to a critique. It does not present empirical data on the effect of trait anxiety or neuroticism on fear conditioning, but rather discusses methodological and theoretical issues in the field of personality and conditioning.

papers.effect_name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait anxiety/neuroticism on conditioning
papers.title: Gazendam et al. 2015
papers.apa: Gazendam, F. J., Kamphuis, J. H., Eigenhuis, A., Huizenga, H. M. H., Soeter, M., Bos, M. G. N., Sevenster, D., & Kindt, M. (2014). Personality Predicts Individual Variation in Fear Learning. Clinical Psychological Science, 3(2), 175–188. https://doi.org/10.1177/2167702614535914
papers.summary: This study (N=225 for conditioning) investigated how personality traits like Stress Reaction and Harmavoidance relate to individual variation in fear learning. It found that higher Stress Reaction was associated with weaker discrimination between threat and safety cues during fear conditioning. Additionally, both retention of differential fear acquisition and extinction were weaker with high levels of Stress Reaction and Harmavoidance, indicating maladaptive fear learning.

papers.effect_name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait anxiety/neuroticism on conditioning
papers.title: Sperl et al. 2016
papers.apa: Sperl, M. F. J., Panitz, C., Hermann, C., & Mueller, E. M. (2016). A pragmatic comparison of noise burst and electric shock unconditioned stimuli for fear conditioning research with many trials. Psychophysiology, 53(9), 1352–1365. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/psyp.12677
papers.summary: This study (N=32) compared the effectiveness of white noise bursts versus electric shocks as unconditioned stimuli in a high-trial differential fear conditioning paradigm. It concluded that white noise bursts led to more reliable, extinction-resistant, and stable conditioning. The paper's focus is on methodological considerations for US selection, not the influence of personality traits on fear conditioning.

papers.effect_name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait anxiety/neuroticism on conditioning
papers.title: Panitz et al. 2018
papers.apa: Panitz, C., Sperl, M. F. J., Hennig, J., Klucken, T., Hermann, C., & Mueller, E. M. (2018). Fearfulness, neuroticism/anxiety, and COMT Val158Met in long-term fear conditioning and extinction. Neurobiology of Learning and Memory, 155, 7–20. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nlm.2018.06.001
papers.summary: Summary not based on provided paper content — needs manual verification.

papers.effect_name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait anxiety/neuroticism on conditioning
papers.title: Sjouwerman et al. (2020
papers.apa: Sjouwerman, R., Scharfenort, R., & Lonsdorf, T. B. (2020). Individual differences in fear acquisition: multivariate analyses of different emotional negativity scales, physiological responding, subjective measures, and neural activation. Scientific Reports, 10(1). https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-020-72007-5
papers.summary: This research (Study 1: N=356; Study 2: N=113) explored the relationship between negative emotionality (trait anxiety, neuroticism, intolerance of uncertainty) and individual differences in fear acquisition. Study 1 identified specific negative associations between trait anxiety and skin conductance response discrimination, and intolerance of uncertainty and startle response discrimination. Study 2 found that trait anxiety was positively associated with CS-discrimination in fear-related brain regions (amygdala, putamen, thalamus), but not consistently with peripheral measures.

papers.effect_name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait anxiety/neuroticism on conditioning
papers.title: Torrents-Rodas et al. (2012
papers.apa: Torrents-Rodas, D., Fullana, M. A., Bonillo, A., Caseras, X., Andión, O., & Torrubia, R. (2013). No effect of trait anxiety on differential fear conditioning or fear generalization. Biological Psychology, 92(2), 185–190. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biopsycho.2012.10.006
papers.summary: This study (N=126) investigated the role of trait anxiety in fear acquisition and generalization using fear-potentiated startle, skin conductance response, and risk ratings. Contrary to their hypotheses, the researchers found no significant effect of trait anxiety on either the acquisition or generalization of fear. They suggest that previous inconsistencies in the literature might be attributable to methodological differences such as task ambiguity or reinforcement rates.

papers.effect_name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait extraversion on conditioning
papers.title: Eysenck, 1962
papers.apa: EYSENCK, H. J. (1962). CONDITIONING AND PERSONALITY. British Journal of Psychology, 53(3), 299–305. Portico. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2044-8295.1962.tb00835.x
papers.summary: This paper is a theoretical discussion on the application of learning theory to personality, specifically addressing a critique of Eysenck's work. It does not present novel empirical data on fear conditioning or specific effect sizes related to extraversion.

papers.effect_name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait extraversion on conditioning
papers.title: Martinez et al. (2012)
papers.apa: Martínez, K. G., Castro-Couch, M., Franco-Chaves, J. A., Ojeda-Arce, B., Segura, G., Milad, M. R., & Quirk, G. J. (2012). Correlations between psychological tests and physiological responses during fear conditioning and renewal. Biology of Mood & Anxiety Disorders, 2(1). https://doi.org/10.1186/2045-5380-2-16
papers.summary: This study investigated correlations between psychological tests and physiological responses (SCR) during fear conditioning, extinction, and renewal in healthy subjects. They found that lower levels of extraversion correlated with greater fear renewal 24 hours after extinction, suggesting this trait may protect against relapse. The study reported an r^2 of 0.15 for SCL and extraversion, likely related to the renewal finding.

papers.effect_name: Fear conditioning - effect of trait extraversion on conditioning
papers.title: Otto et al. (2007)
papers.apa: Otto, M. W., Leyro, T. M., Christian, K., Deveney, C. M., Reese, H., Pollack, M. H., & Orr, S. P. (2007). Prediction of “Fear” Acquisition in Healthy Control Participants in a De Novo Fear-Conditioning Paradigm. Behavior Modification, 31(1), 32–51. https://doi.org/10.1177/0145445506295054
papers.summary: This study assessed predictors of fear acquisition in 72 healthy adults, including personality dimensions like Extraversion. The authors found that higher levels of subsyndromal worry and lower behavioral avoidance predicted heightened conditionability. The abstract does not report specific findings or effect sizes for trait extraversion's direct effect on fear conditioning.
